parts.title: PART I: HEPATOLOGY

blocks.text: Chronic hepatitis B (CHB) = HBsAg positive for >6 months. Five phases:
Immune-tolerant: HBeAg+, HBV DNA ≥107 IU/mL, normal ALT
HBeAg-positive immune active: HBeAg+, ALT ≥2x ULN, HBV DNA ≥20,000 IU/mL
HBeAg-negative immune active: HBeAg−, ALT ≥2x ULN, HBV DNA ≥2,000 IU/mL
Inactive carrier: HBeAg−, normal ALT, HBV DNA 2,000 IU/mL
HBsAg-negative (resolved): HBV DNA undetectable ± anti-HBs
Vertical transmission (Asia/Africa), sexual/parenteral (Western)
Risk factors for progression: male sex, age >40, alcohol, HDV/HCV/HIV coinfection, family history of HCC
HBV is a DNA virus (Hepadnaviridae) with cccDNA in hepatocyte nucleus → persistent infection
Liver damage is immune-mediated (cytotoxic T-cells), NOT directly cytopathic
HBV integrates into host genome → HCC risk even without cirrhosis
Often asymptomatic; fatigue, RUQ discomfort
Acute: jaundice, nausea, fever (90% adults clear spontaneously; 90% neonates become chronic)
Extrahepatic: polyarteritis nodosa, membranous nephropathy, aplastic anemia
Triple panel: HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc (total)
If HBsAg+: HBeAg, anti-HBe, HBV DNA quantitative, ALT
Fibrosis assessment: FibroScan (transient elastography) or FIB-4
Acute HBV: IgM anti-HBc positive
AASLD ULN for ALT: male 35 U/L, female 25 U/L
APASL/EASL ULN: 40 U/L (sex-neutral)
Treatment indications vary by guideline (see table below)

rows: Cirrhosis + detectable HBV DNA → treat regardless of ALT

blocks.text: Acute HBV: supportive care; antivirals only if acute liver failure (jaundice + coagulopathy + encephalopathy) or protracted course >4 weeks
Check for coinfections: HDV, HCV, HIV
Determine phase of infection
Assess fibrosis (FibroScan/FIB-4)
If treatment indicated → start preferred NA
Monitor HBV DNA + ALT every 3–6 months
HCC surveillance: ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (if cirrhosis, or Asian male >40, Asian female >50, African >20, family history HCC)

rows: Avoid in renal/bone disease; safe in pregnancy
Better renal/bone profile; avoid if CrCl 15 not on dialysis
0.5 mg daily (1 mg if prior lamivudine)
Avoid in pregnancy; avoid if prior lamivudine resistance
Finite therapy; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis

blocks.text: Cirrhosis, HCC, hepatic decompensation
HBV reactivation with immunosuppression (rituximab, chemotherapy, steroids)
Extrahepatic: GN, PAN, aplastic anemia
15–40% of chronic carriers develop cirrhosis/HCC
HCC annual incidence: 0.5–1% (non-cirrhotic), 2–5% (cirrhotic)
HBsAg loss (functional cure): 1%/year on NA therapy
Acute liver failure: INR >1.5 + encephalopathy → urgent transplant evaluation
HBV flare with bilirubin >3 mg/dL → start antivirals immediately
HBV reactivation during immunosuppression → can be fatal
Q: When do you start antivirals in HBV? A: Cirrhosis + any detectable DNA; immune-active phase; before immunosuppression
Q: Can you stop antivirals? A: Generally indefinite; consider stopping if HBsAg loss + anti-HBs seroconversion sustained ≥12 months (non-cirrhotic only)
Q: Which antiviral in pregnancy? A: TDF (start at 28 weeks if DNA >200,000 IU/mL)
"Isolated anti-HBc" = past infection, occult HBV, or window period
HBV can cause HCC WITHOUT cirrhosis (unlike HCV)
Always check HBV status before rituximab, chemotherapy, or high-dose steroids
HBV has a glucocorticoid-responsive element → steroids increase viral replication
Forgetting to screen for HBV before immunosuppression
Using lamivudine monotherapy (high resistance rate ~70% at 5 years)
Not monitoring for HBV reactivation after stopping antivirals
Confusing "inactive carrier" with "immune-tolerant" phase

HBV = DNA virus, immune-mediated damage, 5 phases. Diagnose with triple panel. Treat immune-active phase and all cirrhotics with detectable DNA. First-line: TDF, TAF, or ETV. Screen for HCC every 6 months. Always check before immunosuppression. HBV can cause HCC without cirrhosis.
Top 10 Testable Facts:
HBV is a DNA virus; liver damage is immune-mediated
90% of neonates become chronic; 90% of adults clear
IgM anti-HBc = acute infection
Isolated anti-HBc = past infection or window period
All cirrhotics with detectable HBV DNA need treatment
TDF is safe in pregnancy; ETV is not
HCC screening: US ± AFP every 6 months
HBV can cause HCC without cirrhosis
Lamivudine has high resistance; avoid as monotherapy
Always screen HBV before rituximab/chemotherapy
Top 5 Ward-Round Pimping Questions:
What are the 5 phases of chronic HBV? (Answer above)
Which antiviral do you use in pregnancy? (TDF)
Can HBV cause HCC without cirrhosis? (Yes — DNA integrates into host genome)
What does "isolated anti-HBc" mean? (Past infection, window period, or occult HBV)
When do you NOT need to treat chronic HBV? (Inactive carrier: HBeAg−, normal ALT, DNA 2,000)
Most Common Management Mistakes:
Using lamivudine as first-line (high resistance)
Not screening for HBV before immunosuppression
Stopping antivirals prematurely → flare/decompensation
Not doing HCC surveillance in at-risk patients
Chronic HCV = HCV RNA detectable for >6 months. Six major genotypes (GT1–6). GT1 most common globally; GT3 associated with steatosis and more aggressive disease.
RNA virus (Flaviviridae); parenteral transmission (IVDU, blood products, needlestick)
Risk factors: IVDU (most common in developed countries), blood transfusion before 1992, tattoos, MSM with HIV
HCV is directly cytopathic AND immune-mediated
Chronic inflammation → fibrosis → cirrhosis (20–30% over 20–30 years)
HCV causes HCC only through cirrhosis (unlike HBV)
Usually asymptomatic until cirrhosis
Extrahepatic: mixed cryoglobulinemia (purpura, arthralgia, GN), porphyria cutanea tarda, lichen planus, lymphoma, insulin resistance
Screen: anti-HCV antibody
Confirm: HCV RNA (quantitative PCR)
If RNA+: genotype (may not be needed for pangenotypic regimens), fibrosis assessment
Anti-HCV+ but RNA− = cleared infection (spontaneous or treated)
Acute HCV: HCV RNA+ with recent risk factor, often anti-HCV negative initially (seroconversion 6–8 weeks)
Chronic HCV: HCV RNA+ for >6 months
Assess fibrosis stage (FibroScan, FIB-4)
Check for HBV coinfection (risk of HBV reactivation during/after HCV treatment)
Assess renal function, drug interactions
Confirm active infection (HCV RNA+)
Assess for cirrhosis
Check HBV status (HBsAg, anti-HBc)
Simplified treatment for treatment-naive, non-cirrhotic:
Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (G/P) x 8 weeks, OR
Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (SOF/VEL) x 12 weeks
Compensated cirrhosis: G/P x 8–12 weeks or SOF/VEL x 12 weeks
Decompensated cirrhosis: SOF/VEL ± ribavirin x 12 weeks (NO protease inhibitors)
SVR12 (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks post-treatment) = cure

rows: Pangenotypic; can use in decompensated cirrhosis

blocks.text: Cirrhosis, HCC, liver failure
Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, membranoproliferative GN
HBV reactivation during HCV treatment
SVR12 = >95% cure rate with DAAs
Post-SVR: if cirrhotic, continue HCC surveillance indefinitely
Reinfection possible (no protective immunity)
Decompensated cirrhosis: NO protease inhibitors (glecaprevir, voxilaprevir)
HBV coinfection: monitor for reactivation
Drug interactions: check with all medications (especially amiodarone → fatal bradycardia with sofosbuvir)
Q: Do you need to know the genotype before treating? A: Not necessarily with pangenotypic regimens (G/P or SOF/VEL)
Q: What is SVR12? A: Undetectable HCV RNA 12 weeks after completing treatment = cure
Q: Do you continue HCC screening after cure? A: Yes, if cirrhosis was present
HCV causes HCC ONLY through cirrhosis (unlike HBV)
Mixed cryoglobulinemia = think HCV
Sofosbuvir + amiodarone = fatal bradycardia (contraindicated)
Anti-HCV remains positive even after cure
Not checking HBV before treating HCV (reactivation risk)
Using protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis
Stopping HCC surveillance after SVR in cirrhotics
Not rechecking HCV RNA to confirm cure (SVR12)

HCV = RNA virus, 6 genotypes. Diagnose: anti-HCV → confirm with RNA. Treat all with DAAs (G/P or SOF/VEL). SVR12 = cure (>95%). No protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis. Check HBV before treatment. Continue HCC surveillance if cirrhotic. Cryoglobulinemia = think HCV.
Top 10 Testable Facts:
HCV is an RNA virus; causes HCC only through cirrhosis
Anti-HCV+ / RNA− = cleared infection
SVR12 = cure (>95% with DAAs)
Pangenotypic regimens: G/P or SOF/VEL
No protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis
Check HBV before treating HCV
Mixed cryoglobulinemia = HCV
Sofosbuvir + amiodarone = contraindicated (bradycardia)
Continue HCC surveillance post-SVR if cirrhotic
Anti-HCV stays positive even after cure
Cirrhosis = end-stage chronic liver disease with diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules. Two stages:
Compensated: no ascites, variceal bleeding, or encephalopathy
Decompensated: presence of ascites, variceal hemorrhage, hepatic encephalopathy, or jaundice
Most common causes: alcohol, HBV, HCV, MASLD/MASH
Others: autoimmune hepatitis, PBC, PSC, Wilson disease, hemochromatosis, alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency, Budd-Chiari
Chronic injury → stellate cell activation → collagen deposition → fibrosis → architectural distortion
Portal hypertension: increased intrahepatic resistance + splanchnic vasodilation
Hyperdynamic circulation → high cardiac output, low SVR
Compensated: may be asymptomatic; fatigue, spider angiomata, palmar erythema
Decompensated: ascites, jaundice, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatorenal syndrome
Signs: caput medusae, splenomegaly, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy, Dupuytren contracture (alcohol)
Labs: CBC (thrombocytopenia), LFTs, albumin, INR, bilirubin
Non-invasive fibrosis: FibroScan (>12.5 kPa = cirrhosis), FIB-4 (>3.25 suggests advanced fibrosis)
Imaging: US with Doppler (nodular liver, splenomegaly, ascites, portal vein flow)
Liver biopsy: gold standard but rarely needed
Etiology workup: viral serologies, autoimmune markers, iron studies, ceruloplasmin, alpha1-AT
Child-Pugh: A (5–6) = well-compensated; B (7–9) = significant functional compromise; C (10–15) = decompensated
Thrombocytopenia: splenic sequestration (portal HTN) + decreased thrombopoietin
Elevated INR: decreased synthetic function (NOT a bleeding risk indicator in cirrhosis — rebalanced hemostasis)
Low albumin: decreased synthesis → edema, ascites
AST > ALT (ratio >2): suggests alcoholic liver disease
Decompensated: ABC, IV access, type and screen
Variceal bleed: octreotide + antibiotics + urgent endoscopy
SBP: empiric antibiotics + albumin
Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + identify precipitant
Treat underlying cause (antiviral, alcohol cessation, etc.)
Screen for varices (EGD if platelets 150,000 or liver stiffness >20 kPa)
HCC surveillance: US ± AFP every 6 months
Vaccinations: HAV, HBV, pneumococcal, influenza
Avoid hepatotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, excess acetaminophen
Nutrition: 35 kcal/kg/day, protein 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day (do NOT restrict protein)
Transplant evaluation: MELD ≥15

rows: If prior SBP, ascitic protein 1.5 g/dL

blocks.text: Ascites, SBP, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, HRS, HCC, hepatopulmonary syndrome, portopulmonary hypertension, coagulopathy
Compensated cirrhosis: median survival >12 years
Decompensated: median survival 2 years
MELD score predicts 3-month mortality
Variceal hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability
Grade 3–4 hepatic encephalopathy
HRS with rising creatinine
ACLF (acute-on-chronic liver failure): organ failures + high short-term mortality
Q: Should you correct INR before paracentesis? A: NO — INR does not predict bleeding in cirrhosis; paracentesis is safe
Q: Should you restrict protein in hepatic encephalopathy? A: NO — protein restriction worsens sarcopenia and outcomes
Q: What is the 100:40 rule? A: Spironolactone 100 mg : furosemide 40 mg ratio for ascites
Thrombocytopenia 150,000 is often the first sign of cirrhosis
AST:ALT >2 = think alcohol
Rebalanced hemostasis: INR does not reflect bleeding risk
Carvedilol is now preferred over propranolol for portal HTN (PREDESCI trial)
Do NOT use NSAIDs in cirrhosis (renal vasoconstriction → HRS)
Giving FFP/platelets before paracentesis (unnecessary)
Restricting protein in hepatic encephalopathy
Using NSAIDs or aminoglycosides
Not screening for varices or HCC
Forgetting to give albumin after large-volume paracentesis (>5L: 6–8 g albumin per liter removed)
Neuropsychiatric syndrome in liver disease due to accumulation of neurotoxins (primarily ammonia). West Haven Criteria:
Grade 0 (covert): subclinical, detected by psychometric testing
Grade 1 (covert): mild confusion, sleep disturbance
Grade 2 (overt): lethargy, asterixis, disorientation
Grade 3 (overt): somnolence, marked confusion
Grade 4 (overt): coma

Precipitants (mnemonic: HEPATICS): Hemorrhage (GI), Electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia, hyponatremia), Protein excess (dietary), Azotemia/dehydration, Tranquilizers (benzodiazepines, opioids), Infection (SBP!), Constipation, Shunts (TIPS, surgical)
Ammonia from gut bacteria + protein metabolism → not cleared by failing liver → crosses BBB
Ammonia → astrocyte swelling (glutamine synthesis) → cerebral edema
GABA-ergic tone increased → neuroinhibition
Asterixis (flapping tremor) = hallmark of grade 2
Disorientation, personality changes, sleep-wake reversal
Fetor hepaticus (sweet, musty breath)
Hyperreflexia early; hyporeflexia late
Clinical diagnosis — no single test is diagnostic
Serum ammonia: supportive but does NOT correlate with severity; do NOT use to titrate treatment
Rule out other causes: glucose, electrolytes, infection workup, CT head (if focal signs)
Psychometric Hepatic Encephalopathy Score (PHES): for covert HE
Scoring: West Haven Criteria (see above)
Ammonia: elevated in most cases but normal ammonia does NOT exclude HE
Always look for precipitant: CBC, BMP, UA, CXR, blood cultures, diagnostic paracentesis
Protect airway if grade 3–4
IV fluids for dehydration
Identify and treat precipitant (infection, GI bleed, electrolytes)
Start lactulose immediately
Identify and treat precipitant
Lactulose: 60 mL initially → 20 mL every 1–2 hours until bowel movement → maintenance 20–30 mL 2–3x daily (target 2–3 soft BMs/day)
If no response or for secondary prophylaxis: add rifaximin 550 mg BID
Nutrition: do NOT restrict protein; 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day
Avoid sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids)
Zinc supplementation may help (cofactor for urea cycle)

rows: Osmotic laxative; acidifies colon → NH3 → NH4+ (trapped)

blocks.text: Aspiration pneumonia (grade 3–4)
Cerebral edema (acute liver failure)
Recurrent episodes → cognitive decline
First episode of overt HE: 1-year mortality ~50% without transplant
Rifaximin reduces recurrence by ~50% (RFHE trial)
Grade 3–4: ICU admission, intubation consideration
New-onset HE without known liver disease → consider acute liver failure
Focal neurological signs → CT head to rule out structural lesion
Q: What is the mechanism of lactulose? A: Osmotic laxative + acidifies colonic pH → converts NH3 to NH4+ (non-absorbable)
Q: Should you use ammonia levels to guide treatment? A: NO — does not correlate with severity
Q: What is the most common precipitant? A: Infection (especially SBP)
Asterixis is NOT specific to HE (also seen in uremia, CO2 narcosis)
Do NOT restrict protein — worsens sarcopenia and outcomes
Lactulose enemas (300 mL in 700 mL water) for patients who cannot take PO
Rifaximin is for secondary prophylaxis (after first episode), not usually first-line acute treatment alone
Restricting protein
Using ammonia levels to titrate lactulose
Giving benzodiazepines for "agitation" (worsens HE)
Not looking for precipitant (especially SBP — always do paracentesis)
Hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) >5 mmHg. Clinically significant portal hypertension (CSPH): HVPG ≥10 mmHg.
Pre-hepatic: portal/splenic vein thrombosis
Intrahepatic: cirrhosis (most common), schistosomiasis (presinusoidal)
Post-hepatic: Budd-Chiari, right heart failure, constrictive pericarditis
Varices (esophageal, gastric, rectal)
Splenomegaly with hypersplenism
Ascites, caput medusae
Hepatic encephalopathy
Primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding: NSBB (carvedilol preferred) or endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL)
Carvedilol 6.25 mg/day → titrate to 12.5 mg/day (PREDESCI trial: prevents decompensation)
Screen with EGD if platelets 150,000 or liver stiffness >20 kPa
Baveno VII criteria: can avoid EGD if platelets >150,000 AND liver stiffness 20 kPa
NSBB contraindicated in: SBP with refractory ascites, SBP 90 mmHg, severe hyponatremia (120), AKI, ACLF
Pathological accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity. Most common cause: cirrhosis (85%).
Diagnostic paracentesis: cell count, albumin, total protein, culture (bedside inoculation in blood culture bottles)
Serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG):
≥1.1 g/dL = portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari)
1.1 g/dL = non-portal hypertension (malignancy, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome)
Grade 1 (mild): sodium restriction 2 g/day
Grade 2 (moderate): sodium restriction + diuretics (spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg; titrate in 100:40 ratio)
Grade 3 (large/tense): large-volume paracentesis (LVP) + albumin replacement (6–8 g per liter removed if >5L)
Refractory ascites: serial LVP, TIPS, or liver transplant
Maximum weight loss: 0.5 kg/day (without edema), 1 kg/day (with edema)
Stop diuretics if: Na 120, AKI, HE, muscle cramps refractory to treatment
TIPS: for refractory ascites; contraindicated if bilirubin >5, HE grade ≥2, Child-Pugh >13
Infection of ascitic fluid without surgically treatable intra-abdominal source. Diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN >250/mm3.
Most common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus pneumoniae (monomicrobial, gram-negative predominant)
Pathogenesis: bacterial translocation from gut
Diagnostic paracentesis: PMN >250/mm3 (even if culture negative = culture-negative neutrocytic ascites → still treat)
Culture: bedside inoculation in blood culture bottles (increases yield from 50% to 80%)
Bacterascites: positive culture but PMN 250 → repeat paracentesis; treat if symptomatic
Empiric: IV ceftriaxone 2 g/day or cefotaxime 2 g q8h x 5 days
IV albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1 + 1 g/kg on day 3 (reduces HRS and mortality — SORT-SBP trial)
Albumin indicated if: creatinine >1 mg/dL, BUN >30, or bilirubin >4
Repeat paracentesis at 48 hours: PMN should decrease by ≥25%; if not, consider secondary peritonitis or resistant organism
Nosocomial SBP: consider piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (local resistance patterns)

rows: Ascitic protein <1.5 g/dL + liver failure (Child-Pugh ≥9 + bilirubin ≥3) OR renal dysfunction (Cr ≥1.2 or Na ≤130)

blocks.text: Runyon's criteria for secondary peritonitis: ≥2 of (glucose <50, protein >1 g/dL, LDH >ULN) → get CT abdomen and surgical consult.
Always do diagnostic paracentesis in ALL cirrhotic patients admitted to hospital
PMN >250 = treat, even if culture negative
Do NOT wait for culture results to start antibiotics
Albumin with antibiotics reduces mortality from 29% to 10%
Not performing paracentesis on admission
Waiting for culture before starting antibiotics
Forgetting albumin infusion
Not repeating paracentesis at 48 hours to assess response
Missing secondary peritonitis (polymicrobial, no response to antibiotics)
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Diagnosis: ascitic PMN >250/mm3
Most common organism: E. coli
Treat even if culture negative
First-line: ceftriaxone or cefotaxime
Albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1 + 1 g/kg day 3
Repeat paracentesis at 48 hours
Secondary prophylaxis: norfloxacin or TMP-SMX indefinitely
GI bleed prophylaxis: ceftriaxone 1 g/day x 7 days
Runyon's criteria differentiate SBP from secondary peritonitis
Always paracentesis on admission for any cirrhotic patient
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What PMN count diagnoses SBP? (>250/mm3)
How do you differentiate SBP from secondary peritonitis? (Runyon's criteria: glucose, protein, LDH, polymicrobial)
When do you give albumin in SBP? (Cr >1, BUN >30, or bilirubin >4)
What is culture-negative neutrocytic ascites? (PMN >250 but culture negative — still treat as SBP)
What is bacterascites? (Positive culture but PMN <250 — repeat tap; treat if symptomatic)
Hemorrhage from esophageal or gastric varices due to portal hypertension. Mortality: 15–20% per episode.
Esophageal varices: small (<5 mm) vs large (≥5 mm)
Gastric varices (Sarin classification): GOV1 (extending along lesser curve), GOV2 (extending along fundus), IGV1 (isolated fundal), IGV2 (isolated ectopic)
ABCs: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch
Restrictive transfusion: target Hb 7–8 g/dL (over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding)
IV octreotide: 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hour infusion x 3–5 days (or terlipressin 2 mg IV q4h)
IV antibiotics: ceftriaxone 1 g/day x 7 days (reduces mortality)
IV PPI: NOT indicated unless concomitant peptic ulcer suspected
Intubation: consider if massive hematemesis or encephalopathy (airway protection)
Urgent EGD: within 12 hours of presentation
Esophageal varices: endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL/banding) — preferred
Gastric varices (GOV2, IGV1): cyanoacrylate glue injection
Repeat EVL every 2–4 weeks until variceal obliteration
Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube): bridge to definitive therapy (max 24 hours)
TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt): definitive rescue
Early/preemptive TIPS: within 72 hours for high-risk patients (Child-Pugh C 10–13 or Child-Pugh B with active bleeding at EGD)
NSBB (propranolol, nadolol, or carvedilol) + EVL
Target: resting HR 55–60 bpm, SBP ≥90 mmHg
TIPS if rebleeding despite combined therapy
Small varices with red wale signs or Child-Pugh C: NSBB
Medium/large varices: NSBB or EVL
Carvedilol preferred NSBB (greater portal pressure reduction)

rows: 2 mg IV q4h → 1 mg q4h after control

blocks.text: Hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation → balloon tamponade + TIPS
Child-Pugh C or B with active bleeding → early TIPS within 72 hours
Do NOT over-transfuse (target Hb 7–8)
Do NOT use NSBB if SBP <90, Na <120, AKI, or ACLF
Antibiotics reduce mortality in variceal bleeding (not just infection prevention)
Restrictive transfusion is better than liberal (less rebleeding)
PPI is NOT indicated for variceal bleeding
Erythromycin 250 mg IV 30–120 min before EGD improves visualization (prokinetic)
TIPS complications: hepatic encephalopathy (30–50%), stent stenosis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the transfusion target in variceal bleeding? (Hb 7–8 g/dL — restrictive)
Why give antibiotics in variceal bleeding? (Reduces mortality, not just infection)
When do you do early TIPS? (Child-Pugh C 10–13 or B with active bleeding at EGD, within 72 hours)
What is the treatment for fundal gastric varices? (Cyanoacrylate glue, not banding)
What is the maximum duration for balloon tamponade? (24 hours — bridge only)
Jaundice caused by mechanical obstruction of bile flow (extrahepatic cholestasis). Characterized by conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia.
Malignant: pancreatic head cancer (most common malignant cause), cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary carcinoma, gallbladder cancer, metastatic lymphadenopathy
Benign: choledocholithiasis (most common overall), strictures (post-surgical, PSC), chronic pancreatitis, Mirizzi syndrome, parasites (Clonorchis, Ascaris)
Painless jaundice: think malignancy (especially pancreatic head cancer)
Painful jaundice + fever: think choledocholithiasis/cholangitis (Charcot triad)
Courvoisier sign: palpable, non-tender gallbladder + jaundice = malignant obstruction (NOT gallstones — gallstones cause fibrotic, non-distensible gallbladder)
Pale/clay-colored stools (acholic), dark urine (conjugated bilirubin in urine), pruritus
Labs: elevated direct bilirubin, ALP, GGT (cholestatic pattern); ALT/AST mildly elevated
First-line imaging: transabdominal ultrasound (dilated CBD >6 mm, or >8 mm post-cholecystectomy)
MRCP: non-invasive, excellent for CBD stones and strictures
CT abdomen: for suspected malignancy (staging)
ERCP: therapeutic (not purely diagnostic) — stone extraction, stenting
EUS: best for small stones and pancreatic masses
Benign (choledocholithiasis): ERCP with stone extraction → laparoscopic cholecystectomy
Malignant: ERCP with stenting (palliative) or surgical resection (Whipple procedure for pancreatic head)
Cholangitis: urgent biliary drainage (ERCP preferred) + IV antibiotics
Do NOT delay biliary drainage in cholangitis
Courvoisier sign = malignant obstruction (not stones)
Painless jaundice in elderly = pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise
ALP >3x ULN with mild transaminase elevation = cholestatic pattern
Prolonged obstruction → vitamin K malabsorption → coagulopathy (correctable with vitamin K, unlike cirrhosis)
Give vitamin K before any procedure if INR elevated in obstructive jaundice
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is Courvoisier sign? (Palpable non-tender gallbladder + jaundice = malignant obstruction)
Painless jaundice in a 65-year-old — top differential? (Pancreatic head cancer)
Why is INR elevated in obstructive jaundice? (Vitamin K malabsorption — fat-soluble vitamin needs bile for absorption)
What is the difference between MRCP and ERCP? (MRCP = diagnostic; ERCP = therapeutic)
What is Charcot triad? (Fever, jaundice, RUQ pain = cholangitis)

parts.searchText: part i: hepatology part i: hepatology topic 1: hepatitis b topic 1 · hepatitis b 1. definition classification 1. definition classification chronic hepatitis b (chb) = hbsag positive for >6 months. five phases: immune-tolerant: hbeag+, hbv dna ≥107 iu/ml, normal alt hbeag-positive immune active: hbeag+, alt ≥2x uln, hbv dna ≥20,000 iu/ml hbeag-negative immune active: hbeag−, alt ≥2x uln, hbv dna ≥2,000 iu/ml inactive carrier: hbeag−, normal alt, hbv dna 2,000 iu/ml hbsag-negative (resolved): hbv dna undetectable ± anti-hbs 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors vertical transmission (asia/africa), sexual/parenteral (western) risk factors for progression: male sex, age >40, alcohol, hdv/hcv/hiv coinfection, family history of hcc 3. pathophysiology (high yield) 3. pathophysiology (high yield) hbv is a dna virus (hepadnaviridae) with cccdna in hepatocyte nucleus → persistent infection liver damage is immune-mediated (cytotoxic t-cells), not directly cytopathic hbv integrates into host genome → hcc risk even without cirrhosis 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation often asymptomatic; fatigue, ruq discomfort acute: jaundice, nausea, fever (90% adults clear spontaneously; 90% neonates become chronic) extrahepatic: polyarteritis nodosa, membranous nephropathy, aplastic anemia 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup triple panel: hbsag, anti-hbs, anti-hbc (total) if hbsag+: hbeag, anti-hbe, hbv dna quantitative, alt fibrosis assessment: fibroscan (transient elastography) or fib-4 acute hbv: igm anti-hbc positive 6. diagnostic criteria / scoring systems 6. diagnostic criteria / scoring systems aasld uln for alt: male 35 u/l, female 25 u/l apasl/easl uln: 40 u/l (sex-neutral) treatment indications vary by guideline (see table below) guideline hbeag+ treatment threshold hbeag− treatment threshold aasld 2018 dna >20,000 + alt ≥2x uln dna >2,000 + alt ≥2x uln easl 2025 dna >2,000 + alt >uln dna >2,000 + alt >uln who 2024 fibrosis ≥f2 regardless; or dna ≥2,000 + alt >uln same all guidelines cirrhosis + detectable hbv dna → treat regardless of alt 7. interpretation of important labs 7. interpretation of important labs marker meaning hbsag+ active infection anti-hbs+ immunity (vaccine or natural) anti-hbc igm+ acute infection anti-hbc total+ only past infection (occult hbv possible) hbeag+ high replication, high infectivity anti-hbe+ lower replication (but not always) 8. initial stabilization 8. initial stabilization acute hbv: supportive care; antivirals only if acute liver failure (jaundice + coagulopathy + encephalopathy) or protracted course >4 weeks check for coinfections: hdv, hcv, hiv 9. stepwise management 9. stepwise management determine phase of infection assess fibrosis (fibroscan/fib-4) if treatment indicated → start preferred na monitor hbv dna + alt every 3–6 months hcc surveillance: ultrasound ± afp every 6 months (if cirrhosis, or asian male >40, asian female >50, african >20, family history hcc) 10. medication summary table 10. medication summary table drug dose key points tenofovir df (tdf) 300 mg daily avoid in renal/bone disease; safe in pregnancy tenofovir af (taf) 25 mg daily better renal/bone profile; avoid if crcl 15 not on dialysis entecavir (etv) 0.5 mg daily (1 mg if prior lamivudine) avoid in pregnancy; avoid if prior lamivudine resistance peg-ifn alpha-2a 180 mcg/week x 48 weeks finite therapy; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis 11. complications 11. complications cirrhosis, hcc, hepatic decompensation hbv reactivation with immunosuppression (rituximab, chemotherapy, steroids) extrahepatic: gn, pan, aplastic anemia 12. prognosis 12. prognosis 15–40% of chronic carriers develop cirrhosis/hcc hcc annual incidence: 0.5–1% (non-cirrhotic), 2–5% (cirrhotic) hbsag loss (functional cure): 1%/year on na therapy 13. red flags / icu criteria 13. red flags / icu criteria acute liver failure: inr >1.5 + encephalopathy → urgent transplant evaluation hbv flare with bilirubin >3 mg/dl → start antivirals immediately hbv reactivation during immunosuppression → can be fatal 14. frequently asked ward questions 14. frequently asked ward questions q: when do you start antivirals in hbv? a: cirrhosis + any detectable dna; immune-active phase; before immunosuppression q: can you stop antivirals? a: generally indefinite; consider stopping if hbsag loss + anti-hbs seroconversion sustained ≥12 months (non-cirrhotic only) q: which antiviral in pregnancy? a: tdf (start at 28 weeks if dna >200,000 iu/ml) 15. clinical pearls 15. clinical pearls "isolated anti-hbc" = past infection, occult hbv, or window period hbv can cause hcc without cirrhosis (unlike hcv) always check hbv status before rituximab, chemotherapy, or high-dose steroids hbv has a glucocorticoid-responsive element → steroids increase viral replication 16. common pitfalls 16. common pitfalls forgetting to screen for hbv before immunosuppression using lamivudine monotherapy (high resistance rate ~70% at 5 years) not monitoring for hbv reactivation after stopping antivirals confusing "inactive carrier" with "immune-tolerant" phase 17. one-page rapid review 17. one-page rapid review hbv = dna virus, immune-mediated damage, 5 phases. diagnose with triple panel. treat immune-active phase and all cirrhotics with detectable dna. first-line: tdf, taf, or etv. screen for hcc every 6 months. always check before immunosuppression. hbv can cause hcc without cirrhosis. top 10 testable facts: hbv is a dna virus; liver damage is immune-mediated 90% of neonates become chronic; 90% of adults clear igm anti-hbc = acute infection isolated anti-hbc = past infection or window period all cirrhotics with detectable hbv dna need treatment tdf is safe in pregnancy; etv is not hcc screening: us ± afp every 6 months hbv can cause hcc without cirrhosis lamivudine has high resistance; avoid as monotherapy always screen hbv before rituximab/chemotherapy top 5 ward-round pimping questions: what are the 5 phases of chronic hbv? (answer above) which antiviral do you use in pregnancy? (tdf) can hbv cause hcc without cirrhosis? (yes — dna integrates into host genome) what does "isolated anti-hbc" mean? (past infection, window period, or occult hbv) when do you not need to treat chronic hbv? (inactive carrier: hbeag−, normal alt, dna 2,000) most common management mistakes: using lamivudine as first-line (high resistance) not screening for hbv before immunosuppression stopping antivirals prematurely → flare/decompensation not doing hcc surveillance in at-risk patients topic 2: hepatitis c topic 2 · hepatitis c 1. definition classification 1. definition classification chronic hcv = hcv rna detectable for >6 months. six major genotypes (gt1–6). gt1 most common globally; gt3 associated with steatosis and more aggressive disease. 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors rna virus (flaviviridae); parenteral transmission (ivdu, blood products, needlestick) risk factors: ivdu (most common in developed countries), blood transfusion before 1992, tattoos, msm with hiv 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology hcv is directly cytopathic and immune-mediated chronic inflammation → fibrosis → cirrhosis (20–30% over 20–30 years) hcv causes hcc only through cirrhosis (unlike hbv) 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation usually asymptomatic until cirrhosis extrahepatic: mixed cryoglobulinemia (purpura, arthralgia, gn), porphyria cutanea tarda, lichen planus, lymphoma, insulin resistance 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup screen: anti-hcv antibody confirm: hcv rna (quantitative pcr) if rna+: genotype (may not be needed for pangenotypic regimens), fibrosis assessment anti-hcv+ but rna− = cleared infection (spontaneous or treated) 6. diagnostic criteria 6. diagnostic criteria acute hcv: hcv rna+ with recent risk factor, often anti-hcv negative initially (seroconversion 6–8 weeks) chronic hcv: hcv rna+ for >6 months 7. interpretation of labs 7. interpretation of labs test interpretation anti-hcv+ / rna+ active infection anti-hcv+ / rna− cleared (past or treated) anti-hcv− / rna+ early acute infection or immunocompromised 8. initial stabilization 8. initial stabilization assess fibrosis stage (fibroscan, fib-4) check for hbv coinfection (risk of hbv reactivation during/after hcv treatment) assess renal function, drug interactions 9. stepwise management (aasld-idsa 2023) 9. stepwise management (aasld-idsa 2023) confirm active infection (hcv rna+) assess for cirrhosis check hbv status (hbsag, anti-hbc) simplified treatment for treatment-naive, non-cirrhotic: glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (g/p) x 8 weeks, or sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (sof/vel) x 12 weeks compensated cirrhosis: g/p x 8–12 weeks or sof/vel x 12 weeks decompensated cirrhosis: sof/vel ± ribavirin x 12 weeks (no protease inhibitors) svr12 (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks post-treatment) = cure 10. medication summary table 10. medication summary table regimen duration notes glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 8 wk (non-cirrhotic); 8–12 wk (compensated cirrhosis) pangenotypic; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 12 wk pangenotypic; can use in decompensated cirrhosis sof/vel + ribavirin 12 wk for decompensated cirrhosis 11. complications 11. complications cirrhosis, hcc, liver failure cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, membranoproliferative gn hbv reactivation during hcv treatment 12. prognosis 12. prognosis svr12 = >95% cure rate with daas post-svr: if cirrhotic, continue hcc surveillance indefinitely reinfection possible (no protective immunity) 13. red flags 13. red flags decompensated cirrhosis: no protease inhibitors (glecaprevir, voxilaprevir) hbv coinfection: monitor for reactivation drug interactions: check with all medications (especially amiodarone → fatal bradycardia with sofosbuvir) 14. frequently asked ward questions 14. frequently asked ward questions q: do you need to know the genotype before treating? a: not necessarily with pangenotypic regimens (g/p or sof/vel) q: what is svr12? a: undetectable hcv rna 12 weeks after completing treatment = cure q: do you continue hcc screening after cure? a: yes, if cirrhosis was present 15. clinical pearls 15. clinical pearls hcv causes hcc only through cirrhosis (unlike hbv) mixed cryoglobulinemia = think hcv sofosbuvir + amiodarone = fatal bradycardia (contraindicated) anti-hcv remains positive even after cure 16. common pitfalls 16. common pitfalls not checking hbv before treating hcv (reactivation risk) using protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis stopping hcc surveillance after svr in cirrhotics not rechecking hcv rna to confirm cure (svr12) 17. one-page rapid review 17. one-page rapid review hcv = rna virus, 6 genotypes. diagnose: anti-hcv → confirm with rna. treat all with daas (g/p or sof/vel). svr12 = cure (>95%). no protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis. check hbv before treatment. continue hcc surveillance if cirrhotic. cryoglobulinemia = think hcv. top 10 testable facts: hcv is an rna virus; causes hcc only through cirrhosis anti-hcv+ / rna− = cleared infection svr12 = cure (>95% with daas) pangenotypic regimens: g/p or sof/vel no protease inhibitors in decompensated cirrhosis check hbv before treating hcv mixed cryoglobulinemia = hcv sofosbuvir + amiodarone = contraindicated (bradycardia) continue hcc surveillance post-svr if cirrhotic anti-hcv stays positive even after cure topic 3: liver cirrhosis topic 3 · liver cirrhosis 1. definition classification 1. definition classification cirrhosis = end-stage chronic liver disease with diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules. two stages: compensated: no ascites, variceal bleeding, or encephalopathy decompensated: presence of ascites, variceal hemorrhage, hepatic encephalopathy, or jaundice 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors most common causes: alcohol, hbv, hcv, masld/mash others: autoimmune hepatitis, pbc, psc, wilson disease, hemochromatosis, alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency, budd-chiari 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology chronic injury → stellate cell activation → collagen deposition → fibrosis → architectural distortion portal hypertension: increased intrahepatic resistance + splanchnic vasodilation hyperdynamic circulation → high cardiac output, low svr 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation compensated: may be asymptomatic; fatigue, spider angiomata, palmar erythema decompensated: ascites, jaundice, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatorenal syndrome signs: caput medusae, splenomegaly, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy, dupuytren contracture (alcohol) 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup labs: cbc (thrombocytopenia), lfts, albumin, inr, bilirubin non-invasive fibrosis: fibroscan (>12.5 kpa = cirrhosis), fib-4 (>3.25 suggests advanced fibrosis) imaging: us with doppler (nodular liver, splenomegaly, ascites, portal vein flow) liver biopsy: gold standard but rarely needed etiology workup: viral serologies, autoimmune markers, iron studies, ceruloplasmin, alpha1-at 6. scoring systems 6. scoring systems score components use child-pugh bilirubin, albumin, inr, ascites, encephalopathy severity classification (a/b/c) meld bilirubin, inr, creatinine (± na) transplant prioritization child-pugh: a (5–6) = well-compensated; b (7–9) = significant functional compromise; c (10–15) = decompensated 7. interpretation of labs 7. interpretation of labs thrombocytopenia: splenic sequestration (portal htn) + decreased thrombopoietin elevated inr: decreased synthetic function (not a bleeding risk indicator in cirrhosis — rebalanced hemostasis) low albumin: decreased synthesis → edema, ascites ast > alt (ratio >2): suggests alcoholic liver disease 8. initial stabilization 8. initial stabilization decompensated: abc, iv access, type and screen variceal bleed: octreotide + antibiotics + urgent endoscopy sbp: empiric antibiotics + albumin hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + identify precipitant 9. stepwise management 9. stepwise management treat underlying cause (antiviral, alcohol cessation, etc.) screen for varices (egd if platelets 150,000 or liver stiffness >20 kpa) hcc surveillance: us ± afp every 6 months vaccinations: hav, hbv, pneumococcal, influenza avoid hepatotoxins: nsaids, aminoglycosides, excess acetaminophen nutrition: 35 kcal/kg/day, protein 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day (do not restrict protein) transplant evaluation: meld ≥15 10. medication summary table 10. medication summary table indication drug notes portal htn prophylaxis carvedilol 6.25–12.5 mg/day or propranolol/nadolol carvedilol preferred (predesci trial) ascites spironolactone ± furosemide (100:40 ratio) max spironolactone 400 mg, furosemide 160 mg sbp prophylaxis norfloxacin 400 mg/day or tmp-smx if prior sbp, ascitic protein 1.5 g/dl he treatment lactulose + rifaximin target 2–3 soft bms/day hrs terlipressin + albumin or norepinephrine + albumin 11. complications 11. complications ascites, sbp, variceal bleeding, hepatic encephalopathy, hrs, hcc, hepatopulmonary syndrome, portopulmonary hypertension, coagulopathy 12. prognosis 12. prognosis compensated cirrhosis: median survival >12 years decompensated: median survival 2 years meld score predicts 3-month mortality 13. red flags / icu criteria 13. red flags / icu criteria variceal hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability grade 3–4 hepatic encephalopathy hrs with rising creatinine aclf (acute-on-chronic liver failure): organ failures + high short-term mortality 14. frequently asked ward questions 14. frequently asked ward questions q: should you correct inr before paracentesis? a: no — inr does not predict bleeding in cirrhosis; paracentesis is safe q: should you restrict protein in hepatic encephalopathy? a: no — protein restriction worsens sarcopenia and outcomes q: what is the 100:40 rule? a: spironolactone 100 mg : furosemide 40 mg ratio for ascites 15. clinical pearls 15. clinical pearls thrombocytopenia 150,000 is often the first sign of cirrhosis ast:alt >2 = think alcohol rebalanced hemostasis: inr does not reflect bleeding risk carvedilol is now preferred over propranolol for portal htn (predesci trial) do not use nsaids in cirrhosis (renal vasoconstriction → hrs) 16. common pitfalls 16. common pitfalls giving ffp/platelets before paracentesis (unnecessary) restricting protein in hepatic encephalopathy using nsaids or aminoglycosides not screening for varices or hcc forgetting to give albumin after large-volume paracentesis (>5l: 6–8 g albumin per liter removed) topic 4: hepatic encephalopathy topic 4 · hepatic encephalopathy 1. definition classification 1. definition classification neuropsychiatric syndrome in liver disease due to accumulation of neurotoxins (primarily ammonia). west haven criteria: grade 0 (covert): subclinical, detected by psychometric testing grade 1 (covert): mild confusion, sleep disturbance grade 2 (overt): lethargy, asterixis, disorientation grade 3 (overt): somnolence, marked confusion grade 4 (overt): coma 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors precipitants (mnemonic: hepatics): hemorrhage (gi), electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia, hyponatremia), protein excess (dietary), azotemia/dehydration, tranquilizers (benzodiazepines, opioids), infection (sbp!), constipation, shunts (tips, surgical) 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology ammonia from gut bacteria + protein metabolism → not cleared by failing liver → crosses bbb ammonia → astrocyte swelling (glutamine synthesis) → cerebral edema gaba-ergic tone increased → neuroinhibition 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation asterixis (flapping tremor) = hallmark of grade 2 disorientation, personality changes, sleep-wake reversal fetor hepaticus (sweet, musty breath) hyperreflexia early; hyporeflexia late 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup clinical diagnosis — no single test is diagnostic serum ammonia: supportive but does not correlate with severity; do not use to titrate treatment rule out other causes: glucose, electrolytes, infection workup, ct head (if focal signs) psychometric hepatic encephalopathy score (phes): for covert he scoring: west haven criteria (see above) 7. lab interpretation 7. lab interpretation ammonia: elevated in most cases but normal ammonia does not exclude he always look for precipitant: cbc, bmp, ua, cxr, blood cultures, diagnostic paracentesis 8. initial stabilization 8. initial stabilization protect airway if grade 3–4 iv fluids for dehydration identify and treat precipitant (infection, gi bleed, electrolytes) start lactulose immediately 9. stepwise management 9. stepwise management identify and treat precipitant lactulose: 60 ml initially → 20 ml every 1–2 hours until bowel movement → maintenance 20–30 ml 2–3x daily (target 2–3 soft bms/day) if no response or for secondary prophylaxis: add rifaximin 550 mg bid nutrition: do not restrict protein; 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day avoid sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids) zinc supplementation may help (cofactor for urea cycle) 10. medication table 10. medication table drug dose mechanism lactulose 20–30 ml 2–3x/day osmotic laxative; acidifies colon → nh3 → nh4+ (trapped) rifaximin 550 mg bid non-absorbable antibiotic; reduces ammonia-producing bacteria l-ornithine l-aspartate 20 g/day iv enhances urea cycle (adjunctive) 11. complications 11. complications aspiration pneumonia (grade 3–4) cerebral edema (acute liver failure) recurrent episodes → cognitive decline 12. prognosis 12. prognosis first episode of overt he: 1-year mortality ~50% without transplant rifaximin reduces recurrence by ~50% (rfhe trial) 13. red flags 13. red flags grade 3–4: icu admission, intubation consideration new-onset he without known liver disease → consider acute liver failure focal neurological signs → ct head to rule out structural lesion 14. pimping questions 14. pimping questions q: what is the mechanism of lactulose? a: osmotic laxative + acidifies colonic ph → converts nh3 to nh4+ (non-absorbable) q: should you use ammonia levels to guide treatment? a: no — does not correlate with severity q: what is the most common precipitant? a: infection (especially sbp) 15. clinical pearls 15. clinical pearls asterixis is not specific to he (also seen in uremia, co2 narcosis) do not restrict protein — worsens sarcopenia and outcomes lactulose enemas (300 ml in 700 ml water) for patients who cannot take po rifaximin is for secondary prophylaxis (after first episode), not usually first-line acute treatment alone 16. common pitfalls 16. common pitfalls restricting protein using ammonia levels to titrate lactulose giving benzodiazepines for "agitation" (worsens he) not looking for precipitant (especially sbp — always do paracentesis) topic 5: portal hypertension topic 5 · portal hypertension 1. definition 1. definition hepatic venous pressure gradient (hvpg) >5 mmhg. clinically significant portal hypertension (csph): hvpg ≥10 mmhg. 2. classification 2. classification pre-hepatic: portal/splenic vein thrombosis intrahepatic: cirrhosis (most common), schistosomiasis (presinusoidal) post-hepatic: budd-chiari, right heart failure, constrictive pericarditis 3. key clinical features 3. key clinical features varices (esophageal, gastric, rectal) splenomegaly with hypersplenism ascites, caput medusae hepatic encephalopathy 4. management 4. management primary prophylaxis of variceal bleeding: nsbb (carvedilol preferred) or endoscopic variceal ligation (evl) carvedilol 6.25 mg/day → titrate to 12.5 mg/day (predesci trial: prevents decompensation) screen with egd if platelets 150,000 or liver stiffness >20 kpa baveno vii criteria: can avoid egd if platelets >150,000 and liver stiffness 20 kpa 5. clinical pearl 5. clinical pearl nsbb contraindicated in: sbp with refractory ascites, sbp 90 mmhg, severe hyponatremia (120), aki, aclf topic 6: ascites topic 6 · ascites 1. definition 1. definition pathological accumulation of fluid in the peritoneal cavity. most common cause: cirrhosis (85%). 2. diagnostic workup 2. diagnostic workup diagnostic paracentesis: cell count, albumin, total protein, culture (bedside inoculation in blood culture bottles) serum-ascites albumin gradient (saag): ≥1.1 g/dl = portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure, budd-chiari) 1.1 g/dl = non-portal hypertension (malignancy, tb peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome) 3. management 3. management grade 1 (mild): sodium restriction 2 g/day grade 2 (moderate): sodium restriction + diuretics (spironolactone 100 mg + furosemide 40 mg; titrate in 100:40 ratio) grade 3 (large/tense): large-volume paracentesis (lvp) + albumin replacement (6–8 g per liter removed if >5l) refractory ascites: serial lvp, tips, or liver transplant 4. key points 4. key points maximum weight loss: 0.5 kg/day (without edema), 1 kg/day (with edema) stop diuretics if: na 120, aki, he, muscle cramps refractory to treatment tips: for refractory ascites; contraindicated if bilirubin >5, he grade ≥2, child-pugh >13 topic 7: spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (sbp) topic 7 · spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (sbp) 1. definition 1. definition infection of ascitic fluid without surgically treatable intra-abdominal source. diagnosis: ascitic fluid pmn >250/mm3. 2. etiology 2. etiology most common organisms: e. coli, klebsiella, streptococcus pneumoniae (monomicrobial, gram-negative predominant) pathogenesis: bacterial translocation from gut 3. diagnosis 3. diagnosis diagnostic paracentesis: pmn >250/mm3 (even if culture negative = culture-negative neutrocytic ascites → still treat) culture: bedside inoculation in blood culture bottles (increases yield from 50% to 80%) bacterascites: positive culture but pmn 250 → repeat paracentesis; treat if symptomatic 4. treatment 4. treatment empiric: iv ceftriaxone 2 g/day or cefotaxime 2 g q8h x 5 days iv albumin: 1.5 g/kg on day 1 + 1 g/kg on day 3 (reduces hrs and mortality — sort-sbp trial) albumin indicated if: creatinine >1 mg/dl, bun >30, or bilirubin >4 repeat paracentesis at 48 hours: pmn should decrease by ≥25%; if not, consider secondary peritonitis or resistant organism nosocomial sbp: consider piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (local resistance patterns) 5. sbp prophylaxis 5. sbp prophylaxis indication regimen references active gi bleed in cirrhosis ceftriaxone 1 g/day x 7 days easl/aasld prior sbp episode norfloxacin 400 mg/day or tmp-smx indefinitely secondary prophylaxis ascitic protein <1.5 g/dl + liver failure (child-pugh ≥9 + bilirubin ≥3) or renal dysfunction (cr ≥1.2 or na ≤130) norfloxacin 400 mg/day primary prophylaxis 6. differentiating sbp from secondary peritonitis 6. differentiating sbp from secondary peritonitis feature sbp secondary peritonitis references organisms monomicrobial polymicrobial ascitic glucose >50 mg/dl <50 mg/dl ascitic protein low high ascitic ldh low >uln serum response to antibiotics yes (48h) no → needs surgery runyon's criteria for secondary peritonitis: ≥2 of (glucose <50, protein >1 g/dl, ldh >uln) → get ct abdomen and surgical consult. 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls always do diagnostic paracentesis in all cirrhotic patients admitted to hospital pmn >250 = treat, even if culture negative do not wait for culture results to start antibiotics albumin with antibiotics reduces mortality from 29% to 10% 8. common pitfalls 8. common pitfalls not performing paracentesis on admission waiting for culture before starting antibiotics forgetting albumin infusion not repeating paracentesis at 48 hours to assess response missing secondary peritonitis (polymicrobial, no response to antibiotics) top 10 testable facts: diagnosis: ascitic pmn >250/mm3 most common organism: e. coli treat even if culture negative first-line: ceftriaxone or cefotaxime albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1 + 1 g/kg day 3 repeat paracentesis at 48 hours secondary prophylaxis: norfloxacin or tmp-smx indefinitely gi bleed prophylaxis: ceftriaxone 1 g/day x 7 days runyon's criteria differentiate sbp from secondary peritonitis always paracentesis on admission for any cirrhotic patient top 5 pimping questions: what pmn count diagnoses sbp? (>250/mm3) how do you differentiate sbp from secondary peritonitis? (runyon's criteria: glucose, protein, ldh, polymicrobial) when do you give albumin in sbp? (cr >1, bun >30, or bilirubin >4) what is culture-negative neutrocytic ascites? (pmn >250 but culture negative — still treat as sbp) what is bacterascites? (positive culture but pmn <250 — repeat tap; treat if symptomatic) topic 8: variceal bleeding topic 8 · variceal bleeding 1. definition 1. definition hemorrhage from esophageal or gastric varices due to portal hypertension. mortality: 15–20% per episode. 2. classification 2. classification esophageal varices: small (<5 mm) vs large (≥5 mm) gastric varices (sarin classification): gov1 (extending along lesser curve), gov2 (extending along fundus), igv1 (isolated fundal), igv2 (isolated ectopic) 3. initial stabilization (first 6 hours) 3. initial stabilization (first 6 hours) abcs: two large-bore ivs, type and crossmatch restrictive transfusion: target hb 7–8 g/dl (over-transfusion increases portal pressure and rebleeding) iv octreotide: 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hour infusion x 3–5 days (or terlipressin 2 mg iv q4h) iv antibiotics: ceftriaxone 1 g/day x 7 days (reduces mortality) iv ppi: not indicated unless concomitant peptic ulcer suspected intubation: consider if massive hematemesis or encephalopathy (airway protection) urgent egd: within 12 hours of presentation 4. endoscopic management 4. endoscopic management esophageal varices: endoscopic variceal ligation (evl/banding) — preferred gastric varices (gov2, igv1): cyanoacrylate glue injection repeat evl every 2–4 weeks until variceal obliteration 5. rescue therapy (failure to control bleeding) 5. rescue therapy (failure to control bleeding) balloon tamponade (sengstaken-blakemore tube): bridge to definitive therapy (max 24 hours) tips (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt): definitive rescue early/preemptive tips: within 72 hours for high-risk patients (child-pugh c 10–13 or child-pugh b with active bleeding at egd) 6. secondary prophylaxis 6. secondary prophylaxis nsbb (propranolol, nadolol, or carvedilol) + evl target: resting hr 55–60 bpm, sbp ≥90 mmhg tips if rebleeding despite combined therapy 7. primary prophylaxis 7. primary prophylaxis small varices with red wale signs or child-pugh c: nsbb medium/large varices: nsbb or evl carvedilol preferred nsbb (greater portal pressure reduction) 8. medication table 8. medication table drug dose duration references octreotide 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hr 3–5 days terlipressin 2 mg iv q4h → 1 mg q4h after control 3–5 days ceftriaxone 1 g iv daily 7 days propranolol 20–40 mg bid (titrate to hr 55–60) indefinite carvedilol 6.25–12.5 mg daily indefinite 9. red flags 9. red flags hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation → balloon tamponade + tips child-pugh c or b with active bleeding → early tips within 72 hours do not over-transfuse (target hb 7–8) do not use nsbb if sbp <90, na <120, aki, or aclf 10. clinical pearls 10. clinical pearls antibiotics reduce mortality in variceal bleeding (not just infection prevention) restrictive transfusion is better than liberal (less rebleeding) ppi is not indicated for variceal bleeding erythromycin 250 mg iv 30–120 min before egd improves visualization (prokinetic) tips complications: hepatic encephalopathy (30–50%), stent stenosis top 5 pimping questions: what is the transfusion target in variceal bleeding? (hb 7–8 g/dl — restrictive) why give antibiotics in variceal bleeding? (reduces mortality, not just infection) when do you do early tips? (child-pugh c 10–13 or b with active bleeding at egd, within 72 hours) what is the treatment for fundal gastric varices? (cyanoacrylate glue, not banding) what is the maximum duration for balloon tamponade? (24 hours — bridge only) topic 9: obstructive jaundice topic 9 · obstructive jaundice 1. definition 1. definition jaundice caused by mechanical obstruction of bile flow (extrahepatic cholestasis). characterized by conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia. 2. etiology 2. etiology malignant: pancreatic head cancer (most common malignant cause), cholangiocarcinoma, ampullary carcinoma, gallbladder cancer, metastatic lymphadenopathy benign: choledocholithiasis (most common overall), strictures (post-surgical, psc), chronic pancreatitis, mirizzi syndrome, parasites (clonorchis, ascaris) 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation painless jaundice: think malignancy (especially pancreatic head cancer) painful jaundice + fever: think choledocholithiasis/cholangitis (charcot triad) courvoisier sign: palpable, non-tender gallbladder + jaundice = malignant obstruction (not gallstones — gallstones cause fibrotic, non-distensible gallbladder) pale/clay-colored stools (acholic), dark urine (conjugated bilirubin in urine), pruritus 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup labs: elevated direct bilirubin, alp, ggt (cholestatic pattern); alt/ast mildly elevated first-line imaging: transabdominal ultrasound (dilated cbd >6 mm, or >8 mm post-cholecystectomy) mrcp: non-invasive, excellent for cbd stones and strictures ct abdomen: for suspected malignancy (staging) ercp: therapeutic (not purely diagnostic) — stone extraction, stenting eus: best for small stones and pancreatic masses 5. lab interpretation 5. lab interpretation pattern alp/ggt alt/ast bilirubin suggests references cholestatic ↑↑↑ ↑ or normal ↑ (direct) obstruction hepatocellular ↑ or normal ↑↑↑ ↑ (mixed) hepatitis mixed ↑↑ ↑↑ ↑ drug-induced, infiltrative 6. management 6. management benign (choledocholithiasis): ercp with stone extraction → laparoscopic cholecystectomy malignant: ercp with stenting (palliative) or surgical resection (whipple procedure for pancreatic head) cholangitis: urgent biliary drainage (ercp preferred) + iv antibiotics do not delay biliary drainage in cholangitis 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls courvoisier sign = malignant obstruction (not stones) painless jaundice in elderly = pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise alp >3x uln with mild transaminase elevation = cholestatic pattern prolonged obstruction → vitamin k malabsorption → coagulopathy (correctable with vitamin k, unlike cirrhosis) give vitamin k before any procedure if inr elevated in obstructive jaundice top 5 pimping questions: what is courvoisier sign? (palpable non-tender gallbladder + jaundice = malignant obstruction) painless jaundice in a 65-year-old — top differential? (pancreatic head cancer) why is inr elevated in obstructive jaundice? (vitamin k malabsorption — fat-soluble vitamin needs bile for absorption) what is the difference between mrcp and ercp? (mrcp = diagnostic; ercp = therapeutic) what is charcot triad? (fever, jaundice, ruq pain = cholangitis)

parts.title: PART II: PULMONOLOGY

blocks.text: Pulmonary TB: infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis affecting the lungs
Latent TB (LTBI): positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious
Active TB: symptomatic, infectious
Drug-sensitive vs drug-resistant (MDR-TB: resistant to INH + rifampicin; XDR-TB: MDR + fluoroquinolone + bedaquiline/linezolid resistance)
WHO classification: new, relapse, treatment after failure, treatment after loss to follow-up
M. tuberculosis (acid-fast bacillus, aerobic, slow-growing)
Risk factors for reactivation: HIV (most important), diabetes, malnutrition, immunosuppression, silicosis, CKD, TNF-alpha inhibitors, age extremes
Indonesia: high-burden country (top 2 globally)
Inhaled droplet nuclei → alveolar macrophages → granuloma formation (caseating)
Primary TB: Ghon focus (parenchymal) + Ranke complex (Ghon focus + hilar lymphadenopathy)
Reactivation: upper lobes (high O2 tension favors mycobacterial growth)
Miliary TB: hematogenous dissemination
Chronic cough ≥2 weeks (Indonesia: ≥2 weeks is screening threshold)
Hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, low-grade fever
Upper lobe cavitary lesion on CXR = classic
Extrapulmonary: lymph node (most common), pleural, meningeal, skeletal (Pott disease), genitourinary, peritoneal
Sputum AFB smear x 2 (spot-morning or spot-spot) — Ziehl-Neelsen stain
GeneXpert MTB/RIF (Xpert): rapid molecular test (results in 2 hours); detects TB AND rifampicin resistance; sensitivity ~98% in smear-positive, ~67% in smear-negative
Sputum culture (Löwenstein-Jensen or MGIT): gold standard but takes 2–8 weeks
CXR: upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, fibrosis, miliary pattern
IGRA or TST: for LTBI diagnosis (NOT for active TB diagnosis)
Bacteriologically confirmed: positive smear, Xpert, or culture
Clinically diagnosed: clinical + radiological findings without bacteriological confirmation
Intensive phase (2 months) → Continuation phase (4 months):
Category 1 (new patients): 2HRZE / 4HR
Isoniazid (H), Rifampicin (R), Pyrazinamide (Z), Ethambutol (E)
Category 2 (retreatment): 2HRZES / 1HRZE / 5HRE (streptomycin added in first 2 months)
Note: WHO now recommends Xpert for all retreatment cases to rule out MDR-TB before starting Category 2

rows: Hepatitis, orange secretions, drug interactions (CYP450 inducer)

blocks.text: Stop all hepatotoxic drugs if ALT 3x ULN with symptoms or 5x ULN without symptoms
Wait for LFTs to normalize
Reintroduce one at a time: R → H → Z (most hepatotoxic last)
If severe hepatitis: may need to permanently exclude pyrazinamide
Bedaquiline-based regimen (BPaL: bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) — WHO 2022
Duration: 6–9 months (shorter regimens now preferred)
Refer to specialized MDR-TB center
Start ART within 2 weeks if CD4 50; within 8 weeks if CD4 50
Exception: TB meningitis — delay ART to 8 weeks (reduces IRIS)
Drug interaction: rifampicin + efavirenz (OK); rifampicin + dolutegravir (double dose DTG to 50 mg BID)
Rifampicin reduces levels of PIs — use rifabutin instead if on PI-based ART
Hemoptysis (massive: 600 mL/24h → bronchial artery embolization)
Pneumothorax, empyema, bronchiectasis
Miliary TB, TB meningitis
Drug-induced hepatitis, IRIS (in HIV)
Massive hemoptysis → position bleeding side down, intubation, bronchial artery embolization
TB meningitis → start dexamethasone + anti-TB immediately
MDR-TB suspected → do NOT start Category 1; get Xpert first
Always give pyridoxine (B6) with isoniazid (prevents peripheral neuropathy)
Rifampicin turns body fluids orange (warn patients — not harmful)
Ethambutol → optic neuritis (test color vision before starting)
Pyrazinamide is the most hepatotoxic first-line drug
Sputum conversion at 2 months is key prognostic marker
DOTS (Directly Observed Therapy, Short-course) is standard of care
Not checking Xpert for rifampicin resistance in retreatment cases
Forgetting pyridoxine supplementation
Not monitoring LFTs during treatment
Stopping treatment early when symptoms improve (→ relapse, resistance)
Not screening for HIV in all TB patients
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Standard regimen: 2HRZE/4HR
Xpert MTB/RIF: detects TB + rifampicin resistance in 2 hours
Upper lobe cavitary lesion = classic CXR finding
Isoniazid → peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine)
Rifampicin → CYP450 inducer (drug interactions!)
Ethambutol → optic neuritis
Pyrazinamide → most hepatotoxic; hyperuricemia
Drug-induced hepatitis: reintroduce R → H → Z
TB-HIV: start ART within 2 weeks if CD4 50
MDR-TB = resistant to INH + rifampicin
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the order of reintroduction after drug-induced hepatitis? (R → H → Z)
Which TB drug causes optic neuritis? (Ethambutol)
When do you start ART in TB-HIV coinfection? (CD4 50: within 2 weeks; CD4 50: within 8 weeks)
What does Xpert MTB/RIF detect? (TB + rifampicin resistance)
Why does TB prefer the upper lobes? (Higher oxygen tension)
Acute infection of the lung parenchyma acquired outside of hospital or healthcare settings. Onset before or within 48 hours of hospital admission.
Most common overall: Streptococcus pneumoniae
Atypical: Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydophila pneumoniae, Legionella
Viral: influenza, SARS-CoV-2, RSV
Aspiration: anaerobes, gram-negatives
In Indonesia: also consider Burkholderia pseudomallei (melioidosis), TB
Typical: acute fever, productive cough, pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, crackles
Atypical: gradual onset, dry cough, extrapulmonary symptoms (headache, myalgia, diarrhea)
Elderly: may present with confusion, falls, without fever
CXR: consolidation, air bronchograms, lobar/patchy infiltrates
Labs: CBC, BMP, procalcitonin (helps distinguish bacterial vs viral)
Sputum culture + Gram stain (before antibiotics if possible)
Blood cultures x 2 (if severe CAP or ICU admission)
Urinary antigens: Legionella, S. pneumoniae (if severe)
Consider: influenza/COVID testing, procalcitonin
CURB-65 (1 point each):
Confusion
Urea 7 mmol/L (BUN 20 mg/dL)
Respiratory rate ≥30
Blood pressure: SBP 90 or DBP ≤60
Age ≥65
Score: 0–1 = outpatient; 2 = consider admission; 3–5 = severe, consider ICU
ATS/IDSA Severe CAP criteria (need ICU if 1 major or ≥3 minor):
Major: mechanical ventilation, septic shock requiring vasopressors
Minor: RR ≥30, PaO2/FiO2 ≤250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, BUN ≥20, WBC 4,000, platelets 100,000, hypothermia 36°C, hypotension requiring fluids
Outpatient (no comorbidities): amoxicillin 1 g TID x 5 days

Outpatient (with comorbidities): amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID + azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg x 4 days; OR respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin 750 mg daily or moxifloxacin 400 mg daily)
Inpatient (non-ICU): IV ceftriaxone 1–2 g daily + azithromycin 500 mg daily; OR respiratory fluoroquinolone alone

Inpatient (ICU/severe): IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily + azithromycin 500 mg IV daily; if MRSA risk: add vancomycin or linezolid; if Pseudomonas risk: piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime or meropenem + azithromycin or fluoroquinolone
Minimum 5 days; can stop when: afebrile ≥48 hours, ≤1 sign of clinical instability
Do NOT treat based on CXR resolution (lags behind clinical improvement)
Steroids: dexamethasone 6 mg x 4 days reduces mortality in severe CAP (CAPE COD trial)
Procalcitonin 0.25 ng/mL: consider withholding antibiotics (viral likely)
"Currant jelly" sputum = Klebsiella pneumoniae
Legionella: think contaminated water, hyponatremia, diarrhea, elevated CK
Mycoplasma: young adults, bullous myringitis, cold agglutinins, erythema multiforme
Treating too long (5 days is sufficient for most CAP)
Not assessing severity (CURB-65 or ATS/IDSA criteria)
Forgetting to consider TB in endemic areas (Indonesia)
Not getting blood cultures in severe CAP
Using fluoroquinolones as first-line in uncomplicated CAP (reserve for penicillin allergy or comorbidities)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most common cause of CAP? (S. pneumoniae)
What are the CURB-65 criteria? (Confusion, Urea, RR, BP, 65)
When do you add MRSA coverage? (Risk factors: prior MRSA, recent hospitalization, severe CAP)
What is the minimum duration of treatment? (5 days)
What organism causes "currant jelly" sputum? (Klebsiella)
HAP: pneumonia occurring ≥48 hours after hospital admission, not incubating at admission
VAP: pneumonia occurring 48 hours after endotracheal intubation
Gram-negatives: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella, Acinetobacter, E. coli, Enterobacter
Gram-positives: MRSA
Often polymicrobial; local antibiogram is critical
Clinical: new/progressive infiltrate + ≥2 of: fever 38°C, leukocytosis/leukopenia, purulent secretions
Respiratory cultures: endotracheal aspirate (quantitative ≥106 CFU/mL) or BAL (≥104 CFU/mL)
Blood cultures x 2
CPIS (Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score): ≥6 suggests pneumonia (but not widely validated)
Must cover: Pseudomonas, MRSA, and gram-negatives
Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h, OR cefepime 2 g q8h, OR meropenem 1 g q8h
PLUS anti-MRSA: vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg q8–12h OR linezolid 600 mg q12h
PLUS second anti-pseudomonal agent if high risk: aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone
De-escalate based on culture results at 48–72 hours
7 days for most HAP/VAP (shorter courses preferred)
Procalcitonin can guide de-escalation
Head of bed elevation 30–45°
Daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial
DVT prophylaxis
Stress ulcer prophylaxis
Oral care with chlorhexidine (controversial — some guidelines no longer recommend)
Subglottic secretion drainage
Acinetobacter: think ICU, MDR, may need colistin/polymyxin
Always de-escalate antibiotics based on cultures
7 days is sufficient for most HAP/VAP
Aminoglycosides: use extended-interval dosing (once daily) for better efficacy and less toxicity
Abnormal fluid accumulation in the pleural space. Classified by Light's criteria:
Exudate (meets ≥1 criterion):
Pleural protein / serum protein 0.5
Pleural LDH / serum LDH 0.6
Pleural LDH 2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH
Transudate: meets none of the above
CXR: blunting of costophrenic angle (≥200 mL on upright, ≥500 mL on supine)
Lateral decubitus: layering 10 mm = safe for thoracentesis
Ultrasound: best for guiding thoracentesis, detecting loculations
Thoracentesis fluid analysis: cell count + differential, protein, LDH, glucose, pH, Gram stain, culture, cytology
Additional tests based on suspicion: ADA (adenosine deaminase) for TB, amylase (pancreatitis/esophageal rupture), triglycerides (chylothorax), hematocrit (hemothorax)

rows: Strongly suggests TB pleuritis (sensitivity ~90% in high-prevalence areas)
Malignant effusion (sensitivity ~60% first tap, ~75% with repeat)
pH <7.2, glucose <60, LDH >1000, or positive Gram stain
Chest tube drainage + antibiotics ± fibrinolytics or surgery

blocks.text: Determine transudative vs exudative (Light's criteria)
Transudate → treat underlying cause (diuretics for CHF, albumin for cirrhosis)
Exudate → determine etiology:
Parapneumonic: antibiotics ± drainage based on classification above
TB pleuritis: standard anti-TB regimen (2HRZE/4HR); steroids NOT routinely recommended
Malignant: therapeutic thoracentesis for symptom relief → pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent effusions
Complicated parapneumonic/empyema: chest tube + intrapleural fibrinolytics (tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg BID x 3 days — MIST2 trial) if loculated
Surgical (VATS) decortication if fibrinolytics fail
pH <7.2 in parapneumonic effusion → urgent drainage
Massive effusion with mediastinal shift → urgent therapeutic thoracentesis
Re-expansion pulmonary edema: risk if >1–1.5 L drained at once; stop if chest tightness or cough
Hemothorax (pleural Hct >50% serum) → chest tube; if >1500 mL initial output or >200 mL/hr → thoracotomy
Light's criteria misclassify ~25% of CHF effusions as exudative (especially after diuretics) → use serum-pleural albumin gradient >1.2 g/dL to reclassify as transudate
ADA >40 U/L in a lymphocytic exudate in Indonesia = TB until proven otherwise
Bilateral effusions in CHF: thoracentesis only if asymmetric, unilateral, febrile, or not responding to diuretics
Hepatic hydrothorax: usually right-sided; treat with diuretics (NOT chest tube — leads to protein/electrolyte depletion)
Yellow nail syndrome: triad of yellow nails, lymphedema, pleural effusion
Not checking pH in parapneumonic effusions (determines need for drainage)
Draining >1.5 L at once (re-expansion pulmonary edema risk)
Placing chest tube for hepatic hydrothorax (contraindicated)
Not sending ADA in endemic TB areas
Forgetting to send cytology for suspected malignancy (repeat if first negative)
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Light's criteria: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, LDH >2/3 ULN
ADA >40 = TB pleuritis in endemic areas
pH <7.2 = complicated parapneumonic → drain
Empyema = frank pus or positive culture → drain
Chylothorax: triglycerides >110 mg/dL
Hemothorax: pleural Hct >50% of serum
Malignant effusion cytology: ~60% sensitivity on first tap
MIST2 trial: tPA + DNase for loculated effusions
Hepatic hydrothorax: right-sided, treat with diuretics, NOT chest tube
Re-expansion pulmonary edema: limit drainage to 1–1.5 L per session
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are Light's criteria? (Protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, LDH >2/3 ULN)
When does a parapneumonic effusion need drainage? (pH <7.2, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or frank pus)
What does ADA >40 suggest? (TB pleuritis)
What is the most common cause of transudative effusion? (Heart failure)
Why shouldn't you place a chest tube for hepatic hydrothorax? (Leads to massive protein/electrolyte loss; treat underlying cause)
Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP): no underlying lung disease; typically tall, thin young males, smokers
Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP): underlying lung disease (COPD most common, also TB, cystic fibrosis, Pneumocystis)
Traumatic: penetrating or blunt chest trauma
Tension pneumothorax: life-threatening; one-way valve mechanism → mediastinal shift → hemodynamic collapse
Iatrogenic: post-central line, thoracentesis, mechanical ventilation, lung biopsy
Sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea
Decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance on affected side
Tension: tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side, JVD, hypotension, tachycardia
CXR (upright, expiratory): visceral pleural line, absent lung markings beyond the line
Measure: apex-to-cupola distance
BTS: >2 cm at hilum = large
ACCP: ≥3 cm apex-to-cupola = large
CT chest: for uncertain cases, bullae assessment, or SSP evaluation
ABG: hypoxemia, respiratory alkalosis (or hypercapnia in SSP)

rows: Needle aspiration (14–16G, 2nd ICS MCL) → if fails, chest tube
SSP, small (<2 cm), age <50, minimal symptoms
Immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS MCL or 4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line) → chest tube

blocks.text: Safe triangle: bordered by anterior border of latissimus dorsi, lateral border of pectoralis major, line superior to horizontal level of nipple, and apex below axilla
Insertion: 4th–5th ICS, mid-axillary line (safe triangle)
Connect to underwater seal drainage
Remove when: no air leak for 24 hours + lung fully expanded on CXR
Persistent air leak >5–7 days
Second ipsilateral pneumothorax
First contralateral pneumothorax
Bilateral pneumothorax
Tension pneumothorax
High-risk occupation (pilot, diver)
Visible bullae on CT
Tension pneumothorax: CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS — do NOT wait for CXR; immediate needle decompression
Bilateral pneumothorax: rare but life-threatening
SSP: always admit (higher morbidity/mortality than PSP)
Catamenial pneumothorax: recurrent right-sided pneumothorax in menstruating women (endometriosis)
Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis, not radiological
PSP recurrence rate: ~30% after first episode, ~50% after second
Smoking cessation reduces recurrence risk
Small-bore chest tubes (8–14 Fr) are as effective as large-bore for pneumothorax
Ambulatory devices (Heimlich valve) allow outpatient management of selected PSP
In mechanically ventilated patients, any pneumothorax can become tension → chest tube
Waiting for CXR in tension pneumothorax
Not admitting SSP patients
Using large-bore chest tubes when small-bore is sufficient
Not advising against flying (wait 1–2 weeks after resolution) and diving (permanent contraindication unless pleurectomy)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the immediate management of tension pneumothorax? (Needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL → chest tube)
What is the safe triangle for chest tube insertion? (Anterior lat dorsi, lateral pec major, above nipple line)
What is the recurrence rate after first PSP? (~30%)
When do you refer for surgery? (Persistent air leak >5–7 days, second ipsilateral, bilateral)
What is catamenial pneumothorax? (Recurrent right-sided pneumothorax with menses — endometriosis)
