xdr-tb: mdr + fluoroquinolone + bedaquiline/linezolid resistance) who classification: new, relapse, treatment after failure, treatment after loss to follow-up 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors m. tuberculosis (acid-fast bacillus, aerobic, slow-growing) risk factors for reactivation: hiv (most important), diabetes, malnutrition, immunosuppression, silicosis, ckd, tnf-alpha inhibitors, age extremes indonesia: high-burden country (top 2 globally) 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology inhaled droplet nuclei → alveolar macrophages → granuloma formation (caseating) primary tb: ghon focus (parenchymal) + ranke complex (ghon focus + hilar lymphadenopathy) reactivation: upper lobes (high o2 tension favors mycobacterial growth) miliary tb: hematogenous dissemination 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation chronic cough ≥2 weeks (indonesia: ≥2 weeks is screening threshold) hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss, low-grade fever upper lobe cavitary lesion on cxr = classic extrapulmonary: lymph node (most common), pleural, meningeal, skeletal (pott disease), genitourinary, peritoneal 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup sputum afb smear x 2 (spot-morning or spot-spot) — ziehl-neelsen stain genexpert mtb/rif (xpert): rapid molecular test (results in 2 hours); detects tb and rifampicin resistance; sensitivity ~98% in smear-positive, ~67% in smear-negative sputum culture (löwenstein-jensen or mgit): gold standard but takes 2–8 weeks cxr: upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, fibrosis, miliary pattern igra or tst: for ltbi diagnosis (not for active tb diagnosis) 6. diagnostic criteria (indonesia/who) 6. diagnostic criteria (indonesia/who) bacteriologically confirmed: positive smear, xpert, or culture clinically diagnosed: clinical + radiological findings without bacteriological confirmation 7. stepwise management (who/pdpi) 7. stepwise management (who/pdpi) intensive phase (2 months) → continuation phase (4 months): category 1 (new patients): 2hrze / 4hr isoniazid (h), rifampicin (r), pyrazinamide (z), ethambutol (e) category 2 (retreatment): 2hrzes / 1hrze / 5hre (streptomycin added in first 2 months) note: who now recommends xpert for all retreatment cases to rule out mdr-tb before starting category 2 8. medication table 8. medication table drug dose (daily) side effects monitoring references isoniazid (h) 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) peripheral neuropathy, hepatitis lfts; give pyridoxine (b6) 25 mg/day rifampicin (r) 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) hepatitis, orange secretions, drug interactions (cyp450 inducer) lfts; check drug interactions pyrazinamide (z) 25 mg/kg hepatitis, hyperuricemia, arthralgia uric acid, lfts ethambutol (e) 15 mg/kg optic neuritis (red-green color blindness) visual acuity, color vision streptomycin (s) 15 mg/kg im ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity audiometry, renal function 9. drug-induced hepatitis protocol 9. drug-induced hepatitis protocol stop all hepatotoxic drugs if alt 3x uln with symptoms or 5x uln without symptoms wait for lfts to normalize reintroduce one at a time: r → h → z (most hepatotoxic last) if severe hepatitis: may need to permanently exclude pyrazinamide 10. mdr-tb management 10. mdr-tb management bedaquiline-based regimen (bpal: bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) — who 2022 duration: 6–9 months (shorter regimens now preferred) refer to specialized mdr-tb center 11. tb-hiv coinfection 11. tb-hiv coinfection start art within 2 weeks if cd4 50; within 8 weeks if cd4 50 exception: tb meningitis — delay art to 8 weeks (reduces iris) drug interaction: rifampicin + efavirenz (ok); rifampicin + dolutegravir (double dose dtg to 50 mg bid) rifampicin reduces levels of pis — use rifabutin instead if on pi-based art 12. complications 12. complications hemoptysis (massive: 600 ml/24h → bronchial artery embolization) pneumothorax, empyema, bronchiectasis miliary tb, tb meningitis drug-induced hepatitis, iris (in hiv) 13. red flags 13. red flags massive hemoptysis → position bleeding side down, intubation, bronchial artery embolization tb meningitis → start dexamethasone + anti-tb immediately mdr-tb suspected → do not start category 1; get xpert first 14. clinical pearls 14. clinical pearls always give pyridoxine (b6) with isoniazid (prevents peripheral neuropathy) rifampicin turns body fluids orange (warn patients — not harmful) ethambutol → optic neuritis (test color vision before starting) pyrazinamide is the most hepatotoxic first-line drug sputum conversion at 2 months is key prognostic marker dots (directly observed therapy, short-course) is standard of care 15. common pitfalls 15. common pitfalls not checking xpert for rifampicin resistance in retreatment cases forgetting pyridoxine supplementation not monitoring lfts during treatment stopping treatment early when symptoms improve (→ relapse, resistance) not screening for hiv in all tb patients top 10 testable facts: standard regimen: 2hrze/4hr xpert mtb/rif: detects tb + rifampicin resistance in 2 hours upper lobe cavitary lesion = classic cxr finding isoniazid → peripheral neuropathy (prevent with pyridoxine) rifampicin → cyp450 inducer (drug interactions!) ethambutol → optic neuritis pyrazinamide → most hepatotoxic; hyperuricemia drug-induced hepatitis: reintroduce r → h → z tb-hiv: start art within 2 weeks if cd4 50 mdr-tb = resistant to inh + rifampicin top 5 pimping questions: what is the order of reintroduction after drug-induced hepatitis? (hemothorax) 4. interpretation of pleural fluid 4. interpretation of pleural fluid parameter significance references ph <7.2 complicated parapneumonic / empyema → needs drainage glucose <60 mg/dl empyema, tb, malignancy, ra ada >40 u/l strongly suggests tb pleuritis (sensitivity ~90% in high-prevalence areas) lymphocyte predominant tb, malignancy, lymphoma neutrophil predominant parapneumonic, pe, early tb triglycerides >110 mg/dl chylothorax (thoracic duct injury) hematocrit >50% of serum hemothorax cytology positive malignant effusion (sensitivity ~60% first tap, ~75% with repeat) 5. classification of parapneumonic effusions 5. classification of parapneumonic effusions type characteristics management references simple parapneumonic ph >7.2, glucose >60, ldh <1000, no organisms antibiotics alone complicated parapneumonic ph <7.2, glucose <60, ldh >1000, or positive gram stain chest tube drainage + antibiotics empyema frank pus or positive culture chest tube drainage + antibiotics ± fibrinolytics or surgery 6. stepwise management 6. stepwise management determine transudative vs exudative (light's criteria) transudate → treat underlying cause (diuretics for chf, albumin for cirrhosis) exudate → determine etiology: parapneumonic: antibiotics ± drainage based on classification above tb pleuritis: standard anti-tb regimen (2hrze/4hr); steroids not routinely recommended malignant: therapeutic thoracentesis for symptom relief → pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent effusions complicated parapneumonic/empyema: chest tube + intrapleural fibrinolytics (tpa 10 mg + dnase 5 mg bid x 3 days — mist2 trial) if loculated surgical (vats) decortication if fibrinolytics fail 7. red flags 7. red flags ph <7.2 in parapneumonic effusion → urgent drainage massive effusion with mediastinal shift → urgent therapeutic thoracentesis re-expansion pulmonary edema: risk if >1–1.5 l drained at once; stop if chest tightness or cough hemothorax (pleural hct >50% serum) → chest tube; if >1500 ml initial output or >200 ml/hr → thoracotomy 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls light's criteria misclassify ~25% of chf effusions as exudative (especially after diuretics) → use serum-pleural albumin gradient >1.2 g/dl to reclassify as transudate ada >40 u/l in a lymphocytic exudate in indonesia = tb until proven otherwise bilateral effusions in chf: thoracentesis only if asymmetric, unilateral, febrile, or not responding to diuretics hepatic hydrothorax: usually right-sided; treat with diuretics (not chest tube — leads to protein/electrolyte depletion) yellow nail syndrome: triad of yellow nails, lymphedema, pleural effusion 9. common pitfalls 9. common pitfalls not checking ph in parapneumonic effusions (determines need for drainage) draining >1.5 l at once (re-expansion pulmonary edema risk) placing chest tube for hepatic hydrothorax (contraindicated) not sending ada in endemic tb areas forgetting to send cytology for suspected malignancy (repeat if first negative) top 10 testable facts: light's criteria: protein ratio >0.5, ldh ratio >0.6, ldh >2/3 uln ada >40 = tb pleuritis in endemic areas ph <7.2 = complicated parapneumonic → drain empyema = frank pus or positive culture → drain chylothorax: triglycerides >110 mg/dl hemothorax: pleural hct >50% of serum malignant effusion cytology: ~60% sensitivity on first tap mist2 trial: tpa + dnase for loculated effusions hepatic hydrothorax: right-sided, treat with diuretics, not chest tube re-expansion pulmonary edema: limit drainage to 1–1.5 l per session top 5 pimping questions: what are light's criteria? (protein ratio >0.5, ldh ratio >0.6, ldh >2/3 uln) when does a parapneumonic effusion need drainage? (ph <7.2, glucose <60, positive gram stain, or frank pus) what does ada >40 suggest? (tb pleuritis) what is the most common cause of transudative effusion? (heart failure) why shouldn't you place a chest tube for hepatic hydrothorax? (leads to massive protein/electrolyte loss; treat underlying cause) topic 14: pneumothorax topic 14 · pneumothorax 1. definition classification 1. definition classification primary spontaneous pneumothorax (psp): no underlying lung disease; typically tall, thin young males, smokers secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (ssp): underlying lung disease (copd most common, also tb, cystic fibrosis, pneumocystis) traumatic: penetrating or blunt chest trauma tension pneumothorax: life-threatening; one-way valve mechanism → mediastinal shift → hemodynamic collapse iatrogenic: post-central line, thoracentesis, mechanical ventilation, lung biopsy 2. clinical presentation 2. clinical presentation sudden pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea decreased breath sounds, hyperresonance on affected side tension: tracheal deviation away from affected side, jvd, hypotension, tachycardia 3. diagnostic workup 3. diagnostic workup cxr (upright, expiratory): visceral pleural line, absent lung markings beyond the line measure: apex-to-cupola distance bts: >2 cm at hilum = large accp: ≥3 cm apex-to-cupola = large ct chest: for uncertain cases, bullae assessment, or ssp evaluation abg: hypoxemia, respiratory alkalosis (or hypercapnia in ssp) 4. management — bts 2023 guidelines 4. management — bts 2023 guidelines type size/symptoms management references psp, small (<2 cm), minimal symptoms observation + repeat cxr in 2–4 hours discharge if stable bts 2023 psp, large (>2 cm) or symptomatic needle aspiration (14–16g, 2nd ics mcl) → if fails, chest tube ssp, small (<2 cm), age <50, minimal symptoms aspiration → if fails, chest tube admit all ssp ssp, large (>2 cm) or symptomatic chest tube (small-bore 8–14 fr preferred) tension pneumothorax immediate needle decompression (2nd ics mcl or 4th/5th ics anterior axillary line) → chest tube do not wait for cxr bilateral/hemodynamically unstable chest tube 5. chest tube placement 5. chest tube placement safe triangle: bordered by anterior border of latissimus dorsi, lateral border of pectoralis major, line superior to horizontal level of nipple, and apex below axilla insertion: 4th–5th ics, mid-axillary line (safe triangle) connect to underwater seal drainage remove when: no air leak for 24 hours + lung fully expanded on cxr 6. indications for surgery (vats) 6. indications for surgery (vats) persistent air leak >5–7 days second ipsilateral pneumothorax first contralateral pneumothorax bilateral pneumothorax tension pneumothorax high-risk occupation (pilot, diver) visible bullae on ct 7. red flags 7. red flags tension pneumothorax: clinical diagnosis — do not wait for cxr; immediate needle decompression bilateral pneumothorax: rare but life-threatening ssp: always admit (higher morbidity/mortality than psp) catamenial pneumothorax: recurrent right-sided pneumothorax in menstruating women (endometriosis) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis, not radiological psp recurrence rate: ~30% after first episode, ~50% after second smoking cessation reduces recurrence risk small-bore chest tubes (8–14 fr) are as effective as large-bore for pneumothorax ambulatory devices (heimlich valve) allow outpatient management of selected psp in mechanically ventilated patients, any pneumothorax can become tension → chest tube 9. common pitfalls 9. common pitfalls waiting for cxr in tension pneumothorax not admitting ssp patients using large-bore chest tubes when small-bore is sufficient not advising against flying (wait 1–2 weeks after resolution) and diving (permanent contraindication unless pleurectomy) top 5 pimping questions: what is the immediate management of tension pneumothorax? (needle decompression 2nd ics mcl → chest tube) what is the safe triangle for chest tube insertion? (anterior lat dorsi, lateral pec major, above nipple line) what is the recurrence rate after first psp? (~30%) when do you refer for surgery? (persistent air leak >5–7 days, second ipsilateral, bilateral) what is catamenial pneumothorax? (recurrent right-sided pneumothorax with menses — endometriosis)

parts.title: PART III: GASTROENTEROLOGY  HEPATOBILIARY

blocks.text: Acute bacterial infection of the biliary system due to biliary obstruction. Life-threatening if not treated promptly.
Most common cause: choledocholithiasis (CBD stones)
Others: malignant obstruction, strictures, post-ERCP, parasites (Clonorchis, Ascaris)
Organisms: E. coli (most common), Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides
Charcot triad: fever + jaundice + RUQ pain (50–70% of cases)
Reynolds pentad: Charcot triad + hypotension + altered mental status (severe/suppurative cholangitis)
Diagnosis requires:
A: Systemic inflammation (fever 38°C and/or shaking chills; or lab evidence: WBC abnormal, CRP ≥1 mg/dL)
B: Cholestasis (jaundice; or abnormal LFTs: ALP, GGT, AST, ALT 1.5x ULN)
C: Imaging (biliary dilation or evidence of etiology: stone, stent, stricture)
Suspected: A + B or A + C; Definite: A + B + C

rows: Does not meet Grade II or III criteria
≥2 of: WBC 12,000 or 4,000; fever ≥39°C; age ≥75; bilirubin ≥5; albumin 0.7x LLN

Organ dysfunction: cardiovascular (hypotension requiring vasopressors), neurological (altered consciousness), respiratory (PaO2/FiO2 300), renal (Cr 2), hepatic (INR 1.5), hematological (platelets 100,000)

blocks.text: IV fluids + NPO
IV antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h; OR ceftriaxone 2 g + metronidazole 500 mg q8h; OR meropenem 1 g q8h (severe)
Biliary drainage:
ERCP (preferred): sphincterotomy + stone extraction or stent placement
Percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD): if ERCP fails or inaccessible
EUS-guided drainage: emerging alternative
Timing: Grade III → urgent (24 hours); Grade II → early (24–48 hours); Grade I → elective
Definitive treatment of underlying cause (cholecystectomy for gallstones)
Reynolds pentad = suppurative cholangitis → ICU + urgent drainage
ERCP is both diagnostic AND therapeutic
Do NOT delay biliary drainage in severe cholangitis — antibiotics alone are insufficient
Blood cultures positive in ~50% of cases
Post-ERCP pancreatitis: most common complication of ERCP (~5%)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is Charcot triad? (Fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)
What is Reynolds pentad? (Charcot triad + hypotension + AMS)
What is the preferred method of biliary drainage? (ERCP)
What grading system is used for cholangitis? (Tokyo Guidelines 2018)
What is the most common organism? (E. coli)
Stones in the common bile duct (CBD). Present in ~10–15% of patients with cholelithiasis.
Biliary colic: episodic RUQ/epigastric pain, often postprandial
Obstructive jaundice: conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, pale stools, dark urine
May be asymptomatic (incidental finding)
Complications: cholangitis, pancreatitis
Labs: elevated direct bilirubin, ALP, GGT; ALT/AST may be transiently very high (can mimic hepatitis)
Transabdominal US: dilated CBD (6 mm, or 8 mm post-cholecystectomy); sensitivity for CBD stones only ~50%
MRCP: sensitivity/specificity ~95% for CBD stones; non-invasive
EUS: sensitivity ~95%; best for small stones (5 mm)
ERCP: therapeutic — reserve for confirmed or high-probability stones

rows: CBD stone on US, cholangitis, bilirubin 4 + dilated CBD
Abnormal LFTs, age 55, dilated CBD without visible stone
Normal labs, normal CBD, no clinical predictors

blocks.text: High probability: ERCP with sphincterotomy + stone extraction → laparoscopic cholecystectomy
Intermediate probability: MRCP or EUS → ERCP if confirmed
Laparoscopic CBD exploration: alternative to ERCP in experienced centers
Large stones (15 mm): may need mechanical lithotripsy or electrohydraulic lithotripsy
Transient ALT spike 1000 U/L can occur with stone passage — mimics acute hepatitis
CBD normally dilates with age (~1 mm per decade after age 60)
Post-cholecystectomy CBD 8 mm may be normal
Mirizzi syndrome: gallstone impacted in cystic duct compressing CBD → obstructive jaundice
Acute inflammation of the gallbladder, usually due to cystic duct obstruction by gallstone (90% calculous).
Calculous (90%): gallstone impacted in cystic duct
Acalculous (10%): critically ill, ICU patients, TPN, HIV, diabetes — higher mortality
RUQ pain (persistent, 6 hours — distinguishes from biliary colic which is 6 hours)
Murphy sign: inspiratory arrest during RUQ palpation (sensitivity ~65%)
Fever, nausea, vomiting
Leukocytosis, mildly elevated LFTs (if CBD not involved)
A: Local signs (Murphy sign, RUQ mass/pain/tenderness)
B: Systemic signs (fever 38°C, elevated CRP, elevated WBC)
C: Imaging confirmation
Suspected: A + B; Definite: A + B + C
RUQ ultrasound (first-line): gallstones + gallbladder wall thickening (3 mm) + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic Murphy sign
HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy): non-visualization of gallbladder after 4 hours = cystic duct obstruction (sensitivity ~97% — most accurate test)
CT: for complications (perforation, abscess, emphysematous cholecystitis)

rows: No organ dysfunction, no severe local inflammation
WBC 18,000, palpable mass, duration 72 hours, marked local inflammation (gangrenous, emphysematous, pericholecystic abscess, biliary peritonitis)
Early cholecystectomy if experienced surgeon; otherwise percutaneous cholecystostomy
Organ dysfunction (cardiovascular, neurological, respiratory, renal, hepatic, hematological)

blocks.text: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia (NSAIDs preferred over opioids — ketorolac; avoid morphine — theoretical sphincter of Oddi spasm)
IV antibiotics: ceftriaxone 2 g daily + metronidazole 500 mg q8h; or piperacillin-tazobactam
Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy: within 72 hours of symptom onset (preferred — reduces complications and hospital stay vs delayed)
If unfit for surgery: percutaneous cholecystostomy (drainage)
Acalculous cholecystitis: percutaneous cholecystostomy → interval cholecystectomy
Gangrenous cholecystitis, perforation, pericholecystic abscess
Emphysematous cholecystitis (gas-forming organisms — Clostridium; seen in diabetics; high mortality)
Gallstone ileus (stone erodes into duodenum → SBO at ileocecal valve; Rigler triad on imaging: pneumobilia + SBO + ectopic gallstone)
Mirizzi syndrome
Biliary colic 6 hours; cholecystitis 6 hours
Murphy sign is absent in gangrenous cholecystitis (denervation)
Emphysematous cholecystitis: air in gallbladder wall on CT → surgical emergency
HIDA scan is the most sensitive test for acute cholecystitis
Early cholecystectomy (72 hours) is superior to delayed (CHOCOLATE trial)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What differentiates biliary colic from cholecystitis? (Duration: 6h vs 6h; fever/leukocytosis in cholecystitis)
What is the most accurate imaging test? (HIDA scan — 97% sensitivity)
What is emphysematous cholecystitis? (Gas-forming infection, diabetics, Clostridium, surgical emergency)
What is Rigler triad? (Pneumobilia + SBO + ectopic gallstone = gallstone ileus)
When should cholecystectomy be performed? (Within 72 hours — early is better)
Acute inflammation of the pancreas. Requires ≥2 of 3 criteria (Revised Atlanta Classification 2012):
Abdominal pain consistent with pancreatitis (epigastric, radiating to back)
Serum lipase or amylase ≥3x ULN
Characteristic findings on imaging (CT/MRI/US)

Idiopathic, Gallstones (40%), Ethanol (30%), Trauma, Steroids, Mumps/malignancy, Autoimmune, Scorpion sting, Hyperlipidemia/Hypercalcemia/Hypothermia, ERCP, Drugs (valproic acid, azathioprine, didanosine, thiazides)
Most common: gallstones (40%) and alcohol (30%)
Premature activation of trypsinogen → trypsin → autodigestion of pancreas
Local inflammation → SIRS → organ failure (severe cases)
Epigastric pain radiating to back, worse supine, better leaning forward
Nausea, vomiting, abdominal tenderness
Severe: Cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis), Grey Turner sign (flank ecchymosis) — indicate retroperitoneal hemorrhage (rare, late finding)

rows: No organ failure, no local complications
Transient organ failure (48h) OR local complications
BUN 25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age 60, Pleural effusion

blocks.text: BISAP ≥3 = severe; Ranson ≥3 = severe; APACHE II ≥8 = severe
Labs: lipase (preferred over amylase — more sensitive and specific), CBC, BMP, LFTs, calcium, triglycerides, lactate
Imaging: CT with contrast NOT needed at admission unless diagnostic uncertainty or no improvement at 48–72 hours
RUQ ultrasound: to evaluate for gallstones (do in ALL cases)
CT severity index (CTSI/Balthazar): assess necrosis extent after 72 hours

Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation: Lactated Ringer's preferred (reduces SIRS vs NS — WATERFALL trial showed goal-directed is preferred over aggressive); goal-directed fluid therapy targeting urine output 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr
Pain management: IV acetaminophen, NSAIDs; opioids if needed (hydromorphone preferred; morphine is acceptable — sphincter of Oddi spasm is largely theoretical)
NPO initially → early oral feeding as tolerated (within 24 hours if tolerated — low-fat solid diet; no need for clear liquids first)
No role for prophylactic antibiotics (even in necrotizing pancreatitis)
Goal-directed IV fluids (LR preferred)
Pain control
Early oral feeding (within 24 hours)
Identify and treat cause:
Gallstone pancreatitis: ERCP within 24 hours ONLY if concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction; cholecystectomy during same admission (mild) or after recovery (severe)
Alcohol: cessation counseling
Hypertriglyceridemia (1000 mg/dL): insulin infusion ± plasmapheresis
Necrotizing pancreatitis:
Sterile necrosis: conservative management

Infected necrosis (suspect if: fever, rising WBC after 7–10 days, gas in necrosis on CT): antibiotics (meropenem) → step-up approach: percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgical necrosectomy (PANTER trial)
Walled-off necrosis (WON, 4 weeks): drain if symptomatic or infected

rows: 1 g q8h; only if infected necrosis confirmed/suspected

blocks.text: Early (2 weeks): organ failure, SIRS, ARDS, AKI
Late (2 weeks): pancreatic necrosis (sterile or infected), pseudocyst (4 weeks, encapsulated), WON, pancreatic duct disruption, splenic vein thrombosis, pseudoaneurysm
Persistent organ failure 48 hours
BISAP ≥3, Ranson ≥3, APACHE II ≥8
Infected necrosis (gas on CT)
Abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure 20 mmHg)
Lipase is preferred over amylase (more sensitive and specific; stays elevated longer)
Do NOT get CT at admission — wait 48–72 hours if needed (necrosis not visible early)
Early feeding improves outcomes (reduces infection, hospital stay)
No prophylactic antibiotics — even in necrotizing pancreatitis
Gallstone pancreatitis: cholecystectomy during same admission for mild cases
ERCP only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction (NOT routine for gallstone pancreatitis)
Step-up approach for infected necrosis (PANTER trial): drain → endoscopic → surgical
Ordering CT on day 1 (necrosis not visible yet)
Giving prophylactic antibiotics
Keeping patient NPO too long (early feeding is better)
Doing ERCP for all gallstone pancreatitis (only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction)
Aggressive fluid resuscitation without monitoring (can cause fluid overload/abdominal compartment syndrome — WATERFALL trial supports goal-directed approach)
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Diagnosis: ≥2 of 3 criteria (pain, lipase ≥3x ULN, imaging)
Most common causes: gallstones (40%), alcohol (30%)
Lipase preferred over amylase
LR preferred over NS for resuscitation
Early oral feeding within 24 hours
No prophylactic antibiotics
CT at 48–72 hours, not on admission
ERCP only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction
Infected necrosis: step-up approach (PANTER trial)
Cholecystectomy during same admission for mild gallstone pancreatitis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are the Revised Atlanta criteria? (≥2 of: pain, lipase ≥3x ULN, imaging)
When do you do ERCP in gallstone pancreatitis? (Only if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction)
What is the step-up approach for infected necrosis? (Percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgical)
What are Cullen and Grey Turner signs? (Periumbilical and flank ecchymosis — retroperitoneal hemorrhage)
Why is LR preferred over NS? (Reduces SIRS; NS may worsen acidosis)
Acute diarrhea (≥3 loose stools/day) with or without vomiting, lasting 14 days, usually infectious.
Viral (most common): norovirus, rotavirus
Bacterial: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli (ETEC — traveler's diarrhea), Vibrio cholerae, C. difficile (antibiotic-associated), S. aureus (preformed toxin)
Parasitic: Entamoeba histolytica (amoebic dysentery — common in Indonesia), Giardia, Cryptosporidium
Toxin-mediated (short incubation 1–6 hours): S. aureus, Bacillus cereus

rows: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, Entamoeba, C. difficile

blocks.text: Watery diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps
Dysentery: bloody/mucoid stool, fever, tenesmus
Dehydration: dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, hypotension, decreased urine output

rows: Alert, normal skin turgor, drinks normally
Restless, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly, slow skin turgor
ORS supervised (Plan B): 75 mL/kg over 4 hours
Lethargic/unconscious, very slow skin turgor, unable to drink
IV fluids (Plan C): Ringer's Lactate 30 mL/kg in 30 min → 70 mL/kg in 2.5 hours

blocks.text: Most cases: clinical diagnosis, no labs needed
Indications for stool studies: bloody diarrhea, severe dehydration, fever >38.5°C, immunocompromised, duration >7 days, suspected outbreak
Stool microscopy: WBCs/RBCs (inflammatory), ova and parasites, trophozoites (Entamoeba)
Stool culture: if bloody diarrhea or severe illness
C. difficile: stool toxin assay (GDH screen → toxin A/B or NAAT confirmation)
Electrolytes, BUN/Cr: if significant dehydration
Rehydration: ORS for mild-moderate; IV LR/NS for severe
Zinc supplementation: 20 mg/day x 10–14 days (WHO recommendation, especially in developing countries)
Antibiotics — only for specific indications:
Empiric: ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID x 3 days (moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea, dysentery)
Shigella: ciprofloxacin or azithromycin
Cholera: doxycycline 300 mg single dose or azithromycin 1 g
C. difficile: vancomycin 125 mg PO QID x 10 days (first-line) or fidaxomicin 200 mg BID x 10 days
Amoebic dysentery: metronidazole 750 mg TID x 7–10 days → paromomycin (luminal agent)
Salmonella (non-typhoidal): antibiotics only if severe, bacteremic, immunocompromised, or extremes of age
Anti-motility agents: loperamide — AVOID in bloody diarrhea or suspected C. difficile (risk of toxic megacolon)
Probiotics: may reduce duration by ~1 day (Saccharomyces boulardii, Lactobacillus)
Bloody diarrhea + no fever + renal failure → suspect EHEC/HUS (do NOT give antibiotics — increases HUS risk)
Severe dehydration with rice-water stools → cholera
Antibiotic use + diarrhea → C. difficile
Returning traveler + bloody diarrhea → Entamoeba (liver abscess risk)
S. aureus food poisoning: vomiting-predominant, onset 1–6 hours, self-limited
Bacillus cereus: two forms — emetic (fried rice, 1–6 hours) and diarrheal (8–16 hours)
EHEC (O157:H7): do NOT give antibiotics (increases HUS risk)
Non-typhoidal Salmonella: antibiotics prolong carrier state in uncomplicated cases
Entamoeba: always follow metronidazole with luminal agent (paromomycin) to eliminate cysts
ORS composition (WHO): Na 75, glucose 75, K 20, Cl 65, citrate 10 (mmol/L)
Giving antibiotics for viral gastroenteritis
Using loperamide in bloody diarrhea or C. difficile
Giving antibiotics for EHEC (increases HUS risk)
Not following metronidazole with paromomycin for amoebiasis
Not considering C. difficile in hospitalized patients on antibiotics
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
When do you NOT give antibiotics in diarrhea? (EHEC — increases HUS risk; uncomplicated non-typhoidal Salmonella)
What is the first-line treatment for C. difficile? (Oral vancomycin 125 mg QID x 10 days)
What is rice-water stool? (Cholera)
What is the luminal agent for amoebiasis? (Paromomycin — to eliminate cysts after metronidazole)
What are the two forms of Bacillus cereus? (Emetic: fried rice, 1–6h; Diarrheal: 8–16h)
Reflux of gastric contents causing troublesome symptoms and/or complications. Lyon Consensus 2018:
Non-erosive reflux disease (NERD): symptoms without mucosal breaks
Erosive esophagitis (ERD): mucosal breaks on endoscopy (Los Angeles classification A–D)
Barrett esophagus: intestinal metaplasia of esophageal mucosa (premalignant)
Obesity, hiatal hernia, pregnancy, smoking, alcohol, fatty/spicy foods, medications (CCBs, nitrates, anticholinergics, bisphosphonates)
Typical: heartburn, regurgitation (both worse postprandially, supine)
Atypical/extraesophageal: chronic cough, laryngitis, asthma, dental erosions, chest pain (non-cardiac)
Alarm symptoms: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia → endoscopy
Clinical diagnosis: empiric PPI trial (8 weeks) is both diagnostic and therapeutic
EGD indications: alarm symptoms, age >50 with new-onset, failure of PPI trial, screening for Barrett's
24-hour pH monitoring (with impedance): gold standard for confirming GERD; indicated when diagnosis uncertain or pre-surgical evaluation
Los Angeles Classification: A (≤5 mm mucosal break), B (>5 mm, non-confluent), C (confluent <75% circumference), D (≥75% circumference)
Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, elevate head of bed, avoid late meals (3 hours before bed), avoid triggers
PPI therapy (first-line): omeprazole 20 mg, lansoprazole 30 mg, esomeprazole 40 mg — once daily, 30 minutes before breakfast x 8 weeks
If partial response: increase to BID PPI
Maintenance: lowest effective PPI dose for LA grade C/D, Barrett's, or recurrent symptoms
H2RA (famotidine 20 mg BID): for mild/intermittent symptoms or as add-on at bedtime
Surgery (Nissen fundoplication): for refractory GERD with confirmed acid exposure on pH monitoring, or patient preference
Diagnosis: endoscopic salmon-colored mucosa + histologic intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells
No dysplasia: EGD every 3–5 years
Low-grade dysplasia: endoscopic eradication therapy (radiofrequency ablation) or EGD every 6–12 months
High-grade dysplasia: endoscopic eradication therapy (RFA or endoscopic mucosal resection)
All Barrett's patients: PPI indefinitely
PPI should be taken 30 minutes before meals (needs acidic environment for activation)
Long-term PPI risks: C. difficile, hypomagnesemia, osteoporosis, B12 deficiency, CKD (but benefits outweigh risks when indicated)
LA grade C/D: need long-term PPI (high relapse rate)
Non-cardiac chest pain: GERD is the most common GI cause
Esomeprazole and rabeprazole have less CYP2C19 variability (more predictable in Asian populations with high CYP2C19 poor metabolizer prevalence)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the Los Angeles classification? (A–D based on mucosal break size and confluence)
When do you do endoscopy for GERD? (Alarm symptoms, age >50 new onset, PPI failure)
What defines Barrett esophagus histologically? (Intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells)
Why take PPI 30 minutes before meals? (Needs active proton pumps for drug activation)
What is the gold standard for GERD diagnosis? (24-hour pH impedance monitoring)
Chronic transmural granulomatous inflammation affecting any part of the GI tract (mouth to anus). Most commonly: terminal ileum and colon. Montreal Classification:
Location: L1 (ileal), L2 (colonic), L3 (ileocolonic), L4 (upper GI)
Behavior: B1 (non-stricturing, non-penetrating), B2 (stricturing), B3 (penetrating), p (perianal)
Genetic: NOD2/CARD15 mutations (most studied)
Environmental: smoking WORSENS Crohn (opposite of UC), appendectomy does NOT protect
Bimodal age: 15–30 and 50–70
Transmural inflammation → skip lesions, granulomas (non-caseating), fistulas, strictures
Th1/Th17-mediated immune response
Chronic diarrhea (usually non-bloody), abdominal pain (RLQ — mimics appendicitis), weight loss, fatigue
Perianal disease: fistulas, abscesses, skin tags (pathognomonic for Crohn)
Extraintestinal: arthritis (most common), erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis/episcleritis, primary sclerosing cholangitis (more common in UC), aphthous ulcers
Complications: strictures → obstruction; fistulas (enteroenteric, enterovesical, enterocutaneous); abscess
Ileocolonoscopy with biopsies: cobblestone mucosa, skip lesions, deep ulcers, non-caseating granulomas
MR enterography: best for small bowel assessment, strictures, fistulas
Labs: CRP, ESR (disease activity), CBC (anemia), albumin, fecal calprotectin (>250 μg/g suggests active inflammation)
Rule out TB before starting biologics (TST/IGRA + CXR) — critical in Indonesia

rows: Mouth to anus (terminal ileum most common)
Colon only (starts at rectum, extends proximally)

blocks.text: Induction of remission:
Mild: budesonide 9 mg/day (ileal/right colon) x 8 weeks; or sulfasalazine (colonic only)
Moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg → taper) as bridge; start steroid-sparing agent early
Steroid-sparing agents: azathioprine/6-MP, methotrexate
Biologics (moderate-severe or steroid-dependent):
Anti-TNF: infliximab, adalimumab (first-line biologics)
Anti-integrin: vedolizumab (gut-selective)
Anti-IL-12/23: ustekinumab
Anti-IL-23: risankizumab (newer, promising)
JAK inhibitor: upadacitinib (approved for Crohn)
Maintenance: azathioprine, methotrexate, or biologics (NOT steroids for maintenance)
Perianal fistula: antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole) + seton placement + infliximab
Surgery: for complications (stricture, abscess, fistula, failure of medical therapy); NOT curative — recurrence common at anastomosis
"String sign" on barium follow-through = terminal ileum stricture (classic but rarely done now)
Always rule out TB before biologics in endemic areas (Indonesia)
Steroids are for induction ONLY — never for maintenance
Fecal calprotectin: non-invasive marker of intestinal inflammation; useful for monitoring
B12 deficiency: terminal ileum disease/resection → impaired B12 absorption
Oxalate kidney stones: fat malabsorption → increased oxalate absorption
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the key histological finding in Crohn? (Non-caseating granulomas)
How does smoking affect Crohn vs UC? (Worsens Crohn, protective in UC)
What must you rule out before starting anti-TNF in Indonesia? (TB — TST/IGRA + CXR)
Is surgery curative in Crohn? (No — recurrence at anastomosis)
What is the most common extraintestinal manifestation? (Arthritis)
Chronic mucosal inflammation limited to the colon, starting at the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. Montreal Classification:
E1: Ulcerative proctitis (rectum only)
E2: Left-sided colitis (up to splenic flexure)
E3: Extensive/pancolitis (beyond splenic flexure)
Bloody diarrhea (hallmark), urgency, tenesmus, abdominal cramping
Severity: Mayo Score or Truelove and Witts criteria
Moderate = between mild and severe
Mild-moderate proctitis: topical mesalamine (suppository 1 g/day) — superior to oral
Mild-moderate left-sided/extensive: oral mesalamine (2–4.8 g/day) + topical mesalamine (combination superior)
Moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg) → taper + start steroid-sparing agent
Steroid-dependent/refractory: biologics (infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab, ustekinumab) or JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib)
Acute severe UC (Truelove and Witts severe): IV methylprednisolone 60 mg/day → if no response in 3–5 days → rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) or colectomy
Maintenance: mesalamine (mild-moderate), azathioprine, or biologics (NOT steroids)
Toxic megacolon: colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity → surgical emergency
Colorectal cancer: increased risk after 8–10 years of extensive colitis → surveillance colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting 8 years after diagnosis
Massive hemorrhage, perforation, stricture (suspect malignancy)
Toxic megacolon not responding to medical therapy
Perforation, massive hemorrhage
Refractory disease despite medical therapy
Dysplasia/cancer
Procedure: total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) — curative
UC is CURABLE with surgery (unlike Crohn)
Toxic megacolon: stop anticholinergics, opioids, loperamide; get surgical consult
5-ASA (mesalamine) is the backbone of mild-moderate UC but NOT effective in Crohn (except sulfasalazine for colonic Crohn)
pANCA positive in ~70% of UC
Primary sclerosing cholangitis: more associated with UC than Crohn
C. difficile must be ruled out in any UC flare
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the hallmark symptom of UC? (Bloody diarrhea)
What defines toxic megacolon? (Colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity)
Is UC curable? (Yes — total proctocolectomy)
When do you start CRC surveillance? (8 years after diagnosis of extensive colitis)
What must you rule out in every UC flare? (C. difficile infection)
Bleeding proximal to the ligament of Treitz. Variceal vs non-variceal.
Peptic ulcer disease (most common non-variceal cause — 40%)
Variceal bleeding (portal hypertension)
Mallory-Weiss tear (post-vomiting mucosal tear at GEJ)
Erosive gastritis/esophagitis
Malignancy, Dieulafoy lesion, angiodysplasia
Hematemesis (red blood or coffee-ground)
Melena (black, tarry stool — as little as 50 mL blood)
Hematochezia: can occur with massive UGIB (brisk bleeding)
Hemodynamic instability: tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes

rows: BUN, Hb, SBP, HR, melena, syncope, liver disease, heart failure
Pre-endoscopy; GBS 0–1 = low risk, outpatient management
Age, shock, comorbidity, diagnosis, stigmata of hemorrhage

blocks.text: ABCs: two large-bore IVs (≥18G), type and crossmatch
Fluid resuscitation: crystalloids → blood products
Transfusion: restrictive strategy Hb <7 g/dL (target 7–9); liberal if ACS or hemodynamic instability
IV PPI: high-dose esomeprazole 80 mg bolus → 8 mg/hr infusion (for suspected peptic ulcer)
If variceal suspected: octreotide 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hr + ceftriaxone 1 g/day + restrictive transfusion (Hb 7–8)
Intubation: if massive hematemesis, encephalopathy, or inability to protect airway
Hold anticoagulants; reverse if life-threatening bleeding
Erythromycin 250 mg IV 30–120 min before EGD (prokinetic — improves visualization)
Timing: within 24 hours for most UGIB; within 12 hours if high-risk features (hemodynamic instability, bloody NG aspirate, in-hospital bleeding)
Forrest classification for peptic ulcers:
Endoscopic therapy: combination therapy (epinephrine injection + thermal coagulation or clips) for Forrest Ia, Ib, IIa
Post-endoscopy: IV PPI infusion x 72 hours for high-risk stigmata (Ia, Ib, IIa), then oral PPI
H. pylori testing in ALL peptic ulcer patients → eradicate if positive
H. pylori eradication: bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline x 14 days) or concomitant therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin + metronidazole x 14 days)
Confirm eradication: urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after completing therapy (≥2 weeks off PPI)
NSAID-related ulcer: stop NSAID; if must continue, use PPI + COX-2 selective
Aspirin: resume within 3–7 days if cardiovascular indication (do not stop indefinitely)
BUN/Cr ratio >30: suggests UGIB (digested blood → urea absorption)
Coffee-ground emesis = partially digested blood (acid converts hemoglobin to hematin)
Melena can persist 3–5 days after bleeding stops
NG tube lavage: no longer routinely recommended
Restrictive transfusion improves outcomes in UGIB (less rebleeding, lower mortality)
PPI infusion is for POST-endoscopy high-risk lesions, not as a substitute for endoscopy
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most common cause of non-variceal UGIB? (Peptic ulcer disease)
What is the Forrest classification? (Endoscopic classification of ulcer bleeding stigmata)
What is the transfusion threshold? (Hb <7 g/dL — restrictive)
When should endoscopy be performed? (Within 24 hours; within 12 hours if high-risk)
What should you test for in ALL peptic ulcers? (H. pylori)
Bleeding distal to the ligament of Treitz. Usually presents as hematochezia.
Diverticular disease (most common in elderly/Western)
Hemorrhoids (most common overall in younger patients)
Angiodysplasia (elderly, associated with aortic stenosis — Heyde syndrome)
Colorectal cancer/polyps
IBD, ischemic colitis, infectious colitis
Post-polypectomy bleeding
First: rule out UGIB (if hemodynamically unstable with hematochezia → consider UGIB with brisk bleeding; NG aspirate or upper endoscopy)
Colonoscopy: first-line diagnostic and therapeutic (after bowel prep; within 24 hours)
CT angiography: if massive bleeding, hemodynamically unstable, or colonoscopy not feasible (detects bleeding rate ≥0.3–0.5 mL/min)
Tagged RBC scan: detects slower bleeding (≥0.1 mL/min) but poor localization
Angiography: therapeutic (embolization) if CT angiography positive
Hemodynamic resuscitation (same as UGIB)
Colonoscopy after bowel prep (within 24 hours) — diagnostic and therapeutic
If massive/unstable: CT angiography → angiographic embolization
Surgery: for refractory/recurrent bleeding not amenable to endoscopic or angiographic therapy
Most diverticular bleeding (80%) stops spontaneously
Hematochezia + hemodynamic instability = consider UGIB (10–15% of hematochezia is from upper GI source)
Diverticular bleeding: painless, large-volume, self-limited in 80%
Ischemic colitis: elderly, post-surgical, watershed areas (splenic flexure), "thumbprinting" on imaging
Angiodysplasia: cecum/right colon, elderly, associated with aortic stenosis and vWD

parts.searchText: part iii: gastroenterology hepatobiliary part iii: gastroenterology hepatobiliary topic 15: acute cholangitis topic 15 · acute cholangitis 1. definition 1. definition acute bacterial infection of the biliary system due to biliary obstruction. life-threatening if not treated promptly. 2. etiology 2. etiology most common cause: choledocholithiasis (cbd stones) others: malignant obstruction, strictures, post-ercp, parasites (clonorchis, ascaris) organisms: e. coli (most common), klebsiella, enterococcus, bacteroides 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation charcot triad: fever + jaundice + ruq pain (50–70% of cases) reynolds pentad: charcot triad + hypotension + altered mental status (severe/suppurative cholangitis) 4. diagnostic criteria — tokyo guidelines 2018 (tg18) 4. diagnostic criteria — tokyo guidelines 2018 (tg18) diagnosis requires: a: systemic inflammation (fever 38°c and/or shaking chills; or lab evidence: wbc abnormal, crp ≥1 mg/dl) b: cholestasis (jaundice; or abnormal lfts: alp, ggt, ast, alt 1.5x uln) c: imaging (biliary dilation or evidence of etiology: stone, stent, stricture) suspected: a + b or a + c; definite: a + b + c 5. severity grading (tg18) 5. severity grading (tg18) grade criteria management references grade i (mild) does not meet grade ii or iii criteria antibiotics + elective biliary drainage tg18 grade ii (moderate) ≥2 of: wbc 12,000 or 4,000; fever ≥39°c; age ≥75; bilirubin ≥5; albumin 0.7x lln antibiotics + early biliary drainage (within 24–48h) grade iii (severe) organ dysfunction: cardiovascular (hypotension requiring vasopressors), neurological (altered consciousness), respiratory (pao2/fio2 300), renal (cr 2), hepatic (inr 1.5), hematological (platelets 100,000) urgent biliary drainage + icu + antibiotics 6. stepwise management 6. stepwise management iv fluids + npo iv antibiotics: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h; or ceftriaxone 2 g + metronidazole 500 mg q8h; or meropenem 1 g q8h (severe) biliary drainage: ercp (preferred): sphincterotomy + stone extraction or stent placement percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (ptbd): if ercp fails or inaccessible eus-guided drainage: emerging alternative timing: grade iii → urgent (24 hours); grade ii → early (24–48 hours); grade i → elective definitive treatment of underlying cause (cholecystectomy for gallstones) 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls reynolds pentad = suppurative cholangitis → icu + urgent drainage ercp is both diagnostic and therapeutic do not delay biliary drainage in severe cholangitis — antibiotics alone are insufficient blood cultures positive in ~50% of cases post-ercp pancreatitis: most common complication of ercp (~5%) top 5 pimping questions: what is charcot triad? (fever, jaundice, ruq pain) what is reynolds pentad? (charcot triad + hypotension + ams) what is the preferred method of biliary drainage? (ercp) what grading system is used for cholangitis? (tokyo guidelines 2018) what is the most common organism? (e. coli) topic 16: choledocholithiasis topic 16 · choledocholithiasis 1. definition 1. definition stones in the common bile duct (cbd). present in ~10–15% of patients with cholelithiasis. 2. clinical presentation 2. clinical presentation biliary colic: episodic ruq/epigastric pain, often postprandial obstructive jaundice: conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, pale stools, dark urine may be asymptomatic (incidental finding) complications: cholangitis, pancreatitis 3. diagnostic workup 3. diagnostic workup labs: elevated direct bilirubin, alp, ggt; alt/ast may be transiently very high (can mimic hepatitis) transabdominal us: dilated cbd (6 mm, or 8 mm post-cholecystectomy); sensitivity for cbd stones only ~50% mrcp: sensitivity/specificity ~95% for cbd stones; non-invasive eus: sensitivity ~95%; best for small stones (5 mm) ercp: therapeutic — reserve for confirmed or high-probability stones 4. risk stratification (asge guidelines) 4. risk stratification (asge guidelines) risk criteria approach references high cbd stone on us, cholangitis, bilirubin 4 + dilated cbd direct to ercp asge intermediate abnormal lfts, age 55, dilated cbd without visible stone mrcp or eus first low normal labs, normal cbd, no clinical predictors proceed to cholecystectomy (± ioc) 5. management 5. management high probability: ercp with sphincterotomy + stone extraction → laparoscopic cholecystectomy intermediate probability: mrcp or eus → ercp if confirmed laparoscopic cbd exploration: alternative to ercp in experienced centers large stones (15 mm): may need mechanical lithotripsy or electrohydraulic lithotripsy 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls transient alt spike 1000 u/l can occur with stone passage — mimics acute hepatitis cbd normally dilates with age (~1 mm per decade after age 60) post-cholecystectomy cbd 8 mm may be normal mirizzi syndrome: gallstone impacted in cystic duct compressing cbd → obstructive jaundice topic 17: acute cholecystitis topic 17 · acute cholecystitis 1. definition 1. definition acute inflammation of the gallbladder, usually due to cystic duct obstruction by gallstone (90% calculous). 2. etiology 2. etiology calculous (90%): gallstone impacted in cystic duct acalculous (10%): critically ill, icu patients, tpn, hiv, diabetes — higher mortality 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation ruq pain (persistent, 6 hours — distinguishes from biliary colic which is 6 hours) murphy sign: inspiratory arrest during ruq palpation (sensitivity ~65%) fever, nausea, vomiting leukocytosis, mildly elevated lfts (if cbd not involved) 4. diagnostic criteria — tokyo guidelines 2018 (tg18) 4. diagnostic criteria — tokyo guidelines 2018 (tg18) a: local signs (murphy sign, ruq mass/pain/tenderness) b: systemic signs (fever 38°c, elevated crp, elevated wbc) c: imaging confirmation suspected: a + b; definite: a + b + c 5. imaging 5. imaging ruq ultrasound (first-line): gallstones + gallbladder wall thickening (3 mm) + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic murphy sign hida scan (cholescintigraphy): non-visualization of gallbladder after 4 hours = cystic duct obstruction (sensitivity ~97% — most accurate test) ct: for complications (perforation, abscess, emphysematous cholecystitis) 6. severity grading (tg18) 6. severity grading (tg18) grade criteria management references grade i (mild) no organ dysfunction, no severe local inflammation early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72 hours) tg18 grade ii (moderate) wbc 18,000, palpable mass, duration 72 hours, marked local inflammation (gangrenous, emphysematous, pericholecystic abscess, biliary peritonitis) early cholecystectomy if experienced surgeon; otherwise percutaneous cholecystostomy grade iii (severe) organ dysfunction (cardiovascular, neurological, respiratory, renal, hepatic, hematological) icu + percutaneous cholecystostomy + delayed cholecystectomy 7. stepwise management 7. stepwise management npo, iv fluids, analgesia (nsaids preferred over opioids — ketorolac; avoid morphine — theoretical sphincter of oddi spasm) iv antibiotics: ceftriaxone 2 g daily + metronidazole 500 mg q8h; or piperacillin-tazobactam early laparoscopic cholecystectomy: within 72 hours of symptom onset (preferred — reduces complications and hospital stay vs delayed) if unfit for surgery: percutaneous cholecystostomy (drainage) acalculous cholecystitis: percutaneous cholecystostomy → interval cholecystectomy 8. complications 8. complications gangrenous cholecystitis, perforation, pericholecystic abscess emphysematous cholecystitis (gas-forming organisms — clostridium; seen in diabetics; high mortality) gallstone ileus (stone erodes into duodenum → sbo at ileocecal valve; rigler triad on imaging: pneumobilia + sbo + ectopic gallstone) mirizzi syndrome 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls biliary colic 6 hours; cholecystitis 6 hours murphy sign is absent in gangrenous cholecystitis (denervation) emphysematous cholecystitis: air in gallbladder wall on ct → surgical emergency hida scan is the most sensitive test for acute cholecystitis early cholecystectomy (72 hours) is superior to delayed (chocolate trial) top 5 pimping questions: what differentiates biliary colic from cholecystitis? (duration: 6h vs 6h; fever/leukocytosis in cholecystitis) what is the most accurate imaging test? (hida scan — 97% sensitivity) what is emphysematous cholecystitis? (gas-forming infection, diabetics, clostridium, surgical emergency) what is rigler triad? (pneumobilia + sbo + ectopic gallstone = gallstone ileus) when should cholecystectomy be performed? (within 72 hours — early is better) topic 18: acute pancreatitis topic 18 · acute pancreatitis 1. definition 1. definition acute inflammation of the pancreas. requires ≥2 of 3 criteria (revised atlanta classification 2012): abdominal pain consistent with pancreatitis (epigastric, radiating to back) serum lipase or amylase ≥3x uln characteristic findings on imaging (ct/mri/us) 2. etiology (mnemonic: i get smashed) 2. etiology (mnemonic: i get smashed) idiopathic, gallstones (40%), ethanol (30%), trauma, steroids, mumps/malignancy, autoimmune, scorpion sting, hyperlipidemia/hypercalcemia/hypothermia, ercp, drugs (valproic acid, azathioprine, didanosine, thiazides) most common: gallstones (40%) and alcohol (30%) 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology premature activation of trypsinogen → trypsin → autodigestion of pancreas local inflammation → sirs → organ failure (severe cases) 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation epigastric pain radiating to back, worse supine, better leaning forward nausea, vomiting, abdominal tenderness severe: cullen sign (periumbilical ecchymosis), grey turner sign (flank ecchymosis) — indicate retroperitoneal hemorrhage (rare, late finding) 5. severity classification (revised atlanta 2012) 5. severity classification (revised atlanta 2012) severity definition mortality references mild no organ failure, no local complications 1% atlanta 2012 moderately severe transient organ failure (48h) or local complications ~5% severe persistent organ failure (48h) 30–50% 6. scoring systems 6. scoring systems score components timing references bisap bun 25, impaired mental status, sirs, age 60, pleural effusion at admission ranson 11 criteria (5 at admission, 6 at 48h) admission + 48h apache ii multiple physiologic variables admission + daily modified marshall pao2/fio2, creatinine, sbp defines organ failure bisap ≥3 = severe; ranson ≥3 = severe; apache ii ≥8 = severe 7. diagnostic workup 7. diagnostic workup labs: lipase (preferred over amylase — more sensitive and specific), cbc, bmp, lfts, calcium, triglycerides, lactate imaging: ct with contrast not needed at admission unless diagnostic uncertainty or no improvement at 48–72 hours ruq ultrasound: to evaluate for gallstones (do in all cases) ct severity index (ctsi/balthazar): assess necrosis extent after 72 hours 8. initial stabilization 8. initial stabilization aggressive iv fluid resuscitation: lactated ringer's preferred (reduces sirs vs ns — waterfall trial showed goal-directed is preferred over aggressive); goal-directed fluid therapy targeting urine output 0.5–1 ml/kg/hr pain management: iv acetaminophen, nsaids; opioids if needed (hydromorphone preferred; morphine is acceptable — sphincter of oddi spasm is largely theoretical) npo initially → early oral feeding as tolerated (within 24 hours if tolerated — low-fat solid diet; no need for clear liquids first) no role for prophylactic antibiotics (even in necrotizing pancreatitis) 9. stepwise management 9. stepwise management goal-directed iv fluids (lr preferred) pain control early oral feeding (within 24 hours) identify and treat cause: gallstone pancreatitis: ercp within 24 hours only if concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction; cholecystectomy during same admission (mild) or after recovery (severe) alcohol: cessation counseling hypertriglyceridemia (1000 mg/dl): insulin infusion ± plasmapheresis necrotizing pancreatitis: sterile necrosis: conservative management infected necrosis (suspect if: fever, rising wbc after 7–10 days, gas in necrosis on ct): antibiotics (meropenem) → step-up approach: percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgical necrosectomy (panter trial) walled-off necrosis (won, 4 weeks): drain if symptomatic or infected 10. medication table 10. medication table drug indication notes references lactated ringer's fluid resuscitation goal-directed; 1.5 ml/kg/hr initially waterfall trial acetaminophen/nsaids first-line analgesia iv ketorolac or paracetamol meropenem infected necrosis 1 g q8h; only if infected necrosis confirmed/suspected insulin infusion hypertriglyceridemia-induced target tg 500 mg/dl 11. complications 11. complications early (2 weeks): organ failure, sirs, ards, aki late (2 weeks): pancreatic necrosis (sterile or infected), pseudocyst (4 weeks, encapsulated), won, pancreatic duct disruption, splenic vein thrombosis, pseudoaneurysm 12. red flags / icu criteria 12. red flags / icu criteria persistent organ failure 48 hours bisap ≥3, ranson ≥3, apache ii ≥8 infected necrosis (gas on ct) abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure 20 mmhg) 13. clinical pearls 13. clinical pearls lipase is preferred over amylase (more sensitive and specific; stays elevated longer) do not get ct at admission — wait 48–72 hours if needed (necrosis not visible early) early feeding improves outcomes (reduces infection, hospital stay) no prophylactic antibiotics — even in necrotizing pancreatitis gallstone pancreatitis: cholecystectomy during same admission for mild cases ercp only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction (not routine for gallstone pancreatitis) step-up approach for infected necrosis (panter trial): drain → endoscopic → surgical 14. common pitfalls 14. common pitfalls ordering ct on day 1 (necrosis not visible yet) giving prophylactic antibiotics keeping patient npo too long (early feeding is better) doing ercp for all gallstone pancreatitis (only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction) aggressive fluid resuscitation without monitoring (can cause fluid overload/abdominal compartment syndrome — waterfall trial supports goal-directed approach) top 10 testable facts: diagnosis: ≥2 of 3 criteria (pain, lipase ≥3x uln, imaging) most common causes: gallstones (40%), alcohol (30%) lipase preferred over amylase lr preferred over ns for resuscitation early oral feeding within 24 hours no prophylactic antibiotics ct at 48–72 hours, not on admission ercp only if cholangitis or persistent obstruction infected necrosis: step-up approach (panter trial) cholecystectomy during same admission for mild gallstone pancreatitis top 5 pimping questions: what are the revised atlanta criteria? (≥2 of: pain, lipase ≥3x uln, imaging) when do you do ercp in gallstone pancreatitis? (only if cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction) what is the step-up approach for infected necrosis? (percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgical) what are cullen and grey turner signs? (periumbilical and flank ecchymosis — retroperitoneal hemorrhage) why is lr preferred over ns? (reduces sirs; ns may worsen acidosis) topic 19: acute gastroenteritis topic 19 · acute gastroenteritis 1. definition 1. definition acute diarrhea (≥3 loose stools/day) with or without vomiting, lasting 14 days, usually infectious. 2. etiology 2. etiology viral (most common): norovirus, rotavirus bacterial: salmonella, shigella, campylobacter, e. coli (etec — traveler's diarrhea), vibrio cholerae, c. difficile (antibiotic-associated), s. aureus (preformed toxin) parasitic: entamoeba histolytica (amoebic dysentery — common in indonesia), giardia, cryptosporidium toxin-mediated (short incubation 1–6 hours): s. aureus, bacillus cereus 3. classification 3. classification type mechanism stool character examples references watery (secretory) enterotoxin, no mucosal invasion large volume, watery, no blood cholera, etec, norovirus, rotavirus inflammatory (dysentery) mucosal invasion/cytotoxin small volume, bloody, mucoid, tenesmus shigella, salmonella, campylobacter, ehec, entamoeba, c. difficile 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation watery diarrhea, nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps dysentery: bloody/mucoid stool, fever, tenesmus dehydration: dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, hypotension, decreased urine output 5. dehydration assessment (who) 5. dehydration assessment (who) severity signs treatment references no dehydration alert, normal skin turgor, drinks normally ors at home (plan a) who some dehydration restless, sunken eyes, drinks eagerly, slow skin turgor ors supervised (plan b): 75 ml/kg over 4 hours severe dehydration lethargic/unconscious, very slow skin turgor, unable to drink iv fluids (plan c): ringer's lactate 30 ml/kg in 30 min → 70 ml/kg in 2.5 hours 6. diagnostic workup 6. diagnostic workup most cases: clinical diagnosis, no labs needed indications for stool studies: bloody diarrhea, severe dehydration, fever >38.5°c, immunocompromised, duration >7 days, suspected outbreak stool microscopy: wbcs/rbcs (inflammatory), ova and parasites, trophozoites (entamoeba) stool culture: if bloody diarrhea or severe illness c. difficile: stool toxin assay (gdh screen → toxin a/b or naat confirmation) electrolytes, bun/cr: if significant dehydration 7. stepwise management 7. stepwise management rehydration: ors for mild-moderate; iv lr/ns for severe zinc supplementation: 20 mg/day x 10–14 days (who recommendation, especially in developing countries) antibiotics — only for specific indications: empiric: ciprofloxacin 500 mg bid x 3 days (moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea, dysentery) shigella: ciprofloxacin or azithromycin cholera: doxycycline 300 mg single dose or azithromycin 1 g c. difficile: vancomycin 125 mg po qid x 10 days (first-line) or fidaxomicin 200 mg bid x 10 days amoebic dysentery: metronidazole 750 mg tid x 7–10 days → paromomycin (luminal agent) salmonella (non-typhoidal): antibiotics only if severe, bacteremic, immunocompromised, or extremes of age anti-motility agents: loperamide — avoid in bloody diarrhea or suspected c. difficile (risk of toxic megacolon) probiotics: may reduce duration by ~1 day (saccharomyces boulardii, lactobacillus) 8. red flags 8. red flags bloody diarrhea + no fever + renal failure → suspect ehec/hus (do not give antibiotics — increases hus risk) severe dehydration with rice-water stools → cholera antibiotic use + diarrhea → c. difficile returning traveler + bloody diarrhea → entamoeba (liver abscess risk) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls s. aureus food poisoning: vomiting-predominant, onset 1–6 hours, self-limited bacillus cereus: two forms — emetic (fried rice, 1–6 hours) and diarrheal (8–16 hours) ehec (o157:h7): do not give antibiotics (increases hus risk) non-typhoidal salmonella: antibiotics prolong carrier state in uncomplicated cases entamoeba: always follow metronidazole with luminal agent (paromomycin) to eliminate cysts ors composition (who): na 75, glucose 75, k 20, cl 65, citrate 10 (mmol/l) 10. common pitfalls 10. common pitfalls giving antibiotics for viral gastroenteritis using loperamide in bloody diarrhea or c. difficile giving antibiotics for ehec (increases hus risk) not following metronidazole with paromomycin for amoebiasis not considering c. difficile in hospitalized patients on antibiotics top 5 pimping questions: when do you not give antibiotics in diarrhea? (ehec — increases hus risk; uncomplicated non-typhoidal salmonella) what is the first-line treatment for c. difficile? (oral vancomycin 125 mg qid x 10 days) what is rice-water stool? (cholera) what is the luminal agent for amoebiasis? (paromomycin — to eliminate cysts after metronidazole) what are the two forms of bacillus cereus? (emetic: fried rice, 1–6h; diarrheal: 8–16h) topic 20: gerd (gastroesophageal reflux disease) topic 20 · gerd (gastroesophageal reflux disease) 1. definition classification 1. definition classification reflux of gastric contents causing troublesome symptoms and/or complications. lyon consensus 2018: non-erosive reflux disease (nerd): symptoms without mucosal breaks erosive esophagitis (erd): mucosal breaks on endoscopy (los angeles classification a–d) barrett esophagus: intestinal metaplasia of esophageal mucosa (premalignant) 2. risk factors 2. risk factors obesity, hiatal hernia, pregnancy, smoking, alcohol, fatty/spicy foods, medications (ccbs, nitrates, anticholinergics, bisphosphonates) 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation typical: heartburn, regurgitation (both worse postprandially, supine) atypical/extraesophageal: chronic cough, laryngitis, asthma, dental erosions, chest pain (non-cardiac) alarm symptoms: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, gi bleeding, anemia → endoscopy 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup clinical diagnosis: empiric ppi trial (8 weeks) is both diagnostic and therapeutic egd indications: alarm symptoms, age >50 with new-onset, failure of ppi trial, screening for barrett's 24-hour ph monitoring (with impedance): gold standard for confirming gerd; indicated when diagnosis uncertain or pre-surgical evaluation los angeles classification: a (≤5 mm mucosal break), b (>5 mm, non-confluent), c (confluent <75% circumference), d (≥75% circumference) 5. stepwise management (acg 2022) 5. stepwise management (acg 2022) lifestyle modifications: weight loss, elevate head of bed, avoid late meals (3 hours before bed), avoid triggers ppi therapy (first-line): omeprazole 20 mg, lansoprazole 30 mg, esomeprazole 40 mg — once daily, 30 minutes before breakfast x 8 weeks if partial response: increase to bid ppi maintenance: lowest effective ppi dose for la grade c/d, barrett's, or recurrent symptoms h2ra (famotidine 20 mg bid): for mild/intermittent symptoms or as add-on at bedtime surgery (nissen fundoplication): for refractory gerd with confirmed acid exposure on ph monitoring, or patient preference 6. barrett esophagus management 6. barrett esophagus management diagnosis: endoscopic salmon-colored mucosa + histologic intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells no dysplasia: egd every 3–5 years low-grade dysplasia: endoscopic eradication therapy (radiofrequency ablation) or egd every 6–12 months high-grade dysplasia: endoscopic eradication therapy (rfa or endoscopic mucosal resection) all barrett's patients: ppi indefinitely 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls ppi should be taken 30 minutes before meals (needs acidic environment for activation) long-term ppi risks: c. difficile, hypomagnesemia, osteoporosis, b12 deficiency, ckd (but benefits outweigh risks when indicated) la grade c/d: need long-term ppi (high relapse rate) non-cardiac chest pain: gerd is the most common gi cause esomeprazole and rabeprazole have less cyp2c19 variability (more predictable in asian populations with high cyp2c19 poor metabolizer prevalence) top 5 pimping questions: what is the los angeles classification? (a–d based on mucosal break size and confluence) when do you do endoscopy for gerd? (alarm symptoms, age >50 new onset, ppi failure) what defines barrett esophagus histologically? (intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells) why take ppi 30 minutes before meals? (needs active proton pumps for drug activation) what is the gold standard for gerd diagnosis? (24-hour ph impedance monitoring) topic 21: crohn disease topic 21 · crohn disease 1. definition classification 1. definition classification chronic transmural granulomatous inflammation affecting any part of the gi tract (mouth to anus). most commonly: terminal ileum and colon. montreal classification: age: a1 (≤16), a2 (17–40), a3 (>40) location: l1 (ileal), l2 (colonic), l3 (ileocolonic), l4 (upper gi) behavior: b1 (non-stricturing, non-penetrating), b2 (stricturing), b3 (penetrating), p (perianal) 2. etiology risk factors 2. etiology risk factors genetic: nod2/card15 mutations (most studied) environmental: smoking worsens crohn (opposite of uc), appendectomy does not protect bimodal age: 15–30 and 50–70 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology transmural inflammation → skip lesions, granulomas (non-caseating), fistulas, strictures th1/th17-mediated immune response 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation chronic diarrhea (usually non-bloody), abdominal pain (rlq — mimics appendicitis), weight loss, fatigue perianal disease: fistulas, abscesses, skin tags (pathognomonic for crohn) extraintestinal: arthritis (most common), erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis/episcleritis, primary sclerosing cholangitis (more common in uc), aphthous ulcers complications: strictures → obstruction; fistulas (enteroenteric, enterovesical, enterocutaneous); abscess 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup ileocolonoscopy with biopsies: cobblestone mucosa, skip lesions, deep ulcers, non-caseating granulomas mr enterography: best for small bowel assessment, strictures, fistulas labs: crp, esr (disease activity), cbc (anemia), albumin, fecal calprotectin (>250 μg/g suggests active inflammation) rule out tb before starting biologics (tst/igra + cxr) — critical in indonesia 6. crohn vs ulcerative colitis 6. crohn vs ulcerative colitis feature crohn disease ulcerative colitis references location mouth to anus (terminal ileum most common) colon only (starts at rectum, extends proximally) distribution skip lesions continuous depth transmural mucosal/submucosal histology non-caseating granulomas crypt abscesses, pseudopolyps bloody diarrhea less common hallmark fistulas/strictures common rare perianal disease common rare smoking worsens protective surgery curative? no (recurrence) yes (proctocolectomy) cancer risk increased (less than uc) increased (higher) asca/panca asca+ / panca− asca− / panca+ 7. stepwise management (ecco 2023 / acg) 7. stepwise management (ecco 2023 / acg) induction of remission: mild: budesonide 9 mg/day (ileal/right colon) x 8 weeks; or sulfasalazine (colonic only) moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg → taper) as bridge; start steroid-sparing agent early steroid-sparing agents: azathioprine/6-mp, methotrexate biologics (moderate-severe or steroid-dependent): anti-tnf: infliximab, adalimumab (first-line biologics) anti-integrin: vedolizumab (gut-selective) anti-il-12/23: ustekinumab anti-il-23: risankizumab (newer, promising) jak inhibitor: upadacitinib (approved for crohn) maintenance: azathioprine, methotrexate, or biologics (not steroids for maintenance) perianal fistula: antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole) + seton placement + infliximab surgery: for complications (stricture, abscess, fistula, failure of medical therapy); not curative — recurrence common at anastomosis 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls "string sign" on barium follow-through = terminal ileum stricture (classic but rarely done now) always rule out tb before biologics in endemic areas (indonesia) steroids are for induction only — never for maintenance fecal calprotectin: non-invasive marker of intestinal inflammation; useful for monitoring b12 deficiency: terminal ileum disease/resection → impaired b12 absorption oxalate kidney stones: fat malabsorption → increased oxalate absorption top 5 pimping questions: what is the key histological finding in crohn? (non-caseating granulomas) how does smoking affect crohn vs uc? (worsens crohn, protective in uc) what must you rule out before starting anti-tnf in indonesia? (tb — tst/igra + cxr) is surgery curative in crohn? (no — recurrence at anastomosis) what is the most common extraintestinal manifestation? (arthritis) topic 22: ulcerative colitis topic 22 · ulcerative colitis 1. definition classification 1. definition classification chronic mucosal inflammation limited to the colon, starting at the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. montreal classification: e1: ulcerative proctitis (rectum only) e2: left-sided colitis (up to splenic flexure) e3: extensive/pancolitis (beyond splenic flexure) 2. clinical presentation 2. clinical presentation bloody diarrhea (hallmark), urgency, tenesmus, abdominal cramping severity: mayo score or truelove and witts criteria 3. severity — truelove and witts criteria 3. severity — truelove and witts criteria parameter mild severe references bloody stools/day <4 ≥6 pulse normal >90 bpm temperature normal >37.8°c hemoglobin >10.5 g/dl <10.5 g/dl esr <30 mm/hr >30 mm/hr moderate = between mild and severe 4. stepwise management (acg / ecco) 4. stepwise management (acg / ecco) mild-moderate proctitis: topical mesalamine (suppository 1 g/day) — superior to oral mild-moderate left-sided/extensive: oral mesalamine (2–4.8 g/day) + topical mesalamine (combination superior) moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40–60 mg) → taper + start steroid-sparing agent steroid-dependent/refractory: biologics (infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab, ustekinumab) or jak inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) acute severe uc (truelove and witts severe): iv methylprednisolone 60 mg/day → if no response in 3–5 days → rescue therapy (infliximab or cyclosporine) or colectomy maintenance: mesalamine (mild-moderate), azathioprine, or biologics (not steroids) 5. complications 5. complications toxic megacolon: colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity → surgical emergency colorectal cancer: increased risk after 8–10 years of extensive colitis → surveillance colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting 8 years after diagnosis massive hemorrhage, perforation, stricture (suspect malignancy) 6. surgical indications 6. surgical indications toxic megacolon not responding to medical therapy perforation, massive hemorrhage refractory disease despite medical therapy dysplasia/cancer procedure: total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (ipaa) — curative 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls uc is curable with surgery (unlike crohn) toxic megacolon: stop anticholinergics, opioids, loperamide; get surgical consult 5-asa (mesalamine) is the backbone of mild-moderate uc but not effective in crohn (except sulfasalazine for colonic crohn) panca positive in ~70% of uc primary sclerosing cholangitis: more associated with uc than crohn c. difficile must be ruled out in any uc flare top 5 pimping questions: what is the hallmark symptom of uc? (bloody diarrhea) what defines toxic megacolon? (colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity) is uc curable? (yes — total proctocolectomy) when do you start crc surveillance? (8 years after diagnosis of extensive colitis) what must you rule out in every uc flare? (c. difficile infection) topic 23: upper gi bleeding topic 23 · upper gi bleeding 1. definition classification 1. definition classification bleeding proximal to the ligament of treitz. variceal vs non-variceal. 2. etiology 2. etiology peptic ulcer disease (most common non-variceal cause — 40%) variceal bleeding (portal hypertension) mallory-weiss tear (post-vomiting mucosal tear at gej) erosive gastritis/esophagitis malignancy, dieulafoy lesion, angiodysplasia 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation hematemesis (red blood or coffee-ground) melena (black, tarry stool — as little as 50 ml blood) hematochezia: can occur with massive ugib (brisk bleeding) hemodynamic instability: tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes 4. risk stratification 4. risk stratification score components use references glasgow-blatchford (gbs) bun, hb, sbp, hr, melena, syncope, liver disease, heart failure pre-endoscopy; gbs 0–1 = low risk, outpatient management rockall (post-endoscopy) age, shock, comorbidity, diagnosis, stigmata of hemorrhage post-endoscopy mortality prediction 5. initial stabilization 5. initial stabilization abcs: two large-bore ivs (≥18g), type and crossmatch fluid resuscitation: crystalloids → blood products transfusion: restrictive strategy hb <7 g/dl (target 7–9); liberal if acs or hemodynamic instability iv ppi: high-dose esomeprazole 80 mg bolus → 8 mg/hr infusion (for suspected peptic ulcer) if variceal suspected: octreotide 50 mcg bolus → 50 mcg/hr + ceftriaxone 1 g/day + restrictive transfusion (hb 7–8) intubation: if massive hematemesis, encephalopathy, or inability to protect airway hold anticoagulants; reverse if life-threatening bleeding erythromycin 250 mg iv 30–120 min before egd (prokinetic — improves visualization) 6. endoscopic management 6. endoscopic management timing: within 24 hours for most ugib; within 12 hours if high-risk features (hemodynamic instability, bloody ng aspirate, in-hospital bleeding) forrest classification for peptic ulcers: forrest class description rebleeding risk endoscopic therapy references ia spurting hemorrhage 90% yes ib oozing hemorrhage 50% yes iia visible vessel (non-bleeding) 50% yes iib adherent clot 30% consider (remove clot → treat underlying lesion) iic flat pigmented spot 10% no iii clean base 5% no endoscopic therapy: combination therapy (epinephrine injection + thermal coagulation or clips) for forrest ia, ib, iia post-endoscopy: iv ppi infusion x 72 hours for high-risk stigmata (ia, ib, iia), then oral ppi 7. post-endoscopy management 7. post-endoscopy management h. pylori testing in all peptic ulcer patients → eradicate if positive h. pylori eradication: bismuth quadruple therapy (ppi + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline x 14 days) or concomitant therapy (ppi + amoxicillin + clarithromycin + metronidazole x 14 days) confirm eradication: urea breath test or stool antigen ≥4 weeks after completing therapy (≥2 weeks off ppi) nsaid-related ulcer: stop nsaid; if must continue, use ppi + cox-2 selective aspirin: resume within 3–7 days if cardiovascular indication (do not stop indefinitely) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls bun/cr ratio >30: suggests ugib (digested blood → urea absorption) coffee-ground emesis = partially digested blood (acid converts hemoglobin to hematin) melena can persist 3–5 days after bleeding stops ng tube lavage: no longer routinely recommended restrictive transfusion improves outcomes in ugib (less rebleeding, lower mortality) ppi infusion is for post-endoscopy high-risk lesions, not as a substitute for endoscopy top 5 pimping questions: what is the most common cause of non-variceal ugib? (peptic ulcer disease) what is the forrest classification? (endoscopic classification of ulcer bleeding stigmata) what is the transfusion threshold? (hb <7 g/dl — restrictive) when should endoscopy be performed? (within 24 hours; within 12 hours if high-risk) what should you test for in all peptic ulcers? (h. pylori) topic 24: lower gi bleeding topic 24 · lower gi bleeding 1. definition 1. definition bleeding distal to the ligament of treitz. usually presents as hematochezia. 2. etiology 2. etiology diverticular disease (most common in elderly/western) hemorrhoids (most common overall in younger patients) angiodysplasia (elderly, associated with aortic stenosis — heyde syndrome) colorectal cancer/polyps ibd, ischemic colitis, infectious colitis post-polypectomy bleeding 3. diagnostic workup 3. diagnostic workup first: rule out ugib (if hemodynamically unstable with hematochezia → consider ugib with brisk bleeding; ng aspirate or upper endoscopy) colonoscopy: first-line diagnostic and therapeutic (after bowel prep; within 24 hours) ct angiography: if massive bleeding, hemodynamically unstable, or colonoscopy not feasible (detects bleeding rate ≥0.3–0.5 ml/min) tagged rbc scan: detects slower bleeding (≥0.1 ml/min) but poor localization angiography: therapeutic (embolization) if ct angiography positive 4. management 4. management hemodynamic resuscitation (same as ugib) colonoscopy after bowel prep (within 24 hours) — diagnostic and therapeutic if massive/unstable: ct angiography → angiographic embolization surgery: for refractory/recurrent bleeding not amenable to endoscopic or angiographic therapy most diverticular bleeding (80%) stops spontaneously 5. clinical pearls 5. clinical pearls hematochezia + hemodynamic instability = consider ugib (10–15% of hematochezia is from upper gi source) diverticular bleeding: painless, large-volume, self-limited in 80% ischemic colitis: elderly, post-surgical, watershed areas (splenic flexure), "thumbprinting" on imaging angiodysplasia: cecum/right colon, elderly, associated with aortic stenosis and vwd

parts.title: PART IV: NEPHROLOGY

blocks.text: Any of the following:
Increase in serum creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours
Increase in serum creatinine ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days
Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6 hours

rows: ≥3x baseline or Cr ≥4.0 or initiation of RRT

blocks.text: FENa <1% with intrinsic AKI: contrast nephropathy, rhabdomyolysis, early sepsis, acute GN
FENa unreliable if on diuretics → use FEUrea instead (<35% = pre-renal)
History: volume status, medications (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, aminoglycosides, contrast), obstruction symptoms
Labs: BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, urine electrolytes (Na, Cr for FENa), CK (rhabdomyolysis)
Renal ultrasound: rule out obstruction (hydronephrosis) — do in ALL AKI
Additional: complement levels, ANCA, anti-GBM, ANA (if GN suspected); urine eosinophils (AIN)
Identify and treat cause
Pre-renal: volume resuscitation (isotonic crystalloid)
Post-renal: relieve obstruction (Foley catheter, nephrostomy, ureteral stent)
Intrinsic: stop offending agents; supportive care
