Optimize hemodynamics: MAP ≥65 mmHg
Avoid nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast, ACEi/ARB (hold in AKI)
Dose-adjust renally cleared medications
Monitor fluid balance, electrolytes (especially K+), acid-base
Nutrition: avoid excessive protein restriction; adequate caloric intake
Renal replacement therapy (RRT) if indicated (see Topic 29)
ATN (most common intrinsic AKI): ischemic or nephrotoxic; muddy brown casts; recovery in 1–3 weeks

AIN (acute interstitial nephritis): drug-induced (NSAIDs, antibiotics — penicillins, cephalosporins, PPIs, rifampicin); triad: fever, rash, eosinophilia (only ~10% have full triad); urine eosinophils; treatment: stop offending drug ± steroids
Contrast-induced nephropathy: Cr rises 24–48h post-contrast, peaks 3–5 days; prevention: IV NS hydration pre/post; hold metformin (lactic acidosis risk if AKI develops)

Rhabdomyolysis: CK >5x ULN (often >10,000); tea-colored urine; myoglobinuria (dipstick positive for blood but no RBCs on microscopy); treatment: aggressive IV NS (target UOP 200–300 mL/hr); avoid LR (contains K+)
Refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >6.5 or ECG changes)
Severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1)
Volume overload/pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics
Uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)
Anuria
→ All are indications for emergent dialysis
Always get renal ultrasound in AKI (rule out obstruction — reversible cause)
FENa <1% does NOT always mean pre-renal (contrast, rhabdomyolysis, early sepsis, GN)
If on diuretics, use FEUrea (<35% = pre-renal)
Muddy brown granular casts = ATN
RBC casts = glomerulonephritis
WBC casts = pyelonephritis or AIN
Cr lags behind actual GFR changes — a "normal" Cr does not exclude AKI in early stages
Not checking post-void residual or renal US to rule out obstruction
Continuing nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides)
Using FENa in patients on diuretics (unreliable)
Over-relying on creatinine (lags behind injury)
Not adjusting medication doses for AKI
Top 10 Testable Facts:
KDIGO: Cr ≥0.3 in 48h, or ≥1.5x in 7 days, or UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr x 6h
FENa <1% = pre-renal; >2% = intrinsic (ATN)
FEUrea <35% = pre-renal (use when on diuretics)
Muddy brown casts = ATN
RBC casts = GN
Always get renal US in AKI
ATN is the most common intrinsic cause
AIN triad: fever, rash, eosinophilia (only 10% have all three)
Rhabdomyolysis: aggressive NS, target UOP 200–300 mL/hr
Contrast nephropathy: Cr peaks at 3–5 days
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are the KDIGO criteria for AKI? (Cr ≥0.3 in 48h, ≥1.5x in 7d, UOP <0.5 mL/kg/hr x 6h)
How do you differentiate pre-renal from ATN? (FENa, urine Na, BUN:Cr, urine sediment)
What is the classic urine sediment in ATN? (Muddy brown granular casts)
When is FENa unreliable? (On diuretics — use FEUrea instead)
What is the dipstick finding in rhabdomyolysis? (Positive for blood, but no RBCs on microscopy — myoglobin cross-reacts)
Abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for >3 months with health implications. Classified by GFR (G) and albuminuria (A).

rows: Normal or high (with kidney damage markers)

blocks.text: Albuminuria: A1 (<30 mg/g), A2 (30–300 mg/g — moderately increased), A3 (>300 mg/g — severely increased)
Most common worldwide: diabetes mellitus (diabetic kidney disease)
Hypertension (second most common)
Glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease, obstructive uropathy, lupus nephritis
Early (G1–G3a): often asymptomatic
Late (G4–G5): fatigue, nausea, anorexia, pruritus, edema, nocturia, cognitive changes
Uremia (G5): pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding, neuropathy, asterixis
Serum creatinine → eGFR (CKD-EPI equation — preferred)
Urinalysis: proteinuria, hematuria
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR): quantify albuminuria
Renal ultrasound: small kidneys (<9 cm) = chronic; normal/large kidneys in CKD: diabetes, amyloid, HIV, PKD
Labs: CBC (anemia), BMP (electrolytes, Ca, PO4), PTH, vitamin D, lipid panel, iron studies
Renal biopsy: if cause unclear, unexplained hematuria/proteinuria, suspected GN
Blood pressure control: target <120/80 mmHg (SPRINT trial); ACEi or ARB first-line (reduce proteinuria and slow progression); do NOT combine ACEi + ARB

Glycemic control in DKD: HbA1c <7% (individualize); SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) — first-line add-on regardless of diabetes status if eGFR ≥20 (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY trials — reduce CKD progression and CV events)
Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA): for DKD with albuminuria on max ACEi/ARB (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD trials)
GLP-1 RA (semaglutide): CV and renal benefit in T2DM with CKD (FLOW trial)
Manage complications:
Anemia: target Hb 10–11.5 g/dL; iron supplementation first (ferritin >100, TSAT >20%); ESA (epoetin, darbepoetin) if iron-replete and Hb <10; HIF-PHI (roxadustat) — oral alternative
CKD-MBD: phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer, lanthanum); calcitriol or active vitamin D if PTH elevated; target PO4 normal range
Metabolic acidosis: sodium bicarbonate if HCO3 <22 mEq/L (slows CKD progression)
Hyperkalemia: dietary restriction, loop diuretics, patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate
Dyslipidemia: statin ± ezetimibe (SHARP trial)
Dietary: protein restriction 0.8 g/kg/day (G3–G5); sodium <2 g/day; potassium restriction if hyperkalemic
Avoid nephrotoxins: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast (if necessary, use iso-osmolar + hydration)
Dialysis preparation: AV fistula creation when eGFR <15–20 (6 months before anticipated need); education about modalities (HD vs PD vs transplant)
eGFR <30 (G4–G5)
Rapid decline (>5 mL/min/year)
Persistent albuminuria A3
Refractory hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities
Suspected glomerulonephritis
SGLT2 inhibitors are now standard of care for CKD regardless of diabetes (DAPA-CKD)
Initial eGFR dip with ACEi/ARB or SGLT2i is expected and acceptable (up to 30% decline); do NOT stop unless >30% decline
Small kidneys on US = chronic and irreversible
CKD G3+ patients: avoid gadolinium (nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk — though newer agents are safer)
Metabolic acidosis accelerates CKD progression — treat with NaHCO3
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Most common cause: diabetes mellitus
CKD-EPI equation for eGFR
SGLT2 inhibitors: standard of care for CKD (DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
ACEi/ARB: first-line for proteinuric CKD; do NOT combine
Anemia target: Hb 10–11.5; iron first, then ESA
CKD-MBD: phosphate binders + vitamin D + PTH management
Finerenone: for DKD with albuminuria (FIDELIO-DKD)
Metabolic acidosis: NaHCO3 if HCO3 <22
AV fistula: create when eGFR <15–20
Small kidneys on US = chronic disease
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most important drug class for CKD progression? (SGLT2 inhibitors — DAPA-CKD)
What is the BP target in CKD? (<120/80 — SPRINT)
When do you start dialysis preparation? (eGFR <15–20, AV fistula 6 months ahead)
What causes large kidneys in CKD? (Diabetes, amyloid, PKD, HIV)
Why do you NOT combine ACEi + ARB? (Hyperkalemia, AKI, no additional benefit — ONTARGET trial)
Proteinuria >3.5 g/day (or UPCR >3.5 g/g)
Hypoalbuminemia (<3 g/dL)
Peripheral edema
Hyperlipidemia and lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese cross under polarized light)

rows: Minimal change disease (most common in children)
Diabetic nephropathy (most common in adults overall)
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS — most common primary in adults)
Membranous nephropathy (most common primary in White adults)

blocks.text: Podocyte injury → loss of glomerular charge/size barrier → massive proteinuria
Albumin loss → decreased oncotic pressure → edema
Liver compensatory increase in lipoprotein synthesis → hyperlipidemia
Loss of antithrombin III, protein C/S → hypercoagulable state
Loss of immunoglobulins → infection risk
Urine: 24-hour urine protein or spot UPCR; urinalysis (oval fat bodies, fatty casts)
Blood: albumin, lipid panel, BMP, CBC
Serologies: ANA, anti-dsDNA (lupus), complement (C3/C4), hepatitis B/C, HIV, PLA2R antibody (membranous nephropathy), SPEP/UPEP (amyloid/myeloma)
Renal biopsy: indicated in adults with nephrotic syndrome (except in diabetic nephropathy with classic features)
Thromboembolism: renal vein thrombosis (especially membranous nephropathy — most common cause of renal vein thrombosis); DVT/PE
Infection: spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (children), cellulitis, pneumonia (encapsulated organisms — loss of IgG)
AKI, hyperlipidemia-related atherosclerosis
Treat underlying cause (disease-specific immunosuppression)
Edema: loop diuretics (furosemide) ± thiazide; salt restriction (<2 g/day); albumin infusion + furosemide if severe/refractory
Proteinuria reduction: ACEi or ARB (reduce intraglomerular pressure)
Hyperlipidemia: statin
Anticoagulation: consider if albumin <2.0–2.5 g/dL (high thrombosis risk), especially membranous nephropathy
Infection prevention: pneumococcal vaccine
Disease-specific:
Minimal change disease: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day x 4–8 weeks → taper (excellent response)
FSGS: prednisone; if steroid-resistant → calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus)
Membranous nephropathy: observe if low risk; rituximab (MENTOR trial) or calcineurin inhibitors if moderate-high risk; PLA2R antibody for monitoring
Lupus nephritis: see SLE topic
Minimal change disease: normal light microscopy, foot process effacement on EM; excellent steroid response
Membranous nephropathy: "spike and dome" on EM; PLA2R antibody positive in ~70% of primary cases
FSGS: most common primary nephrotic syndrome in adults (especially African Americans); associated with HIV (collapsing variant)
Renal vein thrombosis: most associated with membranous nephropathy
Nephrotic syndrome + flank pain + hematuria → suspect renal vein thrombosis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children? (Minimal change disease)
What is the most common primary cause in adults? (FSGS overall; membranous in White adults)
Which nephrotic syndrome has highest thrombosis risk? (Membranous nephropathy)
What antibody is specific for primary membranous nephropathy? (PLA2R)
What is seen under polarized light in nephrotic syndrome? (Maltese cross — lipid droplets)
Hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts — hallmark)
Mild-moderate proteinuria (usually <3.5 g/day)
Hypertension
Oliguria
Azotemia (elevated BUN/Cr)
Edema (less prominent than nephrotic)

rows: 1–3 weeks after pharyngitis, 3–6 weeks after skin infection; children; "lumpy bumpy" IF; subepithelial humps on EM
Most common GN worldwide; episodic gross hematuria 1–2 days after URI ("synpharyngitic"); mesangial IgA deposits
Class III/IV most severe; "full house" IF (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q)
Linear IgG on IF; pulmonary hemorrhage + GN
"Tram-track" appearance; associated with hepatitis C, cryoglobulinemia

blocks.text: Urinalysis: dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts (pathognomonic for GN)
Labs: BMP, CBC, complement (C3, C4), ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA (c-ANCA/PR3, p-ANCA/MPO), anti-GBM, ASO titer, hepatitis B/C, cryoglobulins, SPEP
Renal biopsy: definitive diagnosis (except PSGN in children with classic presentation)
Supportive: BP control (ACEi/ARB), salt/fluid restriction, diuretics
Disease-specific immunosuppression:
PSGN: supportive only (self-limited in children; antibiotics do NOT prevent GN but treat active infection)
IgA nephropathy: ACEi/ARB; if persistent proteinuria >1 g/day despite max RAS blockade → corticosteroids (TESTING trial); SGLT2 inhibitors (DAPA-CKD)
Lupus nephritis III/IV: induction with mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide + steroids; maintenance with mycophenolate
Anti-GBM: plasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + steroids (emergency)
ANCA vasculitis: rituximab or cyclophosphamide + steroids; avacopan (C5a receptor inhibitor — ADVOCATE trial)
RBC casts = glomerulonephritis (pathognomonic)
Low C3 + low C4 = lupus nephritis, cryoglobulinemia
Low C3 + normal C4 = PSGN, MPGN (alternative pathway)
Normal complement = IgA nephropathy, anti-GBM, ANCA vasculitis
IgA nephropathy: hematuria DURING URI (synpharyngitic); PSGN: hematuria AFTER URI (1–3 weeks)
Goodpasture = anti-GBM + pulmonary hemorrhage (young male smoker)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the pathognomonic finding in nephritic syndrome? (RBC casts)
What is the most common GN worldwide? (IgA nephropathy)
How do you differentiate IgA nephropathy from PSGN by timing? (IgA: during URI; PSGN: 1–3 weeks after)
What complement pattern suggests lupus nephritis? (Low C3 AND low C4)
What is "full house" immunofluorescence? (IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C1q — lupus nephritis)
A — Acidosis: severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.1) refractory to medical therapy
E — Electrolytes: refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >6.5 with ECG changes, not responding to medical management)
I — Intoxication: toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates)
O — Overload: volume overload/pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics
U — Uremia: uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)
eGFR <5–10 mL/min with symptoms
Refractory fluid overload, hyperkalemia, acidosis
Uremic symptoms (nausea, anorexia, cognitive decline, neuropathy, pericarditis)
Failure to thrive despite optimal medical management
No absolute GFR cutoff — clinical judgment based on symptoms

rows: AV fistula (preferred), AV graft, tunneled catheter
Home-based, hemodynamically gentler, preserves residual renal function longer
Hemodynamic instability, vascular access complications, in-center
Peritonitis risk, protein loss, limited in obese/prior abdominal surgery
Patient preference, children, remote areas, CV instability

blocks.text: AV fistula: preferred (lowest infection/thrombosis rate); create 6 months before anticipated need; "Rule of 6s" — 6 mm diameter, <6 mm depth, 600 mL/min flow
AV graft: if unsuitable veins; can use in 2–4 weeks
Tunneled dialysis catheter: temporary or bridge; highest infection risk
Non-tunneled catheter: emergent dialysis only
Indication: hemodynamically unstable patients (ICU, septic shock)
Slower, continuous solute/fluid removal over 24 hours
Modalities: CVVH, CVVHD, CVVHDF
AEIOU = emergent dialysis indications
AV fistula is the gold standard access (lowest complication rate)
Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome: cerebral edema during/after first HD sessions (headache, nausea, seizures) — prevent with slower initial sessions
First-use syndrome: anaphylactoid reaction to dialysis membrane
PD peritonitis: cloudy effluate, abdominal pain; treat with intraperitoneal antibiotics (vancomycin + gentamicin or ceftazidime)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are the emergent dialysis indications? (AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uremia)
What is the preferred vascular access? (AV fistula)
When should AV fistula be created? (6 months before anticipated dialysis)
What is dialysis disequilibrium syndrome? (Cerebral edema during first HD — osmotic shift)
What is the indication for CRRT over HD? (Hemodynamic instability)
Serum sodium <135 mEq/L. Most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients.
Mild: 130–134; Moderate: 125–129; Severe: <125

rows: Hyperglycemia, mannitol (Na drops ~1.6 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100)
Most cases — further classify by volume status

sections.title: 3. Hypotonic Hyponatremia — Classify by Volume Status
sections.display: 3. Hypotonic Hyponatremia — Classify By Volume Status

rows: Diuretics (thiazides!), adrenal insufficiency, cerebral salt wasting
SIADH (most common euvolemic cause), hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, psychogenic polydipsia

blocks.text: Serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg
Urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated)
Urine Na >30 mEq/L
Euvolemic
Normal thyroid and adrenal function
No diuretics
Causes: CNS disorders, pulmonary disease (pneumonia, TB), malignancy (small cell lung cancer), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, opioids), pain, nausea
Acute symptomatic (seizures, severe AMS, <48 hours):
Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl): 100 mL bolus over 10 minutes, can repeat x 2
Target: raise Na by 4–6 mEq/L in first 1–2 hours (enough to stop symptoms)
Do NOT exceed 8 mEq/L in any 24-hour period (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome/ODS)
Chronic (≥48 hours or unknown duration):
Correct slowly: ≤8 mEq/L per 24 hours (some recommend ≤6 in high-risk patients: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver disease)
Hypovolemic: isotonic saline (NS)
Euvolemic (SIADH): fluid restriction (800–1000 mL/day) first-line; salt tablets + loop diuretic; vaptans (tolvaptan — use cautiously, monitor Na q6h); urea (emerging)
Hypervolemic: fluid + salt restriction; treat underlying cause; loop diuretics
Previously called central pontine myelinolysis
Caused by overly rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia
Presents 2–6 days after correction: dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, "locked-in" syndrome
Risk factors: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver disease
If overcorrected: re-lower Na with D5W ± desmopressin (DDAVP)
Thiazides cause hyponatremia; loop diuretics rarely do (thiazides impair diluting segment without affecting concentrating ability)
Always check serum osmolality first to rule out hypertonic/isotonic causes
Correct hypokalemia concurrently (K+ correction raises Na — each mEq K+ repleted raises Na by ~1 mEq/L)
Beer potomania: low solute intake → limited free water excretion → hyponatremia
Exercise-associated hyponatremia: excessive free water intake during endurance events
Tolvaptan: contraindicated in liver disease (hepatotoxicity)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the maximum safe correction rate? (≤8 mEq/L per 24 hours)
What happens if you correct too fast? (Osmotic demyelination syndrome)
What is the first-line treatment for SIADH? (Fluid restriction)
Why do thiazides cause hyponatremia but loops usually don't? (Thiazides impair diluting segment; loops impair both diluting and concentrating)
How do you manage overcorrection? (D5W ± desmopressin to re-lower Na)
Serum sodium >145 mEq/L. Always indicates water deficit (hyperosmolar state).
Water loss: insensible losses (fever, burns), GI losses, osmotic diuresis (hyperglycemia, mannitol), diabetes insipidus
Inadequate water intake: altered mental status, no access to water, elderly, intubated patients
Sodium gain (rare): hypertonic saline, sodium bicarbonate, salt tablets

rows: Pituitary surgery, trauma, tumors, Sheehan syndrome
Lithium (most common drug cause), hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, CKD

blocks.text: Free Water Deficit (FWD) = TBW x (serum [Na] -140) / 140
TBW = 0.6 x weight (males), 0.5 x weight (females), 0.5/0.45 in elderly
Identify and treat underlying cause
Replace free water deficit:
Oral water preferred if patient can drink
IV: D5W or 0.45% NaCl (half-normal saline)
Correction rate: ≤10 mEq/L per 24 hours (chronic); can correct faster if acute (<24 hours onset)
Too-rapid correction → cerebral edema (brain cells have generated idiogenic osmoles)
Central DI: desmopressin (DDAVP) — intranasal 10–20 mcg BID or IV/SC 1–2 mcg BID
Nephrogenic DI: treat underlying cause; thiazide diuretics (paradoxically reduce urine volume by enhancing proximal reabsorption); amiloride (for lithium-induced); low-sodium diet; NSAIDs
Monitor Na every 4–6 hours during correction
Hypernatremia = always a water problem (not a sodium problem)
Most common in hospitalized patients: elderly, intubated, AMS — cannot access water
Lithium is the most common drug cause of nephrogenic DI
Thiazides paradoxically treat nephrogenic DI (volume contraction → increased proximal reabsorption → less water delivery to collecting duct)
Acute hypernatremia (<24h): can correct faster; chronic: ≤10 mEq/L per 24h
Always account for ongoing losses when calculating replacement
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the formula for free water deficit? (TBW x [Na/140 − 1])
What is the maximum correction rate for chronic hypernatremia? (≤10 mEq/L per 24 hours)
How do you differentiate central from nephrogenic DI? (Desmopressin challenge — central responds, nephrogenic does not)
What is the most common drug cause of nephrogenic DI? (Lithium)
Why do thiazides help in nephrogenic DI? (Paradoxical: volume contraction → increased proximal reabsorption → decreased free water excretion)
Serum K+ <3.5 mEq/L. Mild: 3.0–3.4; Moderate: 2.5–2.9; Severe: <2.5
Decreased intake: malnutrition, anorexia
Increased loss:
Renal: diuretics (loops, thiazides — most common cause), hyperaldosteronism, RTA type I/II, hypomagnesemia, amphotericin B
GI: vomiting (metabolic alkalosis → renal K+ wasting), diarrhea, laxative abuse, NG suction
Transcellular shift (into cells): insulin, beta2-agonists, alkalosis, refeeding syndrome
Mild: often asymptomatic
Moderate-severe: muscle weakness, cramps, fatigue, constipation/ileus
Severe: rhabdomyolysis, respiratory failure, cardiac arrhythmias
ECG changes (progressive): flattened T waves → ST depression → U waves → prolonged QT → arrhythmias (PVCs, VT/VF)
Check urine K+:
Urine K+ <20 mEq/L (or TTKG <3): extrarenal loss (GI, transcellular shift)
Urine K+ >20 mEq/L (or TTKG >7): renal loss → check BP and acid-base:
High BP: hyperaldosteronism, Cushing, renovascular HTN, Liddle syndrome
Normal BP + metabolic alkalosis: diuretics, vomiting, Bartter/Gitelman
Normal BP + metabolic acidosis: RTA type I or II
Mild-moderate (K+ 3.0–3.4, asymptomatic): oral KCl 40–80 mEq/day in divided doses
Moderate (K+ 2.5–2.9) or symptomatic: oral + IV KCl
Severe (<2.5) or ECG changes or arrhythmias: IV KCl
IV rate: max 10–20 mEq/hr via peripheral line; up to 40 mEq/hr via central line with cardiac monitoring
Concentration: max 40 mEq/L peripheral; 60–80 mEq/L central
NEVER give IV KCl as bolus push (cardiac arrest risk)
Always check and correct magnesium (hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia — Mg2+ is needed for ROMK channel function)
Treat underlying cause
Hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia: correct Mg2+ FIRST (K+ will not correct until Mg2+ is repleted)
Each 10 mEq KCl raises serum K+ by ~0.1 mEq/L (rough estimate)
Vomiting causes hypokalemia primarily through renal losses (metabolic alkalosis → bicarbonaturia → obligate K+ loss), NOT through gastric K+ loss
Digoxin toxicity is potentiated by hypokalemia
U waves are relatively specific for hypokalemia
Metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia perpetuate each other
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
Why must you correct Mg2+ before K+? (Mg2+ needed for ROMK channel; K+ refractory until Mg2+ repleted)
What is the maximum IV KCl rate peripherally? (10–20 mEq/hr)
What are the ECG changes in hypokalemia? (Flattened T → ST depression → U waves → prolonged QT)
Why does vomiting cause hypokalemia? (Metabolic alkalosis → renal K+ wasting, not gastric loss)
What syndrome presents with hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis + normal BP? (Bartter or Gitelman syndrome)
Serum K+ >5.0 mEq/L. Mild: 5.0–5.5; Moderate: 5.5–6.5; Severe: >6.5
First step: rule out pseudohyperkalemia (hemolyzed sample, tourniquet, fist clenching, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis)
Decreased excretion: AKI/CKD (most common), hypoaldosteronism (type IV RTA), K+-sparing diuretics, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs, trimethoprim, heparin
Transcellular shift (out of cells): acidosis, insulin deficiency, beta-blockers, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, massive hemolysis), succinylcholine, digoxin toxicity
Increased intake: rarely sole cause unless renal impairment present
Peaked T waves (earliest) → 2. Prolonged PR → 3. Widened QRS → 4. Loss of P waves → 5. Sine wave pattern → 6. VF/asystole

rows: 10 mL IV over 2–3 min (repeat in 5 min if no ECG improvement)
Stabilizes cardiac membrane (does NOT lower K+)
Only if acidotic (limited benefit if no acidosis)
GI K+ exchange (limited evidence, risk of bowel necrosis)
Patiromer 8.4 g PO; SZC 10 g PO

blocks.text: Confirm true hyperkalemia (repeat if suspected pseudohyperkalemia)
Get ECG immediately
If ECG changes or K+ >6.5: calcium gluconate → insulin/glucose → albuterol → furosemide → consider dialysis
If K+ 5.5–6.5 without ECG changes: dietary restriction, stop offending drugs, furosemide, K+ binders
Treat underlying cause
Monitor K+ every 1–2 hours during acute management; continuous cardiac monitoring
Calcium gluconate does NOT lower K+ — it stabilizes the myocardium (buys time)
Always give glucose WITH insulin (prevent hypoglycemia — monitor glucose q1h x 4h)
If patient is already hyperglycemic (>250), can give insulin without glucose
Calcium chloride has 3x more elemental calcium than calcium gluconate but causes tissue necrosis if extravasated → use gluconate peripherally, chloride via central line
Succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED in hyperkalemia (causes further K+ release)
Digoxin toxicity + hyperkalemia: do NOT give calcium (can cause "stone heart") → give digoxin-specific antibody (Digibind)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the first step in hyperkalemia with ECG changes? (IV calcium gluconate)
Does calcium gluconate lower potassium? (No — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane)
What are the progressive ECG changes? (Peaked T → prolonged PR → wide QRS → loss of P → sine wave → VF)
Why give glucose with insulin? (Prevent hypoglycemia)
When is calcium contraindicated in hyperkalemia? (Digoxin toxicity — risk of "stone heart")
Metabolic acidosis: Winter's formula → Expected pCO2 = (1.5 x HCO3-) + 8 ± 2
Metabolic alkalosis: Expected pCO2 = (0.7 x HCO3-) + 21 ± 2
Acute respiratory acidosis: HCO3- rises 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ pCO2
Chronic respiratory acidosis: HCO3- rises 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ pCO2
Acute respiratory alkalosis: HCO3- falls 2 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↓ pCO2
Chronic respiratory alkalosis: HCO3- falls 5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↓ pCO2
If actual pCO2 ≠ expected → mixed disorder
Look at pH → acidemia or alkalemia?
Identify primary disorder (which value explains the pH?)
Calculate expected compensation → if not matching, mixed disorder
If metabolic acidosis → calculate anion gap: AG = Na+ − (Cl- + HCO3-); normal = 12 ± 2
If elevated AG → calculate delta-delta ratio: ΔAG/ΔHCO3-
<1: concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis
1–2: pure AG metabolic acidosis
>2: concurrent metabolic alkalosis

sections.title: 4. Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (mnemonic: MUDPILES or GOLDMARK)
sections.display: 4. Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (Mnemonic: Mudpiles Or Goldmark)

blocks.text: GOLDMARK (preferred):
G — Glycols (ethylene glycol, propylene glycol)
O — Oxoproline (acetaminophen metabolite)
L — L-Lactate (most common: shock, sepsis, ischemia, metformin)
D — D-Lactate (short bowel syndrome)
M — Methanol
A — Aspirin (salicylates)
R — Renal failure (uremia)
K — Ketoacidosis (DKA, alcoholic, starvation)
Mnemonic: HARDUPS
H — Hyperalimentation (TPN)
A — Addison disease (adrenal insufficiency)
R — Renal tubular acidosis
D — Diarrhea (most common cause — loss of HCO3-)
U — Ureteral diversion
P — Pancreatic fistula
S — Saline (dilutional — large-volume NS resuscitation)

rows: Cannot secrete H+ in distal tubule
Nephrocalcinosis, kidney stones, Sjögren, SLE, amphotericin B
Cannot reabsorb HCO3- in proximal tubule
Fanconi syndrome, multiple myeloma, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors
Diabetes (most common), ACEi/ARB, K+-sparing diuretics, adrenal insufficiency

blocks.text: Saline-responsive (urine Cl- <20): vomiting, NG suction, diuretics (after discontinuation) → treat with NS
Saline-resistant (urine Cl- >20): hyperaldosteronism, Cushing, Bartter, Gitelman, current diuretic use → treat underlying cause; acetazolamide if severe
Calculated osmolality = 2(Na) + glucose/18 + BUN/2.8
Osmolar gap = measured − calculated; normal <10
Elevated osmolar gap + AG metabolic acidosis: methanol, ethylene glycol (toxic alcohols)
Lactic acidosis is the most common cause of AG metabolic acidosis in hospitalized patients
Type A lactic acidosis: tissue hypoperfusion (shock, sepsis); Type B: no hypoperfusion (metformin, liver failure, malignancy)
Salicylate toxicity: mixed respiratory alkalosis + AG metabolic acidosis
DKA: AG metabolic acidosis; as treated with NS, AG closes but non-AG acidosis develops (hyperchloremic)
Normal saline causes hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (reason to prefer LR/balanced crystalloids)
Delta-delta ratio is essential for detecting hidden disorders
Top 10 Testable Facts:
AG = Na − (Cl + HCO3); normal 12 ± 2
Winter's formula: expected pCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ± 2
GOLDMARK for AG metabolic acidosis
Lactic acidosis = most common AG metabolic acidosis
Delta-delta: <1 = concurrent non-AG acidosis; >2 = concurrent metabolic alkalosis
RTA type 1: distal, hypoK, urine pH >5.5, nephrocalcinosis
RTA type 4: hyperK, diabetes, hypoaldosteronism
Saline-responsive alkalosis: urine Cl <20 → give NS
Osmolar gap >10 + AG acidosis → toxic alcohols
Salicylate toxicity: mixed respiratory alkalosis + AG metabolic acidosis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is Winter's formula? (Expected pCO2 = 1.5 x HCO3 + 8 ± 2)
What does a delta-delta >2 indicate? (Concurrent metabolic alkalosis)
What is the most common cause of AG metabolic acidosis? (Lactic acidosis)
Which RTA causes hyperkalemia? (Type 4 — hypoaldosteronism)
What causes elevated osmolar gap + AG acidosis? (Methanol, ethylene glycol)

parts.searchText: part iv: nephrology part iv: nephrology topic 25: acute kidney injury (aki) topic 25 · acute kidney injury (aki) 1. definition — kdigo 2012 1. definition — kdigo 2012 any of the following: increase in serum creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dl within 48 hours increase in serum creatinine ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hr for 6 hours 2. staging — kdigo 2. staging — kdigo stage serum creatinine urine output references 1 ↑ ≥0.3 mg/dl or 1.5–1.9x baseline <0.5 ml/kg/hr for 6–12h kdigo 2012 2 2.0–2.9x baseline <0.5 ml/kg/hr for ≥12h 3 ≥3x baseline or cr ≥4.0 or initiation of rrt <0.3 ml/kg/hr for ≥24h or anuria ≥12h 3. classification: pre-renal, intrinsic, post-renal 3. classification: pre-renal, intrinsic, post-renal type cause fena urine na bun:cr urine osmolality urine sediment references pre-renal hypovolemia, chf, cirrhosis, sepsis (early) <1% <20 meq/l >20:1 >500 mosm/kg bland/hyaline casts intrinsic (atn) ischemia, nephrotoxins >2% >40 meq/l <15:1 <350 mosm/kg muddy brown granular casts post-renal obstruction (bph, stones, tumor) variable variable variable variable bland 4. important caveats for fena 4. important caveats for fena fena <1% with intrinsic aki: contrast nephropathy, rhabdomyolysis, early sepsis, acute gn fena unreliable if on diuretics → use feurea instead (<35% = pre-renal) 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup history: volume status, medications (nsaids, acei/arb, aminoglycosides, contrast), obstruction symptoms labs: bmp, cbc, urinalysis with microscopy, urine electrolytes (na, cr for fena), ck (rhabdomyolysis) renal ultrasound: rule out obstruction (hydronephrosis) — do in all aki additional: complement levels, anca, anti-gbm, ana (if gn suspected); urine eosinophils (ain) 6. stepwise management 6. stepwise management identify and treat cause pre-renal: volume resuscitation (isotonic crystalloid) post-renal: relieve obstruction (foley catheter, nephrostomy, ureteral stent) intrinsic: stop offending agents; supportive care optimize hemodynamics: map ≥65 mmhg avoid nephrotoxins: nsaids, aminoglycosides, contrast, acei/arb (hold in aki) dose-adjust renally cleared medications monitor fluid balance, electrolytes (especially k+), acid-base nutrition: avoid excessive protein restriction; adequate caloric intake renal replacement therapy (rrt) if indicated (see topic 29) 7. specific causes 7. specific causes atn (most common intrinsic aki): ischemic or nephrotoxic; muddy brown casts; recovery in 1–3 weeks ain (acute interstitial nephritis): drug-induced (nsaids, antibiotics — penicillins, cephalosporins, ppis, rifampicin); triad: fever, rash, eosinophilia (only ~10% have full triad); urine eosinophils; treatment: stop offending drug ± steroids contrast-induced nephropathy: cr rises 24–48h post-contrast, peaks 3–5 days; prevention: iv ns hydration pre/post; hold metformin (lactic acidosis risk if aki develops) rhabdomyolysis: ck >5x uln (often >10,000); tea-colored urine; myoglobinuria (dipstick positive for blood but no rbcs on microscopy); treatment: aggressive iv ns (target uop 200–300 ml/hr); avoid lr (contains k+) 8. red flags / icu criteria 8. red flags / icu criteria refractory hyperkalemia (k+ >6.5 or ecg changes) severe metabolic acidosis (ph <7.1) volume overload/pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding) anuria → all are indications for emergent dialysis 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls always get renal ultrasound in aki (rule out obstruction — reversible cause) fena <1% does not always mean pre-renal (contrast, rhabdomyolysis, early sepsis, gn) if on diuretics, use feurea (<35% = pre-renal) muddy brown granular casts = atn rbc casts = glomerulonephritis wbc casts = pyelonephritis or ain cr lags behind actual gfr changes — a "normal" cr does not exclude aki in early stages 10. common pitfalls 10. common pitfalls not checking post-void residual or renal us to rule out obstruction continuing nephrotoxins (nsaids, aminoglycosides) using fena in patients on diuretics (unreliable) over-relying on creatinine (lags behind injury) not adjusting medication doses for aki top 10 testable facts: kdigo: cr ≥0.3 in 48h, or ≥1.5x in 7 days, or uop <0.5 ml/kg/hr x 6h fena <1% = pre-renal; >2% = intrinsic (atn) feurea <35% = pre-renal (use when on diuretics) muddy brown casts = atn rbc casts = gn always get renal us in aki atn is the most common intrinsic cause ain triad: fever, rash, eosinophilia (only 10% have all three) rhabdomyolysis: aggressive ns, target uop 200–300 ml/hr contrast nephropathy: cr peaks at 3–5 days top 5 pimping questions: what are the kdigo criteria for aki? (cr ≥0.3 in 48h, ≥1.5x in 7d, uop <0.5 ml/kg/hr x 6h) how do you differentiate pre-renal from atn? (fena, urine na, bun:cr, urine sediment) what is the classic urine sediment in atn? (muddy brown granular casts) when is fena unreliable? (on diuretics — use feurea instead) what is the dipstick finding in rhabdomyolysis? (positive for blood, but no rbcs on microscopy — myoglobin cross-reacts) topic 26: chronic kidney disease (ckd) topic 26 · chronic kidney disease (ckd) 1. definition — kdigo 2012 1. definition — kdigo 2012 abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for >3 months with health implications. classified by gfr (g) and albuminuria (a). 2. staging 2. staging stage gfr (ml/min/1.73m2) description references g1 ≥90 normal or high (with kidney damage markers) kdigo 2012 g2 60–89 mildly decreased g3a 45–59 mild-moderately decreased g3b 30–44 moderate-severely decreased g4 15–29 severely decreased g5 <15 kidney failure albuminuria: a1 (<30 mg/g), a2 (30–300 mg/g — moderately increased), a3 (>300 mg/g — severely increased) 3. etiology 3. etiology most common worldwide: diabetes mellitus (diabetic kidney disease) hypertension (second most common) glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease, obstructive uropathy, lupus nephritis 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation early (g1–g3a): often asymptomatic late (g4–g5): fatigue, nausea, anorexia, pruritus, edema, nocturia, cognitive changes uremia (g5): pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding, neuropathy, asterixis 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup serum creatinine → egfr (ckd-epi equation — preferred) urinalysis: proteinuria, hematuria urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (uacr): quantify albuminuria renal ultrasound: small kidneys (<9 cm) = chronic; normal/large kidneys in ckd: diabetes, amyloid, hiv, pkd labs: cbc (anemia), bmp (electrolytes, ca, po4), pth, vitamin d, lipid panel, iron studies renal biopsy: if cause unclear, unexplained hematuria/proteinuria, suspected gn 6. stepwise management (kdigo 2024) 6. stepwise management (kdigo 2024) blood pressure control: target <120/80 mmhg (sprint trial); acei or arb first-line (reduce proteinuria and slow progression); do not combine acei + arb glycemic control in dkd: hba1c <7% (individualize); sglt2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) — first-line add-on regardless of diabetes status if egfr ≥20 (dapa-ckd, empa-kidney trials — reduce ckd progression and cv events) finerenone (non-steroidal mra): for dkd with albuminuria on max acei/arb (fidelio-dkd, figaro-dkd trials) glp-1 ra (semaglutide): cv and renal benefit in t2dm with ckd (flow trial) manage complications: anemia: target hb 10–11.5 g/dl; iron supplementation first (ferritin >100, tsat >20%); esa (epoetin, darbepoetin) if iron-replete and hb <10; hif-phi (roxadustat) — oral alternative ckd-mbd: phosphate binders (calcium carbonate, sevelamer, lanthanum); calcitriol or active vitamin d if pth elevated; target po4 normal range metabolic acidosis: sodium bicarbonate if hco3 <22 meq/l (slows ckd progression) hyperkalemia: dietary restriction, loop diuretics, patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate dyslipidemia: statin ± ezetimibe (sharp trial) dietary: protein restriction 0.8 g/kg/day (g3–g5); sodium <2 g/day; potassium restriction if hyperkalemic avoid nephrotoxins: nsaids, aminoglycosides, contrast (if necessary, use iso-osmolar + hydration) dialysis preparation: av fistula creation when egfr <15–20 (6 months before anticipated need); education about modalities (hd vs pd vs transplant) 7. indications for referral to nephrology 7. indications for referral to nephrology egfr <30 (g4–g5) rapid decline (>5 ml/min/year) persistent albuminuria a3 refractory hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities suspected glomerulonephritis 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls sglt2 inhibitors are now standard of care for ckd regardless of diabetes (dapa-ckd) initial egfr dip with acei/arb or sglt2i is expected and acceptable (up to 30% decline); do not stop unless >30% decline small kidneys on us = chronic and irreversible ckd g3+ patients: avoid gadolinium (nephrogenic systemic fibrosis risk — though newer agents are safer) metabolic acidosis accelerates ckd progression — treat with nahco3 top 10 testable facts: most common cause: diabetes mellitus ckd-epi equation for egfr sglt2 inhibitors: standard of care for ckd (dapa-ckd, empa-kidney) acei/arb: first-line for proteinuric ckd; do not combine anemia target: hb 10–11.5; iron first, then esa ckd-mbd: phosphate binders + vitamin d + pth management finerenone: for dkd with albuminuria (fidelio-dkd) metabolic acidosis: nahco3 if hco3 <22 av fistula: create when egfr <15–20 small kidneys on us = chronic disease top 5 pimping questions: what is the most important drug class for ckd progression? (sglt2 inhibitors — dapa-ckd) what is the bp target in ckd? (<120/80 — sprint) when do you start dialysis preparation? (egfr <15–20, av fistula 6 months ahead) what causes large kidneys in ckd? (diabetes, amyloid, pkd, hiv) why do you not combine acei + arb? (hyperkalemia, aki, no additional benefit — ontarget trial) topic 27: nephrotic syndrome topic 27 · nephrotic syndrome 1. definition 1. definition proteinuria >3.5 g/day (or upcr >3.5 g/g) hypoalbuminemia (<3 g/dl) peripheral edema hyperlipidemia and lipiduria (oval fat bodies, maltese cross under polarized light) 2. etiology 2. etiology primary (glomerular) secondary minimal change disease (most common in children) diabetic nephropathy (most common in adults overall) focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (fsgs — most common primary in adults) lupus nephritis (class v — membranous) membranous nephropathy (most common primary in white adults) amyloidosis membranoproliferative gn hepatitis b/c, hiv 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology podocyte injury → loss of glomerular charge/size barrier → massive proteinuria albumin loss → decreased oncotic pressure → edema liver compensatory increase in lipoprotein synthesis → hyperlipidemia loss of antithrombin iii, protein c/s → hypercoagulable state loss of immunoglobulins → infection risk 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup urine: 24-hour urine protein or spot upcr; urinalysis (oval fat bodies, fatty casts) blood: albumin, lipid panel, bmp, cbc serologies: ana, anti-dsdna (lupus), complement (c3/c4), hepatitis b/c, hiv, pla2r antibody (membranous nephropathy), spep/upep (amyloid/myeloma) renal biopsy: indicated in adults with nephrotic syndrome (except in diabetic nephropathy with classic features) 5. complications 5. complications thromboembolism: renal vein thrombosis (especially membranous nephropathy — most common cause of renal vein thrombosis); dvt/pe infection: spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (children), cellulitis, pneumonia (encapsulated organisms — loss of igg) aki, hyperlipidemia-related atherosclerosis 6. management 6. management treat underlying cause (disease-specific immunosuppression) edema: loop diuretics (furosemide) ± thiazide; salt restriction (<2 g/day); albumin infusion + furosemide if severe/refractory proteinuria reduction: acei or arb (reduce intraglomerular pressure) hyperlipidemia: statin anticoagulation: consider if albumin <2.0–2.5 g/dl (high thrombosis risk), especially membranous nephropathy infection prevention: pneumococcal vaccine disease-specific: minimal change disease: prednisone 1 mg/kg/day x 4–8 weeks → taper (excellent response) fsgs: prednisone; if steroid-resistant → calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) membranous nephropathy: observe if low risk; rituximab (mentor trial) or calcineurin inhibitors if moderate-high risk; pla2r antibody for monitoring lupus nephritis: see sle topic 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls minimal change disease: normal light microscopy, foot process effacement on em; excellent steroid response membranous nephropathy: "spike and dome" on em; pla2r antibody positive in ~70% of primary cases fsgs: most common primary nephrotic syndrome in adults (especially african americans); associated with hiv (collapsing variant) renal vein thrombosis: most associated with membranous nephropathy nephrotic syndrome + flank pain + hematuria → suspect renal vein thrombosis top 5 pimping questions: what is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children? (minimal change disease) what is the most common primary cause in adults? (fsgs overall; membranous in white adults) which nephrotic syndrome has highest thrombosis risk? (membranous nephropathy) what antibody is specific for primary membranous nephropathy? (pla2r) what is seen under polarized light in nephrotic syndrome? (maltese cross — lipid droplets) topic 28: nephritic syndrome topic 28 · nephritic syndrome 1. definition 1. definition hematuria (dysmorphic rbcs, rbc casts — hallmark) mild-moderate proteinuria (usually <3.5 g/day) hypertension oliguria azotemia (elevated bun/cr) edema (less prominent than nephrotic) 2. etiology 2. etiology disease key features complement references post-streptococcal gn (psgn) 1–3 weeks after pharyngitis, 3–6 weeks after skin infection; children; "lumpy bumpy" if; subepithelial humps on em low c3, normal c4 iga nephropathy (berger disease) most common gn worldwide; episodic gross hematuria 1–2 days after uri ("synpharyngitic"); mesangial iga deposits normal complement lupus nephritis class iii/iv most severe; "full house" if (igg, iga, igm, c3, c1q) low c3 and c4 anti-gbm disease (goodpasture) linear igg on if; pulmonary hemorrhage + gn normal complement anca vasculitis (gpa, mpa) pauci-immune (no/minimal if deposits); crescentic gn normal complement mpgn "tram-track" appearance; associated with hepatitis c, cryoglobulinemia low c3 ± c4 3. nephrotic vs nephritic 3. nephrotic vs nephritic feature nephrotic nephritic references proteinuria >3.5 g/day (massive) <3.5 g/day (mild-moderate) hematuria minimal prominent (rbc casts) edema severe (periorbital, anasarca) mild-moderate hypertension less common common complement usually normal often low (psgn, lupus, mpgn) lipids elevated normal thrombosis risk high low 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup urinalysis: dysmorphic rbcs, rbc casts (pathognomonic for gn) labs: bmp, cbc, complement (c3, c4), ana, anti-dsdna, anca (c-anca/pr3, p-anca/mpo), anti-gbm, aso titer, hepatitis b/c, cryoglobulins, spep renal biopsy: definitive diagnosis (except psgn in children with classic presentation) 5. management 5. management supportive: bp control (acei/arb), salt/fluid restriction, diuretics disease-specific immunosuppression: psgn: supportive only (self-limited in children; antibiotics do not prevent gn but treat active infection) iga nephropathy: acei/arb; if persistent proteinuria >1 g/day despite max ras blockade → corticosteroids (testing trial); sglt2 inhibitors (dapa-ckd) lupus nephritis iii/iv: induction with mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide + steroids; maintenance with mycophenolate anti-gbm: plasmapheresis + cyclophosphamide + steroids (emergency) anca vasculitis: rituximab or cyclophosphamide + steroids; avacopan (c5a receptor inhibitor — advocate trial) 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls rbc casts = glomerulonephritis (pathognomonic) low c3 + low c4 = lupus nephritis, cryoglobulinemia low c3 + normal c4 = psgn, mpgn (alternative pathway) normal complement = iga nephropathy, anti-gbm, anca vasculitis iga nephropathy: hematuria during uri (synpharyngitic); psgn: hematuria after uri (1–3 weeks) goodpasture = anti-gbm + pulmonary hemorrhage (young male smoker) top 5 pimping questions: what is the pathognomonic finding in nephritic syndrome? (rbc casts) what is the most common gn worldwide? (iga nephropathy) how do you differentiate iga nephropathy from psgn by timing? (iga: during uri; psgn: 1–3 weeks after) what complement pattern suggests lupus nephritis? (low c3 and low c4) what is "full house" immunofluorescence? (igg, iga, igm, c3, c1q — lupus nephritis) topic 29: dialysis indications topic 29 · dialysis indications 1. indications for emergent dialysis (mnemonic: aeiou) 1. indications for emergent dialysis (mnemonic: aeiou) a — acidosis: severe metabolic acidosis (ph <7.1) refractory to medical therapy e — electrolytes: refractory hyperkalemia (k+ >6.5 with ecg changes, not responding to medical management) i — intoxication: toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates) o — overload: volume overload/pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics u — uremia: uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding) 2. indications for chronic dialysis 2. indications for chronic dialysis egfr <5–10 ml/min with symptoms refractory fluid overload, hyperkalemia, acidosis uremic symptoms (nausea, anorexia, cognitive decline, neuropathy, pericarditis) failure to thrive despite optimal medical management no absolute gfr cutoff — clinical judgment based on symptoms 3. modalities 3. modalities feature hemodialysis (hd) peritoneal dialysis (pd) access av fistula (preferred), av graft, tunneled catheter tenckhoff catheter frequency 3x/week, 3–4 hours/session daily (capd) or nightly (apd) advantages efficient solute clearance, supervised home-based, hemodynamically gentler, preserves residual renal function longer disadvantages hemodynamic instability, vascular access complications, in-center peritonitis risk, protein loss, limited in obese/prior abdominal surgery preferred in most patients; urgent need patient preference, children, remote areas, cv instability 4. vascular access 4. vascular access av fistula: preferred (lowest infection/thrombosis rate); create 6 months before anticipated need; "rule of 6s" — 6 mm diameter, <6 mm depth, 600 ml/min flow av graft: if unsuitable veins; can use in 2–4 weeks tunneled dialysis catheter: temporary or bridge; highest infection risk non-tunneled catheter: emergent dialysis only 5. continuous renal replacement therapy (crrt) 5. continuous renal replacement therapy (crrt) indication: hemodynamically unstable patients (icu, septic shock) slower, continuous solute/fluid removal over 24 hours modalities: cvvh, cvvhd, cvvhdf 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls aeiou = emergent dialysis indications av fistula is the gold standard access (lowest complication rate) dialysis disequilibrium syndrome: cerebral edema during/after first hd sessions (headache, nausea, seizures) — prevent with slower initial sessions first-use syndrome: anaphylactoid reaction to dialysis membrane pd peritonitis: cloudy effluate, abdominal pain; treat with intraperitoneal antibiotics (vancomycin + gentamicin or ceftazidime) top 5 pimping questions: what are the emergent dialysis indications? (aeiou: acidosis, electrolytes, intoxication, overload, uremia) what is the preferred vascular access? (av fistula) when should av fistula be created? (6 months before anticipated dialysis) what is dialysis disequilibrium syndrome? (cerebral edema during first hd — osmotic shift) what is the indication for crrt over hd? (hemodynamic instability) topic 30: hyponatremia topic 30 · hyponatremia 1. definition 1. definition serum sodium <135 meq/l. most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients. mild: 130–134; moderate: 125–129; severe: <125 2. classification by osmolality 2. classification by osmolality type serum osmolality causes references hypertonic (translocational) >295 mosm/kg hyperglycemia, mannitol (na drops ~1.6 meq/l per 100 mg/dl glucose above 100) isotonic (pseudohyponatremia) 275–295 mosm/kg hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia hypotonic (true hyponatremia) <275 mosm/kg most cases — further classify by volume status 3. hypotonic hyponatremia — classify by volume status 3. hypotonic hyponatremia — classify by volume status volume status urine na causes hypovolemic, una <20 <20 meq/l gi losses (vomiting, diarrhea), third-spacing, burns hypovolemic, una >20 >20 meq/l diuretics (thiazides!), adrenal insufficiency, cerebral salt wasting euvolemic >20 meq/l siadh (most common euvolemic cause), hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, psychogenic polydipsia hypervolemic variable chf, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, ckd 4. siadh diagnostic criteria 4. siadh diagnostic criteria serum osmolality <275 mosm/kg urine osmolality >100 mosm/kg (inappropriately concentrated) urine na >30 meq/l euvolemic normal thyroid and adrenal function no diuretics causes: cns disorders, pulmonary disease (pneumonia, tb), malignancy (small cell lung cancer), drugs (ssris, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, opioids), pain, nausea 5. stepwise management 5. stepwise management acute symptomatic (seizures, severe ams, <48 hours): hypertonic saline (3% nacl): 100 ml bolus over 10 minutes, can repeat x 2 target: raise na by 4–6 meq/l in first 1–2 hours (enough to stop symptoms) do not exceed 8 meq/l in any 24-hour period (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome/ods) chronic (≥48 hours or unknown duration): correct slowly: ≤8 meq/l per 24 hours (some recommend ≤6 in high-risk patients: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver disease) hypovolemic: isotonic saline (ns) euvolemic (siadh): fluid restriction (800–1000 ml/day) first-line; salt tablets + loop diuretic; vaptans (tolvaptan — use cautiously, monitor na q6h); urea (emerging) hypervolemic: fluid + salt restriction; treat underlying cause; loop diuretics 6. osmotic demyelination syndrome (ods) 6. osmotic demyelination syndrome (ods) previously called central pontine myelinolysis caused by overly rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia presents 2–6 days after correction: dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, "locked-in" syndrome risk factors: alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia, liver disease if overcorrected: re-lower na with d5w ± desmopressin (ddavp) 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls thiazides cause hyponatremia; loop diuretics rarely do (thiazides impair diluting segment without affecting concentrating ability) always check serum osmolality first to rule out hypertonic/isotonic causes correct hypokalemia concurrently (k+ correction raises na — each meq k+ repleted raises na by ~1 meq/l) beer potomania: low solute intake → limited free water excretion → hyponatremia exercise-associated hyponatremia: excessive free water intake during endurance events tolvaptan: contraindicated in liver disease (hepatotoxicity) top 5 pimping questions: what is the maximum safe correction rate? (≤8 meq/l per 24 hours) what happens if you correct too fast? (osmotic demyelination syndrome) what is the first-line treatment for siadh? (fluid restriction) why do thiazides cause hyponatremia but loops usually don't? (thiazides impair diluting segment; loops impair both diluting and concentrating) how do you manage overcorrection? (d5w ± desmopressin to re-lower na) topic 31: hypernatremia topic 31 · hypernatremia 1. definition 1. definition serum sodium >145 meq/l. always indicates water deficit (hyperosmolar state). 2. etiology 2. etiology water loss: insensible losses (fever, burns), gi losses, osmotic diuresis (hyperglycemia, mannitol), diabetes insipidus inadequate water intake: altered mental status, no access to water, elderly, intubated patients sodium gain (rare): hypertonic saline, sodium bicarbonate, salt tablets 3. diabetes insipidus (di) 3. diabetes insipidus (di) type mechanism urine osmolality response to desmopressin causes central di decreased adh production <300 mosm/kg (dilute) urine concentrates (>50% increase) pituitary surgery, trauma, tumors, sheehan syndrome nephrogenic di kidney resistant to adh <300 mosm/kg (dilute) no response lithium (most common drug cause), hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, ckd 4. calculating free water deficit 4. calculating free water deficit free water deficit (fwd) = tbw x (serum [na] -140) / 140 tbw = 0.6 x weight (males), 0.5 x weight (females), 0.5/0.45 in elderly 5. stepwise management 5. stepwise management identify and treat underlying cause replace free water deficit: oral water preferred if patient can drink iv: d5w or 0.45% nacl (half-normal saline) correction rate: ≤10 meq/l per 24 hours (chronic); can correct faster if acute (<24 hours onset) too-rapid correction → cerebral edema (brain cells have generated idiogenic osmoles) central di: desmopressin (ddavp) — intranasal 10–20 mcg bid or iv/sc 1–2 mcg bid nephrogenic di: treat underlying cause; thiazide diuretics (paradoxically reduce urine volume by enhancing proximal reabsorption); amiloride (for lithium-induced); low-sodium diet; nsaids monitor na every 4–6 hours during correction 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls hypernatremia = always a water problem (not a sodium problem) most common in hospitalized patients: elderly, intubated, ams — cannot access water lithium is the most common drug cause of nephrogenic di thiazides paradoxically treat nephrogenic di (volume contraction → increased proximal reabsorption → less water delivery to collecting duct) acute hypernatremia (<24h): can correct faster; chronic: ≤10 meq/l per 24h always account for ongoing losses when calculating replacement top 5 pimping questions: what is the formula for free water deficit? (tbw x [na/140 − 1]) what is the maximum correction rate for chronic hypernatremia? (≤10 meq/l per 24 hours) how do you differentiate central from nephrogenic di? (desmopressin challenge — central responds, nephrogenic does not) what is the most common drug cause of nephrogenic di? (lithium) why do thiazides help in nephrogenic di? (paradoxical: volume contraction → increased proximal reabsorption → decreased free water excretion) topic 32: hypokalemia topic 32 · hypokalemia 1. definition 1. definition serum k+ <3.5 meq/l. mild: 3.0–3.4; moderate: 2.5–2.9; severe: <2.5 2. etiology 2. etiology decreased intake: malnutrition, anorexia increased loss: renal: diuretics (loops, thiazides — most common cause), hyperaldosteronism, rta type i/ii, hypomagnesemia, amphotericin b gi: vomiting (metabolic alkalosis → renal k+ wasting), diarrhea, laxative abuse, ng suction transcellular shift (into cells): insulin, beta2-agonists, alkalosis, refeeding syndrome 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation mild: often asymptomatic moderate-severe: muscle weakness, cramps, fatigue, constipation/ileus severe: rhabdomyolysis, respiratory failure, cardiac arrhythmias ecg changes (progressive): flattened t waves → st depression → u waves → prolonged qt → arrhythmias (pvcs, vt/vf) 4. diagnostic approach 4. diagnostic approach check urine k+: urine k+ <20 meq/l (or ttkg <3): extrarenal loss (gi, transcellular shift) urine k+ >20 meq/l (or ttkg >7): renal loss → check bp and acid-base: high bp: hyperaldosteronism, cushing, renovascular htn, liddle syndrome normal bp + metabolic alkalosis: diuretics, vomiting, bartter/gitelman normal bp + metabolic acidosis: rta type i or ii 5. stepwise management 5. stepwise management mild-moderate (k+ 3.0–3.4, asymptomatic): oral kcl 40–80 meq/day in divided doses moderate (k+ 2.5–2.9) or symptomatic: oral + iv kcl severe (<2.5) or ecg changes or arrhythmias: iv kcl iv rate: max 10–20 meq/hr via peripheral line; up to 40 meq/hr via central line with cardiac monitoring concentration: max 40 meq/l peripheral; 60–80 meq/l central never give iv kcl as bolus push (cardiac arrest risk) always check and correct magnesium (hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia — mg2+ is needed for romk channel function) treat underlying cause 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia: correct mg2+ first (k+ will not correct until mg2+ is repleted) each 10 meq kcl raises serum k+ by ~0.1 meq/l (rough estimate) vomiting causes hypokalemia primarily through renal losses (metabolic alkalosis → bicarbonaturia → obligate k+ loss), not through gastric k+ loss digoxin toxicity is potentiated by hypokalemia u waves are relatively specific for hypokalemia metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia perpetuate each other top 5 pimping questions: why must you correct mg2+ before k+? (mg2+ needed for romk channel; k+ refractory until mg2+ repleted) what is the maximum iv kcl rate peripherally? (10–20 meq/hr) what are the ecg changes in hypokalemia? (flattened t → st depression → u waves → prolonged qt) why does vomiting cause hypokalemia? (metabolic alkalosis → renal k+ wasting, not gastric loss) what syndrome presents with hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis + normal bp? (bartter or gitelman syndrome) topic 33: hyperkalemia topic 33 · hyperkalemia 1. definition 1. definition serum k+ >5.0 meq/l. mild: 5.0–5.5; moderate: 5.5–6.5; severe: >6.5 first step: rule out pseudohyperkalemia (hemolyzed sample, tourniquet, fist clenching, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis) 2. etiology 2. etiology decreased excretion: aki/ckd (most common), hypoaldosteronism (type iv rta), k+-sparing diuretics, acei/arb, nsaids, trimethoprim, heparin transcellular shift (out of cells): acidosis, insulin deficiency, beta-blockers, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, massive hemolysis), succinylcholine, digoxin toxicity increased intake: rarely sole cause unless renal impairment present 3. ecg changes (progressive — must memorize) 3. ecg changes (progressive — must memorize) peaked t waves (earliest) → 2. prolonged pr → 3. widened qrs → 4. loss of p waves → 5. sine wave pattern → 6. vf/asystole 4. emergency management (k+ >6.5 or ecg changes) 4. emergency management (k+ >6.5 or ecg changes) step agent dose onset mechanism duration 1. stabilize membrane calcium gluconate 10% 10 ml iv over 2–3 min (repeat in 5 min if no ecg improvement) 1–3 min stabilizes cardiac membrane (does not lower k+) 30–60 min 2. shift k+ into cells insulin (regular) + glucose 10 units iv + 25 g dextrose (d50w 50 ml) 15–30 min drives k+ intracellularly 4–6 hours 2. shift k+ into cells sodium bicarbonate 50 meq iv 15–30 min only if acidotic (limited benefit if no acidosis) 2 hours 2. shift k+ into cells albuterol (nebulized) 10–20 mg nebulized 15–30 min beta2-mediated k+ shift 2–4 hours 3. remove k+ from body furosemide 40–80 mg iv 30 min renal k+ excretion until diuresis 3. remove k+ from body sodium polystyrene sulfonate (kayexalate) 15–30 g po/pr 1–2 hours gi k+ exchange (limited evidence, risk of bowel necrosis) 4–6 hours 3. remove k+ from body patiromer or szc patiromer 8.4 g po; szc 10 g po 1–2 hours gi k+ binding (newer, safer) hours 3. remove k+ from body hemodialysis emergent immediate most effective k+ removal during session 5. stepwise approach 5. stepwise approach confirm true hyperkalemia (repeat if suspected pseudohyperkalemia) get ecg immediately if ecg changes or k+ >6.5: calcium gluconate → insulin/glucose → albuterol → furosemide → consider dialysis if k+ 5.5–6.5 without ecg changes: dietary restriction, stop offending drugs, furosemide, k+ binders treat underlying cause monitor k+ every 1–2 hours during acute management; continuous cardiac monitoring 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls calcium gluconate does not lower k+ — it stabilizes the myocardium (buys time) always give glucose with insulin (prevent hypoglycemia — monitor glucose q1h x 4h) if patient is already hyperglycemic (>250), can give insulin without glucose calcium chloride has 3x more elemental calcium than calcium gluconate but causes tissue necrosis if extravasated → use gluconate peripherally, chloride via central line succinylcholine is contraindicated in hyperkalemia (causes further k+ release) digoxin toxicity + hyperkalemia: do not give calcium (can cause "stone heart") → give digoxin-specific antibody (digibind) top 5 pimping questions: what is the first step in hyperkalemia with ecg changes? (iv calcium gluconate) does calcium gluconate lower potassium? (no — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane) what are the progressive ecg changes? (peaked t → prolonged pr → wide qrs → loss of p → sine wave → vf) why give glucose with insulin? (prevent hypoglycemia) when is calcium contraindicated in hyperkalemia? (digoxin toxicity — risk of "stone heart") topic 34: acid-base disorders topic 34 · acid-base disorders 1. primary disorders 1. primary disorders disorder ph primary change compensation metabolic acidosis <7.35 ↓ hco3- ↓ pco2 (hyperventilation) metabolic alkalosis >7.45 ↑ hco3- ↑ pco2 (hypoventilation) respiratory acidosis <7.35 ↑ pco2 ↑ hco3- (renal) respiratory alkalosis >7.45 ↓ pco2 ↓ hco3- (renal) 2. compensation rules (must memorize) 2. compensation rules (must memorize) metabolic acidosis: winter's formula → expected pco2 = (1.5 x hco3-) + 8 ± 2 metabolic alkalosis: expected pco2 = (0.7 x hco3-) + 21 ± 2 acute respiratory acidosis: hco3- rises 1 meq/l per 10 mmhg ↑ pco2 chronic respiratory acidosis: hco3- rises 3.5 meq/l per 10 mmhg ↑ pco2 acute respiratory alkalosis: hco3- falls 2 meq/l per 10 mmhg ↓ pco2 chronic respiratory alkalosis: hco3- falls 5 meq/l per 10 mmhg ↓ pco2 if actual pco2 ≠ expected → mixed disorder 3. systematic abg interpretation (5 steps) 3. systematic abg interpretation (5 steps) look at ph → acidemia or alkalemia? identify primary disorder (which value explains the ph?) calculate expected compensation → if not matching, mixed disorder if metabolic acidosis → calculate anion gap: ag = na+ − (cl- + hco3-); normal = 12 ± 2 if elevated ag → calculate delta-delta ratio: δag/δhco3- <1: concurrent non-ag metabolic acidosis 1–2: pure ag metabolic acidosis >2: concurrent metabolic alkalosis 4. anion gap metabolic acidosis (mnemonic: mudpiles or goldmark) 4. anion gap metabolic acidosis (mnemonic: mudpiles or goldmark) goldmark (preferred): g — glycols (ethylene glycol, propylene glycol) o — oxoproline (acetaminophen metabolite) l — l-lactate (most common: shock, sepsis, ischemia, metformin) d — d-lactate (short bowel syndrome) m — methanol a — aspirin (salicylates) r — renal failure (uremia) k — ketoacidosis (dka, alcoholic, starvation) 5. non-anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis 5. non-anion gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis mnemonic: hardups h — hyperalimentation (tpn) a — addison disease (adrenal insufficiency) r — renal tubular acidosis d — diarrhea (most common cause — loss of hco3-) u — ureteral diversion p — pancreatic fistula s — saline (dilutional — large-volume ns resuscitation) 6. renal tubular acidosis (rta) 6. renal tubular acidosis (rta) type defect serum k+ urine ph associations type 1 (distal) cannot secrete h+ in distal tubule low (hypokalemia) >5.5 nephrocalcinosis, kidney stones, sjögren, sle, amphotericin b type 2 (proximal) cannot reabsorb hco3- in proximal tubule low (hypokalemia) <5.5 (after hco3- threshold reached) fanconi syndrome, multiple myeloma, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors type 4 (hypoaldosteronism) decreased aldosterone or resistance high (hyperkalemia) <5.5 diabetes (most common), acei/arb, k+-sparing diuretics, adrenal insufficiency 7. metabolic alkalosis 7. metabolic alkalosis saline-responsive (urine cl- <20): vomiting, ng suction, diuretics (after discontinuation) → treat with ns saline-resistant (urine cl- >20): hyperaldosteronism, cushing, bartter, gitelman, current diuretic use → treat underlying cause; acetazolamide if severe 8. osmolar gap 8. osmolar gap calculated osmolality = 2(na) + glucose/18 + bun/2.8 osmolar gap = measured − calculated; normal <10 elevated osmolar gap + ag metabolic acidosis: methanol, ethylene glycol (toxic alcohols) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls lactic acidosis is the most common cause of ag metabolic acidosis in hospitalized patients type a lactic acidosis: tissue hypoperfusion (shock, sepsis); type b: no hypoperfusion (metformin, liver failure, malignancy) salicylate toxicity: mixed respiratory alkalosis + ag metabolic acidosis dka: ag metabolic acidosis; as treated with ns, ag closes but non-ag acidosis develops (hyperchloremic) normal saline causes hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (reason to prefer lr/balanced crystalloids) delta-delta ratio is essential for detecting hidden disorders top 10 testable facts: ag = na − (cl + hco3); normal 12 ± 2 winter's formula: expected pco2 = 1.5(hco3) + 8 ± 2 goldmark for ag metabolic acidosis lactic acidosis = most common ag metabolic acidosis delta-delta: <1 = concurrent non-ag acidosis; >2 = concurrent metabolic alkalosis rta type 1: distal, hypok, urine ph >5.5, nephrocalcinosis rta type 4: hyperk, diabetes, hypoaldosteronism saline-responsive alkalosis: urine cl <20 → give ns osmolar gap >10 + ag acidosis → toxic alcohols salicylate toxicity: mixed respiratory alkalosis + ag metabolic acidosis top 5 pimping questions: what is winter's formula? (expected pco2 = 1.5 x hco3 + 8 ± 2) what does a delta-delta >2 indicate? (concurrent metabolic alkalosis) what is the most common cause of ag metabolic acidosis? (lactic acidosis) which rta causes hyperkalemia? (type 4 — hypoaldosteronism) what causes elevated osmolar gap + ag acidosis? (methanol, ethylene glycol)

parts.title: PART V: HEMATOLOGY ONCOLOGY

blocks.text: Microcytic hypochromic anemia due to insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis. Most common anemia worldwide.
Blood loss: menstruation (most common in premenopausal women), GI bleeding (most common in men and postmenopausal women — must rule out malignancy)
Decreased intake: poor diet, vegetarian
Decreased absorption: celiac disease, gastrectomy, PPI use, H. pylori
Increased demand: pregnancy, growth
Hookworm infection (Ancylostoma, Necator — common in Indonesia/tropical regions)
General anemia symptoms: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia
Specific to IDA: pica (craving ice = pagophagia, dirt), koilonychia (spoon nails), glossitis, angular cheilitis, Plummer-Vinson syndrome (esophageal web + IDA + dysphagia)
Peripheral smear: microcytic hypochromic, target cells, pencil cells (elliptocytes)
Reticulocyte count: low (inadequate production)
Ferritin <30 = diagnostic of IDA; ferritin <100 in setting of inflammation still suggests IDA
Identify and treat underlying cause (GI workup in men and postmenopausal women — colonoscopy + EGD)
Oral iron: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) TID on empty stomach with vitamin C (enhances absorption)
Avoid with PPIs, calcium, tea, coffee (decrease absorption)
Side effects: constipation, nausea, black stools
Monitor: reticulocyte count peaks at 7–10 days; Hb should rise 1–2 g/dL in 2–4 weeks
Continue for 3–6 months after Hb normalizes (to replete stores)
IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose, ferumoxytol): indications — oral intolerance, malabsorption, CKD on ESA, severe anemia, ongoing losses exceeding oral replacement
Transfusion: only if hemodynamically unstable or Hb <7 (or <8 with cardiac disease)
Ferritin is the FIRST lab to drop in iron deficiency (even before anemia develops)
Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — can be falsely normal/elevated in inflammation; use cutoff <100 in inflammatory states
Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR): not affected by inflammation — helps differentiate IDA from ACD
Plummer-Vinson syndrome: IDA + esophageal web + dysphagia (premalignant for esophageal SCC)
Hookworm: common cause of IDA in tropical regions — check stool O
Thrombocytosis is common in IDA (reactive)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most sensitive single test for IDA? (Ferritin 30)
What is Plummer-Vinson syndrome? (IDA + esophageal web + dysphagia)
When do reticulocytes peak after iron replacement? (7–10 days)
How do you differentiate IDA from ACD? (Ferritin, TIBC, sTfR)
When do you give IV iron? (Oral intolerance, malabsorption, CKD, severe anemia)
Anemia associated with chronic inflammation, infection, or malignancy. Second most common anemia worldwide. Usually normocytic (can be microcytic if severe/prolonged).
Hepcidin (key mediator): upregulated by IL-6 in inflammation → blocks ferroportin → traps iron in macrophages and enterocytes → decreased serum iron despite adequate stores
Decreased erythropoietin response
Shortened RBC survival
Serum iron: ↓; Ferritin: ↑ or normal; TIBC: ↓; Transferrin saturation: ↓; sTfR: normal
Treat underlying disease (most important)
ESA (epoetin, darbepoetin): if Hb 10 and symptomatic (especially CKD, cancer on chemotherapy)
IV iron: if concurrent iron deficiency (ferritin 100 or TSAT 20%)
Transfusion: restrictive threshold (Hb 7)
Hepcidin is the master regulator of iron metabolism
ACD is a diagnosis of exclusion — always rule out IDA first
Combined IDA + ACD is common (e.g., RA patient with GI bleeding)
sTfR/log ferritin ratio 2 suggests IDA component in mixed picture
Macrocytic anemia (MCV 100 fL) caused by impaired DNA synthesis, most commonly due to vitamin B12 or folate deficiency.

rows: Pernicious anemia (most common in developed countries), gastrectomy, terminal ileum disease (Crohn), vegan diet, metformin, PPI, fish tapeworm (Diphyllobothrium latum)
Poor diet, alcoholism, pregnancy, methotrexate, trimethoprim, phenytoin
YES — subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts): paresthesias, ataxia, loss of proprioception/vibration

blocks.text: CBC: macrocytic anemia (MCV 100), pancytopenia possible
Peripheral smear: hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes — pathognomonic), macro-ovalocytes
Serum B12 and folate levels
If B12 borderline (200–400 pg/mL): check MMA and homocysteine (both elevated = B12 deficiency)
Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: specific for pernicious anemia (~60% sensitivity)
Anti-parietal cell antibodies: sensitive but less specific
B12 deficiency:
IM cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily x 7 days → weekly x 4 weeks → monthly indefinitely (if pernicious anemia or malabsorption)
High-dose oral B12 (1000–2000 mcg/day) is effective even in pernicious anemia (1% absorbed by passive diffusion)
Neurological symptoms may be irreversible if treatment delayed
Folate deficiency:
Folic acid 1–5 mg/day PO
ALWAYS rule out B12 deficiency before treating with folate alone (folate corrects anemia but NOT neurological damage — can mask B12 deficiency and worsen neuropathy)
Hypersegmented neutrophils = megaloblastic anemia until proven otherwise
MMA differentiates B12 from folate (elevated only in B12 deficiency)
NEVER give folate alone without checking B12 (masks hematologic findings while neuropathy progresses)
Subacute combined degeneration: posterior columns (proprioception, vibration) + lateral corticospinal tracts (spasticity, weakness)
Pernicious anemia: autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → no intrinsic factor → B12 malabsorption; associated with other autoimmune diseases
Reticulocyte crisis: reticulocyte count peaks 5–7 days after B12/folate replacement
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is pathognomonic on peripheral smear? (Hypersegmented neutrophils)
How do you differentiate B12 from folate deficiency? (MMA — elevated only in B12)
Why is it dangerous to give folate without checking B12? (Masks anemia but neuropathy progresses)
What is subacute combined degeneration? (Posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts — B12 deficiency)
What is pernicious anemia? (Autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → loss of intrinsic factor)
Premature destruction of RBCs (lifespan 120 days). Classified by mechanism and location.

rows: ↑ free Hb, ↓↓ haptoglobin, hemoglobinuria, hemosiderinuria, ↑ LDH
TTP/HUS, PNH, mechanical valves, DIC, transfusion reactions, G6PD (acute crisis), cold AIHA
Warm AIHA, hereditary spherocytosis, sickle cell, thalassemia, hypersplenism

blocks.text: ↑ Reticulocyte count (compensatory — hallmark of hemolysis)
↑ LDH
↑ Indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin
↓ Haptoglobin (binds free hemoglobin — consumed)
↑ Urobilinogen
Peripheral smear: varies by cause (spherocytes, schistocytes, sickle cells, target cells, bite cells)

rows: Membrane: hereditary spherocytosis, hereditary elliptocytosis, PNH
Immune: warm AIHA (IgG), cold AIHA (IgM), drug-induced
Enzyme: G6PD deficiency, pyruvate kinase deficiency
Mechanical: TTP/HUS, DIC, mechanical valves, HELLP, march hemoglobinuria

blocks.text: Warm AIHA (IgG-mediated)
Most common AIHA; IgG antibodies → extravascular hemolysis (spleen)
Causes: idiopathic, SLE, CLL, drugs (methyldopa, penicillin, cephalosporins)
Smear: spherocytes
Diagnosis: positive direct Coombs test (DAT) — IgG ± C3
Treatment: corticosteroids (first-line) → rituximab → splenectomy
Cold AIHA (IgM-mediated / Cold Agglutinin Disease)
IgM antibodies activate complement → intravascular hemolysis at cold temperatures
Causes: Mycoplasma pneumoniae, EBV (infectious mononucleosis), lymphoproliferative disorders
Smear: RBC agglutination
Diagnosis: DAT positive for C3 only; cold agglutinin titer elevated
Treatment: avoid cold exposure; rituximab (steroids less effective); do NOT give warm AIHA treatments (splenectomy ineffective — intravascular hemolysis)
G6PD Deficiency
X-linked recessive; most common enzyme deficiency worldwide
Episodic hemolysis triggered by: oxidative stress — infections (most common trigger), fava beans (favism), drugs (primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin)
Smear: bite cells, Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin — seen with supravital stain)
Diagnosis: G6PD enzyme level — check AFTER acute episode resolves (levels falsely normal during crisis due to reticulocytosis — young RBCs have higher G6PD)
Treatment: avoid triggers; supportive; transfuse if severe
Hereditary Spherocytosis
Autosomal dominant; spectrin/ankyrin deficiency → spherocytes → trapped in spleen
Smear: spherocytes; ↑ MCHC (most common cause of elevated MCHC)
Diagnosis: osmotic fragility test (increased) or eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test
Complications: aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19), pigment gallstones
Treatment: folate supplementation; splenectomy if severe (curative — wait until age >5; vaccinate against encapsulated organisms: pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenzae)
Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)
Acquired mutation in PIGA gene → loss of GPI-anchored proteins (CD55, CD59) → complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis
Triad: hemolytic anemia + pancytopenia + thrombosis (especially hepatic/Budd-Chiari, cerebral)
Diagnosis: flow cytometry for CD55/CD59 (gold standard); Ham test (historical)
Treatment: eculizumab (anti-C5 monoclonal antibody — first-line); ravulizumab (longer-acting); meningococcal vaccine before starting (risk of Neisseria meningitidis)
Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA)
Schistocytes on smear (fragmented RBCs) — hallmark
Causes: TTP, HUS, DIC, HELLP, malignant hypertension, mechanical valves

TTP: pentad — MAHA, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, fever, neurological symptoms; ADAMTS13 <10%; treatment: plasma exchange (emergent) + steroids + caplacizumab; do NOT give platelets (worsens thrombosis)
HUS: MAHA + thrombocytopenia + AKI; typical (EHEC/Shiga toxin — children, post-diarrheal) vs atypical (complement-mediated — treat with eculizumab)
Confirm hemolysis: ↑ retics, ↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↓ haptoglobin
Peripheral smear: spherocytes (AIHA, HS), schistocytes (MAHA), bite cells (G6PD), sickle cells, target cells
Direct Coombs test (DAT): positive → immune-mediated (warm vs cold)
If DAT negative → intrinsic RBC defect (G6PD, HS, PNH, hemoglobinopathy) or non-immune extrinsic (MAHA, infection)
Coombs test: Direct = "D"etect antibodies on RBC surface; Indirect = antibodies in serum
Schistocytes = MAHA until proven otherwise
Spherocytes + positive DAT = AIHA; spherocytes + negative DAT = hereditary spherocytosis
G6PD levels: check AFTER crisis (falsely normal during acute episode)
TTP: NEVER transfuse platelets (fuels thrombosis)
PNH: only hemolytic anemia with pancytopenia + thrombosis
Parvovirus B19 causes aplastic crisis in ANY chronic hemolytic anemia (transient red cell aplasia)
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Hemolysis labs: ↑ retics, ↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bili, ↓ haptoglobin
Warm AIHA: IgG, extravascular, spherocytes, DAT positive (IgG ± C3)
Cold AIHA: IgM, intravascular, DAT positive (C3 only), Mycoplasma/EBV
G6PD: X-linked, bite cells, Heinz bodies, check levels AFTER crisis
Hereditary spherocytosis: AD, spherocytes, ↑ MCHC, osmotic fragility, splenectomy curative
PNH: PIGA mutation, CD55/CD59 loss, eculizumab, meningococcal vaccine
TTP: ADAMTS13 <10%, plasma exchange, do NOT give platelets
Schistocytes = MAHA
Parvovirus B19 → aplastic crisis in chronic hemolysis
DAT positive = immune; DAT negative = non-immune
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most important initial test in hemolytic anemia? (Direct Coombs test — differentiates immune vs non-immune)
Why check G6PD levels AFTER the crisis? (Reticulocytes have higher G6PD → falsely normal during crisis)
Why NOT give platelets in TTP? (Fuels microvascular thrombosis)
What is the triad of PNH? (Hemolytic anemia + pancytopenia + thrombosis)
What smear finding is pathognomonic for MAHA? (Schistocytes)
Pancytopenia with hypocellular bone marrow (<25% cellularity) in the absence of abnormal infiltrate or fibrosis.
Idiopathic (most common — ~70%, likely autoimmune T-cell mediated)
Drugs: chloramphenicol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, chemotherapy
Infections: hepatitis (seronegative hepatitis → aplastic anemia), EBV, CMV, parvovirus B19, HIV
Toxins: benzene, radiation
Inherited: Fanconi anemia (AR; short stature, café-au-lait spots, thumb/radial anomalies; chromosome breakage test)
Anemia: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea
Thrombocytopenia: petechiae, purpura, bleeding
Neutropenia: recurrent infections
NO hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy (distinguishes from leukemia/lymphoma)
CBC: pancytopenia with low reticulocyte count
Peripheral smear: normocytic normochromic anemia, no abnormal cells
Bone marrow biopsy (definitive): hypocellular (<25%), fat spaces replacing hematopoietic tissue, no fibrosis or infiltrate
Rule out: PNH (flow cytometry for CD55/CD59 — PNH and aplastic anemia overlap), MDS, leukemia

rows: Criteria (≥2 of 3 peripheral blood criteria + hypocellular marrow)

blocks.text: Age <40 with matched sibling donor: allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) — curative, first-line
Age >40 or no matched donor: immunosuppressive therapy (IST) — horse ATG (anti-thymocyte globulin) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag (RACE trial — addition of eltrombopag improves response)
Supportive: transfusions (irradiated, leukoreduced — avoid CMV+ products if transplant candidate), antibiotics for infections, growth factors (G-CSF controversial)
Eltrombopag (TPO receptor agonist): added to first-line IST; also used in refractory cases
Avoid unnecessary transfusions in transplant candidates (alloimmunization risk)
Infections (leading cause of death)
Bleeding
Transformation to MDS/AML (~15% over 10 years)
PNH clone evolution (~10–15%)
Iron overload from chronic transfusions
