Aplastic anemia vs MDS: aplastic = hypocellular marrow, no dysplasia; MDS = usually hypercellular, dysplastic changes, may have blasts
Fanconi anemia: suspect in young patient with aplastic anemia + congenital anomalies; confirm with chromosome breakage test (DEB/MMC)
PNH and aplastic anemia are related — always check PNH flow cytometry
Seronegative hepatitis → aplastic anemia: classic association (hepatitis-associated aplastic anemia)
Do NOT give live vaccines to immunosuppressed patients
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the definitive diagnostic test? (Bone marrow biopsy — hypocellular <25%)
What is the first-line treatment for young patients with matched sibling? (Allogeneic HSCT)
What inherited condition causes aplastic anemia? (Fanconi anemia — chromosome breakage test)
What related condition must you screen for? (PNH — flow cytometry CD55/CD59)
What is the Camitta criteria for severe aplastic anemia? (ANC <500, platelets <20K, retics <1%)
Clonal proliferation of myeloid precursors (blasts) in bone marrow. Defined by ≥20% blasts in marrow or blood (WHO), or by specific genetic abnormalities regardless of blast count.
Age (>65 most common), prior chemotherapy/radiation (therapy-related AML), myelodysplastic syndrome, benzene exposure, Down syndrome (AMKL — M7), Fanconi anemia
Based on genetic abnormalities (most important for prognosis and treatment):
Favorable: t(8;21) — AML with RUNX1-RUNX1T1; inv(16)/t(16;16) — CBFB-MYH11; APL t(15;17) — PML-RARA; NPM1 mutated
Intermediate: normal karyotype, FLT3-ITD
Adverse: complex karyotype, monosomy 5/7, TP53 mutation, FLT3-ITD (high ratio)
Bone marrow failure: anemia (fatigue, pallor), thrombocytopenia (bleeding, petechiae), neutropenia (infections)
Leukocytosis with circulating blasts (but can present with leukopenia)
Tissue infiltration: gingival hyperplasia (M4/M5 — monocytic), skin (leukemia cutis), CNS
DIC: especially in APL (M3) — medical emergency
Leukostasis: WBC >100,000 → respiratory distress, neurological symptoms → emergency leukapheresis + hydroxyurea
Tumor lysis syndrome risk with treatment initiation
CBC with differential: blasts on peripheral smear
Peripheral smear: Auer rods (pathognomonic for AML — eosinophilic needle-like inclusions); "faggot cells" (bundles of Auer rods) in APL
Bone marrow biopsy: ≥20% blasts; flow cytometry (CD13, CD33, CD34, MPO+)
Cytogenetics and molecular testing: essential for classification and prognosis (FLT3, NPM1, CEBPA, TP53, IDH1/2)
Coagulation studies: DIC screen (especially if APL suspected)
t(15;17) → PML-RARA fusion
Presents with DIC (most dangerous complication)
Treatment: all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) + arsenic trioxide (ATO) — curative in most cases without chemotherapy (Lo-Coco trial)
Start ATRA immediately if APL suspected (do NOT wait for confirmation)
Differentiation syndrome (formerly ATRA syndrome): fever, dyspnea, pulmonary infiltrates, weight gain, pleural/pericardial effusion → treat with dexamethasone 10 mg BID
APL is the most curable AML subtype (~90% cure rate)
Induction: "7+3" — cytarabine (7 days) + daunorubicin (3 days); goal: complete remission (CR)
Consolidation: high-dose cytarabine (HiDAC) for favorable risk; allogeneic HSCT for intermediate/adverse risk
Targeted therapies:
FLT3 inhibitors: midostaurin (with induction — RATIFY trial), gilteritinib (relapsed/refractory)
IDH1/2 inhibitors: ivosidenib (IDH1), enasidenib (IDH2)
Venetoclax + azacitidine: for unfit/elderly patients (VIALE-A trial — standard of care)
Gemtuzumab ozogamicin: anti-CD33 ADC for favorable-risk AML
Tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis: allopurinol or rasburicase + aggressive hydration
Transfusions: platelets <10,000 (or <20,000 if febrile/bleeding); pRBC for Hb <7
Neutropenic fever: see Topic 45
Leukostasis (WBC >100K): hydroxyurea + leukapheresis; avoid transfusing pRBC before reducing WBC (increases viscosity)
Auer rods = AML (never in ALL)
APL + DIC = start ATRA immediately
"7+3" = standard induction
Venetoclax + azacitidine = standard for unfit elderly AML
Leukostasis: do NOT transfuse RBCs before reducing WBC count
Down syndrome children: increased risk of AMKL (M7) but also have better prognosis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is pathognomonic for AML on smear? (Auer rods)
What is the emergency treatment for APL? (ATRA — start immediately; do not wait for confirmation)
What is the standard induction regimen? ("7+3" — cytarabine + daunorubicin)
What is differentiation syndrome? (ATRA/ATO complication: fever, dyspnea, effusions → dexamethasone)
What is the treatment for unfit elderly AML? (Venetoclax + azacitidine — VIALE-A)
Clonal proliferation of lymphoid precursors (lymphoblasts). Most common cancer in children (peak 2–5 years). Bimodal: children and adults >60.
B-cell ALL (~85%): CD10 (CALLA)+, CD19+, CD22+; t(12;21) TEL-AML1 in children (favorable); t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL) in adults (unfavorable — ~25% of adult ALL)
T-cell ALL (~15%): CD3+, CD7+; thymic mass common; male predominance
Bone marrow failure: anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia
Lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly (more common than AML)
CNS involvement: headache, cranial nerve palsies (more common in ALL than AML)
Mediastinal mass: T-cell ALL (anterior mediastinum — SVC syndrome risk)
Testicular involvement (sanctuary site)
Bone pain (children — may mimic JIA)
Peripheral smear: lymphoblasts (no Auer rods — distinguishes from AML)
Bone marrow: ≥20% lymphoblasts; flow cytometry (B vs T markers)
Cytogenetics: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) — critical for treatment decisions
TdT positive (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase — marker of immature lymphoid cells; positive in ALL, negative in mature lymphomas)
LP: CNS staging (all patients)
Induction → consolidation → maintenance (2–3 years — unlike AML)

Phases: induction (vincristine, prednisone, daunorubicin, asparaginase, ± cyclophosphamide); consolidation (high-dose methotrexate, cytarabine); maintenance (6-MP + methotrexate daily/weekly x 2–3 years)
CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal methotrexate ± cytarabine (ALL has high CNS relapse risk)
Philadelphia chromosome positive (Ph+ ALL): add TKI (imatinib, dasatinib) — dramatically improves outcomes
Relapsed/refractory: blinatumomab (bispecific T-cell engager, anti-CD19/CD3), inotuzumab ozogamicin (anti-CD22 ADC), CAR-T cell therapy (tisagenlecleucel — anti-CD19)
ALL has MAINTENANCE phase (2–3 years); AML does not
TdT positive = ALL; TdT negative = AML or mature lymphoma
Philadelphia chromosome: poor prognosis in ALL but treatable with TKI
CNS prophylaxis is mandatory in ALL
Tumor lysis syndrome: high risk at treatment initiation (high tumor burden)
Asparaginase: unique to ALL treatment; complications — pancreatitis, thrombosis, hepatotoxicity
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What marker differentiates ALL from AML? (TdT — positive in ALL, negative in AML)
What is the Philadelphia chromosome? (t(9;22) BCR-ABL — poor prognosis in ALL; add TKI)
Why does ALL need CNS prophylaxis? (High risk of CNS relapse)
What is unique about ALL maintenance? (2–3 years of 6-MP + methotrexate — AML has no maintenance)
What is CAR-T therapy for ALL? (Tisagenlecleucel — anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor T cells)
Clonal plasma cell neoplasm producing monoclonal immunoglobulin (M protein). Median age 65–70.
Clonal bone marrow plasma cells ≥10% AND one or more myeloma-defining events:
CRAB criteria (end-organ damage):
C — Calcium elevated (>11 mg/dL or >1 mg/dL above ULN)
R — Renal insufficiency (Cr >2 mg/dL or CrCl <40)
A — Anemia (Hb <10 g/dL or >2 g/dL below normal)
B — Bone lesions (lytic lesions on skeletal survey, CT, or PET-CT)
OR biomarkers of malignancy (SLiM criteria):
S — Sixty percent (≥60%) clonal plasma cells in marrow
Li — Light chain ratio ≥100 (involved/uninvolved)
M — MRI with >1 focal lesion ≥5 mm
Bone pain (most common presenting symptom — especially back); pathological fractures; lytic lesions (punched-out on X-ray)
Anemia (fatigue)
Renal failure: light chain cast nephropathy (myeloma kidney), hypercalcemia, amyloidosis
Hypercalcemia: confusion, constipation, polyuria
Recurrent infections (immunoparesis — decreased normal immunoglobulins)
Hyperviscosity syndrome (rare, more common in Waldenström): blurred vision, headache, bleeding
Amyloidosis (AL type): nephrotic syndrome, cardiomyopathy, macroglossia, neuropathy
SPEP + UPEP with immunofixation: M spike (monoclonal protein)
Serum free light chains (kappa/lambda ratio)
Bone marrow biopsy: clonal plasma cells ≥10%
CBC, BMP (calcium, creatinine), albumin, beta2-microglobulin, LDH
Skeletal survey (X-ray) or low-dose whole-body CT or PET-CT: lytic lesions
24-hour urine: Bence Jones protein (light chains)
Cytogenetics/FISH: t(4;14), t(14;16), del(17p) — high risk

rows: beta2M <3.5 + albumin ≥3.5 + standard-risk cytogenetics + normal LDH
beta2M ≥5.5 + high-risk cytogenetics [t(4;14), t(14;16), del(17p)] or elevated LDH

blocks.text: Transplant-eligible (age <65–70, fit):
Induction: VRd — bortezomib (V) + lenalidomide (R) + dexamethasone (d) x 4–6 cycles (SWOG S0777 trial)
Stem cell collection
Autologous HSCT (melphalan conditioning)
Maintenance: lenalidomide indefinitely (CALGB 100104 — improves PFS and OS)
Transplant-ineligible:
VRd or DRd (daratumumab + lenalidomide + dexamethasone — MAIA trial) — DRd increasingly preferred
Maintenance: lenalidomide
Relapsed/refractory: carfilzomib, pomalidomide, elotuzumab, isatuximab, selinexor, belantamab mafodotin, CAR-T (idecabtagene vicleucel, ciltacabtagene autoleucel), bispecific antibodies (teclistamab)
Bone: bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid monthly) or denosumab; dental exam before starting (ONJ risk)
Renal: avoid NSAIDs, contrast; hydration
Infection: pneumococcal/influenza vaccines; consider acyclovir (VZV prophylaxis with bortezomib); IVIG if recurrent infections
VTE prophylaxis: aspirin (standard risk) or LMWH/warfarin (high risk) with lenalidomide-based regimens
Hypercalcemia: IV fluids + zoledronic acid + calcitonin ± steroids

rows: Monitor (1% per year progression to myeloma)
Monitor closely (higher progression risk); consider early treatment in high-risk

blocks.text: Myeloma causes LYTIC lesions (osteoclast activation, osteoblast suppression) → bone scan is NEGATIVE (no uptake — use skeletal survey or CT instead)
Rouleaux formation on peripheral smear (stacked coins — due to M protein)
ESR markedly elevated (M protein effect)
Bence Jones proteinuria: light chains in urine (not detected by standard dipstick — need sulfosalicylic acid test or UPEP)
Myeloma kidney: light chain cast nephropathy — most common renal lesion
MGUS → 1% per year risk of progression to myeloma
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the CRAB criteria? (Calcium, Renal, Anemia, Bone)
Why is bone scan negative in myeloma? (Lytic lesions — no osteoblastic activity for uptake)
What is the standard induction regimen? (VRd — bortezomib, lenalidomide, dexamethasone)
What is MGUS and its progression risk? (Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance; 1%/year to myeloma)
What is Bence Jones protein? (Monoclonal light chains in urine)
Systemic activation of coagulation leading to widespread microvascular thrombosis AND consumption of clotting factors/platelets → simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding.
Sepsis (most common cause overall)
Obstetric: placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, eclampsia, HELLP, retained dead fetus
Malignancy: APL (M3), mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, gastric)
Trauma/surgery (massive tissue injury)
Transfusion reactions (acute hemolytic)
Snake envenomation (relevant in Indonesia)

Tissue factor release → systemic thrombin generation → widespread fibrin deposition in microvasculature → consumption of platelets and clotting factors → bleeding + organ damage from microvascular thrombosis
Acute DIC: bleeding predominant — petechiae, purpura, oozing from IV sites, mucosal bleeding, surgical site bleeding
Chronic DIC: thrombosis predominant (Trousseau syndrome — migratory thrombophlebitis in malignancy); DVT/PE, arterial thrombosis
Organ dysfunction: renal failure, hepatic dysfunction, ARDS, altered mental status
Purpura fulminans: severe DIC with skin necrosis (meningococcemia, protein C deficiency)
Score ≥5 = compatible with overt DIC; repeat daily. Score <5 = suggestive, not affirmative; repeat in 1–2 days.
↓ Platelets (consumed)
↑ PT/aPTT (clotting factors consumed)
↓ Fibrinogen (consumed — late finding; may be normal early as acute phase reactant)
↑ D-dimer / FDP (fibrin degradation — most sensitive)
Schistocytes on peripheral smear (MAHA component)
↑ LDH, ↓ haptoglobin (hemolysis)
TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE (most important — DIC will not resolve without this)
Supportive replacement therapy (if actively bleeding or high bleeding risk):
Platelets: transfuse if <10,000 (or <20,000 if bleeding, or <50,000 if invasive procedure)
FFP: if PT/aPTT prolonged and bleeding (15–20 mL/kg)
Cryoprecipitate: if fibrinogen <100–150 mg/dL (each unit raises fibrinogen ~5–10 mg/dL; give 10 units)
pRBC: for anemia
Heparin: controversial; consider in chronic DIC with thrombosis predominant (e.g., Trousseau syndrome); generally AVOID in acute DIC with active bleeding
Antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid): generally CONTRAINDICATED in DIC (risk of widespread thrombosis) — exception: life-threatening bleeding with hyperfibrinolysis (e.g., APL)
APL-associated DIC: start ATRA immediately (resolves DIC by treating underlying cause)
DIC is ALWAYS secondary — never a primary diagnosis; find and treat the cause
D-dimer is the most sensitive marker (elevated early); fibrinogen drops late
Fibrinogen is an acute phase reactant — a "normal" fibrinogen in a septic patient may actually represent consumption (should be elevated in inflammation)
Schistocytes + prolonged PT/aPTT + low fibrinogen = DIC (TTP/HUS have normal coagulation)
APL is the classic malignancy causing acute DIC — start ATRA immediately
Do NOT give tranexamic acid in DIC (worsens thrombosis)
Chronic DIC (malignancy): thrombosis predominant → consider heparin
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most important treatment for DIC? (Treat the underlying cause)
How do you differentiate DIC from TTP? (DIC: prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen; TTP: normal coagulation, ADAMTS13 <10%)
What is the most sensitive lab marker? (D-dimer)
When is heparin used in DIC? (Chronic DIC with thrombosis predominant — e.g., Trousseau syndrome)
Why is tranexamic acid contraindicated? (Risk of widespread microvascular thrombosis)
Neutropenia: ANC <500/μL (or ANC <1000 expected to decline to <500 within 48 hours)
Fever: single oral temperature ≥38.3°C (101°F) OR ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) sustained over 1 hour
ANC = WBC x (% neutrophils + % bands)
Most common cause of neutropenia: chemotherapy (nadir typically 7–14 days post-chemo)
Infections: bacterial most common (60–70%); fungal if prolonged neutropenia (>7 days)
Common organisms: gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas); gram-positives increasing (coagulase-negative Staph, S. aureus, Streptococcus — especially with central lines)
Source often unidentified (~50% — empiric treatment is critical)

rows: Burden of illness: no or mild symptoms
Solid tumor or no previous fungal infection

blocks.text: Score ≥21: low risk → consider outpatient oral antibiotics
Score <21: high risk → inpatient IV antibiotics
Within 60 minutes of presentation (treat as emergency):
Blood cultures: 2 sets (peripheral + from each central line lumen if present) — BEFORE antibiotics
Urinalysis, urine culture, chest X-ray
Additional cultures based on symptoms (sputum, stool, wound, LP if meningeal signs)
Start empiric antibiotics IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for culture results

rows: Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy: cefepime, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam
Hemodynamic instability, suspected catheter infection, skin/soft tissue infection, MRSA colonization, severe mucositis, pneumonia
Persistent fever after 4–7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics → add empiric antifungal (caspofungin, voriconazole, or liposomal amphotericin B)

blocks.text: NOT routine — only for specific indications:
Hemodynamic instability / septic shock
Suspected catheter-related infection
Skin/soft tissue infection
Known MRSA colonization
Blood culture positive for gram-positive cocci (pending identification)
Severe mucositis (fluoroquinolone prophylaxis setting)
Reassess at 48–72 hours: if cultures negative and patient stable → STOP vancomycin
Continue until ANC ≥500 AND afebrile for ≥48 hours AND clinically stable
If source identified: treat for appropriate duration for that infection
If ANC remains <500 but afebrile and stable: can consider step-down to oral (controversial)
Persistent fever ≥4–7 days despite broad-spectrum antibiotics → suspect invasive fungal infection
CT chest (look for halo sign — invasive aspergillosis)
Galactomannan antigen (Aspergillus), beta-D-glucan (pan-fungal)
Empiric antifungal: caspofungin or liposomal amphotericin B or voriconazole
Voriconazole: first-line for invasive aspergillosis
Fluoroquinolone prophylaxis (levofloxacin): for high-risk patients expected to have prolonged neutropenia (>7 days)
Antifungal prophylaxis: fluconazole or posaconazole (for AML induction, HSCT)
Antiviral: acyclovir/valacyclovir (HSV prophylaxis during intensive chemo)
G-CSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim): secondary prophylaxis if prior neutropenic fever; primary prophylaxis if regimen has >20% risk of febrile neutropenia
Neutropenic fever is a medical EMERGENCY — antibiotics within 60 minutes
Do NOT wait for cultures to start antibiotics
Neutropenic patients may NOT mount typical inflammatory signs (no pus, no infiltrate on CXR, no meningeal signs) — fever may be the ONLY sign of infection
Rectal exam and rectal temperature are CONTRAINDICATED (risk of bacteremia from mucosal breach)
Avoid IM injections (hematoma risk with concurrent thrombocytopenia)
Monotherapy with anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam is standard (dual gram-negative coverage NOT routinely needed)
Vancomycin is NOT part of initial empiric regimen unless specific indications
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the definition of neutropenic fever? (ANC <500 + temp ≥38.3°C single or ≥38.0°C sustained)
What is the first-line empiric antibiotic? (Anti-pseudomonal monotherapy: cefepime, meropenem, or pip-tazo)
When do you add vancomycin? (Hemodynamic instability, catheter infection, MRSA, skin infection, severe mucositis)
When do you add antifungals? (Persistent fever after 4–7 days of antibiotics)
Why is rectal exam contraindicated? (Risk of bacteremia from mucosal breach in neutropenic patient)

rows: Fever, chills, flank pain, dark urine, hypotension, DIC
STOP transfusion immediately; aggressive IV NS; send blood bank workup (DAT, repeat type screen); monitor for DIC, renal failure
Cytokines from donor WBCs or recipient antibodies to donor WBCs
Stop transfusion; rule out hemolytic reaction; antipyretics; use leukoreduced products in future
Slow/stop transfusion; antihistamines (diphenhydramine); can resume if mild and symptoms resolve
STOP transfusion; epinephrine; supportive care; future: use washed or IgA-deficient products
Donor antibodies to recipient WBC antigens → non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema
Acute dyspnea, hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates, NO volume overload
Stop transfusion; supportive (O2, ventilation); NO diuretics (not cardiogenic); usually resolves 48–96 hours
Mild fever, falling Hb, jaundice, positive DAT
Fever, rash, diarrhea, liver dysfunction, pancytopenia
Usually fatal; prevention: irradiated blood products for at-risk patients
Clinical judgment (do not wait for lab)

blocks.text: Definition: ≥10 units pRBC in 24 hours, or ≥4 units in 1 hour with ongoing need
Ratio: 1:1:1 (pRBC : FFP : platelets) — PROPPR trial
Monitor: calcium (citrate toxicity → hypocalcemia), potassium (hyperkalemia from stored blood), temperature (hypothermia), coagulation
Give calcium gluconate empirically during massive transfusion
Tranexamic acid: within 3 hours of trauma (CRASH-2 trial)
Leukoreduced: prevents FNHTR, CMV transmission, HLA alloimmunization; standard in most blood banks
Irradiated: prevents TA-GVHD; for immunocompromised (HSCT, Hodgkin lymphoma, fludarabine, congenital immunodeficiency, intrauterine transfusion)
Washed: removes plasma proteins; for IgA-deficient patients (anaphylaxis prevention)
CMV-negative: for CMV-seronegative transplant candidates (leukoreduction is acceptable alternative)
Acute hemolytic reaction: most dangerous; ABO mismatch (usually clerical error) — STOP transfusion, aggressive hydration, monitor for DIC and renal failure
TRALI: leading cause of transfusion-related mortality; NO diuretics (not volume overload)
TACO: most common serious transfusion reaction; responds to diuretics
Febrile non-hemolytic: most common overall transfusion reaction; diagnosis of exclusion (must rule out hemolytic reaction first)
IgA deficiency + transfusion → anaphylaxis (use washed products)
Citrate toxicity in massive transfusion → hypocalcemia → give calcium
Stored blood: ↑ K+, ↓ 2,3-DPG (left-shifted O2 curve), ↓ pH
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Acute hemolytic: ABO mismatch, intravascular hemolysis, DIC — STOP transfusion + NS
FNHTR: most common reaction; fever/chills without hemolysis
TRALI: non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, hypotension, NO diuretics
TACO: volume overload, hypertension, elevated BNP, give diuretics
Anaphylactic: IgA deficiency; use washed products
Delayed hemolytic: 2–10 days, IgG, extravascular, usually mild
TA-GVHD: prevent with irradiated products
Restrictive threshold: Hb 7 (general), 8 (cardiac)
Massive transfusion: 1:1:1 ratio (PROPPR); give calcium
Tranexamic acid: within 3 hours of trauma (CRASH-2)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
How do you differentiate TRALI from TACO? (TRALI: hypotension, normal BNP, no diuretics; TACO: hypertension, elevated BNP, give diuretics)
What is the most common transfusion reaction? (Febrile non-hemolytic)
What causes anaphylaxis during transfusion? (IgA deficiency — anti-IgA antibodies)
What is the transfusion threshold for stable patients? (Hb 7 — TRICC trial)
What is the massive transfusion ratio? (1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets — PROPPR)

parts.searchText: part v: hematology oncology part v: hematology oncology topic 35: iron deficiency anemia (ida) topic 35 · iron deficiency anemia (ida) 1. definition 1. definition microcytic hypochromic anemia due to insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis. most common anemia worldwide. 2. etiology 2. etiology blood loss: menstruation (most common in premenopausal women), gi bleeding (most common in men and postmenopausal women — must rule out malignancy) decreased intake: poor diet, vegetarian decreased absorption: celiac disease, gastrectomy, ppi use, h. pylori increased demand: pregnancy, growth hookworm infection (ancylostoma, necator — common in indonesia/tropical regions) 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation general anemia symptoms: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia specific to ida: pica (craving ice = pagophagia, dirt), koilonychia (spoon nails), glossitis, angular cheilitis, plummer-vinson syndrome (esophageal web + ida + dysphagia) 4. diagnostic workup — iron studies 4. diagnostic workup — iron studies parameter ida anemia of chronic disease combined ida + acd serum iron ↓ ↓ ↓ ferritin ↓ (<30 ng/ml — most sensitive/specific single test) ↑ or normal (acute phase reactant) low-normal (30–100) tibc ↑ ↓ variable transferrin saturation ↓ (<20%) ↓ ↓ soluble transferrin receptor ↑ normal ↑ rdw ↑ (elevated) normal ↑ peripheral smear: microcytic hypochromic, target cells, pencil cells (elliptocytes) reticulocyte count: low (inadequate production) ferritin <30 = diagnostic of ida; ferritin <100 in setting of inflammation still suggests ida 5. management 5. management identify and treat underlying cause (gi workup in men and postmenopausal women — colonoscopy + egd) oral iron: ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) tid on empty stomach with vitamin c (enhances absorption) avoid with ppis, calcium, tea, coffee (decrease absorption) side effects: constipation, nausea, black stools monitor: reticulocyte count peaks at 7–10 days; hb should rise 1–2 g/dl in 2–4 weeks continue for 3–6 months after hb normalizes (to replete stores) iv iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose, ferumoxytol): indications — oral intolerance, malabsorption, ckd on esa, severe anemia, ongoing losses exceeding oral replacement transfusion: only if hemodynamically unstable or hb <7 (or <8 with cardiac disease) 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls ferritin is the first lab to drop in iron deficiency (even before anemia develops) ferritin is an acute phase reactant — can be falsely normal/elevated in inflammation; use cutoff <100 in inflammatory states soluble transferrin receptor (stfr): not affected by inflammation — helps differentiate ida from acd plummer-vinson syndrome: ida + esophageal web + dysphagia (premalignant for esophageal scc) hookworm: common cause of ida in tropical regions — check stool o thrombocytosis is common in ida (reactive) top 5 pimping questions: what is the most sensitive single test for ida? (ferritin 30) what is plummer-vinson syndrome? (ida + esophageal web + dysphagia) when do reticulocytes peak after iron replacement? (7–10 days) how do you differentiate ida from acd? (ferritin, tibc, stfr) when do you give iv iron? (oral intolerance, malabsorption, ckd, severe anemia) topic 36: anemia of chronic disease (acd) topic 36 · anemia of chronic disease (acd) 1. definition 1. definition anemia associated with chronic inflammation, infection, or malignancy. second most common anemia worldwide. usually normocytic (can be microcytic if severe/prolonged). 2. pathophysiology 2. pathophysiology hepcidin (key mediator): upregulated by il-6 in inflammation → blocks ferroportin → traps iron in macrophages and enterocytes → decreased serum iron despite adequate stores decreased erythropoietin response shortened rbc survival 3. iron studies 3. iron studies serum iron: ↓; ferritin: ↑ or normal; tibc: ↓; transferrin saturation: ↓; stfr: normal 4. management 4. management treat underlying disease (most important) esa (epoetin, darbepoetin): if hb 10 and symptomatic (especially ckd, cancer on chemotherapy) iv iron: if concurrent iron deficiency (ferritin 100 or tsat 20%) transfusion: restrictive threshold (hb 7) 5. clinical pearls 5. clinical pearls hepcidin is the master regulator of iron metabolism acd is a diagnosis of exclusion — always rule out ida first combined ida + acd is common (e.g., ra patient with gi bleeding) stfr/log ferritin ratio 2 suggests ida component in mixed picture topic 37: megaloblastic anemia topic 37 · megaloblastic anemia 1. definition 1. definition macrocytic anemia (mcv 100 fl) caused by impaired dna synthesis, most commonly due to vitamin b12 or folate deficiency. 2. b12 vs folate deficiency 2. b12 vs folate deficiency feature b12 deficiency folate deficiency sources meat, dairy, eggs green leafy vegetables, legumes stores 3–5 years 3–4 months absorption terminal ileum (requires intrinsic factor) proximal jejunum causes pernicious anemia (most common in developed countries), gastrectomy, terminal ileum disease (crohn), vegan diet, metformin, ppi, fish tapeworm (diphyllobothrium latum) poor diet, alcoholism, pregnancy, methotrexate, trimethoprim, phenytoin neurological symptoms yes — subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts): paresthesias, ataxia, loss of proprioception/vibration no (or very rare) homocysteine ↑ ↑ methylmalonic acid (mma) ↑ (specific for b12) normal 3. diagnostic workup 3. diagnostic workup cbc: macrocytic anemia (mcv 100), pancytopenia possible peripheral smear: hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes — pathognomonic), macro-ovalocytes serum b12 and folate levels if b12 borderline (200–400 pg/ml): check mma and homocysteine (both elevated = b12 deficiency) anti-intrinsic factor antibodies: specific for pernicious anemia (~60% sensitivity) anti-parietal cell antibodies: sensitive but less specific 4. management 4. management b12 deficiency: im cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily x 7 days → weekly x 4 weeks → monthly indefinitely (if pernicious anemia or malabsorption) high-dose oral b12 (1000–2000 mcg/day) is effective even in pernicious anemia (1% absorbed by passive diffusion) neurological symptoms may be irreversible if treatment delayed folate deficiency: folic acid 1–5 mg/day po always rule out b12 deficiency before treating with folate alone (folate corrects anemia but not neurological damage — can mask b12 deficiency and worsen neuropathy) 5. clinical pearls 5. clinical pearls hypersegmented neutrophils = megaloblastic anemia until proven otherwise mma differentiates b12 from folate (elevated only in b12 deficiency) never give folate alone without checking b12 (masks hematologic findings while neuropathy progresses) subacute combined degeneration: posterior columns (proprioception, vibration) + lateral corticospinal tracts (spasticity, weakness) pernicious anemia: autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → no intrinsic factor → b12 malabsorption; associated with other autoimmune diseases reticulocyte crisis: reticulocyte count peaks 5–7 days after b12/folate replacement top 5 pimping questions: what is pathognomonic on peripheral smear? (hypersegmented neutrophils) how do you differentiate b12 from folate deficiency? (mma — elevated only in b12) why is it dangerous to give folate without checking b12? (masks anemia but neuropathy progresses) what is subacute combined degeneration? (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts — b12 deficiency) what is pernicious anemia? (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → loss of intrinsic factor) topic 38: hemolytic anemia topic 38 · hemolytic anemia 1. definition 1. definition premature destruction of rbcs (lifespan 120 days). classified by mechanism and location. 2. classification (continued) 2. classification (continued) category intravascular extravascular references location within blood vessels spleen/liver (reticuloendothelial system) labs ↑ free hb, ↓↓ haptoglobin, hemoglobinuria, hemosiderinuria, ↑ ldh ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↑ ldh, ↓ haptoglobin, splenomegaly examples ttp/hus, pnh, mechanical valves, dic, transfusion reactions, g6pd (acute crisis), cold aiha warm aiha, hereditary spherocytosis, sickle cell, thalassemia, hypersplenism 3. general hemolysis labs (must memorize) 3. general hemolysis labs (must memorize) ↑ reticulocyte count (compensatory — hallmark of hemolysis) ↑ ldh ↑ indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin ↓ haptoglobin (binds free hemoglobin — consumed) ↑ urobilinogen peripheral smear: varies by cause (spherocytes, schistocytes, sickle cells, target cells, bite cells) 4. intrinsic vs extrinsic causes 4. intrinsic vs extrinsic causes intrinsic (rbc defect) extrinsic (external to rbc) references membrane: hereditary spherocytosis, hereditary elliptocytosis, pnh immune: warm aiha (igg), cold aiha (igm), drug-induced enzyme: g6pd deficiency, pyruvate kinase deficiency mechanical: ttp/hus, dic, mechanical valves, hellp, march hemoglobinuria hemoglobin: sickle cell, thalassemia infection: malaria, clostridium, babesia hypersplenism, toxins (lead, copper — wilson disease) 5. key specific conditions 5. key specific conditions warm aiha (igg-mediated) most common aiha; igg antibodies → extravascular hemolysis (spleen) causes: idiopathic, sle, cll, drugs (methyldopa, penicillin, cephalosporins) smear: spherocytes diagnosis: positive direct coombs test (dat) — igg ± c3 treatment: corticosteroids (first-line) → rituximab → splenectomy cold aiha (igm-mediated / cold agglutinin disease) igm antibodies activate complement → intravascular hemolysis at cold temperatures causes: mycoplasma pneumoniae, ebv (infectious mononucleosis), lymphoproliferative disorders smear: rbc agglutination diagnosis: dat positive for c3 only; cold agglutinin titer elevated treatment: avoid cold exposure; rituximab (steroids less effective); do not give warm aiha treatments (splenectomy ineffective — intravascular hemolysis) g6pd deficiency x-linked recessive; most common enzyme deficiency worldwide episodic hemolysis triggered by: oxidative stress — infections (most common trigger), fava beans (favism), drugs (primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin) smear: bite cells, heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin — seen with supravital stain) diagnosis: g6pd enzyme level — check after acute episode resolves (levels falsely normal during crisis due to reticulocytosis — young rbcs have higher g6pd) treatment: avoid triggers; supportive; transfuse if severe hereditary spherocytosis autosomal dominant; spectrin/ankyrin deficiency → spherocytes → trapped in spleen smear: spherocytes; ↑ mchc (most common cause of elevated mchc) diagnosis: osmotic fragility test (increased) or eosin-5-maleimide (ema) binding test complications: aplastic crisis (parvovirus b19), pigment gallstones treatment: folate supplementation; splenectomy if severe (curative — wait until age >5; vaccinate against encapsulated organisms: pneumococcus, meningococcus, h. influenzae) paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (pnh) acquired mutation in piga gene → loss of gpi-anchored proteins (cd55, cd59) → complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis triad: hemolytic anemia + pancytopenia + thrombosis (especially hepatic/budd-chiari, cerebral) diagnosis: flow cytometry for cd55/cd59 (gold standard); ham test (historical) treatment: eculizumab (anti-c5 monoclonal antibody — first-line); ravulizumab (longer-acting); meningococcal vaccine before starting (risk of neisseria meningitidis) microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (maha) schistocytes on smear (fragmented rbcs) — hallmark causes: ttp, hus, dic, hellp, malignant hypertension, mechanical valves ttp: pentad — maha, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, fever, neurological symptoms; adamts13 <10%; treatment: plasma exchange (emergent) + steroids + caplacizumab; do not give platelets (worsens thrombosis) hus: maha + thrombocytopenia + aki; typical (ehec/shiga toxin — children, post-diarrheal) vs atypical (complement-mediated — treat with eculizumab) 6. diagnostic approach to hemolytic anemia 6. diagnostic approach to hemolytic anemia confirm hemolysis: ↑ retics, ↑ ldh, ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↓ haptoglobin peripheral smear: spherocytes (aiha, hs), schistocytes (maha), bite cells (g6pd), sickle cells, target cells direct coombs test (dat): positive → immune-mediated (warm vs cold) if dat negative → intrinsic rbc defect (g6pd, hs, pnh, hemoglobinopathy) or non-immune extrinsic (maha, infection) 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls coombs test: direct = "d"etect antibodies on rbc surface; indirect = antibodies in serum schistocytes = maha until proven otherwise spherocytes + positive dat = aiha; spherocytes + negative dat = hereditary spherocytosis g6pd levels: check after crisis (falsely normal during acute episode) ttp: never transfuse platelets (fuels thrombosis) pnh: only hemolytic anemia with pancytopenia + thrombosis parvovirus b19 causes aplastic crisis in any chronic hemolytic anemia (transient red cell aplasia) top 10 testable facts: hemolysis labs: ↑ retics, ↑ ldh, ↑ indirect bili, ↓ haptoglobin warm aiha: igg, extravascular, spherocytes, dat positive (igg ± c3) cold aiha: igm, intravascular, dat positive (c3 only), mycoplasma/ebv g6pd: x-linked, bite cells, heinz bodies, check levels after crisis hereditary spherocytosis: ad, spherocytes, ↑ mchc, osmotic fragility, splenectomy curative pnh: piga mutation, cd55/cd59 loss, eculizumab, meningococcal vaccine ttp: adamts13 <10%, plasma exchange, do not give platelets schistocytes = maha parvovirus b19 → aplastic crisis in chronic hemolysis dat positive = immune; dat negative = non-immune top 5 pimping questions: what is the most important initial test in hemolytic anemia? (direct coombs test — differentiates immune vs non-immune) why check g6pd levels after the crisis? (reticulocytes have higher g6pd → falsely normal during crisis) why not give platelets in ttp? (fuels microvascular thrombosis) what is the triad of pnh? (hemolytic anemia + pancytopenia + thrombosis) what smear finding is pathognomonic for maha? (schistocytes) topic 39: aplastic anemia topic 39 · aplastic anemia 1. definition 1. definition pancytopenia with hypocellular bone marrow (<25% cellularity) in the absence of abnormal infiltrate or fibrosis. 2. etiology 2. etiology idiopathic (most common — ~70%, likely autoimmune t-cell mediated) drugs: chloramphenicol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, sulfonamides, nsaids, chemotherapy infections: hepatitis (seronegative hepatitis → aplastic anemia), ebv, cmv, parvovirus b19, hiv toxins: benzene, radiation inherited: fanconi anemia (ar; short stature, café-au-lait spots, thumb/radial anomalies; chromosome breakage test) 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation anemia: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea thrombocytopenia: petechiae, purpura, bleeding neutropenia: recurrent infections no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy (distinguishes from leukemia/lymphoma) 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup cbc: pancytopenia with low reticulocyte count peripheral smear: normocytic normochromic anemia, no abnormal cells bone marrow biopsy (definitive): hypocellular (<25%), fat spaces replacing hematopoietic tissue, no fibrosis or infiltrate rule out: pnh (flow cytometry for cd55/cd59 — pnh and aplastic anemia overlap), mds, leukemia 5. severity classification (camitta criteria) 5. severity classification (camitta criteria) severity criteria (≥2 of 3 peripheral blood criteria + hypocellular marrow) references severe (saa) anc <500, platelets <20,000, reticulocytes <1% (or absolute retic <60,000) very severe (vsaa) same as saa but anc <200 non-severe does not meet saa criteria 6. management 6. management age <40 with matched sibling donor: allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (hsct) — curative, first-line age >40 or no matched donor: immunosuppressive therapy (ist) — horse atg (anti-thymocyte globulin) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag (race trial — addition of eltrombopag improves response) supportive: transfusions (irradiated, leukoreduced — avoid cmv+ products if transplant candidate), antibiotics for infections, growth factors (g-csf controversial) eltrombopag (tpo receptor agonist): added to first-line ist; also used in refractory cases avoid unnecessary transfusions in transplant candidates (alloimmunization risk) 7. complications 7. complications infections (leading cause of death) bleeding transformation to mds/aml (~15% over 10 years) pnh clone evolution (~10–15%) iron overload from chronic transfusions 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls aplastic anemia vs mds: aplastic = hypocellular marrow, no dysplasia; mds = usually hypercellular, dysplastic changes, may have blasts fanconi anemia: suspect in young patient with aplastic anemia + congenital anomalies; confirm with chromosome breakage test (deb/mmc) pnh and aplastic anemia are related — always check pnh flow cytometry seronegative hepatitis → aplastic anemia: classic association (hepatitis-associated aplastic anemia) do not give live vaccines to immunosuppressed patients top 5 pimping questions: what is the definitive diagnostic test? (bone marrow biopsy — hypocellular <25%) what is the first-line treatment for young patients with matched sibling? (allogeneic hsct) what inherited condition causes aplastic anemia? (fanconi anemia — chromosome breakage test) what related condition must you screen for? (pnh — flow cytometry cd55/cd59) what is the camitta criteria for severe aplastic anemia? (anc <500, platelets <20k, retics <1%) topic 40: acute myeloid leukemia (aml) topic 40 · acute myeloid leukemia (aml) 1. definition 1. definition clonal proliferation of myeloid precursors (blasts) in bone marrow. defined by ≥20% blasts in marrow or blood (who), or by specific genetic abnormalities regardless of blast count. 2. risk factors 2. risk factors age (>65 most common), prior chemotherapy/radiation (therapy-related aml), myelodysplastic syndrome, benzene exposure, down syndrome (amkl — m7), fanconi anemia 3. classification — who/icc 2022 3. classification — who/icc 2022 based on genetic abnormalities (most important for prognosis and treatment): favorable: t(8;21) — aml with runx1-runx1t1; inv(16)/t(16;16) — cbfb-myh11; apl t(15;17) — pml-rara; npm1 mutated intermediate: normal karyotype, flt3-itd adverse: complex karyotype, monosomy 5/7, tp53 mutation, flt3-itd (high ratio) 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation bone marrow failure: anemia (fatigue, pallor), thrombocytopenia (bleeding, petechiae), neutropenia (infections) leukocytosis with circulating blasts (but can present with leukopenia) tissue infiltration: gingival hyperplasia (m4/m5 — monocytic), skin (leukemia cutis), cns dic: especially in apl (m3) — medical emergency leukostasis: wbc >100,000 → respiratory distress, neurological symptoms → emergency leukapheresis + hydroxyurea tumor lysis syndrome risk with treatment initiation 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup cbc with differential: blasts on peripheral smear peripheral smear: auer rods (pathognomonic for aml — eosinophilic needle-like inclusions); "faggot cells" (bundles of auer rods) in apl bone marrow biopsy: ≥20% blasts; flow cytometry (cd13, cd33, cd34, mpo+) cytogenetics and molecular testing: essential for classification and prognosis (flt3, npm1, cebpa, tp53, idh1/2) coagulation studies: dic screen (especially if apl suspected) 6. acute promyelocytic leukemia (apl) — m3 — emergency 6. acute promyelocytic leukemia (apl) — m3 — emergency t(15;17) → pml-rara fusion presents with dic (most dangerous complication) treatment: all-trans retinoic acid (atra) + arsenic trioxide (ato) — curative in most cases without chemotherapy (lo-coco trial) start atra immediately if apl suspected (do not wait for confirmation) differentiation syndrome (formerly atra syndrome): fever, dyspnea, pulmonary infiltrates, weight gain, pleural/pericardial effusion → treat with dexamethasone 10 mg bid apl is the most curable aml subtype (~90% cure rate) 7. general aml management 7. general aml management induction: "7+3" — cytarabine (7 days) + daunorubicin (3 days); goal: complete remission (cr) consolidation: high-dose cytarabine (hidac) for favorable risk; allogeneic hsct for intermediate/adverse risk targeted therapies: flt3 inhibitors: midostaurin (with induction — ratify trial), gilteritinib (relapsed/refractory) idh1/2 inhibitors: ivosidenib (idh1), enasidenib (idh2) venetoclax + azacitidine: for unfit/elderly patients (viale-a trial — standard of care) gemtuzumab ozogamicin: anti-cd33 adc for favorable-risk aml 8. supportive care 8. supportive care tumor lysis syndrome prophylaxis: allopurinol or rasburicase + aggressive hydration transfusions: platelets <10,000 (or <20,000 if febrile/bleeding); prbc for hb <7 neutropenic fever: see topic 45 leukostasis (wbc >100k): hydroxyurea + leukapheresis; avoid transfusing prbc before reducing wbc (increases viscosity) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls auer rods = aml (never in all) apl + dic = start atra immediately "7+3" = standard induction venetoclax + azacitidine = standard for unfit elderly aml leukostasis: do not transfuse rbcs before reducing wbc count down syndrome children: increased risk of amkl (m7) but also have better prognosis top 5 pimping questions: what is pathognomonic for aml on smear? (auer rods) what is the emergency treatment for apl? (atra — start immediately; do not wait for confirmation) what is the standard induction regimen? ("7+3" — cytarabine + daunorubicin) what is differentiation syndrome? (atra/ato complication: fever, dyspnea, effusions → dexamethasone) what is the treatment for unfit elderly aml? (venetoclax + azacitidine — viale-a) topic 41: acute lymphoblastic leukemia (all) topic 41 · acute lymphoblastic leukemia (all) 1. definition 1. definition clonal proliferation of lymphoid precursors (lymphoblasts). most common cancer in children (peak 2–5 years). bimodal: children and adults >60. 2. classification 2. classification b-cell all (~85%): cd10 (calla)+, cd19+, cd22+; t(12;21) tel-aml1 in children (favorable); t(9;22) philadelphia chromosome (bcr-abl) in adults (unfavorable — ~25% of adult all) t-cell all (~15%): cd3+, cd7+; thymic mass common; male predominance 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation bone marrow failure: anemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly (more common than aml) cns involvement: headache, cranial nerve palsies (more common in all than aml) mediastinal mass: t-cell all (anterior mediastinum — svc syndrome risk) testicular involvement (sanctuary site) bone pain (children — may mimic jia) 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup peripheral smear: lymphoblasts (no auer rods — distinguishes from aml) bone marrow: ≥20% lymphoblasts; flow cytometry (b vs t markers) cytogenetics: philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) — critical for treatment decisions tdt positive (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase — marker of immature lymphoid cells; positive in all, negative in mature lymphomas) lp: cns staging (all patients) 5. all vs aml 5. all vs aml feature all aml references age children (peak 2–5); adults >60 adults (>65) auer rods no yes (pathognomonic) tdt positive negative mpo negative positive lymphadenopathy/hsm common less common cns involvement more common less common mediastinal mass t-all rare pas stain positive (b-all) negative 6. management 6. management induction → consolidation → maintenance (2–3 years — unlike aml) phases: induction (vincristine, prednisone, daunorubicin, asparaginase, ± cyclophosphamide); consolidation (high-dose methotrexate, cytarabine); maintenance (6-mp + methotrexate daily/weekly x 2–3 years) cns prophylaxis: intrathecal methotrexate ± cytarabine (all has high cns relapse risk) philadelphia chromosome positive (ph+ all): add tki (imatinib, dasatinib) — dramatically improves outcomes relapsed/refractory: blinatumomab (bispecific t-cell engager, anti-cd19/cd3), inotuzumab ozogamicin (anti-cd22 adc), car-t cell therapy (tisagenlecleucel — anti-cd19) 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls all has maintenance phase (2–3 years); aml does not tdt positive = all; tdt negative = aml or mature lymphoma philadelphia chromosome: poor prognosis in all but treatable with tki cns prophylaxis is mandatory in all tumor lysis syndrome: high risk at treatment initiation (high tumor burden) asparaginase: unique to all treatment; complications — pancreatitis, thrombosis, hepatotoxicity top 5 pimping questions: what marker differentiates all from aml? (tdt — positive in all, negative in aml) what is the philadelphia chromosome? (t(9;22) bcr-abl — poor prognosis in all; add tki) why does all need cns prophylaxis? (high risk of cns relapse) what is unique about all maintenance? (2–3 years of 6-mp + methotrexate — aml has no maintenance) what is car-t therapy for all? (tisagenlecleucel — anti-cd19 chimeric antigen receptor t cells) topic 42: multiple myeloma topic 42 · multiple myeloma 1. definition 1. definition clonal plasma cell neoplasm producing monoclonal immunoglobulin (m protein). median age 65–70. 2. diagnostic criteria — imwg 2014 (updated) 2. diagnostic criteria — imwg 2014 (updated) clonal bone marrow plasma cells ≥10% and one or more myeloma-defining events: crab criteria (end-organ damage): c — calcium elevated (>11 mg/dl or >1 mg/dl above uln) r — renal insufficiency (cr >2 mg/dl or crcl <40) a — anemia (hb <10 g/dl or >2 g/dl below normal) b — bone lesions (lytic lesions on skeletal survey, ct, or pet-ct) or biomarkers of malignancy (slim criteria): s — sixty percent (≥60%) clonal plasma cells in marrow li — light chain ratio ≥100 (involved/uninvolved) m — mri with >1 focal lesion ≥5 mm 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation bone pain (most common presenting symptom — especially back); pathological fractures; lytic lesions (punched-out on x-ray) anemia (fatigue) renal failure: light chain cast nephropathy (myeloma kidney), hypercalcemia, amyloidosis hypercalcemia: confusion, constipation, polyuria recurrent infections (immunoparesis — decreased normal immunoglobulins) hyperviscosity syndrome (rare, more common in waldenström): blurred vision, headache, bleeding amyloidosis (al type): nephrotic syndrome, cardiomyopathy, macroglossia, neuropathy 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup spep + upep with immunofixation: m spike (monoclonal protein) serum free light chains (kappa/lambda ratio) bone marrow biopsy: clonal plasma cells ≥10% cbc, bmp (calcium, creatinine), albumin, beta2-microglobulin, ldh skeletal survey (x-ray) or low-dose whole-body ct or pet-ct: lytic lesions 24-hour urine: bence jones protein (light chains) cytogenetics/fish: t(4;14), t(14;16), del(17p) — high risk 5. staging — revised international staging system (r-iss) 5. staging — revised international staging system (r-iss) stage criteria median survival references i beta2m <3.5 + albumin ≥3.5 + standard-risk cytogenetics + normal ldh ~82 months r-iss ii not stage i or iii ~62 months iii beta2m ≥5.5 + high-risk cytogenetics [t(4;14), t(14;16), del(17p)] or elevated ldh ~40 months 6. management 6. management transplant-eligible (age <65–70, fit): induction: vrd — bortezomib (v) + lenalidomide (r) + dexamethasone (d) x 4–6 cycles (swog s0777 trial) stem cell collection autologous hsct (melphalan conditioning) maintenance: lenalidomide indefinitely (calgb 100104 — improves pfs and os) transplant-ineligible: vrd or drd (daratumumab + lenalidomide + dexamethasone — maia trial) — drd increasingly preferred maintenance: lenalidomide relapsed/refractory: carfilzomib, pomalidomide, elotuzumab, isatuximab, selinexor, belantamab mafodotin, car-t (idecabtagene vicleucel, ciltacabtagene autoleucel), bispecific antibodies (teclistamab) 7. supportive care 7. supportive care bone: bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid monthly) or denosumab; dental exam before starting (onj risk) renal: avoid nsaids, contrast; hydration infection: pneumococcal/influenza vaccines; consider acyclovir (vzv prophylaxis with bortezomib); ivig if recurrent infections vte prophylaxis: aspirin (standard risk) or lmwh/warfarin (high risk) with lenalidomide-based regimens hypercalcemia: iv fluids + zoledronic acid + calcitonin ± steroids 8. important distinctions 8. important distinctions condition plasma cells m protein crab/slim action references mgus <10% <3 g/dl absent monitor (1% per year progression to myeloma) smoldering myeloma ≥10% ≥3 g/dl absent monitor closely (higher progression risk); consider early treatment in high-risk multiple myeloma ≥10% any present (crab or slim) treat 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls myeloma causes lytic lesions (osteoclast activation, osteoblast suppression) → bone scan is negative (no uptake — use skeletal survey or ct instead) rouleaux formation on peripheral smear (stacked coins — due to m protein) esr markedly elevated (m protein effect) bence jones proteinuria: light chains in urine (not detected by standard dipstick — need sulfosalicylic acid test or upep) myeloma kidney: light chain cast nephropathy — most common renal lesion mgus → 1% per year risk of progression to myeloma top 5 pimping questions: what is the crab criteria? (calcium, renal, anemia, bone) why is bone scan negative in myeloma? (lytic lesions — no osteoblastic activity for uptake) what is the standard induction regimen? (vrd — bortezomib, lenalidomide, dexamethasone) what is mgus and its progression risk? (monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance; 1%/year to myeloma) what is bence jones protein? (monoclonal light chains in urine) topic 43: disseminated intravascular coagulation (dic) topic 43 · disseminated intravascular coagulation (dic) 1. definition 1. definition systemic activation of coagulation leading to widespread microvascular thrombosis and consumption of clotting factors/platelets → simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding. 2. etiology 2. etiology sepsis (most common cause overall) obstetric: placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, eclampsia, hellp, retained dead fetus malignancy: apl (m3), mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, gastric) trauma/surgery (massive tissue injury) transfusion reactions (acute hemolytic) snake envenomation (relevant in indonesia) 3. pathophysiology 3. pathophysiology tissue factor release → systemic thrombin generation → widespread fibrin deposition in microvasculature → consumption of platelets and clotting factors → bleeding + organ damage from microvascular thrombosis 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation acute dic: bleeding predominant — petechiae, purpura, oozing from iv sites, mucosal bleeding, surgical site bleeding chronic dic: thrombosis predominant (trousseau syndrome — migratory thrombophlebitis in malignancy); dvt/pe, arterial thrombosis organ dysfunction: renal failure, hepatic dysfunction, ards, altered mental status purpura fulminans: severe dic with skin necrosis (meningococcemia, protein c deficiency) 5. diagnostic workup — isth dic score 5. diagnostic workup — isth dic score parameter 0 points 1 point 2 points 3 points references platelet count >100k 50–100k <50k — isth fibrin markers (d-dimer/fdp) no increase moderate increase strong increase — prolonged pt <3 sec 3–6 sec >6 sec — fibrinogen >100 mg/dl — <100 mg/dl — score ≥5 = compatible with overt dic; repeat daily. score <5 = suggestive, not affirmative; repeat in 1–2 days. 6. lab pattern (must memorize) 6. lab pattern (must memorize) ↓ platelets (consumed) ↑ pt/aptt (clotting factors consumed) ↓ fibrinogen (consumed — late finding; may be normal early as acute phase reactant) ↑ d-dimer / fdp (fibrin degradation — most sensitive) schistocytes on peripheral smear (maha component) ↑ ldh, ↓ haptoglobin (hemolysis) 7. management 7. management treat the underlying cause (most important — dic will not resolve without this) supportive replacement therapy (if actively bleeding or high bleeding risk): platelets: transfuse if <10,000 (or <20,000 if bleeding, or <50,000 if invasive procedure) ffp: if pt/aptt prolonged and bleeding (15–20 ml/kg) cryoprecipitate: if fibrinogen <100–150 mg/dl (each unit raises fibrinogen ~5–10 mg/dl; give 10 units) prbc: for anemia heparin: controversial; consider in chronic dic with thrombosis predominant (e.g., trousseau syndrome); generally avoid in acute dic with active bleeding antifibrinolytics (tranexamic acid): generally contraindicated in dic (risk of widespread thrombosis) — exception: life-threatening bleeding with hyperfibrinolysis (e.g., apl) apl-associated dic: start atra immediately (resolves dic by treating underlying cause) 8. dic vs ttp vs hus 8. dic vs ttp vs hus feature dic ttp hus references pt/aptt prolonged normal normal fibrinogen low normal normal d-dimer elevated normal/mild ↑ normal/mild ↑ schistocytes present present present platelets low low low key differentiator coagulation cascade activated adamts13 <10% shiga toxin / complement treatment treat cause + replace factors plasma exchange supportive / eculizumab (atypical) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls dic is always secondary — never a primary diagnosis; find and treat the cause d-dimer is the most sensitive marker (elevated early); fibrinogen drops late fibrinogen is an acute phase reactant — a "normal" fibrinogen in a septic patient may actually represent consumption (should be elevated in inflammation) schistocytes + prolonged pt/aptt + low fibrinogen = dic (ttp/hus have normal coagulation) apl is the classic malignancy causing acute dic — start atra immediately do not give tranexamic acid in dic (worsens thrombosis) chronic dic (malignancy): thrombosis predominant → consider heparin top 5 pimping questions: what is the most important treatment for dic? (treat the underlying cause) how do you differentiate dic from ttp? (dic: prolonged pt/aptt, low fibrinogen; ttp: normal coagulation, adamts13 <10%) what is the most sensitive lab marker? (d-dimer) when is heparin used in dic? (chronic dic with thrombosis predominant — e.g., trousseau syndrome) why is tranexamic acid contraindicated? (risk of widespread microvascular thrombosis) topic 44: neutropenic fever topic 44 · neutropenic fever 1. definition 1. definition neutropenia: anc <500/μl (or anc <1000 expected to decline to <500 within 48 hours) fever: single oral temperature ≥38.3°c (101°f) or ≥38.0°c (100.4°f) sustained over 1 hour anc = wbc x (% neutrophils + % bands) 2. etiology 2. etiology most common cause of neutropenia: chemotherapy (nadir typically 7–14 days post-chemo) infections: bacterial most common (60–70%); fungal if prolonged neutropenia (>7 days) common organisms: gram-negatives (e. coli, klebsiella, pseudomonas); gram-positives increasing (coagulase-negative staph, s. aureus, streptococcus — especially with central lines) source often unidentified (~50% — empiric treatment is critical) 3. risk stratification — mascc score 3. risk stratification — mascc score characteristic score references burden of illness: no or mild symptoms 5 mascc burden of illness: moderate symptoms 3 no hypotension (sbp ≥90) 5 no copd 4 solid tumor or no previous fungal infection 4 no dehydration 3 outpatient at onset of fever 3 age <60 2 score ≥21: low risk → consider outpatient oral antibiotics score <21: high risk → inpatient iv antibiotics 4. emergency management — idsa guidelines 4. emergency management — idsa guidelines within 60 minutes of presentation (treat as emergency): blood cultures: 2 sets (peripheral + from each central line lumen if present) — before antibiotics urinalysis, urine culture, chest x-ray additional cultures based on symptoms (sputum, stool, wound, lp if meningeal signs) start empiric antibiotics immediately — do not wait for culture results 5. empiric antibiotic selection 5. empiric antibiotic selection risk regimen references high risk (inpatient) anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy: cefepime, meropenem, or piperacillin-tazobactam idsa 2010 low risk (outpatient) ciprofloxacin + amoxicillin-clavulanate po add vancomycin if: hemodynamic instability, suspected catheter infection, skin/soft tissue infection, mrsa colonization, severe mucositis, pneumonia add antifungal if: persistent fever after 4–7 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics → add empiric antifungal (caspofungin, voriconazole, or liposomal amphotericin b) 6. when to add vancomycin 6. when to add vancomycin not routine — only for specific indications: hemodynamic instability / septic shock suspected catheter-related infection skin/soft tissue infection known mrsa colonization blood culture positive for gram-positive cocci (pending identification) severe mucositis (fluoroquinolone prophylaxis setting) reassess at 48–72 hours: if cultures negative and patient stable → stop vancomycin 7. duration of antibiotics 7. duration of antibiotics continue until anc ≥500 and afebrile for ≥48 hours and clinically stable if source identified: treat for appropriate duration for that infection if anc remains <500 but afebrile and stable: can consider step-down to oral (controversial) 8. antifungal escalation 8. antifungal escalation persistent fever ≥4–7 days despite broad-spectrum antibiotics → suspect invasive fungal infection ct chest (look for halo sign — invasive aspergillosis) galactomannan antigen (aspergillus), beta-d-glucan (pan-fungal) empiric antifungal: caspofungin or liposomal amphotericin b or voriconazole voriconazole: first-line for invasive aspergillosis 9. prophylaxis 9. prophylaxis fluoroquinolone prophylaxis (levofloxacin): for high-risk patients expected to have prolonged neutropenia (>7 days) antifungal prophylaxis: fluconazole or posaconazole (for aml induction, hsct) antiviral: acyclovir/valacyclovir (hsv prophylaxis during intensive chemo) g-csf (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim): secondary prophylaxis if prior neutropenic fever; primary prophylaxis if regimen has >20% risk of febrile neutropenia 10. clinical pearls 10. clinical pearls neutropenic fever is a medical emergency — antibiotics within 60 minutes do not wait for cultures to start antibiotics neutropenic patients may not mount typical inflammatory signs (no pus, no infiltrate on cxr, no meningeal signs) — fever may be the only sign of infection rectal exam and rectal temperature are contraindicated (risk of bacteremia from mucosal breach) avoid im injections (hematoma risk with concurrent thrombocytopenia) monotherapy with anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam is standard (dual gram-negative coverage not routinely needed) vancomycin is not part of initial empiric regimen unless specific indications top 5 pimping questions: what is the definition of neutropenic fever? (anc <500 + temp ≥38.3°c single or ≥38.0°c sustained) what is the first-line empiric antibiotic? (anti-pseudomonal monotherapy: cefepime, meropenem, or pip-tazo) when do you add vancomycin? (hemodynamic instability, catheter infection, mrsa, skin infection, severe mucositis) when do you add antifungals? (persistent fever after 4–7 days of antibiotics) why is rectal exam contraindicated? (risk of bacteremia from mucosal breach in neutropenic patient) topic 45: blood transfusion reactions topic 45 · blood transfusion reactions 1. classification 1. classification reaction timing mechanism presentation management references acute hemolytic minutes to hours abo incompatibility (igm) → intravascular hemolysis fever, chills, flank pain, dark urine, hypotension, dic stop transfusion immediately; aggressive iv ns; send blood bank workup (dat, repeat type screen); monitor for dic, renal failure febrile non-hemolytic (fnhtr) 1–6 hours cytokines from donor wbcs or recipient antibodies to donor wbcs fever, chills (no hemolysis) stop transfusion; rule out hemolytic reaction; antipyretics; use leukoreduced products in future allergic (mild) minutes to hours ige to donor plasma proteins urticaria, pruritus, flushing slow/stop transfusion; antihistamines (diphenhydramine); can resume if mild and symptoms resolve anaphylactic minutes anti-iga antibodies in iga-deficient recipient hypotension, bronchospasm, angioedema, shock stop transfusion; epinephrine; supportive care; future: use washed or iga-deficient products trali within 6 hours donor antibodies to recipient wbc antigens → non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema acute dyspnea, hypoxia, bilateral infiltrates, no volume overload stop transfusion; supportive (o2, ventilation); no diuretics (not cardiogenic); usually resolves 48–96 hours taco during/shortly after volume overload dyspnea, hypertension, jvd, pulmonary edema stop/slow transfusion; diuretics (furosemide); upright positioning delayed hemolytic 2–10 days anamnestic antibody response (igg) → extravascular hemolysis mild fever, falling hb, jaundice, positive dat usually mild/self-limited; monitor; supportive transfusion-associated gvhd 1–6 weeks donor t-cells attack immunocompromised recipient fever, rash, diarrhea, liver dysfunction, pancytopenia usually fatal; prevention: irradiated blood products for at-risk patients 2. trali vs taco (commonly tested) 2. trali vs taco (commonly tested) feature trali taco references mechanism non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (capillary leak) cardiogenic pulmonary edema (volume overload) bp hypotension hypertension jvp normal/low elevated bnp normal elevated cxr bilateral infiltrates bilateral infiltrates + cardiomegaly treatment supportive; no diuretics diuretics (furosemide) fluid balance negative or neutral positive 3. transfusion thresholds (restrictive strategy) 3. transfusion thresholds (restrictive strategy) population transfuse if hb references general (hemodynamically stable) 7 g/dl tricc trial cardiac disease / acs 8 g/dl active bleeding / hemodynamic instability clinical judgment (do not wait for lab) platelets (prophylactic) 10,000 platelets (active bleeding or pre-procedure) 50,000 (or 100,000 for neurosurgery/ocular) ffp active bleeding + prolonged pt/aptt 4. massive transfusion protocol 4. massive transfusion protocol definition: ≥10 units prbc in 24 hours, or ≥4 units in 1 hour with ongoing need ratio: 1:1:1 (prbc : ffp : platelets) — proppr trial monitor: calcium (citrate toxicity → hypocalcemia), potassium (hyperkalemia from stored blood), temperature (hypothermia), coagulation give calcium gluconate empirically during massive transfusion tranexamic acid: within 3 hours of trauma (crash-2 trial) 5. special products 5. special products leukoreduced: prevents fnhtr, cmv transmission, hla alloimmunization; standard in most blood banks irradiated: prevents ta-gvhd; for immunocompromised (hsct, hodgkin lymphoma, fludarabine, congenital immunodeficiency, intrauterine transfusion) washed: removes plasma proteins; for iga-deficient patients (anaphylaxis prevention) cmv-negative: for cmv-seronegative transplant candidates (leukoreduction is acceptable alternative) 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls acute hemolytic reaction: most dangerous; abo mismatch (usually clerical error) — stop transfusion, aggressive hydration, monitor for dic and renal failure trali: leading cause of transfusion-related mortality; no diuretics (not volume overload) taco: most common serious transfusion reaction; responds to diuretics febrile non-hemolytic: most common overall transfusion reaction; diagnosis of exclusion (must rule out hemolytic reaction first) iga deficiency + transfusion → anaphylaxis (use washed products) citrate toxicity in massive transfusion → hypocalcemia → give calcium stored blood: ↑ k+, ↓ 2,3-dpg (left-shifted o2 curve), ↓ ph top 10 testable facts: acute hemolytic: abo mismatch, intravascular hemolysis, dic — stop transfusion + ns fnhtr: most common reaction; fever/chills without hemolysis trali: non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, hypotension, no diuretics taco: volume overload, hypertension, elevated bnp, give diuretics anaphylactic: iga deficiency; use washed products delayed hemolytic: 2–10 days, igg, extravascular, usually mild ta-gvhd: prevent with irradiated products restrictive threshold: hb 7 (general), 8 (cardiac) massive transfusion: 1:1:1 ratio (proppr); give calcium tranexamic acid: within 3 hours of trauma (crash-2) top 5 pimping questions: how do you differentiate trali from taco? (trali: hypotension, normal bnp, no diuretics; taco: hypertension, elevated bnp, give diuretics) what is the most common transfusion reaction? (febrile non-hemolytic) what causes anaphylaxis during transfusion? (iga deficiency — anti-iga antibodies) what is the transfusion threshold for stable patients? (hb 7 — tricc trial) what is the massive transfusion ratio? (1:1:1 prbc:ffp:platelets — proppr)

parts.title: PART VI: CARDIOLOGY

blocks.text: New onset or worsening of HF signs/symptoms requiring urgent therapy. Most common cause of hospitalization in patients 65 years.
F — Forgot medications (non-compliance)
A — Arrhythmia (especially new AF)
I — Ischemia / Infarction
L — Lifestyle (dietary indiscretion — salt/fluid)
U — Upregulation (pregnancy, hyperthyroidism)
R — Rheumatic / valvular disease
E — Embolism (pulmonary)
S — Stenosis (renal artery) / Sepsis

rows: Adjust oral meds; may not be ADHF

blocks.text: Congestion (left-sided): dyspnea, orthopnea, PND, crackles, S3 gallop, elevated JVP, peripheral edema
Congestion (right-sided): JVD, hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema
Low perfusion: cool extremities, altered mental status, narrow pulse pressure, oliguria, hypotension
BNP ≥100 pg/mL or NT-proBNP ≥300 pg/mL (rules in HF; 100/300 essentially rules out)
CXR: cardiomegaly, cephalization, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions, bat-wing pulmonary edema
ECG: arrhythmias, ischemia, LVH
Echocardiography: EF, wall motion, valvular disease, diastolic function
Labs: BMP (renal function, electrolytes), troponin (rule out ACS), CBC, LFTs, TSH
Identify precipitant
Position: sit upright; supplemental O2 if SpO2 90%

IV loop diuretics (cornerstone): furosemide IV bolus (if on chronic diuretics: give 1–2.5x home oral dose as IV); can use continuous infusion if refractory; target UOP 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr; monitor weight, I/O, electrolytes, renal function
Vasodilators (if SBP 90 and no cardiogenic shock): IV nitroglycerin (preload reduction, especially with pulmonary edema); nitroprusside (afterload reduction — use with caution, cyanide toxicity)
Diuretic resistance: increase dose; add thiazide (metolazone 30 min before furosemide — sequential nephron blockade); consider ultrafiltration
Inotropes (if cold wet / cardiogenic shock): dobutamine (beta1 agonist — first-line inotrope); milrinone (PDE3 inhibitor — vasodilation + inotropy; avoid in renal failure)
Vasopressors (if SBP 80 or cardiogenic shock): norepinephrine (preferred over dopamine — SOAP II trial)
Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP): for acute pulmonary edema with respiratory distress (reduces preload, improves oxygenation, decreases work of breathing — 3CPO trial)
Do NOT start or continue beta-blockers in acute decompensation with hemodynamic instability; can continue at reduced dose if stable; restart/uptitrate before discharge
Optimize GDMT before discharge: ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor (the "4 pillars")
Stable on oral diuretics ≥24 hours
Euvolemic or near-euvolemic
Stable vital signs, adequate renal function
GDMT initiated/optimized (all 4 pillars if tolerated)
Follow-up within 7 days
Patient education: daily weights, fluid/salt restriction, medication adherence
Most ADHF is "warm wet" (Profile B) — IV diuretics are the mainstay
BNP/NT-proBNP: excellent negative predictive value; obesity falsely lowers BNP; renal failure falsely elevates NT-proBNP
Cardiorenal syndrome: worsening renal function during diuresis — often acceptable if patient is decongesting; do NOT reflexively stop diuretics
SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin): can be initiated during hospitalization (EMPULSE trial — benefit even when started inpatient)
Morphine: previously used for acute pulmonary edema; now AVOIDED (increases mortality — ADHERE registry)
Nesiritide (BNP analog): no longer recommended (no mortality benefit — ASCEND-HF)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most common hemodynamic profile in ADHF? (Warm Wet — Profile B)
What is the first-line treatment? (IV loop diuretics)
How do you manage diuretic resistance? (Increase dose, add thiazide/metolazone, consider ultrafiltration)
When do you use inotropes? (Cold Wet — cardiogenic shock, low perfusion)
Why is morphine avoided in ADHF? (Increased mortality — ADHERE registry)

rows: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Heart failure with mildly reduced EF

blocks.text: Class I: no limitation of physical activity
Class II: slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms
Class III: marked limitation; comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes symptoms
Class IV: symptoms at rest
Stage A: at risk (HTN, DM, CAD) — no structural disease, no symptoms
Stage B: structural heart disease, no symptoms (e.g., prior MI, LVH, low EF)
Stage C: structural disease + current or prior symptoms
Stage D: refractory HF requiring advanced therapies (LVAD, transplant, palliative)

sections.title: 4. Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF — The 4 Pillars
sections.display: 4. Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (Gdmt) For Hfref — The 4 Pillars

blocks.text: All four should be initiated as early as possible (can start simultaneously or in rapid sequence):
Additional therapies:
Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate: for African Americans (A-HeFT trial) or if ACEi/ARB/ARNI intolerant
Ivabradine: if HR ≥70 bpm despite max beta-blocker, sinus rhythm, EF ≤35% (SHIFT trial)
Loop diuretics: for volume management (symptom relief only — no mortality benefit)
Digoxin: reduces hospitalizations but no mortality benefit (DIG trial); narrow therapeutic window
ICD: primary prevention if EF ≤35% despite ≥3 months GDMT, NYHA II–III, life expectancy 1 year
CRT (cardiac resynchronization therapy): EF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥150 ms + NYHA II–IV despite GDMT
Less evidence-based than HFrEF
SGLT2 inhibitors: empagliflozin (EMPEROR-Preserved) and dapagliflozin (DELIVER) — first drugs to show benefit in HFpEF
Diuretics for congestion
Treat comorbidities: HTN, AF, obesity, CAD
Spironolactone: may reduce hospitalizations (TOPCAT — regional variation in results)
Exercise training
ACEi/ARB/ARNI: monitor K+ and creatinine; contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy, angioedema (ACEi); 36-hour washout from ACEi before starting ARNI (angioedema risk)
beta-blockers: do NOT initiate during acute decompensation; start low, go slow; only 3 evidence-based: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate (NOT tartrate), bisoprolol
MRA: monitor K+ (hyperkalemia risk); contraindicated if K+ 5.0 or eGFR 30
SGLT2i: monitor for genital mycotic infections, euglycemic DKA (rare); can use regardless of diabetes status
All 4 pillars reduce mortality in HFrEF — initiate early, uptitrate to target doses
ARNI is superior to ACEi (PARADIGM-HF) — switch when stable
Only 3 beta-blockers have mortality benefit in HF: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol
SGLT2 inhibitors: benefit in HFrEF AND HFpEF — broadest indication
HFimpEF: continue GDMT even if EF improves (TRED-HF — withdrawal leads to relapse)
Avoid: NSAIDs (fluid retention), non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil — negative inotropy), thiazolidinediones (fluid retention)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are the 4 pillars of HFrEF therapy? (ARNI/ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i)
Which beta-blockers have mortality benefit in HF? (Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol)
What is the washout period from ACEi to ARNI? (36 hours — angioedema risk)
What drugs are proven in HFpEF? (SGLT2 inhibitors — EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER)
Should you stop GDMT if EF improves? (No — TRED-HF showed relapse with withdrawal)
Hypertensive emergency: SBP 180 and/or DBP 120 WITH acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, ACS, aortic dissection, acute HF/pulmonary edema, AKI, eclampsia, retinal hemorrhage/papilledema, MAHA)
Hypertensive urgency: SBP 180 and/or DBP 120 WITHOUT acute target organ damage
The distinction is based on organ damage, NOT the absolute BP number
Brain: headache, confusion, seizures, focal deficits → CT head (stroke vs encephalopathy)
Heart: chest pain, dyspnea → ECG, troponin, CXR, echo
Aorta: tearing chest/back pain → CT angiography (aortic dissection)
Kidney: oliguria, rising creatinine → BMP, UA
Eyes: blurred vision → fundoscopy (papilledema, hemorrhages, exudates)
Blood: schistocytes on smear (MAHA)
Admit to ICU; continuous BP monitoring (arterial line preferred)
IV antihypertensives (choice depends on specific emergency):

rows: Nicardipine: 5 mg/hr, titrate q5–15 min (max 15 mg/hr); Labetalol: 20 mg IV bolus, then infusion
beta-blocker FIRST to reduce HR 60 and shear stress BEFORE vasodilator; target SBP 120 in 20 min
Labetalol IV or hydralazine IV + magnesium sulfate
beta-blocker ONLY after alpha-blockade (avoid unopposed alpha-stimulation)
Fenoldopam: dopamine-1 agonist, increases renal perfusion

blocks.text: BP reduction targets:
General: reduce MAP by no more than 25% in first hour → then to 160/100 over next 2–6 hours → then to normal over 24–48 hours
Aortic dissection: SBP 120 mmHg within 20 minutes (more aggressive)
Ischemic stroke: do NOT lower BP unless 220/120 (or 185/110 if thrombolysis candidate)
Too-rapid reduction → watershed infarction (brain, kidney, heart)
Oral antihypertensives (NOT IV): restart/adjust home medications
Amlodipine, captopril, clonidine, labetalol PO
Reduce BP gradually over 24–48 hours
Outpatient follow-up within 1 week
Do NOT use sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable, rapid drop → stroke/MI)
Hypertensive emergency = organ damage (not just high numbers)
Aortic dissection: beta-blocker FIRST, then vasodilator; target SBP 120 rapidly
Ischemic stroke: do NOT aggressively lower BP (autoregulation shifted)
Sublingual nifedipine: CONTRAINDICATED (unpredictable hypotension)
Pheochromocytoma: alpha-blocker BEFORE beta-blocker (phenoxybenzamine → then propranolol)
Nitroprusside: cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (48 hours); monitor thiocyanate levels
Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES): associated with hypertensive encephalopathy; MRI shows posterior white matter edema; reversible with BP control
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What defines hypertensive emergency vs urgency? (Presence of target organ damage)
What is the BP target in the first hour? (Reduce MAP by ≤25%)
Why give beta-blocker before vasodilator in aortic dissection? (Reduce shear stress; vasodilator alone causes reflex tachycardia → increased aortic wall stress)
Why is sublingual nifedipine contraindicated? (Unpredictable rapid BP drop → stroke/MI)
When should you NOT lower BP in stroke? (Ischemic stroke with BP 220/120 and not a thrombolysis candidate)

sections.title: 1. Classification — ACC/AHA 2017 vs ESC 2018 vs JNC 8
sections.display: 1. Classification — Acc/Aha 2017 Vs Esc 2018 Vs Jnc 8

blocks.text: Confirm with ≥2 readings on ≥2 occasions; ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) is gold standard
White coat hypertension: elevated in clinic, normal at home/ABPM → do NOT treat
Masked hypertension: normal in clinic, elevated at home/ABPM → TREAT (higher CV risk)
Assess target organ damage: ECG (LVH), echo, fundoscopy, BMP (creatinine, K+), urinalysis (proteinuria), lipid panel, fasting glucose/HbA1c
Screen for secondary causes if: age 30, resistant HTN, sudden onset, hypokalemia, renal bruit

rows: Renal bruit, flash pulmonary edema, Cr rise with ACEi/ARB
Renal duplex US or CT/MR angiography
24-hr urine metanephrines or plasma free metanephrines
24-hr urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test
Young, upper lower extremity BP, rib notching on CXR
Obesity, snoring, daytime somnolence, resistant HTN

blocks.text: General: 130/80 (ACC/AHA 2017, supported by SPRINT trial)
ESC: 140/90 initially, then 130/80 if tolerated (age 65); 140/90 for age ≥65
Diabetes: 130/80
CKD with proteinuria: 130/80
Elderly (65): 130/80 if tolerated (SPRINT); avoid 120 systolic

rows: DM, CKD with proteinuria, HF, post-MI
Elderly, isolated systolic HTN, African descent

blocks.text: Start monotherapy if stage 1 with low CV risk; start dual therapy if stage 2 or high CV risk
Preferred combinations: ACEi/ARB + CCB; ACEi/ARB + thiazide; CCB + thiazide
Do NOT combine ACEi + ARB (ONTARGET — no benefit, more adverse effects)
Definition: BP above goal despite 3 drugs at optimal doses (including a diuretic), or controlled on ≥4 drugs
Confirm adherence, exclude white coat effect (ABPM), rule out secondary causes
Add spironolactone as 4th agent (PATHWAY-2 trial — most effective add-on)
SPRINT trial: intensive target (SBP 120) reduced CV events and mortality vs standard (140) — but excluded diabetes and stroke patients
Chlorthalidone is more potent and longer-acting than HCTZ (preferred thiazide-like)
ACEi cough: due to bradykinin accumulation; switch to ARB
ACEi angioedema: do NOT switch to ARB (small cross-reactivity risk); use CCB or other class
First-dose hypotension with ACEi: more common in volume-depleted patients
beta-blockers: no longer first-line for uncomplicated HTN (inferior stroke prevention)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the BP target per ACC/AHA 2017? (130/80 — SPRINT)
What is the best add-on for resistant HTN? (Spironolactone — PATHWAY-2)
What screening test for primary aldosteronism? (Aldosterone/renin ratio)
Why NOT combine ACEi + ARB? (No benefit, increased adverse effects — ONTARGET)
What is the most common cause of secondary HTN? (Renal parenchymal disease; renovascular in young women — fibromuscular dysplasia)

rows: ST depression, T-wave inversion, or normal
Partial occlusion or microembolization → subendocardial infarction
ST depression, T-wave inversion, or normal

blocks.text: Chest pain: substernal, pressure/squeezing, radiating to left arm/jaw/back, 20 minutes, not relieved by rest/NTG
Associated: diaphoresis, dyspnea, nausea/vomiting
