Atypical presentations (elderly, women, diabetics): dyspnea, fatigue, epigastric pain, syncope — may have NO chest pain
M — Morphine (only if pain refractory to NTG; use with caution — may increase mortality in NSTEMI)
O — Oxygen (only if SpO2 90% — DETO2X-AMI trial: routine O2 not beneficial)
N — Nitroglycerin (sublingual 0.4 mg q5min x 3; contraindicated if SBP 90, RV infarction, PDE5 inhibitor use within 24–48 hours)
A — Aspirin 162–325 mg chewed immediately (non-enteric coated)
B — beta-blocker (oral; within 24 hours if no contraindications: cardiogenic shock, bradycardia, acute HF, cocaine use)
Aspirin 325 mg chewed + P2Y12 inhibitor loading (ticagrelor 180 mg or prasugrel 60 mg preferred over clopidogrel 600 mg)
Anticoagulation: heparin (UFH bolus + infusion) or bivalirudin
Primary PCI (preferred): door-to-balloon time ≤90 minutes (≤120 if transfer needed)
Fibrinolysis (if PCI not available within 120 minutes): tenecteplase, alteplase, or streptokinase; door-to-needle ≤30 minutes; most effective within 12 hours of symptom onset
Contraindications to fibrinolysis: active bleeding, prior ICH, ischemic stroke 3 months, aortic dissection, brain tumor, recent major surgery (3 weeks)
Post-PCI: DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) for 12 months (minimum 6 months with DES)
Aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor preferred; clopidogrel if high bleeding risk)
Anticoagulation: UFH, enoxaparin, or fondaparinux
Risk stratification: TIMI score, GRACE score → determines invasive vs conservative strategy
Early invasive strategy (angiography within 24 hours): high-risk features — recurrent angina, hemodynamic instability, elevated troponin, ST changes, GRACE 140, diabetes
Conservative strategy: low-risk patients; stress test → angiography if positive

rows: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
Lifelong (especially if EF ≤40%, anterior MI, DM, HTN)

blocks.text: Hours: arrhythmias (VF — most common cause of death in first 48 hours), cardiogenic shock
Days 1–3: pericarditis (early), RV infarction (inferior MI)
Days 3–5: papillary muscle rupture → acute mitral regurgitation (new systolic murmur + pulmonary edema)
Days 3–7: ventricular septal rupture (new holosystolic murmur + step-up in O2 saturation)
Days 5–14: free wall rupture → cardiac tamponade (PEA, hypotension — usually fatal)
Weeks 2–8: Dressler syndrome (autoimmune pericarditis — fever, pleuritic chest pain, pericardial effusion; treat with aspirin/NSAIDs)
Weeks-months: ventricular aneurysm (persistent ST elevation), HF, mural thrombus (anterior MI → anticoagulate)
Suspect with inferior STEMI (RCA occlusion) + hypotension + clear lungs + JVD
Diagnosis: right-sided ECG → ST elevation in V4R
Treatment: IV fluids (preload-dependent); avoid nitroglycerin, morphine, diuretics (reduce preload → worsen hypotension)
Troponin: rises 3–6 hours after onset; high-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTn) detects earlier; serial measurements (0 and 3 hours, or 0/1 hour with hs-cTn)
Prasugrel: contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA, age ≥75, weight 60 kg
Ticagrelor: reversible P2Y12 inhibitor; side effects — dyspnea, bradycardia
Cocaine-induced MI: avoid beta-blockers (unopposed alpha-stimulation → coronary vasospasm); use benzodiazepines, NTG, CCB
Wellens syndrome: deeply inverted T waves in V2–V3 (critical LAD stenosis — needs angiography, NOT stress test)
Sgarbossa criteria: diagnose STEMI in presence of LBBB
Top 10 Testable Facts:
STEMI: door-to-balloon ≤90 min; door-to-needle ≤30 min
Aspirin 325 mg chewed immediately for all ACS
Primary PCI is preferred over fibrinolysis
DAPT: aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor x 12 months
Post-MI: aspirin, P2Y12, statin, ACEi, beta-blocker
RV infarction: fluids, avoid NTG/diuretics; V4R ST elevation
Papillary muscle rupture: day 3–5, new MR murmur
Free wall rupture: day 5–14, tamponade, usually fatal
Cocaine MI: no beta-blockers; use benzos, NTG, CCB
Dressler syndrome: weeks 2–8, autoimmune pericarditis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the door-to-balloon time for STEMI? (≤90 minutes)
How do you diagnose RV infarction? (Right-sided ECG — ST elevation V4R)
What is the most common cause of death in first 48 hours of MI? (Ventricular fibrillation)
When does papillary muscle rupture occur? (Days 3–5 post-MI)
Why avoid beta-blockers in cocaine-induced MI? (Unopposed alpha-stimulation → coronary vasospasm)
Irregularly irregular supraventricular tachyarrhythmia with disorganized atrial activity. Most common sustained arrhythmia.
Paroxysmal: self-terminating within 7 days
Persistent: sustained 7 days (requires cardioversion)
Long-standing persistent: continuous 12 months
Permanent: accepted by patient and physician (rate control only)
Valvular AF: associated with moderate-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valve → requires warfarin (DOACs contraindicated)
P — Pulmonary disease (COPD, PE)
I — Ischemia / MI
R — Rheumatic heart disease (mitral stenosis)
A — Alcohol (holiday heart), Anemia, Age
T — Thyrotoxicosis
E — Electrolytes, Elevated BP (HTN — most common cause)
S — Sepsis, Sleep apnea, Surgery (post-cardiac)

sections.title: 4. Management — Two Pillars: Rate Control + Anticoagulation
sections.display: 4. Management — Two Pillars: Rate Control + Anticoagulation

blocks.text: Rate Control (target HR 110 at rest — RACE II trial; 80 if symptomatic)

rows: First-line; avoid in decompensated HF, asthma
Add-on; for sedentary/HF patients; narrow therapeutic index
For rate control in critically ill / HF when others fail

blocks.text: Rhythm Control (consider if symptomatic despite rate control, young, first episode, HF)
Cardioversion: electrical (synchronized DC) or pharmacological (amiodarone, flecainide, propafenone)
If AF 48 hours or unknown duration: anticoagulate ≥3 weeks before cardioversion OR TEE to rule out LA thrombus → cardiovert → anticoagulate ≥4 weeks after
If AF 48 hours: can cardiovert without prior anticoagulation (still give heparin peri-procedure)

Long-term rhythm control: amiodarone (most effective but toxicity), flecainide/propafenone (only if no structural heart disease), sotalol, dofetilide, dronedarone (contraindicated in permanent AF and HF — PALLAS trial)
Catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation): increasingly first-line for symptomatic paroxysmal AF; EAST-AFNET 4 trial — early rhythm control improves outcomes
CHA2DS2-VASc Score:
C — CHF (1)
H — Hypertension (1)
D — Diabetes (1)
S2 — Stroke/TIA/thromboembolism (2)
V — Vascular disease (prior MI, PAD, aortic plaque) (1)
Sc — Sex category — female (1)
Anticoagulation decision:
Score 0 (males) or 1 (females): no anticoagulation
Score 1 (males): consider anticoagulation
Score ≥2: anticoagulate
Anticoagulant choice:
Non-valvular AF: DOACs preferred over warfarin (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) — RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48
Valvular AF (mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis): warfarin ONLY (DOACs contraindicated — RE-ALIGN trial)
Apixaban: preferred in elderly, CKD (can use down to CrCl 15); lowest bleeding risk (ARISTOTLE)
Dabigatran: only DOAC with reversal agent (idarucizumab); avoid if CrCl 30
HAS-BLED Score (bleeding risk):
H — Hypertension (uncontrolled)
A — Abnormal renal/liver function
S — Stroke history
B — Bleeding history
L — Labile INR
E — Elderly (65)
D — Drugs (antiplatelets, NSAIDs) or alcohol
Score ≥3: high bleeding risk — does NOT contraindicate anticoagulation; optimize modifiable risk factors
AF with WPW (pre-excitation): wide complex irregular tachycardia; AVOID AV nodal blockers (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, adenosine) → can cause VF; use procainamide or electrical cardioversion
AF with RVR and hemodynamic instability: immediate synchronized cardioversion
Hyperthyroidism-related AF: rate control with beta-blocker; treat thyroid; AF often converts after euthyroid
AF is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke
DOACs are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF (better safety profile)
CHA2DS2-VASc: anticoagulate if ≥2 (or ≥1 in males with shared decision-making)
HAS-BLED ≥3: does NOT mean "don't anticoagulate" — means optimize modifiable factors
AF + WPW: NEVER give AV nodal blockers → procainamide or cardioversion
EAST-AFNET 4: early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) improves CV outcomes
Amiodarone toxicity: pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid (hypo/hyper), hepatotoxicity, corneal deposits, blue-gray skin, peripheral neuropathy — monitor PFTs, TFTs, LFTs, eye exams
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the CHA2DS2-VASc threshold for anticoagulation? (≥2; consider at 1 in males)
When are DOACs contraindicated in AF? (Mechanical valve, moderate-severe mitral stenosis — use warfarin)
What do you avoid in AF with WPW? (AV nodal blockers — beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin, adenosine)
How long to anticoagulate before cardioversion if AF 48 hours? (≥3 weeks, or TEE to exclude thrombus)
What are the toxicities of amiodarone? (Pulmonary, thyroid, liver, corneal, skin, neuro)
Rate: 300 ÷ (number of large boxes between R-R) OR count R waves in 6-second strip x 10
Rhythm: regular vs irregular; P waves present? P before every QRS? QRS after every P?
Quick method: Lead I and aVF both positive = normal axis
Lead I positive, aVF negative = LAD
Lead I negative, aVF positive = RAD
Intervals: PR (0.12–0.20 sec), QRS (0.12 sec), QTc (0.44 sec males, 0.46 sec females)
ST segment and T waves: elevation, depression, inversions
Hypertrophy: LVH (Sokolow-Lyon: S in V1 + R in V5/V6 35 mm), RVH (R in V1 7 mm, RAD)

rows: ST elevation (concave up, diffuse) + PR depression
Peaked T waves → wide QRS → sine wave
Flattened T → U waves → prolonged QT
Delta wave + short PR + wide QRS
Flutter waves (best in II, III, aVF, V1)
2nd degree AV block Type I (Mobitz I)
Constant PR with sudden dropped QRS
2nd degree AV block Type II (Mobitz II) — may need pacemaker
Broad notched R in I, aVL, V5–V6 + absent septal Q
S in I, Q in III, inverted T in III
RCA or LCx (do posterior leads if ST depression V1–V3)
Usually benign; observe; atropine if symptomatic
High risk of progression to complete block → pacemaker

blocks.text: Mobitz I: block at AV node level → narrow QRS, responds to atropine
Mobitz II: block below AV node (His-Purkinje) → often wide QRS, does NOT respond to atropine → needs pacemaker
RBBB: RSR' in V1–V2 ("M-shaped"), wide S in I and V6; QRS ≥0.12 sec
Mnemonic: "MaRRoW" — M in V1 (R'), W in V6 (S wave)
Can be normal variant; also seen in PE, RV strain, ASD
LBBB: broad notched R in I, aVL, V5–V6; absent septal Q waves; QRS ≥0.12 sec
Mnemonic: "WiLLiaM" — W in V1 (QS or rS), M in V5–V6 (notched R)
Always pathological — suggests structural heart disease
New LBBB + chest pain = treat as STEMI equivalent
LBBB makes ST segment interpretation unreliable → use Sgarbossa criteria
Concordant ST elevation ≥1 mm in leads with positive QRS (5 points — most specific)
Concordant ST depression ≥1 mm in V1–V3 (3 points)
Discordant ST elevation ≥5 mm in leads with negative QRS (2 points)
Score ≥3: highly specific for STEMI
Modified Sgarbossa (Smith): discordant ST elevation/S wave ratio ≥0.25 (more sensitive)
Always use a systematic approach: Rate → Rhythm → Axis → Intervals → ST/T changes → Hypertrophy
Hyperkalemia ECG changes progress: peaked T → prolonged PR → wide QRS → sine wave → VF/asystole — treat immediately with calcium gluconate (membrane stabilizer)
Hypokalemia: flattened T → U waves → prolonged QT → risk of torsades de pointes
Long QT: risk of torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT); causes — drugs (sotalol, amiodarone, macrolides, antipsychotics, methadone), electrolytes (hypoK, hypoMg, hypoCa), congenital
Digoxin effect: "Salvador Dali mustache" — downsloping ST depression (scooped), shortened QT, T wave flattening/inversion (NOT toxicity)
Digoxin toxicity: any arrhythmia (classically bidirectional VT, accelerated junctional rhythm, atrial tachycardia with block); treat with digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Digibind)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
How do you differentiate Mobitz I from Mobitz II? (Mobitz I: progressive PR prolongation; Mobitz II: constant PR with sudden dropped QRS)
What is the mnemonic for RBBB vs LBBB? (MaRRoW for RBBB; WiLLiaM for LBBB)
What are the ECG changes of hyperkalemia in order? (Peaked T → prolonged PR → wide QRS → sine wave → VF)
What is a new LBBB with chest pain? (STEMI equivalent — activate cath lab)
What ECG finding suggests digoxin toxicity? (Bidirectional VT, atrial tachycardia with block — any arrhythmia)

parts.searchText: part vi: cardiology part vi: cardiology topic 46: acute decompensated heart failure (adhf) topic 46 · acute decompensated heart failure (adhf) 1. definition 1. definition new onset or worsening of hf signs/symptoms requiring urgent therapy. most common cause of hospitalization in patients 65 years. 2. precipitating factors (mnemonic: failures) 2. precipitating factors (mnemonic: failures) f — forgot medications (non-compliance) a — arrhythmia (especially new af) i — ischemia / infarction l — lifestyle (dietary indiscretion — salt/fluid) u — upregulation (pregnancy, hyperthyroidism) r — rheumatic / valvular disease e — embolism (pulmonary) s — stenosis (renal artery) / sepsis 3. classification — forrester hemodynamic profiles 3. classification — forrester hemodynamic profiles profile congestion (wet/dry) perfusion (warm/cold) management references a (warm dry) no adequate adjust oral meds; may not be adhf esc 2021 b (warm wet) yes adequate most common; iv diuretics c (cold wet) yes poor iv diuretics + inotropes ± vasopressors l (cold dry) no poor cautious fluid challenge; inotropes 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation congestion (left-sided): dyspnea, orthopnea, pnd, crackles, s3 gallop, elevated jvp, peripheral edema congestion (right-sided): jvd, hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema low perfusion: cool extremities, altered mental status, narrow pulse pressure, oliguria, hypotension 5. diagnostic workup 5. diagnostic workup bnp ≥100 pg/ml or nt-probnp ≥300 pg/ml (rules in hf; 100/300 essentially rules out) cxr: cardiomegaly, cephalization, kerley b lines, pleural effusions, bat-wing pulmonary edema ecg: arrhythmias, ischemia, lvh echocardiography: ef, wall motion, valvular disease, diastolic function labs: bmp (renal function, electrolytes), troponin (rule out acs), cbc, lfts, tsh identify precipitant 6. stepwise management 6. stepwise management position: sit upright; supplemental o2 if spo2 90% iv loop diuretics (cornerstone): furosemide iv bolus (if on chronic diuretics: give 1–2.5x home oral dose as iv); can use continuous infusion if refractory; target uop 0.5–1 ml/kg/hr; monitor weight, i/o, electrolytes, renal function vasodilators (if sbp 90 and no cardiogenic shock): iv nitroglycerin (preload reduction, especially with pulmonary edema); nitroprusside (afterload reduction — use with caution, cyanide toxicity) diuretic resistance: increase dose; add thiazide (metolazone 30 min before furosemide — sequential nephron blockade); consider ultrafiltration inotropes (if cold wet / cardiogenic shock): dobutamine (beta1 agonist — first-line inotrope); milrinone (pde3 inhibitor — vasodilation + inotropy; avoid in renal failure) vasopressors (if sbp 80 or cardiogenic shock): norepinephrine (preferred over dopamine — soap ii trial) non-invasive ventilation (cpap/bipap): for acute pulmonary edema with respiratory distress (reduces preload, improves oxygenation, decreases work of breathing — 3cpo trial) do not start or continue beta-blockers in acute decompensation with hemodynamic instability; can continue at reduced dose if stable; restart/uptitrate before discharge optimize gdmt before discharge: acei/arb/arni, beta-blocker, mra, sglt2 inhibitor (the "4 pillars") 7. discharge criteria transition 7. discharge criteria transition stable on oral diuretics ≥24 hours euvolemic or near-euvolemic stable vital signs, adequate renal function gdmt initiated/optimized (all 4 pillars if tolerated) follow-up within 7 days patient education: daily weights, fluid/salt restriction, medication adherence 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls most adhf is "warm wet" (profile b) — iv diuretics are the mainstay bnp/nt-probnp: excellent negative predictive value; obesity falsely lowers bnp; renal failure falsely elevates nt-probnp cardiorenal syndrome: worsening renal function during diuresis — often acceptable if patient is decongesting; do not reflexively stop diuretics sglt2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin): can be initiated during hospitalization (empulse trial — benefit even when started inpatient) morphine: previously used for acute pulmonary edema; now avoided (increases mortality — adhere registry) nesiritide (bnp analog): no longer recommended (no mortality benefit — ascend-hf) top 5 pimping questions: what is the most common hemodynamic profile in adhf? (warm wet — profile b) what is the first-line treatment? (iv loop diuretics) how do you manage diuretic resistance? (increase dose, add thiazide/metolazone, consider ultrafiltration) when do you use inotropes? (cold wet — cardiogenic shock, low perfusion) why is morphine avoided in adhf? (increased mortality — adhere registry) topic 47: chronic heart failure topic 47 · chronic heart failure 1. classification by ef — universal definition (2021) 1. classification by ef — universal definition (2021) type ef description references hfref ≤40% heart failure with reduced ejection fraction aha/acc 2022, esc 2021 hfmref 41–49% heart failure with mildly reduced ef hfpef ≥50% heart failure with preserved ef hfimpef previously ≤40%, now 40% hf with improved ef (continue gdmt) 2. nyha functional classification 2. nyha functional classification class i: no limitation of physical activity class ii: slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms class iii: marked limitation; comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes symptoms class iv: symptoms at rest 3. acc/aha stages 3. acc/aha stages stage a: at risk (htn, dm, cad) — no structural disease, no symptoms stage b: structural heart disease, no symptoms (e.g., prior mi, lvh, low ef) stage c: structural disease + current or prior symptoms stage d: refractory hf requiring advanced therapies (lvad, transplant, palliative) 4. guideline-directed medical therapy (gdmt) for hfref — the 4 pillars 4. guideline-directed medical therapy (gdmt) for hfref — the 4 pillars all four should be initiated as early as possible (can start simultaneously or in rapid sequence): pillar drug class specific agents key trial mortality benefit references 1 arni (preferred) or acei/arb sacubitril/valsartan; enalapril, lisinopril, losartan, valsartan paradigm-hf (arni superior to acei) yes aha/acc 2022 2 beta-blocker (evidence-based only) carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol merit-hf, cibis-ii, copernicus yes 3 mra spironolactone, eplerenone rales, emphasis-hf yes 4 sglt2 inhibitor dapagliflozin, empagliflozin dapa-hf, emperor-reduced yes additional therapies: hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate: for african americans (a-heft trial) or if acei/arb/arni intolerant ivabradine: if hr ≥70 bpm despite max beta-blocker, sinus rhythm, ef ≤35% (shift trial) loop diuretics: for volume management (symptom relief only — no mortality benefit) digoxin: reduces hospitalizations but no mortality benefit (dig trial); narrow therapeutic window icd: primary prevention if ef ≤35% despite ≥3 months gdmt, nyha ii–iii, life expectancy 1 year crt (cardiac resynchronization therapy): ef ≤35% + lbbb + qrs ≥150 ms + nyha ii–iv despite gdmt 5. hfpef management 5. hfpef management less evidence-based than hfref sglt2 inhibitors: empagliflozin (emperor-preserved) and dapagliflozin (deliver) — first drugs to show benefit in hfpef diuretics for congestion treat comorbidities: htn, af, obesity, cad spironolactone: may reduce hospitalizations (topcat — regional variation in results) exercise training 6. important drug considerations 6. important drug considerations acei/arb/arni: monitor k+ and creatinine; contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy, angioedema (acei); 36-hour washout from acei before starting arni (angioedema risk) beta-blockers: do not initiate during acute decompensation; start low, go slow; only 3 evidence-based: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate (not tartrate), bisoprolol mra: monitor k+ (hyperkalemia risk); contraindicated if k+ 5.0 or egfr 30 sglt2i: monitor for genital mycotic infections, euglycemic dka (rare); can use regardless of diabetes status 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls all 4 pillars reduce mortality in hfref — initiate early, uptitrate to target doses arni is superior to acei (paradigm-hf) — switch when stable only 3 beta-blockers have mortality benefit in hf: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol sglt2 inhibitors: benefit in hfref and hfpef — broadest indication hfimpef: continue gdmt even if ef improves (tred-hf — withdrawal leads to relapse) avoid: nsaids (fluid retention), non-dhp ccbs (diltiazem, verapamil — negative inotropy), thiazolidinediones (fluid retention) top 5 pimping questions: what are the 4 pillars of hfref therapy? (arni/acei/arb, beta-blocker, mra, sglt2i) which beta-blockers have mortality benefit in hf? (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol) what is the washout period from acei to arni? (36 hours — angioedema risk) what drugs are proven in hfpef? (sglt2 inhibitors — emperor-preserved, deliver) should you stop gdmt if ef improves? (no — tred-hf showed relapse with withdrawal) topic 48: hypertensive emergency/urgency topic 48 · hypertensive emergency/urgency 1. definitions 1. definitions hypertensive emergency: sbp 180 and/or dbp 120 with acute target organ damage (encephalopathy, acs, aortic dissection, acute hf/pulmonary edema, aki, eclampsia, retinal hemorrhage/papilledema, maha) hypertensive urgency: sbp 180 and/or dbp 120 without acute target organ damage the distinction is based on organ damage, not the absolute bp number 2. target organ damage assessment 2. target organ damage assessment brain: headache, confusion, seizures, focal deficits → ct head (stroke vs encephalopathy) heart: chest pain, dyspnea → ecg, troponin, cxr, echo aorta: tearing chest/back pain → ct angiography (aortic dissection) kidney: oliguria, rising creatinine → bmp, ua eyes: blurred vision → fundoscopy (papilledema, hemorrhages, exudates) blood: schistocytes on smear (maha) 3. management — hypertensive emergency 3. management — hypertensive emergency admit to icu; continuous bp monitoring (arterial line preferred) iv antihypertensives (choice depends on specific emergency): clinical scenario preferred agent notes references general / encephalopathy nicardipine iv or labetalol iv nicardipine: 5 mg/hr, titrate q5–15 min (max 15 mg/hr); labetalol: 20 mg iv bolus, then infusion aha/acc aortic dissection esmolol iv (first) + nicardipine or nitroprusside beta-blocker first to reduce hr 60 and shear stress before vasodilator; target sbp 120 in 20 min acute pulmonary edema nitroglycerin iv or nitroprusside + furosemide reduce preload and afterload acs nitroglycerin iv + labetalol avoid nitroprusside (coronary steal) eclampsia/preeclampsia labetalol iv or hydralazine iv + magnesium sulfate avoid acei/arb (teratogenic); deliver baby pheochromocytoma phentolamine iv (alpha-blocker) beta-blocker only after alpha-blockade (avoid unopposed alpha-stimulation) acute kidney injury fenoldopam or nicardipine fenoldopam: dopamine-1 agonist, increases renal perfusion bp reduction targets: general: reduce map by no more than 25% in first hour → then to 160/100 over next 2–6 hours → then to normal over 24–48 hours aortic dissection: sbp 120 mmhg within 20 minutes (more aggressive) ischemic stroke: do not lower bp unless 220/120 (or 185/110 if thrombolysis candidate) too-rapid reduction → watershed infarction (brain, kidney, heart) 4. management — hypertensive urgency 4. management — hypertensive urgency oral antihypertensives (not iv): restart/adjust home medications amlodipine, captopril, clonidine, labetalol po reduce bp gradually over 24–48 hours outpatient follow-up within 1 week do not use sublingual nifedipine (unpredictable, rapid drop → stroke/mi) 5. clinical pearls 5. clinical pearls hypertensive emergency = organ damage (not just high numbers) aortic dissection: beta-blocker first, then vasodilator; target sbp 120 rapidly ischemic stroke: do not aggressively lower bp (autoregulation shifted) sublingual nifedipine: contraindicated (unpredictable hypotension) pheochromocytoma: alpha-blocker before beta-blocker (phenoxybenzamine → then propranolol) nitroprusside: cyanide toxicity with prolonged use (48 hours); monitor thiocyanate levels posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (pres): associated with hypertensive encephalopathy; mri shows posterior white matter edema; reversible with bp control top 5 pimping questions: what defines hypertensive emergency vs urgency? (presence of target organ damage) what is the bp target in the first hour? (reduce map by ≤25%) why give beta-blocker before vasodilator in aortic dissection? (reduce shear stress; vasodilator alone causes reflex tachycardia → increased aortic wall stress) why is sublingual nifedipine contraindicated? (unpredictable rapid bp drop → stroke/mi) when should you not lower bp in stroke? (ischemic stroke with bp 220/120 and not a thrombolysis candidate) topic 49: hypertension topic 49 · hypertension 1. classification — acc/aha 2017 vs esc 2018 vs jnc 8 1. classification — acc/aha 2017 vs esc 2018 vs jnc 8 category acc/aha 2017 esc 2018 jnc 8 references normal 120/80 120/80 (optimal) — elevated / high-normal 120–129/80 130–139/85–89 — stage 1 / grade 1 130–139/80–89 140–159/90–99 — stage 2 / grade 2 ≥140/90 160–179/100–109 ≥140/90 grade 3 — ≥180/110 — 2. diagnosis 2. diagnosis confirm with ≥2 readings on ≥2 occasions; ambulatory bp monitoring (abpm) is gold standard white coat hypertension: elevated in clinic, normal at home/abpm → do not treat masked hypertension: normal in clinic, elevated at home/abpm → treat (higher cv risk) 3. initial workup 3. initial workup assess target organ damage: ecg (lvh), echo, fundoscopy, bmp (creatinine, k+), urinalysis (proteinuria), lipid panel, fasting glucose/hba1c screen for secondary causes if: age 30, resistant htn, sudden onset, hypokalemia, renal bruit 4. secondary hypertension causes 4. secondary hypertension causes cause clue screening test references renal artery stenosis renal bruit, flash pulmonary edema, cr rise with acei/arb renal duplex us or ct/mr angiography primary aldosteronism (conn) hypokalemia, resistant htn aldosterone/renin ratio (arr) 30 pheochromocytoma episodic htn, headache, sweating, palpitations 24-hr urine metanephrines or plasma free metanephrines cushing syndrome moon face, striae, central obesity 24-hr urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test coarctation of aorta young, upper lower extremity bp, rib notching on cxr echo, ct/mr angiography obstructive sleep apnea obesity, snoring, daytime somnolence, resistant htn polysomnography thyroid disease hypo/hyperthyroidism symptoms tsh 5. treatment targets 5. treatment targets general: 130/80 (acc/aha 2017, supported by sprint trial) esc: 140/90 initially, then 130/80 if tolerated (age 65); 140/90 for age ≥65 diabetes: 130/80 ckd with proteinuria: 130/80 elderly (65): 130/80 if tolerated (sprint); avoid 120 systolic 6. first-line medications 6. first-line medications drug class examples compelling indications contraindications/cautions references acei enalapril, lisinopril, ramipril dm, ckd with proteinuria, hf, post-mi pregnancy, bilateral ras, angioedema, k+ 5.5 arb losartan, valsartan, telmisartan same as acei; acei-intolerant (cough) same as acei except angioedema (rare) ccb (dhp) amlodipine, nifedipine elderly, isolated systolic htn, african descent peripheral edema (dose-dependent) thiazide/thiazide-like hctz, chlorthalidone, indapamide elderly, osteoporosis, african descent gout, hyponatremia, hypokalemia beta-blocker bisoprolol, metoprolol, atenolol post-mi, hf, rate control af asthma (non-selective), bradycardia, severe pad start monotherapy if stage 1 with low cv risk; start dual therapy if stage 2 or high cv risk preferred combinations: acei/arb + ccb; acei/arb + thiazide; ccb + thiazide do not combine acei + arb (ontarget — no benefit, more adverse effects) 7. resistant hypertension 7. resistant hypertension definition: bp above goal despite 3 drugs at optimal doses (including a diuretic), or controlled on ≥4 drugs confirm adherence, exclude white coat effect (abpm), rule out secondary causes add spironolactone as 4th agent (pathway-2 trial — most effective add-on) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls sprint trial: intensive target (sbp 120) reduced cv events and mortality vs standard (140) — but excluded diabetes and stroke patients chlorthalidone is more potent and longer-acting than hctz (preferred thiazide-like) acei cough: due to bradykinin accumulation; switch to arb acei angioedema: do not switch to arb (small cross-reactivity risk); use ccb or other class first-dose hypotension with acei: more common in volume-depleted patients beta-blockers: no longer first-line for uncomplicated htn (inferior stroke prevention) top 5 pimping questions: what is the bp target per acc/aha 2017? (130/80 — sprint) what is the best add-on for resistant htn? (spironolactone — pathway-2) what screening test for primary aldosteronism? (aldosterone/renin ratio) why not combine acei + arb? (no benefit, increased adverse effects — ontarget) what is the most common cause of secondary htn? (renal parenchymal disease; renovascular in young women — fibromuscular dysplasia) topic 50: acute coronary syndrome (acs) topic 50 · acute coronary syndrome (acs) 1. classification 1. classification type ecg troponin pathology references stemi st elevation (or new lbbb) elevated complete coronary occlusion → transmural infarction aha/acc, esc nstemi st depression, t-wave inversion, or normal elevated partial occlusion or microembolization → subendocardial infarction unstable angina st depression, t-wave inversion, or normal normal partial occlusion → ischemia without necrosis 2. clinical presentation 2. clinical presentation chest pain: substernal, pressure/squeezing, radiating to left arm/jaw/back, 20 minutes, not relieved by rest/ntg associated: diaphoresis, dyspnea, nausea/vomiting atypical presentations (elderly, women, diabetics): dyspnea, fatigue, epigastric pain, syncope — may have no chest pain 3. initial management — "mona-b" (within 10 minutes) 3. initial management — "mona-b" (within 10 minutes) m — morphine (only if pain refractory to ntg; use with caution — may increase mortality in nstemi) o — oxygen (only if spo2 90% — deto2x-ami trial: routine o2 not beneficial) n — nitroglycerin (sublingual 0.4 mg q5min x 3; contraindicated if sbp 90, rv infarction, pde5 inhibitor use within 24–48 hours) a — aspirin 162–325 mg chewed immediately (non-enteric coated) b — beta-blocker (oral; within 24 hours if no contraindications: cardiogenic shock, bradycardia, acute hf, cocaine use) 4. stemi management 4. stemi management aspirin 325 mg chewed + p2y12 inhibitor loading (ticagrelor 180 mg or prasugrel 60 mg preferred over clopidogrel 600 mg) anticoagulation: heparin (ufh bolus + infusion) or bivalirudin primary pci (preferred): door-to-balloon time ≤90 minutes (≤120 if transfer needed) fibrinolysis (if pci not available within 120 minutes): tenecteplase, alteplase, or streptokinase; door-to-needle ≤30 minutes; most effective within 12 hours of symptom onset contraindications to fibrinolysis: active bleeding, prior ich, ischemic stroke 3 months, aortic dissection, brain tumor, recent major surgery (3 weeks) post-pci: dapt (aspirin + p2y12 inhibitor) for 12 months (minimum 6 months with des) 5. nstemi/ua management 5. nstemi/ua management aspirin + p2y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor preferred; clopidogrel if high bleeding risk) anticoagulation: ufh, enoxaparin, or fondaparinux risk stratification: timi score, grace score → determines invasive vs conservative strategy early invasive strategy (angiography within 24 hours): high-risk features — recurrent angina, hemodynamic instability, elevated troponin, st changes, grace 140, diabetes conservative strategy: low-risk patients; stress test → angiography if positive 6. post-acs long-term therapy (must memorize) 6. post-acs long-term therapy (must memorize) medication purpose duration references aspirin antiplatelet lifelong p2y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor/clopidogrel/prasugrel) antiplatelet 12 months (dapt) high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) ldl reduction lifelong acei/arb remodeling prevention lifelong (especially if ef ≤40%, anterior mi, dm, htn) beta-blocker reduce mortality at least 1 year (lifelong if ef ≤40%) mra (eplerenone) if ef ≤40% + hf or dm lifelong ephesus 7. complications of mi (by timeline) 7. complications of mi (by timeline) hours: arrhythmias (vf — most common cause of death in first 48 hours), cardiogenic shock days 1–3: pericarditis (early), rv infarction (inferior mi) days 3–5: papillary muscle rupture → acute mitral regurgitation (new systolic murmur + pulmonary edema) days 3–7: ventricular septal rupture (new holosystolic murmur + step-up in o2 saturation) days 5–14: free wall rupture → cardiac tamponade (pea, hypotension — usually fatal) weeks 2–8: dressler syndrome (autoimmune pericarditis — fever, pleuritic chest pain, pericardial effusion; treat with aspirin/nsaids) weeks-months: ventricular aneurysm (persistent st elevation), hf, mural thrombus (anterior mi → anticoagulate) 8. rv infarction (commonly tested) 8. rv infarction (commonly tested) suspect with inferior stemi (rca occlusion) + hypotension + clear lungs + jvd diagnosis: right-sided ecg → st elevation in v4r treatment: iv fluids (preload-dependent); avoid nitroglycerin, morphine, diuretics (reduce preload → worsen hypotension) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls troponin: rises 3–6 hours after onset; high-sensitivity troponin (hs-ctn) detects earlier; serial measurements (0 and 3 hours, or 0/1 hour with hs-ctn) prasugrel: contraindicated in prior stroke/tia, age ≥75, weight 60 kg ticagrelor: reversible p2y12 inhibitor; side effects — dyspnea, bradycardia cocaine-induced mi: avoid beta-blockers (unopposed alpha-stimulation → coronary vasospasm); use benzodiazepines, ntg, ccb wellens syndrome: deeply inverted t waves in v2–v3 (critical lad stenosis — needs angiography, not stress test) sgarbossa criteria: diagnose stemi in presence of lbbb top 10 testable facts: stemi: door-to-balloon ≤90 min; door-to-needle ≤30 min aspirin 325 mg chewed immediately for all acs primary pci is preferred over fibrinolysis dapt: aspirin + p2y12 inhibitor x 12 months post-mi: aspirin, p2y12, statin, acei, beta-blocker rv infarction: fluids, avoid ntg/diuretics; v4r st elevation papillary muscle rupture: day 3–5, new mr murmur free wall rupture: day 5–14, tamponade, usually fatal cocaine mi: no beta-blockers; use benzos, ntg, ccb dressler syndrome: weeks 2–8, autoimmune pericarditis top 5 pimping questions: what is the door-to-balloon time for stemi? (≤90 minutes) how do you diagnose rv infarction? (right-sided ecg — st elevation v4r) what is the most common cause of death in first 48 hours of mi? (ventricular fibrillation) when does papillary muscle rupture occur? (days 3–5 post-mi) why avoid beta-blockers in cocaine-induced mi? (unopposed alpha-stimulation → coronary vasospasm) topic 51: atrial fibrillation topic 51 · atrial fibrillation 1. definition 1. definition irregularly irregular supraventricular tachyarrhythmia with disorganized atrial activity. most common sustained arrhythmia. 2. classification 2. classification paroxysmal: self-terminating within 7 days persistent: sustained 7 days (requires cardioversion) long-standing persistent: continuous 12 months permanent: accepted by patient and physician (rate control only) valvular af: associated with moderate-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valve → requires warfarin (doacs contraindicated) 3. etiology (mnemonic: pirates) 3. etiology (mnemonic: pirates) p — pulmonary disease (copd, pe) i — ischemia / mi r — rheumatic heart disease (mitral stenosis) a — alcohol (holiday heart), anemia, age t — thyrotoxicosis e — electrolytes, elevated bp (htn — most common cause) s — sepsis, sleep apnea, surgery (post-cardiac) 4. management — two pillars: rate control + anticoagulation 4. management — two pillars: rate control + anticoagulation rate control (target hr 110 at rest — race ii trial; 80 if symptomatic) agent dose notes references beta-blocker (preferred) metoprolol, bisoprolol, atenolol first-line; avoid in decompensated hf, asthma race ii non-dhp ccb diltiazem, verapamil alternative; avoid in hfref (negative inotropy) digoxin 0.125–0.25 mg/day add-on; for sedentary/hf patients; narrow therapeutic index amiodarone iv or po for rate control in critically ill / hf when others fail rhythm control (consider if symptomatic despite rate control, young, first episode, hf) cardioversion: electrical (synchronized dc) or pharmacological (amiodarone, flecainide, propafenone) if af 48 hours or unknown duration: anticoagulate ≥3 weeks before cardioversion or tee to rule out la thrombus → cardiovert → anticoagulate ≥4 weeks after if af 48 hours: can cardiovert without prior anticoagulation (still give heparin peri-procedure) long-term rhythm control: amiodarone (most effective but toxicity), flecainide/propafenone (only if no structural heart disease), sotalol, dofetilide, dronedarone (contraindicated in permanent af and hf — pallas trial) catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation): increasingly first-line for symptomatic paroxysmal af; east-afnet 4 trial — early rhythm control improves outcomes 5. anticoagulation — stroke prevention 5. anticoagulation — stroke prevention cha2ds2-vasc score: c — chf (1) h — hypertension (1) a2 — age ≥75 (2) d — diabetes (1) s2 — stroke/tia/thromboembolism (2) v — vascular disease (prior mi, pad, aortic plaque) (1) a — age 65–74 (1) sc — sex category — female (1) anticoagulation decision: score 0 (males) or 1 (females): no anticoagulation score 1 (males): consider anticoagulation score ≥2: anticoagulate anticoagulant choice: non-valvular af: doacs preferred over warfarin (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) — re-ly, rocket-af, aristotle, engage af-timi 48 valvular af (mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis): warfarin only (doacs contraindicated — re-align trial) apixaban: preferred in elderly, ckd (can use down to crcl 15); lowest bleeding risk (aristotle) dabigatran: only doac with reversal agent (idarucizumab); avoid if crcl 30 has-bled score (bleeding risk): h — hypertension (uncontrolled) a — abnormal renal/liver function s — stroke history b — bleeding history l — labile inr e — elderly (65) d — drugs (antiplatelets, nsaids) or alcohol score ≥3: high bleeding risk — does not contraindicate anticoagulation; optimize modifiable risk factors 6. special situations 6. special situations af with wpw (pre-excitation): wide complex irregular tachycardia; avoid av nodal blockers (beta-blockers, ccbs, digoxin, adenosine) → can cause vf; use procainamide or electrical cardioversion af with rvr and hemodynamic instability: immediate synchronized cardioversion hyperthyroidism-related af: rate control with beta-blocker; treat thyroid; af often converts after euthyroid 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls af is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke doacs are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular af (better safety profile) cha2ds2-vasc: anticoagulate if ≥2 (or ≥1 in males with shared decision-making) has-bled ≥3: does not mean "don't anticoagulate" — means optimize modifiable factors af + wpw: never give av nodal blockers → procainamide or cardioversion east-afnet 4: early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) improves cv outcomes amiodarone toxicity: pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid (hypo/hyper), hepatotoxicity, corneal deposits, blue-gray skin, peripheral neuropathy — monitor pfts, tfts, lfts, eye exams top 5 pimping questions: what is the cha2ds2-vasc threshold for anticoagulation? (≥2; consider at 1 in males) when are doacs contraindicated in af? (mechanical valve, moderate-severe mitral stenosis — use warfarin) what do you avoid in af with wpw? (av nodal blockers — beta-blockers, ccbs, digoxin, adenosine) how long to anticoagulate before cardioversion if af 48 hours? (≥3 weeks, or tee to exclude thrombus) what are the toxicities of amiodarone? (pulmonary, thyroid, liver, corneal, skin, neuro) topic 52: basic ecg interpretation topic 52 · basic ecg interpretation 1. systematic approach (must memorize) 1. systematic approach (must memorize) rate: 300 ÷ (number of large boxes between r-r) or count r waves in 6-second strip x 10 rhythm: regular vs irregular; p waves present? p before every qrs? qrs after every p? axis: normal (−30° to +90°); lad (−30° to −90°); rad (+90° to +180°) quick method: lead i and avf both positive = normal axis lead i positive, avf negative = lad lead i negative, avf positive = rad intervals: pr (0.12–0.20 sec), qrs (0.12 sec), qtc (0.44 sec males, 0.46 sec females) st segment and t waves: elevation, depression, inversions hypertrophy: lvh (sokolow-lyon: s in v1 + r in v5/v6 35 mm), rvh (r in v1 7 mm, rad) 2. key ecg patterns (high-yield) 2. key ecg patterns (high-yield) pattern ecg finding diagnosis st elevation (convex up) + reciprocal depression territorial st elevation stemi st elevation (concave up, diffuse) + pr depression diffuse st elevation, pr depression pericarditis peaked t waves → wide qrs → sine wave progressive changes hyperkalemia flattened t → u waves → prolonged qt progressive changes hypokalemia short qt, shortened st hypercalcemia prolonged qt hypocalcemia delta wave + short pr + wide qrs slurred upstroke of qrs wpw (pre-excitation) irregularly irregular, no p waves fibrillatory baseline atrial fibrillation sawtooth pattern flutter waves (best in ii, iii, avf, v1) atrial flutter progressively prolonging pr → dropped qrs wenckebach 2nd degree av block type i (mobitz i) constant pr with sudden dropped qrs 2nd degree av block type ii (mobitz ii) — may need pacemaker complete av dissociation p waves and qrs independent 3rd degree (complete) heart block — needs pacemaker rsr' in v1–v2 + wide qrs rabbit ears rbbb broad notched r in i, avl, v5–v6 + absent septal q lbbb s1q3t3 s in i, q in iii, inverted t in iii pulmonary embolism (not sensitive) low voltage + electrical alternans pericardial effusion / tamponade osborn (j) waves positive deflection at j point hypothermia epsilon wave + t inversion v1–v3 arvc 3. st elevation — stemi localization 3. st elevation — stemi localization leads territory artery v1–v4 anterior lad v1–v6, i, avl extensive anterior proximal lad i, avl, v5–v6 lateral lcx ii, iii, avf inferior rca (85%) or lcx (15%) v7–v9 posterior rca or lcx (do posterior leads if st depression v1–v3) v3r–v4r right ventricle proximal rca 4. av blocks 4. av blocks type pr interval dropped qrs? treatment 1st degree prolonged (>0.20 sec), constant no observation (benign) 2nd degree mobitz i (wenckebach) progressively prolonging yes (grouped beating) usually benign; observe; atropine if symptomatic 2nd degree mobitz ii constant (normal or prolonged) yes (sudden) high risk of progression to complete block → pacemaker 3rd degree (complete) av dissociation p and qrs independent temporary pacing → permanent pacemaker mobitz i: block at av node level → narrow qrs, responds to atropine mobitz ii: block below av node (his-purkinje) → often wide qrs, does not respond to atropine → needs pacemaker 5. bundle branch blocks 5. bundle branch blocks rbbb: rsr' in v1–v2 ("m-shaped"), wide s in i and v6; qrs ≥0.12 sec mnemonic: "marrow" — m in v1 (r'), w in v6 (s wave) can be normal variant; also seen in pe, rv strain, asd lbbb: broad notched r in i, avl, v5–v6; absent septal q waves; qrs ≥0.12 sec mnemonic: "william" — w in v1 (qs or rs), m in v5–v6 (notched r) always pathological — suggests structural heart disease new lbbb + chest pain = treat as stemi equivalent lbbb makes st segment interpretation unreliable → use sgarbossa criteria 6. sgarbossa criteria (stemi diagnosis in lbbb) 6. sgarbossa criteria (stemi diagnosis in lbbb) concordant st elevation ≥1 mm in leads with positive qrs (5 points — most specific) concordant st depression ≥1 mm in v1–v3 (3 points) discordant st elevation ≥5 mm in leads with negative qrs (2 points) score ≥3: highly specific for stemi modified sgarbossa (smith): discordant st elevation/s wave ratio ≥0.25 (more sensitive) 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls always use a systematic approach: rate → rhythm → axis → intervals → st/t changes → hypertrophy hyperkalemia ecg changes progress: peaked t → prolonged pr → wide qrs → sine wave → vf/asystole — treat immediately with calcium gluconate (membrane stabilizer) hypokalemia: flattened t → u waves → prolonged qt → risk of torsades de pointes long qt: risk of torsades de pointes (polymorphic vt); causes — drugs (sotalol, amiodarone, macrolides, antipsychotics, methadone), electrolytes (hypok, hypomg, hypoca), congenital digoxin effect: "salvador dali mustache" — downsloping st depression (scooped), shortened qt, t wave flattening/inversion (not toxicity) digoxin toxicity: any arrhythmia (classically bidirectional vt, accelerated junctional rhythm, atrial tachycardia with block); treat with digoxin-specific antibody fragments (digibind) top 5 pimping questions: how do you differentiate mobitz i from mobitz ii? (mobitz i: progressive pr prolongation; mobitz ii: constant pr with sudden dropped qrs) what is the mnemonic for rbbb vs lbbb? (marrow for rbbb; william for lbbb) what are the ecg changes of hyperkalemia in order? (peaked t → prolonged pr → wide qrs → sine wave → vf) what is a new lbbb with chest pain? (stemi equivalent — activate cath lab) what ecg finding suggests digoxin toxicity? (bidirectional vt, atrial tachycardia with block — any arrhythmia)

parts.title: PART VII: ENDOCRINOLOGY  METABOLIC

blocks.text: Diagnosis requires 2 abnormal tests on separate occasions (unless random glucose ≥200 + symptoms)
HbA1c may be unreliable in: hemoglobinopathies, hemolytic anemia, iron deficiency, pregnancy, CKD (falsely low or high depending on condition)

rows: Elderly, multiple comorbidities, limited life expectancy

blocks.text: First-line: Metformin + lifestyle modification (for all patients)
Metformin: 500 mg → titrate to 2000 mg/day; contraindicated if eGFR 30; hold before contrast; side effects — GI (diarrhea, nausea), lactic acidosis (rare), B12 deficiency (monitor)
Second-line: Based on comorbidities (patient-centered approach):

rows: GLP-1 RA (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) or SGLT2i
LEADER, SUSTAIN-6, REWIND, EMPA-REG OUTCOME, CANVAS
SGLT2 inhibitor (if eGFR ≥20) or GLP-1 RA
GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) or dual GIP/GLP-1 RA (tirzepatide)

blocks.text: If HbA1c still above target: add third agent or insulin
Insulin initiation: if HbA1c 10%, symptomatic hyperglycemia, or catabolic features (weight loss, ketosis)
Start basal insulin (glargine or detemir): 10 units or 0.1–0.2 U/kg/day at bedtime; titrate by 2 units every 3 days to fasting glucose target (80–130 mg/dL)
If still uncontrolled: add prandial insulin (basal-bolus) or premixed insulin

rows: UTI, genital mycotic infections, euglycemic DKA, Fournier gangrene (rare)
GI (nausea, vomiting), pancreatitis (rare), medullary thyroid cancer (animal data — contraindicated in MEN2/MTC)
Fluid retention, HF exacerbation, fractures, bladder cancer (controversial)

blocks.text: HbA1c: every 3 months (if not at goal) or every 6 months (if stable)
Self-monitoring blood glucose (SMBG): especially if on insulin or sulfonylurea
Annual screening: retinopathy (fundoscopy), nephropathy (urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio + eGFR), neuropathy (monofilament test), foot exam, lipid panel, BP
Metformin: first-line for ALL T2DM; hold if eGFR 30; hold 48 hours before/after IV contrast (risk of lactic acidosis with AKI)
SGLT2 inhibitors: cardiorenal benefits independent of glucose lowering; can use even in non-diabetic HF and CKD
GLP-1 RAs: best for weight loss + CV benefit; oral semaglutide available
Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1 RA): most potent glucose and weight reduction
Sulfonylureas: cheap and effective but cause hypoglycemia and weight gain; avoid in elderly, CKD
Insulin is NOT a failure — it is necessary when beta-cell function declines
Sick day rules: hold metformin, SGLT2i during acute illness; continue insulin (may need to increase)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the first-line drug for T2DM? (Metformin)
What drug class is preferred in T2DM with ASCVD? (GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i)
What drug class is preferred in T2DM with HF? (SGLT2 inhibitor)
What is the HbA1c target for most adults? (7.0% — ADA)
What are the contraindications to metformin? (eGFR 30, acute illness with risk of lactic acidosis)

Metabolic emergency in diabetes characterized by the triad: hyperglycemia + ketosis + metabolic acidosis. More common in T1DM but can occur in T2DM (especially ketosis-prone T2DM, common in Southeast Asia/Africa).
Infection (most common precipitant — pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)
Insulin non-compliance / Inadequate insulin
Infarction (MI, stroke)
Initial presentation (new-onset T1DM)
Intoxication / drugs (cocaine, steroids, SGLT2 inhibitors — euglycemic DKA)

Insulin deficiency + counter-regulatory hormone excess (glucagon, cortisol, catecholamines, GH) → unrestrained lipolysis → free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis → beta-hydroxybutyrate + acetoacetate → anion gap metabolic acidosis. Hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → dehydration + electrolyte losses.
Step 1: Aggressive IV Fluid Resuscitation
0.9% NS: 1–1.5 L/hr in first hour (15–20 mL/kg/hr)
Then: if corrected Na+ is low → continue NS; if corrected Na+ is normal/high → switch to 0.45% NS at 250–500 mL/hr
When glucose reaches 200 mg/dL → add D5W to IV fluids (D5 0.45% NS) to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing insulin
Step 2: Potassium Replacement (BEFORE insulin if K+ low)

rows: HOLD insulin; replace K+ aggressively (20–40 mEq/hr) until K+ 3.3
Give 20–30 mEq K+ in each liter of IV fluid; start insulin
Do NOT give K+; recheck every 2 hours; start insulin

blocks.text: Total body K+ is ALWAYS depleted in DKA (even if serum K+ is normal/high — acidosis shifts K+ out of cells)
Insulin drives K+ into cells → can cause fatal hypokalemia if not replaced
Step 3: Insulin
Regular insulin IV infusion: 0.1–0.14 U/kg/hr (no bolus needed per updated ADA)
Alternative: 0.1 U/kg IV bolus, then 0.1 U/kg/hr infusion
Target glucose decrease: 50–70 mg/dL per hour
When glucose reaches 200 mg/dL: reduce insulin to 0.02–0.05 U/kg/hr AND add D5W; keep glucose 150–200 until DKA resolves
DKA resolution criteria: glucose 200 + at least 2 of: pH 7.3, bicarbonate ≥15, anion gap ≤12
Step 4: Bicarbonate
Only if pH 6.9 (severe acidosis with hemodynamic compromise)
100 mEq NaHCO3 in 400 mL water + 20 mEq KCl over 2 hours
Routine bicarbonate is NOT recommended (may worsen intracellular acidosis, hypokalemia, paradoxical CNS acidosis)
Step 5: Transition to Subcutaneous Insulin
When DKA resolved AND patient eating → overlap SC insulin with IV insulin for 1–2 hours before stopping IV
Calculate basal insulin dose: ~0.5 U/kg/day (or 80% of total daily IV insulin requirement)
Give SC insulin 1–2 hours BEFORE stopping IV drip (to prevent rebound DKA)
Glucose: every 1 hour
BMP (K+, Na+, BUN, Cr): every 2–4 hours
Venous blood gas: every 2–4 hours (pH, bicarbonate)
Anion gap: every 2–4 hours
Urine output: hourly (Foley if needed)
DKA with glucose 250 mg/dL
Causes: SGLT2 inhibitors (most common cause now), pregnancy, starvation, alcohol, low-carb diet
Diagnosis often delayed because glucose is not markedly elevated
Treatment: same as DKA but start D5W earlier; hold SGLT2 inhibitor
Hypokalemia (most dangerous — from insulin without K+ replacement)
Hypoglycemia (from insulin without D5W when glucose drops)
Cerebral edema (children — from too-rapid correction of osmolality; avoid rapid fluid boluses in pediatric DKA)
Non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (hyperchloremic — from NS resuscitation; resolves spontaneously)
ALWAYS check K+ before starting insulin — if K+ 3.3, hold insulin and replace K+ first
Kussmaul breathing: deep, rapid respirations — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis
Fruity breath: acetone (ketone body)
Serum Na+ is falsely low in DKA (pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia); corrected Na+ = measured Na+ + 1.6 x [(glucose − 100) / 100]
beta-hydroxybutyrate is the predominant ketone in DKA; urine ketone dipstick detects acetoacetate (may be falsely negative early); serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is preferred
Anion gap = Na+ − (Cl- + HCO3-); normal 8–12; DKA causes elevated AG
DKA vs HHS: DKA = acidosis + ketosis + moderate hyperglycemia; HHS = severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + no significant ketosis
Top 10 Testable Facts:
DKA triad: hyperglycemia + ketosis + anion gap metabolic acidosis
Most common precipitant: infection
Check K+ BEFORE insulin — hold insulin if K+ 3.3
IV fluids first: NS 1–1.5 L in first hour
Insulin drip: 0.1–0.14 U/kg/hr; target glucose drop 50–70/hr
Add D5W when glucose reaches 200 mg/dL
DKA resolution: glucose 200 + pH 7.3 + bicarb ≥15 + AG ≤12
Overlap SC insulin 1–2 hours before stopping IV insulin
Bicarbonate only if pH 6.9
Euglycemic DKA: SGLT2 inhibitors — glucose may be normal
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What do you check before starting insulin in DKA? (Potassium — hold insulin if K+ 3.3)
When do you add dextrose to IV fluids? (When glucose reaches 200 mg/dL)
What are the criteria for DKA resolution? (Glucose 200, pH 7.3, bicarb ≥15, AG ≤12)
When do you give bicarbonate? (Only if pH 6.9)
What causes euglycemic DKA? (SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation)
Severe hyperglycemia with hyperosmolality and dehydration WITHOUT significant ketoacidosis. Occurs in T2DM (usually elderly with limited water access).
Effective osmolality = 2(Na+) + glucose/18 (do NOT include BUN — freely crosses membranes)
HHS has higher mortality than DKA (5–20% vs 1–5%) — due to older age, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Infection (most common — pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)
Non-compliance with medications
New-onset T2DM
Drugs: steroids, thiazides, atypical antipsychotics
Acute illness: MI, stroke, surgery

sections.title: 4. Management — Similar to DKA but Key Differences
sections.display: 4. Management — Similar To DKA But Key Differences

blocks.text: IV Fluids (most critical — patients are profoundly dehydrated, often 8–10 L deficit):
NS 1–1.5 L/hr in first hour
Then: corrected Na+ low → continue NS; corrected Na+ normal/high → 0.45% NS at 250–500 mL/hr
More aggressive fluid resuscitation than DKA (dehydration is more severe)
Fluids alone will significantly reduce glucose
Potassium: same protocol as DKA (check before insulin)
Insulin: lower dose may be needed (0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr); some guidelines recommend fluids first for 1–2 hours before starting insulin (glucose drops significantly with fluids alone)
Target glucose decrease: 50–70 mg/dL per hour (same as DKA)
When glucose reaches 300 mg/dL → add D5W and reduce insulin rate
Bicarbonate: NOT needed (no significant acidosis)
DVT prophylaxis: important (hyperosmolality → hypercoagulable state)
Monitor: glucose hourly, BMP every 2–4 hours, osmolality, mental status, I/O
HHS = severe dehydration + severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + altered mental status + NO ketoacidosis
Fluids are MORE important than insulin in HHS (unlike DKA where insulin is critical)
Mental status changes correlate with osmolality (not glucose level)
Avoid rapid correction of osmolality → risk of cerebral edema (especially in elderly)
Mixed DKA/HHS can occur (~30% of cases) — treat as DKA
DVT prophylaxis is essential (hyperviscosity)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the key difference between HHS and DKA? (HHS: no significant ketoacidosis, higher glucose, higher osmolality, higher mortality)
What is the most important initial treatment in HHS? (Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation)
What correlates with mental status in HHS? (Serum osmolality, not glucose)
When do you add D5W in HHS? (When glucose reaches 300 mg/dL — higher threshold than DKA)
Why is DVT prophylaxis important? (Hyperosmolality → hypercoagulable state)
Symptoms of hypoglycemia
Low plasma glucose (70 mg/dL — alert value; 54 mg/dL — clinically significant)
Resolution of symptoms with glucose administration
Level 1: glucose 70 mg/dL (alert value — treat)
Level 2: glucose 54 mg/dL (clinically significant — requires immediate action)
Level 3: severe hypoglycemia — altered mental status, requires assistance from another person
In diabetic patients:
Insulin excess (most common): too much insulin, sulfonylurea, meglitinide
Missed meals, increased exercise, alcohol
Renal failure (decreased insulin clearance)
Drug interactions
In non-diabetic patients (less common, more complex workup):
Insulinoma: fasting hypoglycemia + elevated insulin + elevated C-peptide + negative sulfonylurea screen
Exogenous insulin: low glucose + elevated insulin + LOW C-peptide (exogenous insulin suppresses endogenous)
Sulfonylurea abuse: elevated insulin + elevated C-peptide + positive sulfonylurea screen
Adrenal insufficiency, liver failure, sepsis, alcohol, non-islet cell tumors (IGF-2)
Autonomic (adrenergic): tremor, palpitations, sweating, anxiety, hunger, pallor (triggered at ~55–65 mg/dL)
Neuroglycopenic: confusion, drowsiness, difficulty speaking, seizures, coma, focal deficits (triggered at 50 mg/dL)
Hypoglycemia unawareness: loss of autonomic warning symptoms (common in T1DM with recurrent hypoglycemia, elderly, beta-blocker use) → presents directly with neuroglycopenic symptoms → very dangerous
Conscious patient:
Rule of 15: give 15–20 g fast-acting glucose (3–4 glucose tablets, 120 mL juice, 1 tablespoon sugar) → recheck in 15 minutes → repeat if still 70
Unconscious / unable to swallow:
IV dextrose: D50W 25–50 mL (12.5–25 g glucose) IV push → recheck in 15 minutes
Glucagon: 1 mg IM/SC/IN if no IV access (stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis — ineffective in liver failure or alcohol-induced hypoglycemia due to depleted glycogen)
Post-treatment:
Eat a meal/snack with complex carbohydrates + protein
If sulfonylurea-induced: observe ≥24–72 hours (long half-life, recurrence risk — especially glibenclamide/glyburide); consider octreotide (inhibits insulin secretion)
Identify and correct the cause; adjust medications
Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia can be prolonged and recurrent — admit and observe
Octreotide: useful adjunct for sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia (suppresses insulin release)
Glucagon is ineffective in: liver failure, alcohol-induced hypoglycemia, glycogen storage diseases (depleted glycogen stores)
beta-blockers mask autonomic symptoms (except sweating) → hypoglycemia unawareness
Insulinoma workup: 72-hour supervised fast → measure glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, sulfonylurea screen when glucose 55
Factitious hypoglycemia (exogenous insulin): high insulin + LOW C-peptide (key differentiator from insulinoma)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is Whipple's triad? (Symptoms + low glucose + resolution with glucose)
How do you differentiate insulinoma from exogenous insulin? (Both have high insulin; insulinoma has HIGH C-peptide, exogenous has LOW C-peptide)
Why observe sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia for 24–72 hours? (Long half-life → recurrent hypoglycemia)
When is glucagon ineffective? (Liver failure, alcohol-induced, depleted glycogen)
What is the Rule of 15? (15 g glucose → recheck in 15 min → repeat if 70)
Elevated LDL-C (most atherogenic — primary target)
Elevated triglycerides (TG): 150 mg/dL; severe 500 mg/dL (pancreatitis risk)
Low HDL-C: 40 mg/dL (men), 50 mg/dL (women)
Mixed dyslipidemia: elevated LDL + elevated TG
Four statin benefit groups:
Clinical ASCVD (secondary prevention): high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg)
LDL ≥190 mg/dL (primary severe hypercholesterolemia): high-intensity statin

Diabetes, age 40–75: moderate-intensity statin (atorvastatin 10–20 mg or rosuvastatin 5–10 mg); high-intensity if multiple ASCVD risk factors (e.g., LDL-C ≥100 mg/dL, high blood pressure, smoking, overweight/obesity) or age 50–75
Age 40–75, no diabetes, LDL 70–189, 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%: moderate-to-high intensity statin (use pooled cohort equation to calculate risk)
Statin Intensity:

rows: Atorvastatin 10–20 mg, rosuvastatin 5–10 mg, simvastatin 20–40 mg
Very high risk (ASCVD, DM with organ damage, eGFR <30, FH with ASCVD)
High risk (single risk factor markedly elevated, DM ≥10 yr, moderate CKD)

blocks.text: ACC/AHA uses a "fire-and-forget" intensity-based approach; ESC uses target-based approach — both are acceptable

sections.title: 4. Non-Statin Therapies (if LDL not at goal on maximally tolerated statin)
sections.display: 4. Non-Statin Therapies (If Ldl Not At Goal On Maximally Tolerated Statin)

rows: Add to statin if LDL not at goal
ASCVD not at goal on statin + ezetimibe; FH
ACL inhibitor (upstream of statin in cholesterol synthesis)

blocks.text: TG 150–499: lifestyle (weight loss, exercise, reduce alcohol/refined carbs, omega-3); optimize statin; treat secondary causes (DM, hypothyroidism, obesity, alcohol, medications)

TG ≥500: HIGH risk of acute pancreatitis → fibrate (fenofibrate preferred — less drug interaction with statins than gemfibrozil); omega-3 fatty acids (icosapent ethyl 4 g/day — REDUCE-IT trial: reduced CV events in patients with TG 135–499 on statin)
Gemfibrozil + statin: AVOID (increased rhabdomyolysis risk — especially with simvastatin/lovastatin); fenofibrate is safer with statins
Myalgia: most common side effect (~5–10%); check CK if symptomatic
Rhabdomyolysis: rare but serious; CK 10x ULN + renal failure; risk factors — high dose, drug interactions (CYP3A4 inhibitors with simvastatin/atorvastatin), hypothyroidism, renal impairment
Hepatotoxicity: check LFTs at baseline; routine monitoring not required unless symptoms; mild transaminase elevation (3x ULN) is acceptable
New-onset diabetes: slight increase in risk (class effect); benefits outweigh risk in those with ASCVD indication
Drug interactions: simvastatin/lovastatin metabolized by CYP3A4 (avoid with azoles, macrolides, protease inhibitors, grapefruit); rosuvastatin/pravastatin have fewer interactions
LDL is the primary target — statins are the cornerstone of therapy
High-intensity statin for ALL patients with clinical ASCVD (secondary prevention)
Statin + gemfibrozil = avoid (rhabdomyolysis); statin + fenofibrate = acceptable
PCSK9 inhibitors: very potent LDL reduction; reserved for high-risk patients not at goal
Icosapent ethyl (Vascepa): the ONLY omega-3 with proven CV benefit (REDUCE-IT); standard fish oil supplements do NOT reduce CV events (STRENGTH, VITAL)
Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH): suspect if LDL ≥190 (adults) or ≥160 (children), tendon xanthomas, premature ASCVD, family history; autosomal dominant; needs aggressive treatment
Statin intolerance: try different statin, lower dose, alternate-day dosing, or bempedoic acid
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are the 4 statin benefit groups? (Clinical ASCVD, LDL ≥190, DM age 40–75, 10-year risk ≥7.5%)
What is the LDL target for very high-risk patients per ESC? (55 mg/dL + ≥50% reduction)
When is TG a pancreatitis risk? (≥500 mg/dL — use fibrate)
Why avoid gemfibrozil with statins? (Increased rhabdomyolysis risk)
What omega-3 has proven CV benefit? (Icosapent ethyl — REDUCE-IT)
Primary hypothyroidism (95%): thyroid gland failure → ↑ TSH, ↓ free T4
Secondary (central): pituitary failure → ↓/normal TSH, ↓ free T4
Subclinical hypothyroidism: ↑ TSH, normal free T4
Most common worldwide: iodine deficiency
Most common in iodine-sufficient areas: Hashimoto thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune — anti-TPO, anti-thyroglobulin antibodies)
Iatrogenic: post-thyroidectomy, post-radioactive iodine (RAI), post-radiation
Drugs: amiodarone (Wolff-Chaikoff effect — iodine excess), lithium, interferon-alpha, checkpoint inhibitors, tyrosine kinase inhibitors
Infiltrative: amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis
Transient: postpartum thyroiditis, subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis (recovery phase)
S — Slow heart rate (bradycardia)
L — Lethargy, fatigue
U — Unexplained weight gain
G — Goiter (Hashimoto)
G — Galactorrhea (↑ TRH → ↑ prolactin)
I — Intolerance to cold
S — Skin dry, hair coarse/brittle, loss of lateral eyebrows (Queen Anne sign)
H — Hyporeflexia (delayed relaxation phase of DTRs)
Other: constipation, menorrhagia, depression, myxedema (non-pitting edema), carpal tunnel syndrome, hyperlipidemia, hyponatremia (↓ free water clearance), pericardial effusion, macrocytic anemia
TSH: best initial screening test (elevated in primary hypothyroidism)
Free T4: low (confirms diagnosis)
Anti-TPO antibodies: positive in Hashimoto (most sensitive antibody)
Anti-thyroglobulin antibodies: also positive in Hashimoto
Lipid panel: hypercholesterolemia common
CBC: macrocytic or normocytic anemia
Levothyroxine (T4): drug of choice
Starting dose: 1.6 μg/kg/day (full replacement in young, healthy patients)
Elderly or cardiac disease: start low (25–50 μg/day), increase by 12.5–25 μg every 4–6 weeks
Take on empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast (or at bedtime, 3 hours after last meal)
Avoid concurrent calcium, iron, PPI (decrease absorption) — separate by ≥4 hours
Monitor TSH: 6–8 weeks after dose change; then annually when stable
Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mU/L (lower half of normal range); in elderly, higher TSH may be acceptable
Pregnancy: increase dose by ~30% as soon as pregnancy confirmed; target TSH per trimester (1st: 2.5; 2nd/3rd: 3.0 — though recent guidelines suggest 4.0 may be acceptable)
TSH 4.5–10 mU/L, normal free T4
Treat if: TSH 10, symptoms present, anti-TPO positive (high risk of progression), pregnancy or planning pregnancy, hyperlipidemia, goiter
If TSH 4.5–10 and asymptomatic: repeat in 6–12 weeks; consider treatment if persistent
Severe, decompensated hypothyroidism — medical emergency (mortality 30–60%)
Triggers: infection, cold exposure, sedatives, surgery, non-compliance
Presentation: hypothermia, altered mental status/coma, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia
Treatment:
IV levothyroxine loading dose: 200–400 μg IV bolus, then 50–100 μg/day IV
IV liothyronine (T3): 5–20 μg IV bolus, then 2.5–10 μg q8h (controversial but used in severe cases — T3 is the active hormone and conversion may be impaired)
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h (empiric — must rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency BEFORE giving thyroid hormone; giving T4 without cortisol can precipitate adrenal crisis)
Supportive: passive rewarming (NOT active — risk of vasodilation and shock), mechanical ventilation if needed, treat precipitant, correct hyponatremia/hypoglycemia
ICU admission
TSH is the BEST screening test for primary hypothyroidism
Hashimoto: most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas; anti-TPO most sensitive
Levothyroxine: take on empty stomach; separate from calcium/iron by 4 hours
Myxedema coma: give hydrocortisone BEFORE or WITH levothyroxine (prevent adrenal crisis)
Amiodarone causes BOTH hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff) and hyperthyroidism (Jod-Basedow)
Hypothyroidism causes: hyperlipidemia, hyponatremia, elevated CK, macrocytic anemia
Delayed relaxation phase of DTRs: classic physical exam finding
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the best initial test for hypothyroidism? (TSH)
What is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas? (Hashimoto thyroiditis)
Why give hydrocortisone before levothyroxine in myxedema coma? (Prevent adrenal crisis — thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism)
What drugs interfere with levothyroxine absorption? (Calcium, iron, PPI — separate by 4 hours)
What is the classic DTR finding? (Delayed relaxation phase — "hung-up" reflexes)
Hyperthyroidism: excess thyroid hormone production by the thyroid gland
Thyrotoxicosis: clinical syndrome of excess thyroid hormone (includes hyperthyroidism + exogenous/ectopic sources)
Primary: ↓ TSH, ↑ free T4 and/or free T3

rows: TSI/TRAb positive; diffuse goiter; ophthalmopathy (proptosis); pretibial myxedema; thyroid acropachy
Solitary functioning nodule; rest of gland suppressed
Painful tender thyroid; post-viral; elevated ESR; self-limited
Destructive thyroiditis; no underlying thyroid disease

blocks.text: RAIU (radioactive iodine uptake): KEY test to differentiate causes — high uptake = overproduction; low uptake = destruction/leakage or exogenous
Hypermetabolic: weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, sweating, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, hyperdefecation (NOT diarrhea)
Cardiovascular: tachycardia, palpitations, atrial fibrillation, widened pulse pressure, high-output heart failure
Neuromuscular: fine tremor, proximal myopathy, hyperreflexia (brisk DTRs)
Graves-specific: exophthalmos/proptosis (most common cause of unilateral or bilateral proptosis in adults), pretibial myxedema (non-pitting, peau d'orange), thyroid acropachy (clubbing)
Elderly: "apathetic thyrotoxicosis" — AF, weight loss, depression (may lack typical hyperadrenergic symptoms)
TSH (suppressed — best initial test)
Free T4 (elevated); if normal, check free T3 (T3 thyrotoxicosis — elevated T3 with normal T4)
TSI / TRAb: positive in Graves disease (pathognomonic)
RAIU scan: differentiates causes (see table above)
Anti-TPO: may be positive in Graves and Hashimoto
ESR: markedly elevated in subacute thyroiditis
A. Graves Disease — Three Options:

rows: First-line in many countries (Asia, Europe); preferred in mild disease, small goiter, pregnancy (1st trimester: PTU)

Methimazole preferred (once daily, fewer side effects); PTU for 1st trimester pregnancy and thyroid storm; Side effects: agranulocytosis (0.2–0.5% — check WBC if fever/sore throat), hepatotoxicity (PTU — hepatic necrosis; methimazole — cholestatic), rash
Preferred definitive therapy in US; contraindicated in pregnancy, severe ophthalmopathy
Most patients become hypothyroid → lifelong levothyroxine; may worsen Graves ophthalmopathy (give steroids if moderate-severe eye disease)
Large goiter, suspicious nodule, severe ophthalmopathy, contraindication to ATD/RAI, patient preference
Risks: recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (hoarseness), hypoparathyroidism (hypocalcemia), bleeding/hematoma; must be euthyroid before surgery (ATD + beta-blocker pre-op)

blocks.text: B. Symptomatic Control:
beta-blocker (propranolol preferred): controls tachycardia, tremor, anxiety; also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion; use in ALL thyrotoxic patients while awaiting definitive treatment
Propranolol: 20–40 mg q6–8h (also inhibits T4→T3 conversion — unique among beta-blockers)
C. Subacute Thyroiditis:
Self-limited (weeks to months)
NSAIDs for pain; steroids if severe
beta-blocker for thyrotoxic symptoms
No ATDs (not overproduction — it's hormone leakage from inflammation)
Life-threatening exacerbation of thyrotoxicosis; mortality 10–30%
Triggers: surgery, infection, trauma, RAI, iodine load, non-compliance with ATDs
Burch-Wartofsky Score ≥45: highly suggestive of thyroid storm
Presentation: high fever (40°C), tachycardia/AF, altered mental status (agitation → delirium → coma), GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice), heart failure
Treatment (in order — mnemonic: PTU-BITCH):
P — PTU (preferred over methimazole — also blocks T4→T3 conversion): 500–1000 mg loading, then 250 mg q4h
T — Thionamide (PTU as above)

U — Uptake block: SSKI (saturated solution of potassium iodide) or Lugol's iodine — give 1 HOUR AFTER PTU (Wolff-Chaikoff effect — blocks hormone release); if given before ATD, iodine will fuel more hormone synthesis
B — beta-Blocker: propranolol IV or PO (high doses may be needed: 60–80 mg q4h); esmolol IV if hemodynamically unstable
I — IV hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h (blocks T4→T3 conversion; treats possible relative adrenal insufficiency)
T — Temperature control: acetaminophen + cooling blankets; AVOID aspirin (displaces T4 from TBG → increases free T4)
C — Cholestyramine (binds thyroid hormone in gut — adjunct)
H — Hemodynamic support: IV fluids, ICU admission, treat precipitant
Mixed Type 1+2 is common → treat with both ATDs + steroids
Amiodarone has very long half-life (40–55 days) — effects persist long after discontinuation
Graves disease: most common cause of hyperthyroidism; TSI/TRAb is pathognomonic
RAIU scan: essential to differentiate high-uptake (overproduction) from low-uptake (destruction/exogenous)
Methimazole: preferred ATD (except 1st trimester pregnancy → use PTU)
Agranulocytosis: rare but life-threatening ATD side effect; instruct patients to report fever/sore throat immediately → check WBC
Thyroid storm: PTU first, then iodine 1 HOUR LATER; never give iodine before ATD
Propranolol: preferred beta-blocker (also blocks T4→T3 conversion)
Avoid aspirin in thyroid storm (increases free T4)
Subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH, normal T4/T3): treat if TSH 0.1, age ≥65, AF, osteoporosis, or cardiac disease
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Graves: most common cause; TSI positive; diffuse uptake on RAIU
TSH is the best initial test (suppressed)
RAIU differentiates causes: high = overproduction; low = destruction
Methimazole: first-line ATD; PTU for 1st trimester and thyroid storm
Agranulocytosis: check WBC if fever/sore throat on ATDs
Thyroid storm: PTU → iodine 1 hour later → beta-blocker → steroids
Never give iodine BEFORE ATD in thyroid storm
Avoid aspirin in thyroid storm (increases free T4)
Propranolol: blocks T4→T3 conversion (unique among beta-blockers)
Subacute thyroiditis: painful, elevated ESR, low RAIU, self-limited; NO ATDs
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism? (Graves disease)
Why give PTU before iodine in thyroid storm? (Iodine without ATD fuels more hormone synthesis; PTU blocks synthesis first)
When do you use PTU instead of methimazole? (1st trimester pregnancy, thyroid storm)
What is the most feared side effect of ATDs? (Agranulocytosis)
Why avoid aspirin in thyroid storm? (Displaces T4 from TBG → increases free T4)

parts.searchText: part vii: endocrinology metabolic part vii: endocrinology metabolic topic 53: type 2 diabetes mellitus topic 53 · type 2 diabetes mellitus 1. diagnostic criteria — ada / perkeni 1. diagnostic criteria — ada / perkeni test normal prediabetes diabetes references fasting plasma glucose (fpg) 100 mg/dl 100–125 mg/dl (ifg) ≥126 mg/dl ada 2024, perkeni 2-hour ogtt 140 mg/dl 140–199 mg/dl (igt) ≥200 mg/dl hba1c 5.7% 5.7–6.4% ≥6.5% random plasma glucose — — ≥200 mg/dl + symptoms diagnosis requires 2 abnormal tests on separate occasions (unless random glucose ≥200 + symptoms) hba1c may be unreliable in: hemoglobinopathies, hemolytic anemia, iron deficiency, pregnancy, ckd (falsely low or high depending on condition) 2. hba1c targets — individualized 2. hba1c targets — individualized population target hba1c references most adults 7.0% ada 2024 young, newly diagnosed, no comorbidities 6.5% (if achievable without hypoglycemia) elderly, multiple comorbidities, limited life expectancy 8.0% (or even 8.5%) pregnancy (pregestational) 6.0–6.5% 3. stepwise pharmacological management — ada/easd consensus 2022 3. stepwise pharmacological management — ada/easd consensus 2022 first-line: metformin + lifestyle modification (for all patients) metformin: 500 mg → titrate to 2000 mg/day; contraindicated if egfr 30; hold before contrast; side effects — gi (diarrhea, nausea), lactic acidosis (rare), b12 deficiency (monitor) second-line: based on comorbidities (patient-centered approach): comorbidity preferred add-on key trial references ascvd or high cv risk glp-1 ra (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) or sglt2i leader, sustain-6, rewind, empa-reg outcome, canvas ada 2024 heart failure sglt2 inhibitor (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) dapa-hf, emperor-reduced ckd (with albuminuria) sglt2 inhibitor (if egfr ≥20) or glp-1 ra credence, dapa-ckd, empa-kidney need for weight loss glp-1 ra (semaglutide) or dual gip/glp-1 ra (tirzepatide) step trials, surpass trials, surmount cost/access concern sulfonylurea or tzd — if hba1c still above target: add third agent or insulin insulin initiation: if hba1c 10%, symptomatic hyperglycemia, or catabolic features (weight loss, ketosis) start basal insulin (glargine or detemir): 10 units or 0.1–0.2 u/kg/day at bedtime; titrate by 2 units every 3 days to fasting glucose target (80–130 mg/dl) if still uncontrolled: add prandial insulin (basal-bolus) or premixed insulin 4. key drug classes 4. key drug classes class mechanism hypoglycemia risk weight effect key side effects references metformin ↓ hepatic glucose production low neutral/slight loss gi, lactic acidosis (rare), b12 deficiency sulfonylurea (glimepiride, gliclazide, glipizide) ↑ insulin secretion high gain hypoglycemia, weight gain sglt2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) ↓ renal glucose reabsorption low loss uti, genital mycotic infections, euglycemic dka, fournier gangrene (rare) glp-1 ra (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon, ↓ gastric emptying, ↑ satiety low significant loss gi (nausea, vomiting), pancreatitis (rare), medullary thyroid cancer (animal data — contraindicated in men2/mtc) dpp-4i (sitagliptin, linagliptin, vildagliptin) ↑ incretin levels low neutral generally well-tolerated; joint pain (rare) tzd (pioglitazone) ↑ insulin sensitivity (ppargamma) low gain fluid retention, hf exacerbation, fractures, bladder cancer (controversial) insulin ↑ glucose uptake high gain hypoglycemia, weight gain 5. monitoring 5. monitoring hba1c: every 3 months (if not at goal) or every 6 months (if stable) self-monitoring blood glucose (smbg): especially if on insulin or sulfonylurea annual screening: retinopathy (fundoscopy), nephropathy (urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio + egfr), neuropathy (monofilament test), foot exam, lipid panel, bp 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls metformin: first-line for all t2dm; hold if egfr 30; hold 48 hours before/after iv contrast (risk of lactic acidosis with aki) sglt2 inhibitors: cardiorenal benefits independent of glucose lowering; can use even in non-diabetic hf and ckd glp-1 ras: best for weight loss + cv benefit; oral semaglutide available tirzepatide (dual gip/glp-1 ra): most potent glucose and weight reduction sulfonylureas: cheap and effective but cause hypoglycemia and weight gain; avoid in elderly, ckd insulin is not a failure — it is necessary when beta-cell function declines sick day rules: hold metformin, sglt2i during acute illness; continue insulin (may need to increase) top 5 pimping questions: what is the first-line drug for t2dm? (metformin) what drug class is preferred in t2dm with ascvd? (glp-1 ra or sglt2i) what drug class is preferred in t2dm with hf? (sglt2 inhibitor) what is the hba1c target for most adults? (7.0% — ada) what are the contraindications to metformin?
