(egfr 30, acute illness with risk of lactic acidosis) topic 54: diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) topic 54 · diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) 1. definition 1. definition metabolic emergency in diabetes characterized by the triad: hyperglycemia + ketosis + metabolic acidosis. more common in t1dm but can occur in t2dm (especially ketosis-prone t2dm, common in southeast asia/africa). 2. diagnostic criteria — ada 2. diagnostic criteria — ada parameter mild moderate severe references glucose 250 mg/dl 250 mg/dl 250 mg/dl ada ph 7.25–7.30 7.00–7.24 7.00 bicarbonate 15–18 meq/l 10–14.9 meq/l 10 meq/l anion gap 10 12 12 ketones positive positive positive mental status alert alert/drowsy stupor/coma 3. precipitating factors (mnemonic: 5 i's) 3. precipitating factors (mnemonic: 5 i's) infection (most common precipitant — pneumonia, uti, sepsis) insulin non-compliance / inadequate insulin infarction (mi, stroke) initial presentation (new-onset t1dm) intoxication / drugs (cocaine, steroids, sglt2 inhibitors — euglycemic dka) 4. pathophysiology (high-yield) 4. pathophysiology (high-yield) insulin deficiency + counter-regulatory hormone excess (glucagon, cortisol, catecholamines, gh) → unrestrained lipolysis → free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis → beta-hydroxybutyrate + acetoacetate → anion gap metabolic acidosis. hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → dehydration + electrolyte losses. 5. stepwise management — ada protocol 5. stepwise management — ada protocol step 1: aggressive iv fluid resuscitation 0.9% ns: 1–1.5 l/hr in first hour (15–20 ml/kg/hr) then: if corrected na+ is low → continue ns; if corrected na+ is normal/high → switch to 0.45% ns at 250–500 ml/hr when glucose reaches 200 mg/dl → add d5w to iv fluids (d5 0.45% ns) to prevent hypoglycemia while continuing insulin step 2: potassium replacement (before insulin if k+ low) serum k+ action references 3.3 meq/l hold insulin; replace k+ aggressively (20–40 meq/hr) until k+ 3.3 ada 3.3–5.3 meq/l give 20–30 meq k+ in each liter of iv fluid; start insulin 5.3 meq/l do not give k+; recheck every 2 hours; start insulin total body k+ is always depleted in dka (even if serum k+ is normal/high — acidosis shifts k+ out of cells) insulin drives k+ into cells → can cause fatal hypokalemia if not replaced step 3: insulin regular insulin iv infusion: 0.1–0.14 u/kg/hr (no bolus needed per updated ada) alternative: 0.1 u/kg iv bolus, then 0.1 u/kg/hr infusion target glucose decrease: 50–70 mg/dl per hour when glucose reaches 200 mg/dl: reduce insulin to 0.02–0.05 u/kg/hr and add d5w; keep glucose 150–200 until dka resolves dka resolution criteria: glucose 200 + at least 2 of: ph 7.3, bicarbonate ≥15, anion gap ≤12 step 4: bicarbonate only if ph 6.9 (severe acidosis with hemodynamic compromise) 100 meq nahco3 in 400 ml water + 20 meq kcl over 2 hours routine bicarbonate is not recommended (may worsen intracellular acidosis, hypokalemia, paradoxical cns acidosis) step 5: transition to subcutaneous insulin when dka resolved and patient eating → overlap sc insulin with iv insulin for 1–2 hours before stopping iv calculate basal insulin dose: ~0.5 u/kg/day (or 80% of total daily iv insulin requirement) give sc insulin 1–2 hours before stopping iv drip (to prevent rebound dka) 6. monitoring 6. monitoring glucose: every 1 hour bmp (k+, na+, bun, cr): every 2–4 hours venous blood gas: every 2–4 hours (ph, bicarbonate) anion gap: every 2–4 hours urine output: hourly (foley if needed) 7. euglycemic dka 7. euglycemic dka dka with glucose 250 mg/dl causes: sglt2 inhibitors (most common cause now), pregnancy, starvation, alcohol, low-carb diet diagnosis often delayed because glucose is not markedly elevated treatment: same as dka but start d5w earlier; hold sglt2 inhibitor 8. complications of dka treatment 8. complications of dka treatment hypokalemia (most dangerous — from insulin without k+ replacement) hypoglycemia (from insulin without d5w when glucose drops) cerebral edema (children — from too-rapid correction of osmolality; avoid rapid fluid boluses in pediatric dka) non-anion gap metabolic acidosis (hyperchloremic — from ns resuscitation; resolves spontaneously) 9. clinical pearls 9. clinical pearls always check k+ before starting insulin — if k+ 3.3, hold insulin and replace k+ first kussmaul breathing: deep, rapid respirations — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis fruity breath: acetone (ketone body) serum na+ is falsely low in dka (pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia); corrected na+ = measured na+ + 1.6 x [(glucose − 100) / 100] beta-hydroxybutyrate is the predominant ketone in dka; urine ketone dipstick detects acetoacetate (may be falsely negative early); serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is preferred anion gap = na+ − (cl- + hco3-); normal 8–12; dka causes elevated ag dka vs hhs: dka = acidosis + ketosis + moderate hyperglycemia; hhs = severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + no significant ketosis top 10 testable facts: dka triad: hyperglycemia + ketosis + anion gap metabolic acidosis most common precipitant: infection check k+ before insulin — hold insulin if k+ 3.3 iv fluids first: ns 1–1.5 l in first hour insulin drip: 0.1–0.14 u/kg/hr; target glucose drop 50–70/hr add d5w when glucose reaches 200 mg/dl dka resolution: glucose 200 + ph 7.3 + bicarb ≥15 + ag ≤12 overlap sc insulin 1–2 hours before stopping iv insulin bicarbonate only if ph 6.9 euglycemic dka: sglt2 inhibitors — glucose may be normal top 5 pimping questions: what do you check before starting insulin in dka? (potassium — hold insulin if k+ 3.3) when do you add dextrose to iv fluids? (when glucose reaches 200 mg/dl) what are the criteria for dka resolution? (glucose 200, ph 7.3, bicarb ≥15, ag ≤12) when do you give bicarbonate? (only if ph 6.9) what causes euglycemic dka? (sglt2 inhibitors, pregnancy, starvation) topic 55: hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (hhs) topic 55 · hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (hhs) 1. definition 1. definition severe hyperglycemia with hyperosmolality and dehydration without significant ketoacidosis. occurs in t2dm (usually elderly with limited water access). 2. diagnostic criteria 2. diagnostic criteria parameter hhs dka (for comparison) references glucose 600 mg/dl 250 mg/dl ada serum osmolality 320 mosm/kg variable ph 7.30 7.30 bicarbonate 18 meq/l 18 meq/l ketones absent or trace moderate to large anion gap 12 12 mental status altered (stupor/coma) variable effective osmolality = 2(na+) + glucose/18 (do not include bun — freely crosses membranes) hhs has higher mortality than dka (5–20% vs 1–5%) — due to older age, comorbidities, delayed presentation 3. precipitants 3. precipitants infection (most common — pneumonia, uti, sepsis) non-compliance with medications new-onset t2dm drugs: steroids, thiazides, atypical antipsychotics acute illness: mi, stroke, surgery 4. management — similar to dka but key differences 4. management — similar to dka but key differences iv fluids (most critical — patients are profoundly dehydrated, often 8–10 l deficit): ns 1–1.5 l/hr in first hour then: corrected na+ low → continue ns; corrected na+ normal/high → 0.45% ns at 250–500 ml/hr more aggressive fluid resuscitation than dka (dehydration is more severe) fluids alone will significantly reduce glucose potassium: same protocol as dka (check before insulin) insulin: lower dose may be needed (0.05–0.1 u/kg/hr); some guidelines recommend fluids first for 1–2 hours before starting insulin (glucose drops significantly with fluids alone) target glucose decrease: 50–70 mg/dl per hour (same as dka) when glucose reaches 300 mg/dl → add d5w and reduce insulin rate bicarbonate: not needed (no significant acidosis) dvt prophylaxis: important (hyperosmolality → hypercoagulable state) monitor: glucose hourly, bmp every 2–4 hours, osmolality, mental status, i/o 5. hhs vs dka comparison 5. hhs vs dka comparison feature dka hhs references dm type usually t1dm usually t2dm (elderly) glucose 250 (often 300–800) 600 (often 1000) ketones significant absent/trace acidosis yes (ag metabolic acidosis) no (or mild) osmolality variable (may be elevated) 320 (always elevated) dehydration moderate (3–6 l) severe (8–10 l) mental status variable often altered (coma) mortality 1–5% 5–20% key treatment insulin + fluids fluids (primary) + insulin 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls hhs = severe dehydration + severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + altered mental status + no ketoacidosis fluids are more important than insulin in hhs (unlike dka where insulin is critical) mental status changes correlate with osmolality (not glucose level) avoid rapid correction of osmolality → risk of cerebral edema (especially in elderly) mixed dka/hhs can occur (~30% of cases) — treat as dka dvt prophylaxis is essential (hyperviscosity) top 5 pimping questions: what is the key difference between hhs and dka? (hhs: no significant ketoacidosis, higher glucose, higher osmolality, higher mortality) what is the most important initial treatment in hhs? (aggressive iv fluid resuscitation) what correlates with mental status in hhs? (serum osmolality, not glucose) when do you add d5w in hhs? (when glucose reaches 300 mg/dl — higher threshold than dka) why is dvt prophylaxis important? (hyperosmolality → hypercoagulable state) topic 56: hypoglycemia topic 56 · hypoglycemia 1. definition — whipple's triad 1. definition — whipple's triad symptoms of hypoglycemia low plasma glucose (70 mg/dl — alert value; 54 mg/dl — clinically significant) resolution of symptoms with glucose administration 2. classification 2. classification level 1: glucose 70 mg/dl (alert value — treat) level 2: glucose 54 mg/dl (clinically significant — requires immediate action) level 3: severe hypoglycemia — altered mental status, requires assistance from another person 3. causes 3. causes in diabetic patients: insulin excess (most common): too much insulin, sulfonylurea, meglitinide missed meals, increased exercise, alcohol renal failure (decreased insulin clearance) drug interactions in non-diabetic patients (less common, more complex workup): insulinoma: fasting hypoglycemia + elevated insulin + elevated c-peptide + negative sulfonylurea screen exogenous insulin: low glucose + elevated insulin + low c-peptide (exogenous insulin suppresses endogenous) sulfonylurea abuse: elevated insulin + elevated c-peptide + positive sulfonylurea screen adrenal insufficiency, liver failure, sepsis, alcohol, non-islet cell tumors (igf-2) 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation autonomic (adrenergic): tremor, palpitations, sweating, anxiety, hunger, pallor (triggered at ~55–65 mg/dl) neuroglycopenic: confusion, drowsiness, difficulty speaking, seizures, coma, focal deficits (triggered at 50 mg/dl) hypoglycemia unawareness: loss of autonomic warning symptoms (common in t1dm with recurrent hypoglycemia, elderly, beta-blocker use) → presents directly with neuroglycopenic symptoms → very dangerous 5. management 5. management conscious patient: rule of 15: give 15–20 g fast-acting glucose (3–4 glucose tablets, 120 ml juice, 1 tablespoon sugar) → recheck in 15 minutes → repeat if still 70 unconscious / unable to swallow: iv dextrose: d50w 25–50 ml (12.5–25 g glucose) iv push → recheck in 15 minutes glucagon: 1 mg im/sc/in if no iv access (stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis — ineffective in liver failure or alcohol-induced hypoglycemia due to depleted glycogen) post-treatment: eat a meal/snack with complex carbohydrates + protein if sulfonylurea-induced: observe ≥24–72 hours (long half-life, recurrence risk — especially glibenclamide/glyburide); consider octreotide (inhibits insulin secretion) identify and correct the cause; adjust medications 6. clinical pearls 6. clinical pearls sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia can be prolonged and recurrent — admit and observe octreotide: useful adjunct for sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia (suppresses insulin release) glucagon is ineffective in: liver failure, alcohol-induced hypoglycemia, glycogen storage diseases (depleted glycogen stores) beta-blockers mask autonomic symptoms (except sweating) → hypoglycemia unawareness insulinoma workup: 72-hour supervised fast → measure glucose, insulin, c-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, sulfonylurea screen when glucose 55 factitious hypoglycemia (exogenous insulin): high insulin + low c-peptide (key differentiator from insulinoma) top 5 pimping questions: what is whipple's triad? (symptoms + low glucose + resolution with glucose) how do you differentiate insulinoma from exogenous insulin? (both have high insulin; insulinoma has high c-peptide, exogenous has low c-peptide) why observe sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia for 24–72 hours? (long half-life → recurrent hypoglycemia) when is glucagon ineffective? (liver failure, alcohol-induced, depleted glycogen) what is the rule of 15? (15 g glucose → recheck in 15 min → repeat if 70) topic 57: dyslipidemia topic 57 · dyslipidemia 1. classification 1. classification elevated ldl-c (most atherogenic — primary target) elevated triglycerides (tg): 150 mg/dl; severe 500 mg/dl (pancreatitis risk) low hdl-c: 40 mg/dl (men), 50 mg/dl (women) mixed dyslipidemia: elevated ldl + elevated tg 2. statin therapy — acc/aha 2018 cholesterol guidelines 2. statin therapy — acc/aha 2018 cholesterol guidelines four statin benefit groups: clinical ascvd (secondary prevention): high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) ldl ≥190 mg/dl (primary severe hypercholesterolemia): high-intensity statin diabetes, age 40–75: moderate-intensity statin (atorvastatin 10–20 mg or rosuvastatin 5–10 mg); high-intensity if multiple ascvd risk factors (e.g., ldl-c ≥100 mg/dl, high blood pressure, smoking, overweight/obesity) or age 50–75 age 40–75, no diabetes, ldl 70–189, 10-year ascvd risk ≥7.5%: moderate-to-high intensity statin (use pooled cohort equation to calculate risk) statin intensity: intensity ldl reduction examples references high ≥50% atorvastatin 40–80 mg, rosuvastatin 20–40 mg acc/aha 2018 moderate 30–49% atorvastatin 10–20 mg, rosuvastatin 5–10 mg, simvastatin 20–40 mg low <30% simvastatin 10 mg, pravastatin 10–20 mg 3. ldl targets (esc/eas 2019 — target-based approach) 3. ldl targets (esc/eas 2019 — target-based approach) risk category ldl target references very high risk (ascvd, dm with organ damage, egfr <30, fh with ascvd) <55 mg/dl and ≥50% reduction esc/eas 2019 high risk (single risk factor markedly elevated, dm ≥10 yr, moderate ckd) <70 mg/dl and ≥50% reduction moderate risk <100 mg/dl low risk <116 mg/dl acc/aha uses a "fire-and-forget" intensity-based approach; esc uses target-based approach — both are acceptable 4. non-statin therapies (if ldl not at goal on maximally tolerated statin) 4. non-statin therapies (if ldl not at goal on maximally tolerated statin) agent mechanism ldl reduction key trial indications references ezetimibe blocks intestinal cholesterol absorption ~18–20% improve-it add to statin if ldl not at goal pcsk9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) ↑ ldl receptor recycling ~50–60% fourier, odyssey outcomes ascvd not at goal on statin + ezetimibe; fh bempedoic acid acl inhibitor (upstream of statin in cholesterol synthesis) ~15–18% clear outcomes statin-intolerant patients inclisiran sirna targeting pcsk9 ~50% orion trials twice-yearly injection; ascvd or fh 5. hypertriglyceridemia management 5. hypertriglyceridemia management tg 150–499: lifestyle (weight loss, exercise, reduce alcohol/refined carbs, omega-3); optimize statin; treat secondary causes (dm, hypothyroidism, obesity, alcohol, medications) tg ≥500: high risk of acute pancreatitis → fibrate (fenofibrate preferred — less drug interaction with statins than gemfibrozil); omega-3 fatty acids (icosapent ethyl 4 g/day — reduce-it trial: reduced cv events in patients with tg 135–499 on statin) gemfibrozil + statin: avoid (increased rhabdomyolysis risk — especially with simvastatin/lovastatin); fenofibrate is safer with statins 6. statin side effects monitoring 6. statin side effects monitoring myalgia: most common side effect (~5–10%); check ck if symptomatic rhabdomyolysis: rare but serious; ck 10x uln + renal failure; risk factors — high dose, drug interactions (cyp3a4 inhibitors with simvastatin/atorvastatin), hypothyroidism, renal impairment hepatotoxicity: check lfts at baseline; routine monitoring not required unless symptoms; mild transaminase elevation (3x uln) is acceptable new-onset diabetes: slight increase in risk (class effect); benefits outweigh risk in those with ascvd indication drug interactions: simvastatin/lovastatin metabolized by cyp3a4 (avoid with azoles, macrolides, protease inhibitors, grapefruit); rosuvastatin/pravastatin have fewer interactions 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls ldl is the primary target — statins are the cornerstone of therapy high-intensity statin for all patients with clinical ascvd (secondary prevention) statin + gemfibrozil = avoid (rhabdomyolysis); statin + fenofibrate = acceptable pcsk9 inhibitors: very potent ldl reduction; reserved for high-risk patients not at goal icosapent ethyl (vascepa): the only omega-3 with proven cv benefit (reduce-it); standard fish oil supplements do not reduce cv events (strength, vital) familial hypercholesterolemia (fh): suspect if ldl ≥190 (adults) or ≥160 (children), tendon xanthomas, premature ascvd, family history; autosomal dominant; needs aggressive treatment statin intolerance: try different statin, lower dose, alternate-day dosing, or bempedoic acid top 5 pimping questions: what are the 4 statin benefit groups? (clinical ascvd, ldl ≥190, dm age 40–75, 10-year risk ≥7.5%) what is the ldl target for very high-risk patients per esc? (55 mg/dl + ≥50% reduction) when is tg a pancreatitis risk? (≥500 mg/dl — use fibrate) why avoid gemfibrozil with statins? (increased rhabdomyolysis risk) what omega-3 has proven cv benefit? (icosapent ethyl — reduce-it) topic 58: hypothyroidism topic 58 · hypothyroidism 1. definition classification 1. definition classification primary hypothyroidism (95%): thyroid gland failure → ↑ tsh, ↓ free t4 secondary (central): pituitary failure → ↓/normal tsh, ↓ free t4 subclinical hypothyroidism: ↑ tsh, normal free t4 2. etiology 2. etiology most common worldwide: iodine deficiency most common in iodine-sufficient areas: hashimoto thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune — anti-tpo, anti-thyroglobulin antibodies) iatrogenic: post-thyroidectomy, post-radioactive iodine (rai), post-radiation drugs: amiodarone (wolff-chaikoff effect — iodine excess), lithium, interferon-alpha, checkpoint inhibitors, tyrosine kinase inhibitors infiltrative: amyloidosis, sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis transient: postpartum thyroiditis, subacute (de quervain) thyroiditis (recovery phase) 3. clinical presentation (mnemonic: sluggish) 3. clinical presentation (mnemonic: sluggish) s — slow heart rate (bradycardia) l — lethargy, fatigue u — unexplained weight gain g — goiter (hashimoto) g — galactorrhea (↑ trh → ↑ prolactin) i — intolerance to cold s — skin dry, hair coarse/brittle, loss of lateral eyebrows (queen anne sign) h — hyporeflexia (delayed relaxation phase of dtrs) other: constipation, menorrhagia, depression, myxedema (non-pitting edema), carpal tunnel syndrome, hyperlipidemia, hyponatremia (↓ free water clearance), pericardial effusion, macrocytic anemia 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup tsh: best initial screening test (elevated in primary hypothyroidism) free t4: low (confirms diagnosis) anti-tpo antibodies: positive in hashimoto (most sensitive antibody) anti-thyroglobulin antibodies: also positive in hashimoto lipid panel: hypercholesterolemia common cbc: macrocytic or normocytic anemia 5. treatment 5. treatment levothyroxine (t4): drug of choice starting dose: 1.6 μg/kg/day (full replacement in young, healthy patients) elderly or cardiac disease: start low (25–50 μg/day), increase by 12.5–25 μg every 4–6 weeks take on empty stomach, 30–60 minutes before breakfast (or at bedtime, 3 hours after last meal) avoid concurrent calcium, iron, ppi (decrease absorption) — separate by ≥4 hours monitor tsh: 6–8 weeks after dose change; then annually when stable target tsh: 0.5–2.5 mu/l (lower half of normal range); in elderly, higher tsh may be acceptable pregnancy: increase dose by ~30% as soon as pregnancy confirmed; target tsh per trimester (1st: 2.5; 2nd/3rd: 3.0 — though recent guidelines suggest 4.0 may be acceptable) 6. subclinical hypothyroidism 6. subclinical hypothyroidism tsh 4.5–10 mu/l, normal free t4 treat if: tsh 10, symptoms present, anti-tpo positive (high risk of progression), pregnancy or planning pregnancy, hyperlipidemia, goiter if tsh 4.5–10 and asymptomatic: repeat in 6–12 weeks; consider treatment if persistent 7. myxedema coma (emergency) 7. myxedema coma (emergency) severe, decompensated hypothyroidism — medical emergency (mortality 30–60%) triggers: infection, cold exposure, sedatives, surgery, non-compliance presentation: hypothermia, altered mental status/coma, bradycardia, hypotension, hypoventilation, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia treatment: iv levothyroxine loading dose: 200–400 μg iv bolus, then 50–100 μg/day iv iv liothyronine (t3): 5–20 μg iv bolus, then 2.5–10 μg q8h (controversial but used in severe cases — t3 is the active hormone and conversion may be impaired) iv hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h (empiric — must rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency before giving thyroid hormone; giving t4 without cortisol can precipitate adrenal crisis) supportive: passive rewarming (not active — risk of vasodilation and shock), mechanical ventilation if needed, treat precipitant, correct hyponatremia/hypoglycemia icu admission 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls tsh is the best screening test for primary hypothyroidism hashimoto: most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas; anti-tpo most sensitive levothyroxine: take on empty stomach; separate from calcium/iron by 4 hours myxedema coma: give hydrocortisone before or with levothyroxine (prevent adrenal crisis) amiodarone causes both hypothyroidism (wolff-chaikoff) and hyperthyroidism (jod-basedow) hypothyroidism causes: hyperlipidemia, hyponatremia, elevated ck, macrocytic anemia delayed relaxation phase of dtrs: classic physical exam finding top 5 pimping questions: what is the best initial test for hypothyroidism? (tsh) what is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient areas? (hashimoto thyroiditis) why give hydrocortisone before levothyroxine in myxedema coma? (prevent adrenal crisis — thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism) what drugs interfere with levothyroxine absorption? (calcium, iron, ppi — separate by 4 hours) what is the classic dtr finding? (delayed relaxation phase — "hung-up" reflexes) topic 59: hyperthyroidism topic 59 · hyperthyroidism 1. definition classification 1. definition classification hyperthyroidism: excess thyroid hormone production by the thyroid gland thyrotoxicosis: clinical syndrome of excess thyroid hormone (includes hyperthyroidism + exogenous/ectopic sources) primary: ↓ tsh, ↑ free t4 and/or free t3 2. etiology 2. etiology cause raiu key features references graves disease (most common) diffusely increased tsi/trab positive; diffuse goiter; ophthalmopathy (proptosis); pretibial myxedema; thyroid acropachy toxic multinodular goiter (plummer) patchy increased elderly; multiple nodules; no ophthalmopathy toxic adenoma single hot nodule solitary functioning nodule; rest of gland suppressed subacute thyroiditis (de quervain) decreased painful tender thyroid; post-viral; elevated esr; self-limited painless/postpartum thyroiditis decreased painless; autoimmune; transient hyperthyroid → hypothyroid factitious/exogenous decreased exogenous t4/t3 ingestion; low thyroglobulin amiodarone-induced (type 1) variable iodine-induced (jod-basedow); underlying thyroid disease amiodarone-induced (type 2) decreased destructive thyroiditis; no underlying thyroid disease raiu (radioactive iodine uptake): key test to differentiate causes — high uptake = overproduction; low uptake = destruction/leakage or exogenous 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation hypermetabolic: weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, sweating, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, hyperdefecation (not diarrhea) cardiovascular: tachycardia, palpitations, atrial fibrillation, widened pulse pressure, high-output heart failure neuromuscular: fine tremor, proximal myopathy, hyperreflexia (brisk dtrs) graves-specific: exophthalmos/proptosis (most common cause of unilateral or bilateral proptosis in adults), pretibial myxedema (non-pitting, peau d'orange), thyroid acropachy (clubbing) elderly: "apathetic thyrotoxicosis" — af, weight loss, depression (may lack typical hyperadrenergic symptoms) 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup tsh (suppressed — best initial test) free t4 (elevated); if normal, check free t3 (t3 thyrotoxicosis — elevated t3 with normal t4) tsi / trab: positive in graves disease (pathognomonic) raiu scan: differentiates causes (see table above) anti-tpo: may be positive in graves and hashimoto esr: markedly elevated in subacute thyroiditis 5. treatment 5. treatment a. graves disease — three options: treatment mechanism indications key points references antithyroid drugs (atds) block thyroid hormone synthesis first-line in many countries (asia, europe); preferred in mild disease, small goiter, pregnancy (1st trimester: ptu) methimazole preferred (once daily, fewer side effects); ptu for 1st trimester pregnancy and thyroid storm; side effects: agranulocytosis (0.2–0.5% — check wbc if fever/sore throat), hepatotoxicity (ptu — hepatic necrosis; methimazole — cholestatic), rash ata 2016 radioactive iodine (rai) destroys thyroid tissue preferred definitive therapy in us; contraindicated in pregnancy, severe ophthalmopathy most patients become hypothyroid → lifelong levothyroxine; may worsen graves ophthalmopathy (give steroids if moderate-severe eye disease) surgery (thyroidectomy) removes thyroid large goiter, suspicious nodule, severe ophthalmopathy, contraindication to atd/rai, patient preference risks: recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (hoarseness), hypoparathyroidism (hypocalcemia), bleeding/hematoma; must be euthyroid before surgery (atd + beta-blocker pre-op) b. symptomatic control: beta-blocker (propranolol preferred): controls tachycardia, tremor, anxiety; also blocks peripheral t4→t3 conversion; use in all thyrotoxic patients while awaiting definitive treatment propranolol: 20–40 mg q6–8h (also inhibits t4→t3 conversion — unique among beta-blockers) c. subacute thyroiditis: self-limited (weeks to months) nsaids for pain; steroids if severe beta-blocker for thyrotoxic symptoms no atds (not overproduction — it's hormone leakage from inflammation) 6. thyroid storm (emergency) 6. thyroid storm (emergency) life-threatening exacerbation of thyrotoxicosis; mortality 10–30% triggers: surgery, infection, trauma, rai, iodine load, non-compliance with atds burch-wartofsky score ≥45: highly suggestive of thyroid storm presentation: high fever (40°c), tachycardia/af, altered mental status (agitation → delirium → coma), gi symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice), heart failure treatment (in order — mnemonic: ptu-bitch): p — ptu (preferred over methimazole — also blocks t4→t3 conversion): 500–1000 mg loading, then 250 mg q4h t — thionamide (ptu as above) u — uptake block: sski (saturated solution of potassium iodide) or lugol's iodine — give 1 hour after ptu (wolff-chaikoff effect — blocks hormone release); if given before atd, iodine will fuel more hormone synthesis b — beta-blocker: propranolol iv or po (high doses may be needed: 60–80 mg q4h); esmolol iv if hemodynamically unstable i — iv hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h (blocks t4→t3 conversion; treats possible relative adrenal insufficiency) t — temperature control: acetaminophen + cooling blankets; avoid aspirin (displaces t4 from tbg → increases free t4) c — cholestyramine (binds thyroid hormone in gut — adjunct) h — hemodynamic support: iv fluids, icu admission, treat precipitant 7. amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (ait) 7. amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis (ait) feature type 1 type 2 references mechanism iodine-induced (jod-basedow) destructive thyroiditis underlying thyroid yes (goiter, graves, mng) no raiu normal/increased decreased treatment atds (methimazole) steroids (prednisone) doppler us increased vascularity decreased vascularity mixed type 1+2 is common → treat with both atds + steroids amiodarone has very long half-life (40–55 days) — effects persist long after discontinuation 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls graves disease: most common cause of hyperthyroidism; tsi/trab is pathognomonic raiu scan: essential to differentiate high-uptake (overproduction) from low-uptake (destruction/exogenous) methimazole: preferred atd (except 1st trimester pregnancy → use ptu) agranulocytosis: rare but life-threatening atd side effect; instruct patients to report fever/sore throat immediately → check wbc thyroid storm: ptu first, then iodine 1 hour later; never give iodine before atd propranolol: preferred beta-blocker (also blocks t4→t3 conversion) avoid aspirin in thyroid storm (increases free t4) subclinical hyperthyroidism (low tsh, normal t4/t3): treat if tsh 0.1, age ≥65, af, osteoporosis, or cardiac disease top 10 testable facts: graves: most common cause; tsi positive; diffuse uptake on raiu tsh is the best initial test (suppressed) raiu differentiates causes: high = overproduction; low = destruction methimazole: first-line atd; ptu for 1st trimester and thyroid storm agranulocytosis: check wbc if fever/sore throat on atds thyroid storm: ptu → iodine 1 hour later → beta-blocker → steroids never give iodine before atd in thyroid storm avoid aspirin in thyroid storm (increases free t4) propranolol: blocks t4→t3 conversion (unique among beta-blockers) subacute thyroiditis: painful, elevated esr, low raiu, self-limited; no atds top 5 pimping questions: what is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism? (graves disease) why give ptu before iodine in thyroid storm? (iodine without atd fuels more hormone synthesis; ptu blocks synthesis first) when do you use ptu instead of methimazole? (1st trimester pregnancy, thyroid storm) what is the most feared side effect of atds? (agranulocytosis) why avoid aspirin in thyroid storm? (displaces t4 from tbg → increases free t4)

parts.title: PART VIII: RHEUMATOLOGY & IMMUNOLOGY

blocks.text: Chronic, systemic autoimmune disease characterized by symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis primarily affecting small joints, leading to joint destruction if untreated.
Female:male = 3:1; peak onset 30–50 years
Risk factors: female sex, smoking (strongest modifiable risk factor — associated with anti-CCP positivity), family history, HLA-DR4 (shared epitope)

Joint involvement: symmetric polyarthritis of small joints — MCP, PIP, wrists, MTP (feet); morning stiffness 1 hour (improves with activity); spares DIP (DIP involvement = osteoarthritis or psoriatic arthritis)
Hand deformities (late/untreated): ulnar deviation (MCP), swan neck (PIP hyperextension + DIP flexion), boutonnière (PIP flexion + DIP hyperextension), Z-thumb

Extra-articular manifestations: rheumatoid nodules (extensor surfaces — pathognomonic), interstitial lung disease, pleural effusion (low glucose, high LDH), pericarditis, Felty syndrome (RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia), scleritis/episcleritis, secondary amyloidosis (AA), atlantoaxial subluxation (C1-C2 — check flexion/extension X-ray before intubation)
RF (rheumatoid factor): sensitive (~70–80%) but NOT specific (positive in many conditions — SLE, Sjögren, infections, elderly)
Anti-CCP (anti-citrullinated peptide antibody): highly specific (~95%) for RA; positive early; associated with more aggressive/erosive disease
ESR/CRP: elevated (disease activity markers)
X-ray hands/feet: early — periarticular osteopenia, soft tissue swelling; late — joint space narrowing, marginal erosions, subluxation
Ultrasound/MRI: more sensitive than X-ray for early synovitis and erosions

rows: Serology: negative RF AND negative anti-CCP
Acute phase reactants: normal ESR AND CRP

blocks.text: Goal: remission or low disease activity within 6 months; early aggressive treatment prevents joint destruction.
Step 1: Start DMARD within 3 months of diagnosis
Methotrexate (MTX): first-line DMARD; 7.5–25 mg/week PO or SC; takes 4–8 weeks for effect
Always co-prescribe folic acid 1 mg/day (reduces side effects — stomatitis, cytopenias, GI)
Side effects: hepatotoxicity (avoid alcohol), bone marrow suppression, pneumonitis, teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy — stop 3 months before conception)
Monitoring: CBC, LFTs, creatinine every 4–8 weeks initially, then every 3 months
Contraindications: pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe renal impairment, active infection, bone marrow failure
Step 2: If inadequate response to MTX at 3–6 months
Add or switch to biologic DMARD:
TNF inhibitors (first-line biologic): adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab, golimumab
Non-TNF biologics: abatacept (CTLA-4), rituximab (anti-CD20 — especially if RF/anti-CCP positive), tocilizumab (anti-IL-6), sarilumab (anti-IL-6)

JAK inhibitors (targeted synthetic DMARDs): tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib — oral; FDA safety warning: increased risk of CV events, malignancy, thrombosis (especially in patients ≥65 or with CV risk factors)
Step 3: Bridging therapy
Low-dose glucocorticoids (prednisone ≤10 mg/day): bridge while waiting for DMARD effect; taper and discontinue within 3–6 months
Intra-articular steroids: for acute flares of individual joints

rows: Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis/cysts

blocks.text: Anti-CCP: most specific test for RA; predicts erosive disease
Methotrexate: first-line DMARD; always give with folic acid
Treat early and aggressively — "window of opportunity" in first 3–6 months
Screen for TB and hepatitis B before starting biologics (reactivation risk)
TNF inhibitors: increased risk of TB reactivation, serious infections, demyelinating disease, HF exacerbation
Atlantoaxial subluxation: check cervical spine X-ray before surgery/intubation in RA patients
RA pleural effusion: very low glucose (25 mg/dL), high LDH — can mimic empyema
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most specific antibody for RA? (Anti-CCP — ~95% specific)
What is the first-line DMARD? (Methotrexate)
What supplement must be given with methotrexate? (Folic acid — prevents side effects)
What joints does RA spare? (DIP joints — DIP involvement suggests OA or psoriatic arthritis)
What must you screen for before starting biologics? (TB and hepatitis B)
Degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage loss, subchondral bone changes, and osteophyte formation. Most common form of arthritis worldwide.
Age (50 — strongest risk factor), obesity (especially knee OA), female sex, joint trauma/overuse, genetics, malalignment
Weight-bearing: knees (most common), hips
Hands: DIP (Heberden nodes), PIP (Bouchard nodes), 1st CMC (thumb base)
Spine: cervical and lumbar (facet joints)
Spares: MCP, wrists, ankles, elbows (unless secondary OA from trauma/occupation)
Pain: worse with activity, relieved by rest; "end-of-day" pain
Stiffness: 30 minutes (brief, unlike RA)
Crepitus on joint movement
Bony enlargement (osteophytes): Heberden nodes (DIP), Bouchard nodes (PIP)
No systemic symptoms (no fever, no fatigue — unlike RA)
Reduced range of motion
Labs: RF negative, anti-CCP negative, ESR/CRP normal (OA is NOT inflammatory on labs)
X-ray (classic findings — mnemonic: LOSS):
L — Loss of joint space (asymmetric narrowing)
O — Osteophytes (bony spurs at joint margins)
S — Subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density)
S — Subchondral cysts
Synovial fluid: non-inflammatory — WBC 2000/μL, clear, viscous (vs RA: WBC 2000–50,000, turbid)
MRI: not routinely needed; useful for meniscal/ligament pathology or atypical presentation
Step 1: Non-pharmacological (foundation for ALL patients)

Patient education, weight loss (if overweight — most impactful modifiable factor for knee OA), exercise (low-impact: swimming, cycling, walking), physical therapy, assistive devices (cane — use in contralateral hand), knee bracing
Step 2: Pharmacological

rows: First-line for hand/knee OA; fewer systemic side effects
Effective but GI/CV/renal risks; use lowest dose, shortest duration; add PPI if GI risk
Weak evidence; no longer strongly recommended as first-line (AAOS downgraded); may use if NSAIDs contraindicated
SNRI; useful for chronic OA pain (especially knee); consider if NSAIDs contraindicated or inadequate
Short-term relief (weeks); limit to 3–4 injections/year per joint; no long-term disease modification
Controversial; weak/conditional recommendation; may benefit some knee OA patients
Weak opioid; last resort; avoid chronic use

blocks.text: Step 3: Surgical
Total joint arthroplasty (TKR/THR): definitive treatment for end-stage OA refractory to conservative management; highly effective for pain relief and function
Glucosamine/chondroitin: no convincing evidence of benefit (GAIT trial — negative); not recommended by ACR
Opioids: avoid chronic use (dependence, no disease modification)
DMARDs/biologics: NOT indicated (OA is not autoimmune)
Colchicine: not standard for OA (used for gout/pseudogout)
OA is a clinical + radiographic diagnosis — no specific blood test
Weight loss is the single most effective non-pharmacological intervention for knee OA
Topical NSAIDs preferred over oral for hand/knee OA (fewer systemic effects)
Heberden nodes (DIP) and Bouchard nodes (PIP) are pathognomonic for OA
Erosive/inflammatory OA: variant affecting DIP/PIP with inflammatory features; can mimic RA but RF/anti-CCP negative
Secondary OA: consider in young patients — trauma, hemochromatosis (2nd/3rd MCP — "iron fist"), acromegaly, calcium pyrophosphate disease
Hemochromatosis OA: classically affects 2nd and 3rd MCP joints with hook-like osteophytes + chondrocalcinosis
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What are Heberden and Bouchard nodes? (Heberden = DIP osteophytes; Bouchard = PIP osteophytes)
What are the classic X-ray findings of OA? (LOSS: Loss of joint space, Osteophytes, Subchondral sclerosis, Subchondral cysts)
What is the most effective non-pharmacological treatment for knee OA? (Weight loss)
What is the first-line pharmacological treatment? (Topical NSAIDs for hand/knee OA)
What joint involvement suggests secondary OA from hemochromatosis? (2nd and 3rd MCP joints)
Chronic, multisystem autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibody production and immune complex deposition, with relapsing-remitting course. Predominantly affects young women of childbearing age.
Female:male = 9:1; peak onset 15–45 years
More common and severe in African, Asian, and Hispanic populations
S — Serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis)
O — Oral ulcers (painless — unlike Behçet which is painful)
A — Arthritis (non-erosive, symmetric polyarthritis — unlike RA which is erosive)
P — Photosensitivity
B — Blood disorders (hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia)
R — Renal disease (lupus nephritis — most important prognostic factor)
A — ANA positive
I — Immunologic (anti-dsDNA, anti-Smith, antiphospholipid antibodies, low complement)
N — Neuropsychiatric (seizures, psychosis, cognitive dysfunction)
M — Malar rash (butterfly rash — spares nasolabial folds)
D — Discoid rash (scarring, can cause alopecia)

rows: Screening test; negative ANA virtually rules out SLE
Lupus nephritis; correlates with disease activity; levels fluctuate
Most specific for SLE; does NOT correlate with activity
Neonatal lupus (congenital heart block); Sjögren overlap; subacute cutaneous lupus; photosensitivity

blocks.text: Entry criterion: ANA ≥1:80 (must be positive)
Then score clinical + immunological domains (weighted scoring); ≥10 points = classified as SLE
Key domains: fever, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal, antiphospholipid, complement, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm
Occurs in ~50% of SLE patients; determines prognosis
Presentation: proteinuria, hematuria, hypertension, rising creatinine, nephrotic/nephritic syndrome
Diagnosis: renal biopsy (gold standard) — ISN/RPS classification:

rows: Induction: MMF or cyclophosphamide + steroids; Maintenance: MMF or azathioprine
Diffuse proliferative (≥50% glomeruli) — MOST COMMON and MOST SEVERE
If nephrotic: MMF or cyclophosphamide + steroids

blocks.text: Class III and IV: most aggressive; require induction with mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) or IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus protocol: low-dose cyclophosphamide equally effective with fewer side effects) + glucocorticoids → maintenance with MMF or azathioprine
Voclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor): recently approved add-on for lupus nephritis (AURORA trial)
Belimumab (anti-BLyS/BAFF): approved for active lupus nephritis as add-on (BLISS-LN trial)

rows: Hydroxychloroquine (ALL SLE patients) + NSAIDs ± low-dose steroids
HCQ + moderate-dose steroids + azathioprine or MTX
Nephritis (III/IV/V), CNS lupus, severe cytopenias, DAH
HCQ + high-dose steroids + MMF or cyclophosphamide ± belimumab/voclosporin

blocks.text: Hydroxychloroquine (HCQ): cornerstone of SLE treatment
ALL SLE patients should be on HCQ (unless contraindicated)
Benefits: reduces flares, reduces organ damage accrual, improves survival, reduces thrombosis risk, improves lipid profile
Dose: ≤5 mg/kg/day (to minimize retinal toxicity)
Side effect: retinal toxicity (bull's eye maculopathy) — baseline eye exam, then annually after 5 years (or sooner if risk factors: renal impairment, high dose, tamoxifen use)
Common drugs (mnemonic: HIP): Hydralazine, Isoniazid, Procainamide (also minocycline, TNF inhibitors, methyldopa)
Features: arthralgia, serositis, fever, rash; typically spares kidneys and CNS
Anti-histone antibody positive (~95%); anti-dsDNA usually negative
Treatment: stop offending drug → symptoms resolve in weeks to months
~30–40% of SLE patients have antiphospholipid antibodies
Clinical: arterial/venous thrombosis, recurrent pregnancy loss (especially 2nd/3rd trimester)
Labs: lupus anticoagulant (most thrombogenic), anticardiolipin, anti-beta2-glycoprotein I
Paradox: lupus anticoagulant causes prolonged aPTT in vitro but THROMBOSIS in vivo
Treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (DOACs inferior — TRAPS trial); aspirin for primary prevention in obstetric APS
Anti-dsDNA titers: rise with flares (especially nephritis)
Complement (C3, C4): decrease with flares (consumed by immune complexes)
ESR: rises with activity; CRP usually normal in SLE flares (if CRP elevated, suspect infection rather than flare — important distinction)
Urinalysis + urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: monitor for nephritis
ANA: most sensitive; anti-Sm: most specific; anti-dsDNA: correlates with nephritis activity
HCQ for ALL SLE patients — reduces flares, organ damage, mortality
SLE flare vs infection: CRP usually normal in flare; elevated CRP suggests infection
Lupus nephritis Class IV: most common and most severe — needs aggressive immunosuppression
Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone positive, spares kidneys/CNS, resolves with drug cessation
Pregnancy in SLE: anti-Ro positive → risk of neonatal lupus/congenital heart block; antiphospholipid antibodies → recurrent pregnancy loss; HCQ is safe in pregnancy (continue it)
Avoid live vaccines in immunosuppressed SLE patients
Sunscreen is essential (photosensitivity triggers flares)
Top 10 Testable Facts:
ANA: most sensitive; anti-Sm: most specific for SLE
Anti-dsDNA: correlates with nephritis activity; levels fluctuate
HCQ: ALL SLE patients; retinal toxicity screening after 5 years
Lupus nephritis Class IV: most common/severe; treat with MMF or cyclophosphamide
Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone positive; spares kidneys/CNS
Complement (C3/C4) decreases during flares
CRP normal in SLE flare; elevated CRP = think infection
Anti-Ro: neonatal lupus, congenital heart block
APS: lupus anticoagulant → thrombosis (not bleeding); warfarin, not DOACs
SLE arthritis is non-erosive (unlike RA)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the most specific antibody for SLE? (Anti-Smith)
What antibody correlates with lupus nephritis activity? (Anti-dsDNA)
How do you differentiate SLE flare from infection? (CRP normal in flare; elevated in infection)
What drug should ALL SLE patients receive? (Hydroxychloroquine)
What is the most common and severe class of lupus nephritis? (Class IV — diffuse proliferative)

sections.title: 1. Classification — Chapel Hill Consensus (by vessel size)
sections.display: 1. Classification — Chapel Hill Consensus (By Vessel Size)

blocks.text: Large Vessel Vasculitis:

rows: Age ≥50; new headache, jaw claudication, visual loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy — EMERGENCY), scalp tenderness, PMR overlap; ESR markedly elevated (often ≥50)
Temporal artery biopsy (gold standard — skip lesions possible → long specimen); temporal artery US (halo sign)
High-dose steroids IMMEDIATELY (do NOT wait for biopsy); prednisone 40–60 mg/day (or IV methylprednisolone if visual symptoms); tocilizumab as steroid-sparing (GiACTA trial)
Young women (15–30); aorta and branches; limb claudication, absent pulses ("pulseless disease"), BP discrepancy between arms, bruits, aortic regurgitation
CT/MR angiography (vessel wall thickening, stenosis); ESR/CRP elevated

blocks.text: Medium Vessel Vasculitis:

rows: Middle-aged men; associated with HBV; skin (livedo reticularis, nodules, ulcers), peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex), renal (hypertension, renal infarcts — NOT glomerulonephritis), GI (mesenteric ischemia), testicular pain; spares lungs
Mesenteric/renal angiography (microaneurysms — "string of beads"); biopsy (fibrinoid necrosis); ANCA negative
Steroids + cyclophosphamide; treat HBV if associated (antivirals + plasma exchange)
Children 5; fever ≥5 days + conjunctivitis, oral changes (strawberry tongue), rash, extremity changes (edema/desquamation), cervical lymphadenopathy; coronary artery aneurysms
IVIG + high-dose aspirin (one of few pediatric aspirin indications)

blocks.text: Small Vessel Vasculitis — ANCA-Associated:

rows: Upper airway (sinusitis, saddle nose, subglottic stenosis), lower airway (pulmonary nodules/cavities, hemoptysis), kidney (rapidly progressive GN — crescentic); granulomatous inflammation
Kidney (RPGN — most common presentation), pulmonary capillaritis (DAH), NO granulomas, NO upper airway involvement
Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, formerly Churg-Strauss)
Asthma (precedes vasculitis by years), eosinophilia (marked), sinusitis, peripheral neuropathy, cardiac involvement; granulomas + eosinophilic infiltration

blocks.text: Small Vessel — Immune Complex:

rows: Children; palpable purpura (buttocks/legs), arthralgia, abdominal pain (intussusception), IgA nephropathy; skin biopsy: IgA deposits
Associated with HCV; palpable purpura, arthralgia, GN, peripheral neuropathy; low complement; cryoglobulins positive
Pulmonary hemorrhage + RPGN; anti-GBM antibodies; linear IgG on IF; young men (lung + kidney)

blocks.text: Suspect vasculitis: multisystem disease + constitutional symptoms + elevated inflammatory markers
Key labs: ESR/CRP, ANCA (c-ANCA/PR3, p-ANCA/MPO), complement, cryoglobulins, hepatitis B/C serologies, urinalysis (RBC casts = GN), creatinine
Biopsy: gold standard for most vasculitides (skin, kidney, nerve, temporal artery)
Imaging: CT/MR angiography for large/medium vessel; CT chest for pulmonary involvement
Induction (active disease): high-dose glucocorticoids + cyclophosphamide or rituximab (RAVE trial — rituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for ANCA-associated vasculitis)
Maintenance: azathioprine, MTX, or rituximab (MAINRITSAN — rituximab superior to azathioprine for maintenance)
Avacopan (C5a receptor inhibitor): approved for ANCA-associated vasculitis as steroid-sparing agent (ADVOCATE trial)
Plasma exchange: consider in severe renal disease (Cr 5.7) or DAH (PEXIVAS trial — no benefit for renal outcomes but may help in DAH)
GCA: NEVER delay steroids for biopsy — visual loss is irreversible; biopsy remains positive for 2 weeks after starting steroids
c-ANCA/PR3 = GPA; p-ANCA/MPO = MPA and EGPA
PAN: ANCA negative; associated with HBV; spares lungs; angiography shows microaneurysms
GPA triad: upper airway + lower airway + kidney
EGPA: asthma + eosinophilia + vasculitis
Goodpasture: linear IgG on immunofluorescence (vs lupus nephritis = granular/"lumpy bumpy")
Rituximab: increasingly first-line for ANCA-associated vasculitis (RAVE, RITUXVAS)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the ANCA pattern in GPA vs MPA? (GPA: c-ANCA/PR3; MPA: p-ANCA/MPO)
What is the emergency in GCA? (Visual loss — start steroids immediately, do not wait for biopsy)
What vasculitis is ANCA-negative and associated with HBV? (PAN)
What is the triad of GPA? (Upper airway + lower airway + kidney)
What is the immunofluorescence pattern in Goodpasture? (Linear IgG along GBM)

rows: Anaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema, drug allergy (penicillin, NSAIDs)
Hemolytic anemia (methyldopa, penicillin), thrombocytopenia (heparin-HIT, quinine), agranulocytosis (clozapine, methimazole)
Serum sickness (antithymocyte globulin, rituximab), serum sickness-like reaction (cefaclor, TMP-SMX), vasculitis
Contact dermatitis, SJS/TEN, DRESS, maculopapular drug eruption, tuberculin skin test

blocks.text: Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (TEN):
SJS: 10% BSA detachment; SJS-TEN overlap: 10–30%; TEN: 30%
Presentation: prodrome (fever, malaise) → painful erythematous macules → targetoid lesions → epidermal detachment (positive Nikolsky sign) → mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital)
Common drugs: allopurinol (most common overall), carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, sulfonamides (TMP-SMX), NSAIDs (piroxicam), nevirapine
HLA associations: HLA-B5801 (allopurinol — screen in Southeast Asian, African American, Korean populations); HLA-B1502 (carbamazepine — screen in Southeast Asian populations)

Management: STOP offending drug immediately; ICU/burn unit; supportive care (wound care, fluid resuscitation, pain management, ophthalmology consult); cyclosporine may improve outcomes; IVIG controversial; avoid steroids (controversial — some guidelines allow short course early)
SCORTEN score: predicts mortality in SJS/TEN (age, malignancy, BSA, tachycardia, BUN, glucose, bicarbonate)
DRESS Syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms):
Onset: 2–8 weeks after drug initiation (delayed)
Presentation: fever, diffuse morbilliform rash (face edema characteristic), eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes, organ involvement (liver most common — hepatitis; also kidney, lung, heart)
Common drugs: allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, dapsone, vancomycin, minocycline
Associated with HHV-6 reactivation
Treatment: stop drug; systemic corticosteroids (unlike SJS/TEN where steroids are controversial, steroids are mainstay for DRESS)
Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis (AGEP):
Onset: 1–5 days after drug
Presentation: acute fever + widespread non-follicular sterile pustules on erythematous base; resolves with drug withdrawal
Common drugs: aminopenicillins, macrolides, quinolones, terbinafine
Treatment: stop drug; self-limited; topical steroids
Most commonly reported drug allergy (~10% of patients); however, ~90% of patients labeled "penicillin allergic" are NOT truly allergic (lost sensitivity over time or had non-allergic reaction)
Cross-reactivity with cephalosporins: ~1–2% (much lower than previously thought — 10% is outdated)
Management: penicillin skin testing → if negative, can safely give penicillin; if positive, desensitization if penicillin is essential (e.g., neurosyphilis, endocarditis)
Amoxicillin rash in EBV (mononucleosis): NOT a true allergy — maculopapular rash due to immune activation; does not predict future penicillin allergy
SJS/TEN: STOP the drug — most important intervention; Nikolsky sign positive; mucosal involvement
DRESS: delayed onset (2–8 weeks); eosinophilia + organ involvement; treat with steroids
HLA-B5801: screen before allopurinol in at-risk populations (Southeast Asian, African American)
HLA-B1502: screen before carbamazepine in Southeast Asian populations
Penicillin allergy: 90% are not truly allergic; skin testing can clarify
Cross-reactivity penicillin-cephalosporin: ~1–2% (not 10%)
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What differentiates SJS from TEN? (BSA detachment: SJS 10%, TEN 30%)
What HLA should be screened before allopurinol? (HLA-B5801)
What is the hallmark of DRESS? (Delayed onset 2–8 weeks, eosinophilia, organ involvement — especially liver)
What is the true cross-reactivity rate between penicillin and cephalosporins? (~1–2%, not 10%)
What is the Nikolsky sign? (Epidermal detachment with lateral pressure — positive in SJS/TEN, pemphigus vulgaris)
Severe, life-threatening, systemic hypersensitivity reaction (Type I — IgE-mediated) with rapid onset. Can also be non-IgE mediated (anaphylactoid — same clinical presentation, same treatment).
Anaphylaxis is highly likely when ANY ONE of the following is present:
Acute onset (minutes to hours) of skin/mucosal involvement (urticaria, angioedema, flushing) + respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheeze, stridor) OR hypotension/end-organ dysfunction
Two or more of the following rapidly after exposure to likely allergen: skin/mucosal involvement, respiratory compromise, hypotension, persistent GI symptoms (cramping, vomiting)
Hypotension after exposure to known allergen (SBP 90 or 30% decrease from baseline)
Foods: peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, milk, eggs (most common cause in children)
Drugs: antibiotics (penicillin — most common drug cause), NSAIDs, neuromuscular blocking agents (most common cause in perioperative anaphylaxis)
Insect stings: Hymenoptera (bees, wasps, hornets)
Latex
Exercise-induced, idiopathic (no identifiable trigger in ~20%)
Radiocontrast media (anaphylactoid — non-IgE mediated)

sections.title: 4. Clinical Presentation (rapid onset — usually within minutes)
sections.display: 4. Clinical Presentation (Rapid Onset — Usually Within Minutes)

blocks.text: Skin (90%): urticaria, flushing, angioedema, pruritus
Respiratory (70%): laryngeal edema (stridor — most dangerous), bronchospasm (wheeze), dyspnea
Cardiovascular (45%): hypotension, tachycardia, syncope, cardiac arrest
GI (45%): nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping
Biphasic reaction: recurrence of symptoms 1–72 hours after initial resolution (occurs in ~5–20%) → observe 4–6 hours minimum (some guidelines say 6–12 hours for severe reactions)
EPINEPHRINE IS THE FIRST-LINE TREATMENT — no contraindications in anaphylaxis

rows: 0.3–0.5 mg (1:1000 = 1 mg/mL) IM in mid-anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5–15 min if no improvement; pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg)
Supine with legs elevated (Trendelenburg); if dyspneic, allow to sit up; if vomiting, recovery position; do NOT stand up suddenly (can cause "empty ventricle syndrome" → cardiac arrest)
NS 1–2 L rapid bolus for hypotension (20 mL/kg in children)

Nebulized salbutamol (bronchospasm); H1-antihistamine (diphenhydramine 25–50 mg IV — for urticaria/pruritus only, NOT a substitute for epinephrine); H2-antihistamine (ranitidine/famotidine); corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg — may prevent biphasic reaction, but no evidence for acute benefit)

Epinephrine IV infusion: 0.1–1 μg/kg/min (1:10,000 concentration = 0.1 mg/mL); titrate to effect Vasopressors: norepinephrine or vasopressin if refractory hypotension despite epinephrine + fluids Glucagon: 1–5 mg IV bolus then 5–15 μg/min infusion — essential for patients on beta-blockers (beta-blockers blunt epinephrine response; glucagon bypasses beta-receptor and activates adenylyl cyclase directly → increases cAMP → inotropic/chronotropic effect) Intubation/surgical airway: if severe laryngeal edema unresponsive to epinephrine

blocks.text: Observe minimum 4–6 hours (some guidelines: 6–12 hours for severe reactions) — biphasic reaction risk
Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) for ALL patients at discharge
Educate on trigger avoidance and auto-injector use
Referral to allergist/immunologist for: skin prick testing, specific IgE, component-resolved diagnostics

Serum tryptase: draw within 1–2 hours of symptom onset (peaks at 1–2 hours, returns to baseline by 6 hours); elevated tryptase supports anaphylaxis diagnosis (especially useful when diagnosis is uncertain); baseline tryptase at ≥24 hours to rule out mastocytosis
Medical alert bracelet/identification
Giving antihistamines INSTEAD of epinephrine — antihistamines do NOT treat hypotension or airway obstruction; they only relieve urticaria/pruritus
Using IV epinephrine as first-line (should be IM first; IV only for refractory/cardiac arrest)
Delaying epinephrine — delays are associated with increased mortality
Not observing for biphasic reaction
Not prescribing auto-injector at discharge
Giving epinephrine subcutaneously (slower absorption than IM; IM in thigh is preferred)
Epinephrine IM in the thigh: FIRST and MOST IMPORTANT treatment — no absolute contraindications
Antihistamines and steroids are ADJUNCTS only — never delay epinephrine for them
beta-blocker patients: may have refractory anaphylaxis → use glucagon
ACE inhibitor patients: higher risk of severe angioedema
Tryptase: confirmatory test; draw within 1–2 hours; elevated in anaphylaxis (not elevated in food-related anaphylaxis in some cases)
Biphasic reaction: 5–20% of cases; observe ≥4–6 hours
Anaphylaxis can occur WITHOUT skin findings (~10–20%) — do not rule out anaphylaxis just because there is no urticaria
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Epinephrine IM (0.3–0.5 mg, 1:1000) in anterolateral thigh — FIRST-LINE
Repeat epinephrine every 5–15 minutes if needed
Antihistamines are NOT a substitute for epinephrine
Glucagon for patients on beta-blockers (bypasses beta-receptor blockade)
Biphasic reaction: observe ≥4–6 hours
Tryptase: draw within 1–2 hours; confirms diagnosis
Prescribe auto-injector at discharge — ALWAYS
Most common trigger in children: food; in adults: drugs and insect stings
Anaphylaxis can occur without skin findings (10–20%)
Do NOT let patient stand up suddenly — risk of "empty ventricle" cardiac arrest
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis? (Epinephrine IM — no exceptions)
What do you give for anaphylaxis in a patient on beta-blockers? (Glucagon — bypasses beta-receptor)
What is the role of antihistamines in anaphylaxis? (Adjunct only — treats urticaria/pruritus, NOT hypotension or airway obstruction)
What lab confirms anaphylaxis? (Serum tryptase — draw within 1–2 hours)
Why observe patients after anaphylaxis? (Biphasic reaction — recurrence in 5–20% within 1–72 hours)

parts.searchText: part viii: rheumatology & immunology part viii: rheumatology & immunology topic 60: rheumatoid arthritis topic 60 · rheumatoid arthritis 1. definition 1. definition chronic, systemic autoimmune disease characterized by symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis primarily affecting small joints, leading to joint destruction if untreated. 2. epidemiology risk factors 2. epidemiology risk factors female:male = 3:1; peak onset 30–50 years risk factors: female sex, smoking (strongest modifiable risk factor — associated with anti-ccp positivity), family history, hla-dr4 (shared epitope) 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation joint involvement: symmetric polyarthritis of small joints — mcp, pip, wrists, mtp (feet); morning stiffness 1 hour (improves with activity); spares dip (dip involvement = osteoarthritis or psoriatic arthritis) hand deformities (late/untreated): ulnar deviation (mcp), swan neck (pip hyperextension + dip flexion), boutonnière (pip flexion + dip hyperextension), z-thumb extra-articular manifestations: rheumatoid nodules (extensor surfaces — pathognomonic), interstitial lung disease, pleural effusion (low glucose, high ldh), pericarditis, felty syndrome (ra + splenomegaly + neutropenia), scleritis/episcleritis, secondary amyloidosis (aa), atlantoaxial subluxation (c1-c2 — check flexion/extension x-ray before intubation) 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup rf (rheumatoid factor): sensitive (~70–80%) but not specific (positive in many conditions — sle, sjögren, infections, elderly) anti-ccp (anti-citrullinated peptide antibody): highly specific (~95%) for ra; positive early; associated with more aggressive/erosive disease esr/crp: elevated (disease activity markers) x-ray hands/feet: early — periarticular osteopenia, soft tissue swelling; late — joint space narrowing, marginal erosions, subluxation ultrasound/mri: more sensitive than x-ray for early synovitis and erosions 5. 2010 acr/eular classification criteria (score ≥6/10) 5. 2010 acr/eular classification criteria (score ≥6/10) category score references joint involvement: 1 large joint 0 acr/eular 2010 2–10 large joints 1 1–3 small joints 2 4–10 small joints 3 10 joints (≥1 small) 5 serology: negative rf and negative anti-ccp 0 low-positive rf or low-positive anti-ccp 2 high-positive rf or high-positive anti-ccp 3 acute phase reactants: normal esr and crp 0 abnormal esr or crp 1 duration: 6 weeks 0 ≥6 weeks 1 6. treatment — treat-to-target strategy (acr/eular) 6. treatment — treat-to-target strategy (acr/eular) goal: remission or low disease activity within 6 months; early aggressive treatment prevents joint destruction. step 1: start dmard within 3 months of diagnosis methotrexate (mtx): first-line dmard; 7.5–25 mg/week po or sc; takes 4–8 weeks for effect always co-prescribe folic acid 1 mg/day (reduces side effects — stomatitis, cytopenias, gi) side effects: hepatotoxicity (avoid alcohol), bone marrow suppression, pneumonitis, teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy — stop 3 months before conception) monitoring: cbc, lfts, creatinine every 4–8 weeks initially, then every 3 months contraindications: pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe renal impairment, active infection, bone marrow failure step 2: if inadequate response to mtx at 3–6 months add or switch to biologic dmard: tnf inhibitors (first-line biologic): adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab, golimumab non-tnf biologics: abatacept (ctla-4), rituximab (anti-cd20 — especially if rf/anti-ccp positive), tocilizumab (anti-il-6), sarilumab (anti-il-6) jak inhibitors (targeted synthetic dmards): tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib — oral; fda safety warning: increased risk of cv events, malignancy, thrombosis (especially in patients ≥65 or with cv risk factors) step 3: bridging therapy low-dose glucocorticoids (prednisone ≤10 mg/day): bridge while waiting for dmard effect; taper and discontinue within 3–6 months intra-articular steroids: for acute flares of individual joints 7. ra vs oa (commonly tested) 7. ra vs oa (commonly tested) feature rheumatoid arthritis osteoarthritis references type inflammatory/autoimmune degenerative/mechanical age 30–50 50 joints mcp, pip, wrists (symmetric) dip, pip, cmc, knees, hips dip involvement no (spares dip) yes (heberden nodes) morning stiffness 1 hour 30 minutes stiffness pattern improves with activity worsens with activity systemic symptoms yes (fatigue, fever) no labs rf+, anti-ccp+, ↑esr/crp normal x-ray erosions, periarticular osteopenia osteophytes, joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis/cysts 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls anti-ccp: most specific test for ra; predicts erosive disease methotrexate: first-line dmard; always give with folic acid treat early and aggressively — "window of opportunity" in first 3–6 months screen for tb and hepatitis b before starting biologics (reactivation risk) tnf inhibitors: increased risk of tb reactivation, serious infections, demyelinating disease, hf exacerbation atlantoaxial subluxation: check cervical spine x-ray before surgery/intubation in ra patients ra pleural effusion: very low glucose (25 mg/dl), high ldh — can mimic empyema top 5 pimping questions: what is the most specific antibody for ra? (anti-ccp — ~95% specific) what is the first-line dmard? (methotrexate) what supplement must be given with methotrexate? (folic acid — prevents side effects) what joints does ra spare? (dip joints — dip involvement suggests oa or psoriatic arthritis) what must you screen for before starting biologics? (tb and hepatitis b) topic 61: osteoarthritis topic 61 · osteoarthritis 1. definition 1. definition degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage loss, subchondral bone changes, and osteophyte formation. most common form of arthritis worldwide. 2. risk factors 2. risk factors age (50 — strongest risk factor), obesity (especially knee oa), female sex, joint trauma/overuse, genetics, malalignment 3. joints affected 3. joints affected weight-bearing: knees (most common), hips hands: dip (heberden nodes), pip (bouchard nodes), 1st cmc (thumb base) spine: cervical and lumbar (facet joints) spares: mcp, wrists, ankles, elbows (unless secondary oa from trauma/occupation) 4. clinical presentation 4. clinical presentation pain: worse with activity, relieved by rest; "end-of-day" pain stiffness: 30 minutes (brief, unlike ra) crepitus on joint movement bony enlargement (osteophytes): heberden nodes (dip), bouchard nodes (pip) no systemic symptoms (no fever, no fatigue — unlike ra) reduced range of motion 5. diagnosis — clinical (no specific lab test) 5. diagnosis — clinical (no specific lab test) labs: rf negative, anti-ccp negative, esr/crp normal (oa is not inflammatory on labs) x-ray (classic findings — mnemonic: loss): l — loss of joint space (asymmetric narrowing) o — osteophytes (bony spurs at joint margins) s — subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density) s — subchondral cysts synovial fluid: non-inflammatory — wbc 2000/μl, clear, viscous (vs ra: wbc 2000–50,000, turbid) mri: not routinely needed; useful for meniscal/ligament pathology or atypical presentation 6. stepwise management — acr/eular 6. stepwise management — acr/eular step 1: non-pharmacological (foundation for all patients) patient education, weight loss (if overweight — most impactful modifiable factor for knee oa), exercise (low-impact: swimming, cycling, walking), physical therapy, assistive devices (cane — use in contralateral hand), knee bracing step 2: pharmacological agent notes references topical nsaids (diclofenac gel) first-line for hand/knee oa; fewer systemic side effects acr 2019 oral nsaids (ibuprofen, naproxen, celecoxib) effective but gi/cv/renal risks; use lowest dose, shortest duration; add ppi if gi risk acetaminophen (paracetamol) weak evidence; no longer strongly recommended as first-line (aaos downgraded); may use if nsaids contraindicated duloxetine snri; useful for chronic oa pain (especially knee); consider if nsaids contraindicated or inadequate intra-articular corticosteroids short-term relief (weeks); limit to 3–4 injections/year per joint; no long-term disease modification intra-articular hyaluronic acid controversial; weak/conditional recommendation; may benefit some knee oa patients tramadol weak opioid; last resort; avoid chronic use step 3: surgical total joint arthroplasty (tkr/thr): definitive treatment for end-stage oa refractory to conservative management; highly effective for pain relief and function 7. what not to use 7. what not to use glucosamine/chondroitin: no convincing evidence of benefit (gait trial — negative); not recommended by acr opioids: avoid chronic use (dependence, no disease modification) dmards/biologics: not indicated (oa is not autoimmune) colchicine: not standard for oa (used for gout/pseudogout) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls oa is a clinical + radiographic diagnosis — no specific blood test weight loss is the single most effective non-pharmacological intervention for knee oa topical nsaids preferred over oral for hand/knee oa (fewer systemic effects) heberden nodes (dip) and bouchard nodes (pip) are pathognomonic for oa erosive/inflammatory oa: variant affecting dip/pip with inflammatory features; can mimic ra but rf/anti-ccp negative secondary oa: consider in young patients — trauma, hemochromatosis (2nd/3rd mcp — "iron fist"), acromegaly, calcium pyrophosphate disease hemochromatosis oa: classically affects 2nd and 3rd mcp joints with hook-like osteophytes + chondrocalcinosis top 5 pimping questions: what are heberden and bouchard nodes? (heberden = dip osteophytes; bouchard = pip osteophytes) what are the classic x-ray findings of oa? (loss: loss of joint space, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, subchondral cysts) what is the most effective non-pharmacological treatment for knee oa? (weight loss) what is the first-line pharmacological treatment? (topical nsaids for hand/knee oa) what joint involvement suggests secondary oa from hemochromatosis? (2nd and 3rd mcp joints) topic 62: systemic lupus erythematosus (sle) topic 62 · systemic lupus erythematosus (sle) 1. definition 1. definition chronic, multisystem autoimmune disease characterized by autoantibody production and immune complex deposition, with relapsing-remitting course. predominantly affects young women of childbearing age. 2. epidemiology 2. epidemiology female:male = 9:1; peak onset 15–45 years more common and severe in african, asian, and hispanic populations 3. clinical presentation (mnemonic: soap brain md) 3. clinical presentation (mnemonic: soap brain md) s — serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis) o — oral ulcers (painless — unlike behçet which is painful) a — arthritis (non-erosive, symmetric polyarthritis — unlike ra which is erosive) p — photosensitivity b — blood disorders (hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia) r — renal disease (lupus nephritis — most important prognostic factor) a — ana positive i — immunologic (anti-dsdna, anti-smith, antiphospholipid antibodies, low complement) n — neuropsychiatric (seizures, psychosis, cognitive dysfunction) m — malar rash (butterfly rash — spares nasolabial folds) d — discoid rash (scarring, can cause alopecia) 4. autoantibodies 4. autoantibodies antibody sensitivity specificity clinical association ana ~95% (most sensitive) low (positive in many conditions) screening test; negative ana virtually rules out sle anti-dsdna ~60–70% high (~95%) lupus nephritis; correlates with disease activity; levels fluctuate anti-smith (anti-sm) ~25–30% highest (~99%) most specific for sle; does not correlate with activity anti-histone ~95% in drug-induced lupus moderate drug-induced lupus (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, minocycline) anti-ro (ssa) variable moderate neonatal lupus (congenital heart block); sjögren overlap; subacute cutaneous lupus; photosensitivity anti-la (ssb) variable moderate sjögren overlap; protective against nephritis antiphospholipid (acl, anti-beta2gp1, lupus anticoagulant) ~30–40% moderate thrombosis, recurrent pregnancy loss (antiphospholipid syndrome) 5. 2019 eular/acr classification criteria 5. 2019 eular/acr classification criteria entry criterion: ana ≥1:80 (must be positive) then score clinical + immunological domains (weighted scoring); ≥10 points = classified as sle key domains: fever, hematologic, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal, antiphospholipid, complement, anti-dsdna, anti-sm 6. lupus nephritis (most important complication) 6. lupus nephritis (most important complication) occurs in ~50% of sle patients; determines prognosis presentation: proteinuria, hematuria, hypertension, rising creatinine, nephrotic/nephritic syndrome diagnosis: renal biopsy (gold standard) — isn/rps classification: class description treatment references i minimal mesangial observation isn/rps ii mesangial proliferative low-dose immunosuppression or observation iii focal proliferative (50% glomeruli) induction: mmf or cyclophosphamide + steroids; maintenance: mmf or azathioprine eular/acr 2024 iv diffuse proliferative (≥50% glomeruli) — most common and most severe same as class iii (aggressive induction) v membranous if nephrotic: mmf or cyclophosphamide + steroids vi advanced sclerotic (≥90% sclerosed) no immunosuppression (irreversible); prepare for dialysis/transplant class iii and iv: most aggressive; require induction with mycophenolate mofetil (mmf) or iv cyclophosphamide (euro-lupus protocol: low-dose cyclophosphamide equally effective with fewer side effects) + glucocorticoids → maintenance with mmf or azathioprine voclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor): recently approved add-on for lupus nephritis (aurora trial) belimumab (anti-blys/baff): approved for active lupus nephritis as add-on (bliss-ln trial) 7. stepwise management of sle (by severity) 7. stepwise management of sle (by severity) severity manifestations treatment references mild skin, arthritis, fatigue hydroxychloroquine (all sle patients) + nsaids ± low-dose steroids eular 2023 moderate serositis, hematologic, refractory skin/joints hcq + moderate-dose steroids + azathioprine or mtx severe nephritis (iii/iv/v), cns lupus, severe cytopenias, dah hcq + high-dose steroids + mmf or cyclophosphamide ± belimumab/voclosporin refractory failure of standard therapy rituximab (off-label), plasma exchange, ivig hydroxychloroquine (hcq): cornerstone of sle treatment all sle patients should be on hcq (unless contraindicated) benefits: reduces flares, reduces organ damage accrual, improves survival, reduces thrombosis risk, improves lipid profile dose: ≤5 mg/kg/day (to minimize retinal toxicity) side effect: retinal toxicity (bull's eye maculopathy) — baseline eye exam, then annually after 5 years (or sooner if risk factors: renal impairment, high dose, tamoxifen use) 8. drug-induced lupus 8. drug-induced lupus common drugs (mnemonic: hip): hydralazine, isoniazid, procainamide (also minocycline, tnf inhibitors, methyldopa) features: arthralgia, serositis, fever, rash; typically spares kidneys and cns anti-histone antibody positive (~95%); anti-dsdna usually negative treatment: stop offending drug → symptoms resolve in weeks to months 9. antiphospholipid syndrome (aps) in sle 9. antiphospholipid syndrome (aps) in sle ~30–40% of sle patients have antiphospholipid antibodies clinical: arterial/venous thrombosis, recurrent pregnancy loss (especially 2nd/3rd trimester) labs: lupus anticoagulant (most thrombogenic), anticardiolipin, anti-beta2-glycoprotein i paradox: lupus anticoagulant causes prolonged aptt in vitro but thrombosis in vivo treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (doacs inferior — traps trial); aspirin for primary prevention in obstetric aps 10. monitoring sle activity 10. monitoring sle activity anti-dsdna titers: rise with flares (especially nephritis) complement (c3, c4): decrease with flares (consumed by immune complexes) esr: rises with activity; crp usually normal in sle flares (if crp elevated, suspect infection rather than flare — important distinction) urinalysis + urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: monitor for nephritis 11. clinical pearls 11. clinical pearls ana: most sensitive; anti-sm: most specific; anti-dsdna: correlates with nephritis activity hcq for all sle patients — reduces flares, organ damage, mortality sle flare vs infection: crp usually normal in flare; elevated crp suggests infection lupus nephritis class iv: most common and most severe — needs aggressive immunosuppression drug-induced lupus: anti-histone positive, spares kidneys/cns, resolves with drug cessation pregnancy in sle: anti-ro positive → risk of neonatal lupus/congenital heart block; antiphospholipid antibodies → recurrent pregnancy loss; hcq is safe in pregnancy (continue it) avoid live vaccines in immunosuppressed sle patients sunscreen is essential (photosensitivity triggers flares) top 10 testable facts: ana: most sensitive; anti-sm: most specific for sle anti-dsdna: correlates with nephritis activity; levels fluctuate hcq: all sle patients; retinal toxicity screening after 5 years lupus nephritis class iv: most common/severe; treat with mmf or cyclophosphamide drug-induced lupus: anti-histone positive; spares kidneys/cns complement (c3/c4) decreases during flares crp normal in sle flare; elevated crp = think infection anti-ro: neonatal lupus, congenital heart block aps: lupus anticoagulant → thrombosis (not bleeding); warfarin, not doacs sle arthritis is non-erosive (unlike ra) top 5 pimping questions: what is the most specific antibody for sle? (anti-smith) what antibody correlates with lupus nephritis activity? (anti-dsdna) how do you differentiate sle flare from infection? (crp normal in flare; elevated in infection) what drug should all sle patients receive? (hydroxychloroquine) what is the most common and severe class of lupus nephritis? (class iv — diffuse proliferative) topic 63: vasculitis overview topic 63 · vasculitis overview 1. classification — chapel hill consensus (by vessel size) 1. classification — chapel hill consensus (by vessel size) large vessel vasculitis: disease key features diagnosis treatment references giant cell arteritis (gca / temporal arteritis) age ≥50; new headache, jaw claudication, visual loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy — emergency), scalp tenderness, pmr overlap; esr markedly elevated (often ≥50) temporal artery biopsy (gold standard — skip lesions possible → long specimen); temporal artery us (halo sign) high-dose steroids immediately (do not wait for biopsy); prednisone 40–60 mg/day (or iv methylprednisolone if visual symptoms); tocilizumab as steroid-sparing (giacta trial) acr/eular takayasu arteritis young women (15–30); aorta and branches; limb claudication, absent pulses ("pulseless disease"), bp discrepancy between arms, bruits, aortic regurgitation ct/mr angiography (vessel wall thickening, stenosis); esr/crp elevated steroids ± mtx/azathioprine; tocilizumab for refractory medium vessel vasculitis: disease key features diagnosis treatment polyarteritis nodosa (pan) middle-aged men; associated with hbv; skin (livedo reticularis, nodules, ulcers), peripheral neuropathy (mononeuritis multiplex), renal (hypertension, renal infarcts — not glomerulonephritis), gi (mesenteric ischemia), testicular pain; spares lungs mesenteric/renal angiography (microaneurysms — "string of beads"); biopsy (fibrinoid necrosis); anca negative steroids + cyclophosphamide; treat hbv if associated (antivirals + plasma exchange) kawasaki disease children 5; fever ≥5 days + conjunctivitis, oral changes (strawberry tongue), rash, extremity changes (edema/desquamation), cervical lymphadenopathy; coronary artery aneurysms clinical criteria; echo (coronary aneurysms) ivig + high-dose aspirin (one of few pediatric aspirin indications) small vessel vasculitis — anca-associated: disease anca key features references granulomatosis with polyangiitis (gpa, formerly wegener) c-anca (anti-pr3) upper airway (sinusitis, saddle nose, subglottic stenosis), lower airway (pulmonary nodules/cavities, hemoptysis), kidney (rapidly progressive gn — crescentic); granulomatous inflammation acr/eular microscopic polyangiitis (mpa) p-anca (anti-mpo) kidney (rpgn — most common presentation), pulmonary capillaritis (dah), no granulomas, no upper airway involvement eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (egpa, formerly churg-strauss) p-anca (anti-mpo) in ~40% asthma (precedes vasculitis by years), eosinophilia (marked), sinusitis, peripheral neuropathy, cardiac involvement; granulomas + eosinophilic infiltration small vessel — immune complex: disease key features references iga vasculitis (henoch-schönlein purpura) children; palpable purpura (buttocks/legs), arthralgia, abdominal pain (intussusception), iga nephropathy; skin biopsy: iga deposits cryoglobulinemic vasculitis associated with hcv; palpable purpura, arthralgia, gn, peripheral neuropathy; low complement; cryoglobulins positive anti-gbm disease (goodpasture) pulmonary hemorrhage + rpgn;
