anti-gbm antibodies; linear igg on if; young men (lung + kidney) 2. diagnostic approach 2. diagnostic approach suspect vasculitis: multisystem disease + constitutional symptoms + elevated inflammatory markers key labs: esr/crp, anca (c-anca/pr3, p-anca/mpo), complement, cryoglobulins, hepatitis b/c serologies, urinalysis (rbc casts = gn), creatinine biopsy: gold standard for most vasculitides (skin, kidney, nerve, temporal artery) imaging: ct/mr angiography for large/medium vessel; ct chest for pulmonary involvement 3. general treatment principles 3. general treatment principles induction (active disease): high-dose glucocorticoids + cyclophosphamide or rituximab (rave trial — rituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for anca-associated vasculitis) maintenance: azathioprine, mtx, or rituximab (mainritsan — rituximab superior to azathioprine for maintenance) avacopan (c5a receptor inhibitor): approved for anca-associated vasculitis as steroid-sparing agent (advocate trial) plasma exchange: consider in severe renal disease (cr 5.7) or dah (pexivas trial — no benefit for renal outcomes but may help in dah) 4. clinical pearls 4. clinical pearls gca: never delay steroids for biopsy — visual loss is irreversible; biopsy remains positive for 2 weeks after starting steroids c-anca/pr3 = gpa; p-anca/mpo = mpa and egpa pan: anca negative; associated with hbv; spares lungs; angiography shows microaneurysms gpa triad: upper airway + lower airway + kidney egpa: asthma + eosinophilia + vasculitis goodpasture: linear igg on immunofluorescence (vs lupus nephritis = granular/"lumpy bumpy") rituximab: increasingly first-line for anca-associated vasculitis (rave, rituxvas) top 5 pimping questions: what is the anca pattern in gpa vs mpa? (gpa: c-anca/pr3; mpa: p-anca/mpo) what is the emergency in gca? (visual loss — start steroids immediately, do not wait for biopsy) what vasculitis is anca-negative and associated with hbv? (pan) what is the triad of gpa? (upper airway + lower airway + kidney) what is the immunofluorescence pattern in goodpasture? (linear igg along gbm) topic 64: drug hypersensitivity reactions topic 64 · drug hypersensitivity reactions 1. gell and coombs classification 1. gell and coombs classification type mechanism timing examples references type i (immediate/anaphylactic) ige-mediated mast cell degranulation minutes to hours anaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema, drug allergy (penicillin, nsaids) type ii (cytotoxic) igg/igm against cell surface antigens hours to days hemolytic anemia (methyldopa, penicillin), thrombocytopenia (heparin-hit, quinine), agranulocytosis (clozapine, methimazole) type iii (immune complex) antigen-antibody complex deposition 1–3 weeks serum sickness (antithymocyte globulin, rituximab), serum sickness-like reaction (cefaclor, tmp-smx), vasculitis type iv (delayed/cell-mediated) t-cell mediated 48–72 hours to weeks contact dermatitis, sjs/ten, dress, maculopapular drug eruption, tuberculin skin test 2. severe cutaneous adverse reactions (scars) 2. severe cutaneous adverse reactions (scars) stevens-johnson syndrome (sjs) / toxic epidermal necrolysis (ten): sjs: 10% bsa detachment; sjs-ten overlap: 10–30%; ten: 30% presentation: prodrome (fever, malaise) → painful erythematous macules → targetoid lesions → epidermal detachment (positive nikolsky sign) → mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital) common drugs: allopurinol (most common overall), carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, sulfonamides (tmp-smx), nsaids (piroxicam), nevirapine hla associations: hla-b5801 (allopurinol — screen in southeast asian, african american, korean populations); hla-b1502 (carbamazepine — screen in southeast asian populations) management: stop offending drug immediately; icu/burn unit; supportive care (wound care, fluid resuscitation, pain management, ophthalmology consult); cyclosporine may improve outcomes; ivig controversial; avoid steroids (controversial — some guidelines allow short course early) scorten score: predicts mortality in sjs/ten (age, malignancy, bsa, tachycardia, bun, glucose, bicarbonate) dress syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms): onset: 2–8 weeks after drug initiation (delayed) presentation: fever, diffuse morbilliform rash (face edema characteristic), eosinophilia, atypical lymphocytes, organ involvement (liver most common — hepatitis; also kidney, lung, heart) common drugs: allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine, dapsone, vancomycin, minocycline associated with hhv-6 reactivation treatment: stop drug; systemic corticosteroids (unlike sjs/ten where steroids are controversial, steroids are mainstay for dress) acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis (agep): onset: 1–5 days after drug presentation: acute fever + widespread non-follicular sterile pustules on erythematous base; resolves with drug withdrawal common drugs: aminopenicillins, macrolides, quinolones, terbinafine treatment: stop drug; self-limited; topical steroids 3. penicillin allergy 3. penicillin allergy most commonly reported drug allergy (~10% of patients); however, ~90% of patients labeled "penicillin allergic" are not truly allergic (lost sensitivity over time or had non-allergic reaction) cross-reactivity with cephalosporins: ~1–2% (much lower than previously thought — 10% is outdated) management: penicillin skin testing → if negative, can safely give penicillin; if positive, desensitization if penicillin is essential (e.g., neurosyphilis, endocarditis) amoxicillin rash in ebv (mononucleosis): not a true allergy — maculopapular rash due to immune activation; does not predict future penicillin allergy 4. clinical pearls 4. clinical pearls sjs/ten: stop the drug — most important intervention; nikolsky sign positive; mucosal involvement dress: delayed onset (2–8 weeks); eosinophilia + organ involvement; treat with steroids hla-b5801: screen before allopurinol in at-risk populations (southeast asian, african american) hla-b1502: screen before carbamazepine in southeast asian populations penicillin allergy: 90% are not truly allergic; skin testing can clarify cross-reactivity penicillin-cephalosporin: ~1–2% (not 10%) top 5 pimping questions: what differentiates sjs from ten? (bsa detachment: sjs 10%, ten 30%) what hla should be screened before allopurinol? (hla-b5801) what is the hallmark of dress? (delayed onset 2–8 weeks, eosinophilia, organ involvement — especially liver) what is the true cross-reactivity rate between penicillin and cephalosporins? (~1–2%, not 10%) what is the nikolsky sign? (epidermal detachment with lateral pressure — positive in sjs/ten, pemphigus vulgaris) topic 65: anaphylaxis topic 65 · anaphylaxis 1. definition 1. definition severe, life-threatening, systemic hypersensitivity reaction (type i — ige-mediated) with rapid onset. can also be non-ige mediated (anaphylactoid — same clinical presentation, same treatment). 2. diagnostic criteria — wao/eaaci 2. diagnostic criteria — wao/eaaci anaphylaxis is highly likely when any one of the following is present: acute onset (minutes to hours) of skin/mucosal involvement (urticaria, angioedema, flushing) + respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheeze, stridor) or hypotension/end-organ dysfunction two or more of the following rapidly after exposure to likely allergen: skin/mucosal involvement, respiratory compromise, hypotension, persistent gi symptoms (cramping, vomiting) hypotension after exposure to known allergen (sbp 90 or 30% decrease from baseline) 3. common triggers 3. common triggers foods: peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, fish, milk, eggs (most common cause in children) drugs: antibiotics (penicillin — most common drug cause), nsaids, neuromuscular blocking agents (most common cause in perioperative anaphylaxis) insect stings: hymenoptera (bees, wasps, hornets) latex exercise-induced, idiopathic (no identifiable trigger in ~20%) radiocontrast media (anaphylactoid — non-ige mediated) 4. clinical presentation (rapid onset — usually within minutes) 4. clinical presentation (rapid onset — usually within minutes) skin (90%): urticaria, flushing, angioedema, pruritus respiratory (70%): laryngeal edema (stridor — most dangerous), bronchospasm (wheeze), dyspnea cardiovascular (45%): hypotension, tachycardia, syncope, cardiac arrest gi (45%): nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramping biphasic reaction: recurrence of symptoms 1–72 hours after initial resolution (occurs in ~5–20%) → observe 4–6 hours minimum (some guidelines say 6–12 hours for severe reactions) 5. management — abcde + epinephrine 5. management — abcde + epinephrine epinephrine is the first-line treatment — no contraindications in anaphylaxis step action details references 1 remove trigger stop infusion, remove stinger wao, eaaci 2 call for help activate emergency response 3 epinephrine im 0.3–0.5 mg (1:1000 = 1 mg/ml) im in mid-anterolateral thigh; repeat every 5–15 min if no improvement; pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.3 mg) 4 position supine with legs elevated (trendelenburg); if dyspneic, allow to sit up; if vomiting, recovery position; do not stand up suddenly (can cause "empty ventricle syndrome" → cardiac arrest) 5 iv fluids ns 1–2 l rapid bolus for hypotension (20 ml/kg in children) 6 oxygen high-flow o2 7 adjuncts nebulized salbutamol (bronchospasm); h1-antihistamine (diphenhydramine 25–50 mg iv — for urticaria/pruritus only, not a substitute for epinephrine); h2-antihistamine (ranitidine/famotidine); corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg — may prevent biphasic reaction, but no evidence for acute benefit) 8 refractory epinephrine iv infusion: 0.1–1 μg/kg/min (1:10,000 concentration = 0.1 mg/ml); titrate to effect vasopressors: norepinephrine or vasopressin if refractory hypotension despite epinephrine + fluids glucagon: 1–5 mg iv bolus then 5–15 μg/min infusion — essential for patients on beta-blockers (beta-blockers blunt epinephrine response; glucagon bypasses beta-receptor and activates adenylyl cyclase directly → increases camp → inotropic/chronotropic effect) intubation/surgical airway: if severe laryngeal edema unresponsive to epinephrine 6. post-anaphylaxis management 6. post-anaphylaxis management observe minimum 4–6 hours (some guidelines: 6–12 hours for severe reactions) — biphasic reaction risk prescribe epinephrine auto-injector (epipen) for all patients at discharge educate on trigger avoidance and auto-injector use referral to allergist/immunologist for: skin prick testing, specific ige, component-resolved diagnostics serum tryptase: draw within 1–2 hours of symptom onset (peaks at 1–2 hours, returns to baseline by 6 hours); elevated tryptase supports anaphylaxis diagnosis (especially useful when diagnosis is uncertain); baseline tryptase at ≥24 hours to rule out mastocytosis medical alert bracelet/identification 7. common mistakes 7. common mistakes giving antihistamines instead of epinephrine — antihistamines do not treat hypotension or airway obstruction; they only relieve urticaria/pruritus using iv epinephrine as first-line (should be im first; iv only for refractory/cardiac arrest) delaying epinephrine — delays are associated with increased mortality not observing for biphasic reaction not prescribing auto-injector at discharge giving epinephrine subcutaneously (slower absorption than im; im in thigh is preferred) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls epinephrine im in the thigh: first and most important treatment — no absolute contraindications antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only — never delay epinephrine for them beta-blocker patients: may have refractory anaphylaxis → use glucagon ace inhibitor patients: higher risk of severe angioedema tryptase: confirmatory test; draw within 1–2 hours; elevated in anaphylaxis (not elevated in food-related anaphylaxis in some cases) biphasic reaction: 5–20% of cases; observe ≥4–6 hours anaphylaxis can occur without skin findings (~10–20%) — do not rule out anaphylaxis just because there is no urticaria top 10 testable facts: epinephrine im (0.3–0.5 mg, 1:1000) in anterolateral thigh — first-line repeat epinephrine every 5–15 minutes if needed antihistamines are not a substitute for epinephrine glucagon for patients on beta-blockers (bypasses beta-receptor blockade) biphasic reaction: observe ≥4–6 hours tryptase: draw within 1–2 hours; confirms diagnosis prescribe auto-injector at discharge — always most common trigger in children: food; in adults: drugs and insect stings anaphylaxis can occur without skin findings (10–20%) do not let patient stand up suddenly — risk of "empty ventricle" cardiac arrest top 5 pimping questions: what is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis? (epinephrine im — no exceptions) what do you give for anaphylaxis in a patient on beta-blockers? (glucagon — bypasses beta-receptor) what is the role of antihistamines in anaphylaxis? (adjunct only — treats urticaria/pruritus, not hypotension or airway obstruction) what lab confirms anaphylaxis? (serum tryptase — draw within 1–2 hours) why observe patients after anaphylaxis? (biphasic reaction — recurrence in 5–20% within 1–72 hours)

parts.title: PART IX: INFECTIOUS DISEASE

blocks.text: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus): retrovirus that infects CD4+ T lymphocytes → progressive immune destruction → AIDS
AIDS: CD4 200 cells/μL OR presence of an AIDS-defining illness (regardless of CD4 count)
Sexual (most common worldwide), parenteral (needle sharing, blood products), vertical (mother-to-child: in utero, delivery, breastfeeding)
NOT transmitted by: casual contact, saliva, tears, sweat, mosquitoes

Acute retroviral syndrome (2–4 weeks post-exposure): fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash, myalgia — resembles mononucleosis; high viral load, may be antibody-negative (window period)
Clinical latency (asymptomatic): years; gradual CD4 decline (~50–80 cells/year without treatment)
AIDS: CD4 200; opportunistic infections and malignancies

rows: 4th generation Ag/Ab combo (HIV-1/2 Ab + p24 Ag)
Confirms acute HIV (before antibodies develop); monitors treatment response
No longer recommended as confirmatory (replaced by Ab differentiation assay)

blocks.text: CDC algorithm: 4th gen screen → if positive → HIV-1/2 Ab differentiation → if indeterminate → HIV RNA
Acute HIV: suspect if mononucleosis-like illness + risk factors; 4th gen may be positive (p24 Ag), but antibodies may be negative → confirm with HIV RNA

rows: Moderate weight loss (10%), minor mucocutaneous (seborrheic dermatitis, angular cheilitis, oral ulcers), herpes zoster, recurrent URTIs
Severe weight loss (10%), chronic diarrhea 1 month, persistent fever 1 month, oral candidiasis, oral hairy leukoplakia, pulmonary TB, severe bacterial infections

HIV wasting syndrome, PCP, cerebral toxoplasmosis, esophageal candidiasis, cryptococcal meningitis, CMV retinitis, Kaposi sarcoma, extrapulmonary TB, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), NHL, cervical cancer

blocks.text: Key Principle: Treat ALL HIV-positive patients regardless of CD4 count (START trial, TEMPRANO trial)
Preferred Initial Regimens (2024):

rows: DTG: INSTI — high barrier to resistance, well-tolerated, once daily; WHO preferred first-line
Older regimen; still used in resource-limited settings; EFV side effects: CNS (vivid dreams, dizziness), teratogenicity (debated — recent data suggests safe)

blocks.text: Drug Classes:

rows: TDF: nephrotoxicity, Fanconi syndrome, ↓ BMD; AZT: bone marrow suppression (anemia, neutropenia); ABC: hypersensitivity (HLA-B*5701 — screen before use); d4T: peripheral neuropathy, lipoatrophy, lactic acidosis (largely discontinued)
EFV: CNS effects, rash; NVP: hepatotoxicity, rash (avoid if CD4 >250 women / >400 men); RPV: requires food, avoid with PPI
Protease inhibitors (boosted with ritonavir or cobicistat)
GI, metabolic (dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, lipodystrophy); ATV: indirect hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice), nephrolithiasis
Well-tolerated; DTG: weight gain, insomnia (rare); RAL: rhabdomyolysis (rare)
Tropism testing required for maraviroc (only works on CCR5-tropic virus)

blocks.text: HIV RNA (viral load): at baseline, 4 weeks after starting/changing ART, then every 3–6 months; goal: undetectable (50 copies/mL) by 6 months
CD4 count: at baseline, then every 3–6 months; can stop monitoring once consistently 250 and virally suppressed for 2 years
Resistance testing (genotype): at diagnosis (before starting ART) and at treatment failure
Metabolic monitoring: lipids, glucose, renal function, hepatic function
HLA-B5701: MUST screen before abacavir (ABC) — if positive, do NOT use (risk of fatal hypersensitivity)
Virologic failure: viral load 200 copies/mL on two consecutive measurements after 6 months of ART
Causes: non-adherence (most common), drug resistance, drug interactions, malabsorption
Management: assess adherence first; genotype resistance testing; switch to new regimen with ≥2 active drugs (ideally from different classes)
PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): TDF/FTC daily (or TAF/FTC) for high-risk HIV-negative individuals; also long-acting cabotegravir IM every 2 months (HPTN 083, HPTN 084 — superior to oral PrEP)
PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): start within 72 hours of exposure; 3-drug regimen (DTG + TDF/FTC or BIC/TAF/FTC) for 28 days
U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable): patients with sustained undetectable viral load do NOT transmit HIV sexually (PARTNER, PARTNER2, HPTN 052 trials)

PMTCT (Prevention of Mother-to-Child Transmission): ART for mother throughout pregnancy + delivery; infant prophylaxis (AZT or NVP); avoid breastfeeding if safe alternatives available (in resource-rich settings); C-section if viral load 1000 at delivery
Treat ALL HIV patients regardless of CD4 (START trial)
DTG-based regimen: preferred first-line worldwide
Screen HLA-B5701 before abacavir — mandatory
U=U: undetectable viral load = no sexual transmission

Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS): paradoxical worsening after starting ART (especially if CD4 very low); occurs weeks to months after ART initiation; common with TB, MAC, cryptococcus, PML; treat underlying OI, continue ART, steroids if severe
When to start ART in OIs: immediately for most OIs; DELAY 4–6 weeks for cryptococcal meningitis (risk of IRIS → increased ICP → death) and TB meningitis (delay 2–8 weeks)
Cryptococcal meningitis: check serum CrAg if CD4 100 before starting ART; if positive, treat cryptococcus first
Top 10 Testable Facts:
4th generation Ag/Ab combo: preferred initial screening test
Treat ALL patients regardless of CD4 count (START trial)
DTG-based regimen: preferred first-line (WHO/DHHS)
HLA-B5701: screen before abacavir
Viral load goal: undetectable (50) by 6 months
U=U: undetectable = untransmittable
PEP: within 72 hours, 28 days of 3-drug ART
IRIS: paradoxical worsening after ART initiation; delay ART for cryptococcal meningitis (4–6 weeks)
Most common cause of treatment failure: non-adherence
CD4 200 or AIDS-defining illness = AIDS
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
When do you start ART? (Immediately for all HIV patients — except delay for cryptococcal meningitis and TB meningitis)
What must you screen before starting abacavir? (HLA-B5701)
What is the preferred first-line ART regimen? (DTG + TDF/TAF + 3TC/FTC)
What is IRIS? (Paradoxical inflammatory worsening after ART initiation due to immune reconstitution)
What does U=U mean? (Undetectable viral load = untransmittable — no sexual transmission)

intro.text: CD4 Thresholds for OIs (high-yield — must memorize)

rows: TB, Kaposi sarcoma, lymphoma, bacterial pneumonia
TMP-SMX DS daily (first-line); alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, aerosolized pentamidine
Toxo: TMP-SMX (same as PCP prophylaxis — covers both); Crypto: screen serum CrAg; fluconazole if CrAg+
CMV retinitis, MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex), PML
MAC: azithromycin 1200 mg weekly (if not on ART); CMV: no primary prophylaxis, regular fundoscopy

blocks.text: CD4 200; most common AIDS-defining illness

Presentation: subacute onset (days to weeks) of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxia (especially with exertion); bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT (interstitial pattern); spontaneous pneumothorax
Diagnosis: induced sputum or BAL → silver stain (cysts), DFA; elevated LDH (nonspecific but sensitive); elevated beta-D-glucan
Treatment: TMP-SMX (15–20 mg/kg/day of TMP component) IV for 21 days; add prednisone if PaO2 70 or A-a gradient ≥35 (reduces mortality — steroids given BEFORE or WITH antibiotics)
Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS daily when CD4 200; discontinue when CD4 200 for ≥3 months on ART
CD4 100; reactivation of latent Toxoplasma gondii
Presentation: headache, fever, focal neurological deficits, seizures, altered mental status
Imaging: multiple ring-enhancing lesions (basal ganglia predilection) on contrast CT/MRI
Diagnosis: clinical + imaging + positive Toxoplasma IgG (serology); empiric treatment → if no improvement in 2 weeks → biopsy (to rule out CNS lymphoma)
Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin (folinic acid — prevents bone marrow toxicity) for ≥6 weeks
Differential: CNS lymphoma (single ring-enhancing lesion, EBV+ in CSF, thallium SPECT positive) vs toxoplasmosis (multiple lesions, Toxo IgG+)
CD4 100; Cryptococcus neoformans
Presentation: subacute headache (weeks), fever, altered mental status, elevated ICP (papilledema, CN VI palsy, vomiting); minimal meningeal signs (indolent)

Diagnosis: CSF — elevated opening pressure (often 25 cmH2O), India ink (budding yeast with capsule — 60–80% sensitive), CSF CrAg (cryptococcal antigen — 95% sensitive), CSF culture (gold standard); serum CrAg (highly sensitive screening test)
CSF profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis (may be minimal), elevated protein, low glucose
Treatment (WHO 2022):
Induction: amphotericin B deoxycholate (1 mg/kg/day) + flucytosine (100 mg/kg/day) for 1 week (preferred); then fluconazole 1200 mg/day for 1 week
Consolidation: fluconazole 800 mg/day for 8 weeks
Maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg/day for ≥1 year (until CD4 200 for ≥6 months on ART)
ICP management: serial therapeutic lumbar punctures (remove 20–30 mL CSF to reduce pressure to 20 cmH2O or 50% of opening pressure); VP shunt if refractory
DELAY ART for 4–6 weeks (COAT trial — early ART increases mortality due to IRIS)
CD4 50; most common cause of blindness in AIDS
Presentation: painless progressive visual loss, floaters, scotomata; fundoscopy: "pizza pie" or "cottage cheese and ketchup" appearance (yellow-white retinal necrosis with hemorrhage)
Treatment: valganciclovir (oral — preferred) or ganciclovir (IV) for induction (14–21 days) then maintenance; foscarnet if ganciclovir-resistant or intolerant
Side effects: ganciclovir — neutropenia (avoid with AZT); foscarnet — nephrotoxicity, electrolyte abnormalities (hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia)
CD4 50; disseminated infection
Presentation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, elevated alkaline phosphatase
Diagnosis: blood cultures (AFB — mycobacterial blood culture); bone marrow biopsy
Treatment: clarithromycin (or azithromycin) + ethambutol ± rifabutin
Prophylaxis: azithromycin 1200 mg weekly when CD4 50 (if not on effective ART)
Associated with HHV-8 (Human Herpesvirus 8); any CD4 count but more common with low CD4
Presentation: violaceous (purple) papules/nodules on skin, oral mucosa, GI tract, lungs
Treatment: ART (immune reconstitution is primary treatment); chemotherapy (liposomal doxorubicin) for extensive/visceral disease
CD4 50–100; JC virus reactivation
Presentation: progressive focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, visual field deficits, cognitive decline) WITHOUT mass effect or enhancement
Imaging: multifocal white matter lesions (non-enhancing, no mass effect) on MRI
Diagnosis: CSF JC virus PCR; brain biopsy if PCR negative
Treatment: NO specific antiviral; ART (immune reconstitution) is the only treatment; prognosis poor
PCP: bilateral GGO + elevated LDH + CD4 200; add steroids if PaO2 70
Toxo vs CNS lymphoma: multiple ring-enhancing = toxo; single = lymphoma; empiric toxo treatment → biopsy if no response in 2 weeks
Crypto meningitis: elevated ICP is the killer → serial LPs; DELAY ART 4–6 weeks
CMV retinitis: CD4 50; "pizza pie" fundus; valganciclovir
MAC: CD4 50; disseminated; clarithromycin + ethambutol
TMP-SMX prophylaxis covers BOTH PCP and toxoplasmosis
Screen serum CrAg when CD4 100 before starting ART
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
At what CD4 count do you start PCP prophylaxis? (200)
What is the treatment for PCP and when do you add steroids? (TMP-SMX 21 days; steroids if PaO2 70 or A-a gradient ≥35)
How do you differentiate toxoplasmosis from CNS lymphoma? (Toxo: multiple ring-enhancing lesions, IgG+; lymphoma: single lesion, EBV+ CSF)
Why delay ART in cryptococcal meningitis? (Risk of IRIS → increased ICP → death; delay 4–6 weeks)
What prophylaxis covers both PCP and toxoplasmosis? (TMP-SMX)
Acute febrile illness caused by dengue virus (DENV serotypes 1–4), transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquito. Endemic in Southeast Asia (including Indonesia — highly relevant for FKUI-RSCM).

rows: Fever + 2 of: nausea/vomiting, rash, aches/pains, leukopenia, positive tourniquet test

Above + any: abdominal pain/tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusion), mucosal bleeding, lethargy/restlessness, hepatomegaly ≥2 cm, rising HCT with rapid platelet drop
Any of: severe plasma leakage (shock — DSS, respiratory distress from fluid accumulation), severe bleeding (GI), severe organ impairment (liver: AST/ALT ≥1000, CNS: encephalopathy, heart)

blocks.text: Febrile phase (days 1–3): high fever (40°C), headache (retro-orbital pain), myalgia/arthralgia ("breakbone fever"), maculopapular rash, facial flushing, positive tourniquet test, leukopenia

Critical phase (days 3–7 — around defervescence): plasma leakage occurs → hemoconcentration (rising HCT ≥20%), pleural effusion, ascites, hypoproteinemia; thrombocytopenia nadir; risk of shock (DSS) and hemorrhage; this is when patients deteriorate — NOT during fever

Recovery phase (days 7–10): reabsorption of extravasated fluid → risk of fluid overload/pulmonary edema if over-resuscitated; rash (confluent with white islands of sparing — "islands of white in a sea of red"); platelet recovery; appetite returns

rows: Most useful for early diagnosis; highly specific
Secondary infection: rises early (day 1–2); Primary: rises later
High IgG early = secondary infection (higher risk of severe dengue)
Leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, rising HCT (hemoconcentration = plasma leakage)
≥20 petechiae per 1 inch2 = positive; suggests capillary fragility

blocks.text: Dengue without warning signs (outpatient):
Oral rehydration, paracetamol for fever/pain
AVOID NSAIDs and aspirin (increase bleeding risk)
AVOID IM injections
Daily CBC monitoring; return immediately if warning signs develop
Dengue WITH warning signs (hospitalize):
IV fluid resuscitation: isotonic crystalloid (NS or Ringer's lactate)
Start: 5–7 mL/kg/hr for 1–2 hours → reduce to 3–5 mL/kg/hr for 2–4 hours → reduce to 2–3 mL/kg/hr → maintain based on clinical response
Monitor: HCT every 4–6 hours, urine output (target ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr), vital signs
Reduce fluids when: HCT drops, stable vitals, adequate urine output
AVOID excessive fluids (risk of fluid overload in recovery phase)
Severe dengue / Dengue Shock Syndrome (DSS):
Aggressive IV fluid bolus: 10–20 mL/kg over 15–30 minutes (crystalloid); repeat if needed
If no improvement: switch to colloid (dextran, gelatin, or albumin)
If HCT rising despite fluids: ongoing plasma leakage → more fluids
If HCT dropping + unstable: suspect hemorrhage → blood transfusion (packed RBC)
Platelet transfusion: NOT routinely indicated for thrombocytopenia alone; only if active significant bleeding + platelets very low (20,000)
ICU admission for DSS, severe bleeding, organ failure
Abdominal pain or tenderness
Persistent vomiting
Clinical fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusion)
Mucosal bleeding
Lethargy or restlessness
Hepatomegaly ≥2 cm
Lab: rising HCT with rapid platelet drop
Critical phase occurs at DEFERVESCENCE (day 3–7) — not during fever; this is when patients crash
Rising HCT = plasma leakage (hemoconcentration) — key monitoring parameter
AVOID NSAIDs/aspirin — use paracetamol only
Secondary dengue infection (different serotype): higher risk of severe dengue/DSS (antibody-dependent enhancement — ADE)
Tourniquet test: simple bedside screening tool
Fluid management is the cornerstone — too little → shock; too much → fluid overload in recovery
Platelet transfusion: NOT for numbers alone; only for significant bleeding
Dengue can mimic: leptospirosis, chikungunya, Zika, typhoid, malaria — consider in differential
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
When does the critical phase occur? (At defervescence — days 3–7, NOT during fever)
What analgesic is safe in dengue? (Paracetamol only — avoid NSAIDs/aspirin)
What does rising HCT indicate? (Plasma leakage / hemoconcentration)
When do you transfuse platelets? (Only for significant active bleeding, NOT for low platelet count alone)
Why is secondary dengue more dangerous? (Antibody-dependent enhancement — ADE)
Parasitic infection caused by Plasmodium species, transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito. Five species infect humans: P. falciparum (most dangerous), P. vivax, P. ovale, P. malariae, P. knowlesi.

rows: No (but recurrence years later — persistent blood stage)

blocks.text: Classic triad: fever (paroxysmal — cold stage → hot stage → sweating stage), anemia, splenomegaly
Nonspecific: headache, myalgia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
Severe/complicated malaria (P. falciparum):
Cerebral malaria: altered consciousness, seizures, coma (GCS ≤11)
Severe anemia: Hb 5 g/dL or HCT 15%
Hypoglycemia: blood glucose 40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L) — caused by parasite glucose consumption + quinine-induced insulin release
Metabolic acidosis: pH 7.25 or bicarbonate 15 mmol/L
Acute kidney injury: creatinine >3 mg/dL
Pulmonary edema / ARDS
Jaundice: bilirubin >3 mg/dL with parasitemia >100,000/μL
Hyperparasitemia: >5% (or >10% in some guidelines)
DIC / spontaneous bleeding
Shock (algid malaria)
Hemoglobinuria ("blackwater fever")

rows: Detects HRP2 (P. falciparum) or pLDH/aldolase (all species); useful in resource-limited settings; HRP2 can remain positive weeks after treatment (false positive)
Anemia, thrombocytopenia (very common — does NOT indicate severity alone), leukopenia or normal WBC
Hypoglycemia, acidosis, renal function, LFTs, bilirubin

blocks.text: Gold standard: microscopy (thick + thin smear) — repeat every 12–24 hours x 3 if initial smear negative and clinical suspicion high
Parasitemia quantification: essential for P. falciparum to assess severity and monitor treatment response
A. Uncomplicated Malaria:

rows: Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT): artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) or DHA-piperaquine
ACT is WHO-recommended first-line for ALL uncomplicated falciparum malaria; 3-day course
ACT or chloroquine (if chloroquine-sensitive area) + primaquine (14 days) for radical cure (kills hypnozoites)
MUST check G6PD before primaquine — risk of hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency; alternative: tafenoquine (single dose — also requires G6PD testing)
Can cause high parasitemia — monitor closely; treat as falciparum if severe

blocks.text: B. Severe/Complicated Malaria (MEDICAL EMERGENCY):
IV artesunate: drug of choice for severe malaria (superior to IV quinine — SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials showed 35% mortality reduction)
Dose: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24 hours, then every 24 hours until patient can take oral medication
Switch to oral ACT to complete 3-day course once patient can tolerate oral
Side effect: delayed hemolytic anemia (post-artesunate delayed hemolysis — PADH; occurs 1–3 weeks after treatment; monitor Hb)

If IV artesunate unavailable: IV quinine dihydrochloride (loading dose 20 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 10 mg/kg q8h); monitor for hypoglycemia (quinine stimulates insulin release), QTc prolongation, cinchonism (tinnitus, hearing loss)
Pre-referral treatment (if unable to reach hospital): rectal artesunate (single dose) or IM artesunate/artemether → then refer immediately
C. Supportive Care for Severe Malaria:
Correct hypoglycemia (dextrose bolus — check glucose frequently, especially with quinine)
Blood transfusion for severe anemia (Hb 5)
Treat seizures (benzodiazepines)
Manage AKI (fluids, dialysis if needed)
Exchange transfusion: no longer routinely recommended (WHO); consider in extreme hyperparasitemia (<30%) with organ dysfunction if available
Avoid: steroids (no benefit, may worsen outcomes), aspirin, heparin
Kills hypnozoites (liver dormant forms) of P. vivax and P. ovale → prevents relapse (radical cure)
Also has gametocidal activity against P. falciparum → reduces transmission (WHO recommends single low-dose primaquine 0.25 mg/kg with ACT for falciparum in low-transmission settings)
MUST check G6PD status before prescribing — primaquine causes oxidative hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients
If G6PD deficient: use weekly primaquine (0.75 mg/kg x 8 weeks) with close monitoring, or tafenoquine is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency

rows: Daily; start 1–2 days before, continue 7 days after
Well-tolerated; expensive; contraindicated in severe renal impairment
Daily; start 1–2 days before, continue 28 days after
Cheap; photosensitivity, GI upset; also covers leptospirosis/rickettsial infections
Weekly; start 2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after
Neuropsychiatric side effects (vivid dreams, anxiety, psychosis); contraindicated in seizure/psychiatric disorders
Weekly; start 1–2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after
Only for chloroquine-sensitive areas (Central America, Middle East); NOT for most of Africa/SE Asia

blocks.text: Higher risk of severe malaria, maternal anemia, low birth weight, stillbirth
Treatment: IV artesunate for severe malaria (all trimesters — benefit outweighs risk)
Uncomplicated: 1st trimester — quinine + clindamycin (7 days); 2nd/3rd trimester — ACT (artemether-lumefantrine)
Primaquine and tafenoquine: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (unknown fetal G6PD status)
Chemoprophylaxis: mefloquine (safe in all trimesters); doxycycline contraindicated
Chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum: widespread (most of Africa, SE Asia, South America) → use ACT
Chloroquine-resistant P. vivax: parts of Indonesia (Papua), PNG → use ACT

Artemisinin partial resistance: Greater Mekong Subregion (Cambodia, Myanmar, Thailand, Vietnam, Laos) — delayed parasite clearance; partner drug resistance emerging → longer ACT courses or triple ACT being studied
IV artesunate: drug of choice for severe malaria (NOT quinine) — SEAQUAMAT/AQUAMAT trials
Check G6PD before primaquine — mandatory
Thick smear: screening (sensitive); thin smear: species ID + parasitemia
Hypoglycemia in malaria: caused by parasite + quinine-induced insulin release → check glucose frequently
Thrombocytopenia is very common in malaria but does NOT define severity
P. vivax/ovale: hypnozoites → relapse → need primaquine for radical cure
P. falciparum: no hypnozoites but can recrudesce (incomplete treatment)
Blackwater fever: massive hemolysis → hemoglobinuria; associated with P. falciparum and quinine use
Post-artesunate delayed hemolysis (PADH): monitor Hb for 4 weeks after IV artesunate
Top 10 Testable Facts:
P. falciparum: most dangerous; cerebral malaria; infects all RBC ages → high parasitemia
IV artesunate: first-line for severe malaria (SEAQUAMAT/AQUAMAT)
ACT: first-line for uncomplicated falciparum malaria
Primaquine: kills hypnozoites (P. vivax/ovale); check G6PD first
Thick smear: sensitive (screening); thin smear: specific (species ID)
Hypoglycemia: parasite consumption + quinine-induced insulin release
P. vivax/ovale: tertian fever (48h); hypnozoites → relapse
P. malariae: quartan fever (72h); no hypnozoites
Chloroquine resistance: widespread in P. falciparum; also in P. vivax (Indonesia/Papua)
Pregnancy: IV artesunate for severe (all trimesters); primaquine contraindicated
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the drug of choice for severe malaria? (IV artesunate — not quinine)
Why must you check G6PD before primaquine? (Risk of oxidative hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency)
Which Plasmodium species have hypnozoites? (P. vivax and P. ovale — require primaquine for radical cure)
What causes hypoglycemia in malaria? (Parasite glucose consumption + quinine-induced insulin release)
What is the difference between thick and thin blood smear? (Thick = sensitive/screening; thin = species identification + parasitemia quantification)
Sepsis: life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection; defined as suspected/documented infection + acute increase in SOFA score ≥2 points

Septic shock: subset of sepsis with circulatory, cellular, and metabolic abnormalities; defined as sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + serum lactate <2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation; mortality <40%

Old SIRS criteria (≥2 of: temp <38°C or 36°C, HR <90, RR <20 or PaCO2 32, WBC <12,000 or 4,000 or <10% bands) — no longer used to define sepsis (too sensitive, not specific) but still clinically useful for screening
≥2 of 3 criteria (no labs needed):
Altered mental status (GCS ≤13)
Respiratory rate ≥22/min
Systolic BP ≤100 mmHg
qSOFA ≥2: identifies patients at higher risk of poor outcomes → prompts further assessment for organ dysfunction (SOFA score), ICU consideration
Note: qSOFA is a prognostic tool, NOT a diagnostic criterion for sepsis; Sepsis-3 uses SOFA score for diagnosis
Assesses 6 organ systems: respiratory (PaO2/FiO2), coagulation (platelets), liver (bilirubin), cardiovascular (MAP/vasopressors), CNS (GCS), renal (creatinine/urine output)
Each scored 0–4; total 0–24
Acute increase ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction = sepsis (in setting of infection)
Most common sources: pulmonary (pneumonia — #1), urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft tissue, bloodstream (line-related)
Most common organisms: gram-negative (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas), gram-positive (S. aureus, Streptococcus, Enterococcus); fungi in immunocompromised
Start ALL within 1 hour of sepsis recognition:
Measure lactate — if <2 mmol/L, remeasure within 2–4 hours (target: lactate clearance ≥20% or normalization)
Obtain blood cultures (≥2 sets, aerobic + anaerobic) BEFORE antibiotics — but do NOT delay antibiotics if cultures cannot be obtained quickly
Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics — within 1 hour of recognition; each hour of delay increases mortality by ~4–8%
Rapid IV fluid resuscitation: 30 mL/kg crystalloid (NS or balanced crystalloid — Ringer's lactate preferred per SMART trial) within first 3 hours for hypotension or lactate ≥4
Vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation → target MAP ≥65 mmHg

rows: Broad-spectrum: piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem, or cefepime ± vancomycin (if MRSA risk)
Piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem or cefepime + vancomycin (or linezolid) if MRSA risk
Ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone; piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem if ESBL risk
Piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem (covers anaerobes); or ceftriaxone + metronidazole
Cefepime or meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam (anti-pseudomonal monotherapy)

blocks.text: De-escalate antibiotics based on culture results and clinical response (within 48–72 hours)
Duration: typically 7–10 days (shorter courses increasingly supported — IDSA)
Procalcitonin: can guide antibiotic de-escalation (trending down → consider stopping)

rows: SECOND-LINE: add to norepinephrine (up to 0.03 U/min) if MAP not at target; catecholamine-sparing
THIRD-LINE: add if MAP not at target with NE + vasopressin; increases HR, lactate (confounds monitoring)
Avoid in septic shock (reduces cardiac output); only if significant tachyarrhythmia
NOT recommended as first-line (more arrhythmias than NE); may consider only in highly selected patients with bradycardia

blocks.text: IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg q6h or continuous infusion): recommended for septic shock refractory to fluids + vasopressors (norepinephrine ≥0.25 μg/kg/min for ≥4 hours)
ADRENAL trial: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone reduced 90-day mortality in septic shock
APPROCCHSS trial: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone improved survival
Do NOT use random cortisol or ACTH stimulation test to guide steroid use in septic shock (SSC 2021)
Taper when vasopressors discontinued
Source control: identify and control source of infection (drain abscess, remove infected device, debride necrotic tissue) — ideally within 6–12 hours
Glucose management: target 144–180 mg/dL (avoid tight glucose control — NICE-SUGAR trial: tight control increased mortality)
DVT prophylaxis: LMWH preferred over UFH
Stress ulcer prophylaxis: PPI or H2 blocker for patients at risk (mechanical ventilation, coagulopathy)
Transfusion: restrictive strategy — transfuse if Hb 7 g/dL (TRICC trial, TRISS trial); exception: active hemorrhage, myocardial ischemia
Mechanical ventilation: if ARDS develops → low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW — ARDSNet protocol)
Renal replacement therapy: for standard indications (refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload, uremia); no benefit of early initiation (STARRT-AKI trial)
Elevated lactate (<2 mmol/L): marker of tissue hypoperfusion and cellular dysfunction
Lactate <4 mmol/L: associated with high mortality; aggressive resuscitation needed
Serial lactate monitoring: target lactate clearance ≥20% every 2 hours (lactate-guided resuscitation reduces mortality — Jansen et al.)
Lactate is NOT just from anaerobic metabolism — also from aerobic glycolysis (stress response), hepatic clearance impairment, epinephrine use
Sepsis = infection + organ dysfunction (SOFA ≥2); septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors + lactate <2
Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors — all within 1 hour
Norepinephrine: first-line vasopressor (NOT dopamine)
Balanced crystalloids (LR) preferred over NS (SMART trial — less hyperchloremic acidosis, less AKI)
Antibiotics within 1 hour — each hour delay increases mortality
Source control is critical — antibiotics alone are insufficient if source not controlled
Hydrocortisone for refractory septic shock (not routine for all sepsis)
Avoid tight glucose control (target 144–180)
Procalcitonin: helps guide antibiotic de-escalation, not initiation
Top 10 Testable Facts:
Sepsis-3: infection + SOFA ≥2 (organ dysfunction)
Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressors + lactate <2 mmol/L
Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors
Norepinephrine: first-line vasopressor
30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus within 3 hours for hypotension/lactate ≥4
Antibiotics within 1 hour — do not delay for cultures
Vasopressin: second-line (add to norepinephrine)
Hydrocortisone: for refractory septic shock (NE ≥0.25 μg/kg/min)
Lactate clearance ≥20% every 2 hours: resuscitation target
Source control within 6–12 hours
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What is the definition of sepsis per Sepsis-3? (Infection + acute SOFA increase ≥2)
What is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock? (Norepinephrine)
What is the initial fluid bolus for sepsis? (30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3 hours)
When do you add hydrocortisone? (Refractory septic shock despite fluids + vasopressors)
What is the target MAP in septic shock? (≥65 mmHg)

intro.text: (Covered comprehensively within Topic 70 — Sepsis. Key additional points below.)

blocks.text: Septic shock = sepsis + persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg + serum lactate <2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation. Hospital mortality <40%.
Distributive shock: low SVR (vasodilation), high cardiac output (initially — hyperdynamic), low MAP
Warm shock (early): warm extremities, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, tachycardia
Cold shock (late/decompensated): cool/mottled extremities, weak pulses, narrow pulse pressure, altered mental status — indicates cardiovascular collapse
Norepinephrine (first-line): start at 0.1 μg/kg/min, titrate to MAP ≥65
Add vasopressin (0.03 U/min — fixed dose, do not titrate) if NE dose escalating
Add epinephrine if still refractory

Consider: dobutamine if evidence of myocardial dysfunction (low cardiac output despite adequate filling pressures — septic cardiomyopathy); angiotensin II (Giapreza) for refractory vasoplegia (ATHOS-3 trial)
Static measures (CVP, PCWP): POOR predictors of fluid responsiveness — do NOT use CVP to guide fluid resuscitation
Dynamic measures (preferred):
Passive leg raise (PLR): raise legs 45° → if cardiac output increases ≥10% → fluid responsive
Pulse pressure variation (PPV) / stroke volume variation (SVV): <12–13% in mechanically ventilated patients → fluid responsive
IVC ultrasound: collapsibility <50% (spontaneous breathing) or distensibility <18% (mechanical ventilation) suggests fluid responsiveness
Fluid challenge: 250–500 mL bolus → reassess hemodynamics
After initial 30 mL/kg, further fluids should be guided by dynamic assessment — avoid fluid overload (associated with worse outcomes)
Myocardial depression occurs in ~40% of septic shock patients
Characterized by: biventricular dilation, reduced ejection fraction, elevated troponin
Usually reversible within 7–10 days if patient survives
Management: dobutamine (inotrope) if low cardiac output; avoid excessive vasopressors (increase afterload on failing heart)
Bedside echo (point-of-care ultrasound — POCUS): essential for assessing cardiac function, volume status, and guiding management
Refractory hypotension despite fluids + vasopressors
History of chronic steroid use (iatrogenic adrenal suppression)
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage — meningococcemia)
Treatment: stress-dose hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus, then 50 mg q6–8h

rows: ≤3 seconds (ANDROMEDA-SHOCK — CRT-guided resuscitation non-inferior to lactate-guided)
≥70% (if available — less emphasized in current guidelines)

blocks.text: Septic shock is DISTRIBUTIVE shock (low SVR) — differentiate from cardiogenic (high SVR, low CO), hypovolemic (low CVP, low CO), obstructive (elevated JVP, low CO)
CVP is NOT a reliable guide for fluid resuscitation — use dynamic measures
Passive leg raise: best bedside test for fluid responsiveness
Bedside echo (POCUS): invaluable in shock — assess cardiac function, IVC, pericardial effusion
Capillary refill time: simple, effective resuscitation endpoint (ANDROMEDA-SHOCK)
Septic cardiomyopathy: reversible; use dobutamine if low CO
Fluid overload is harmful — reassess after initial bolus; do not blindly continue fluids
Top 5 Pimping Questions:
What type of shock is septic shock? (Distributive — low SVR, high CO initially)
How do you assess fluid responsiveness at the bedside? (Passive leg raise — CO increase ≥10%)
What is the vasopressor escalation order? (Norepinephrine → vasopressin → epinephrine)
When do you add dobutamine? (Septic cardiomyopathy — low cardiac output despite adequate volume)
Why is CVP unreliable for guiding fluid resuscitation? (Poor predictor of fluid responsiveness — does not reflect preload responsiveness)

parts.searchText: part ix: infectious disease part ix: infectious disease topic 66: hiv/aids topic 66 · hiv/aids 1. definition 1. definition hiv (human immunodeficiency virus): retrovirus that infects cd4+ t lymphocytes → progressive immune destruction → aids aids: cd4 200 cells/μl or presence of an aids-defining illness (regardless of cd4 count) 2. transmission 2. transmission sexual (most common worldwide), parenteral (needle sharing, blood products), vertical (mother-to-child: in utero, delivery, breastfeeding) not transmitted by: casual contact, saliva, tears, sweat, mosquitoes 3. natural history 3. natural history acute retroviral syndrome (2–4 weeks post-exposure): fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash, myalgia — resembles mononucleosis; high viral load, may be antibody-negative (window period) clinical latency (asymptomatic): years; gradual cd4 decline (~50–80 cells/year without treatment) aids: cd4 200; opportunistic infections and malignancies 4. diagnosis — who/cdc 4. diagnosis — who/cdc test use window period references 4th generation ag/ab combo (hiv-1/2 ab + p24 ag) preferred initial screening test ~2 weeks (shortest window) cdc, who hiv-1/2 antibody differentiation immunoassay confirmatory (replaces western blot) — hiv rna (viral load) confirms acute hiv (before antibodies develop); monitors treatment response detectable ~10 days western blot no longer recommended as confirmatory (replaced by ab differentiation assay) — rapid tests (antibody-based) point-of-care; useful in resource-limited settings ~3–4 weeks who cdc algorithm: 4th gen screen → if positive → hiv-1/2 ab differentiation → if indeterminate → hiv rna acute hiv: suspect if mononucleosis-like illness + risk factors; 4th gen may be positive (p24 ag), but antibodies may be negative → confirm with hiv rna 5. who clinical staging 5. who clinical staging stage features references 1 asymptomatic, persistent generalized lymphadenopathy who 2 moderate weight loss (10%), minor mucocutaneous (seborrheic dermatitis, angular cheilitis, oral ulcers), herpes zoster, recurrent urtis 3 severe weight loss (10%), chronic diarrhea 1 month, persistent fever 1 month, oral candidiasis, oral hairy leukoplakia, pulmonary tb, severe bacterial infections 4 (aids) hiv wasting syndrome, pcp, cerebral toxoplasmosis, esophageal candidiasis, cryptococcal meningitis, cmv retinitis, kaposi sarcoma, extrapulmonary tb, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (pml), nhl, cervical cancer 6. antiretroviral therapy (art) — who/dhhs 6. antiretroviral therapy (art) — who/dhhs key principle: treat all hiv-positive patients regardless of cd4 count (start trial, temprano trial) preferred initial regimens (2024): regimen components notes references dolutegravir (dtg)-based (preferred) dtg + tdf/taf + 3tc/ftc (or dtg + 3tc dual therapy) dtg: insti — high barrier to resistance, well-tolerated, once daily; who preferred first-line who 2024, dhhs bictegravir-based bic/taf/ftc (biktarvy) single-tablet regimen; dhhs preferred alternative efv + tdf + 3tc/ftc older regimen; still used in resource-limited settings; efv side effects: cns (vivid dreams, dizziness), teratogenicity (debated — recent data suggests safe) drug classes: class examples mechanism key side effects references nrtis (backbone) tdf, taf, 3tc, ftc, azt, abc nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors tdf: nephrotoxicity, fanconi syndrome, ↓ bmd; azt: bone marrow suppression (anemia, neutropenia); abc: hypersensitivity (hla-b*5701 — screen before use); d4t: peripheral neuropathy, lipoatrophy, lactic acidosis (largely discontinued) nnrtis efv, nvp, rpv, dor non-nucleoside rti efv: cns effects, rash; nvp: hepatotoxicity, rash (avoid if cd4 >250 women / >400 men); rpv: requires food, avoid with ppi pis atv/r, drv/r, lpv/r protease inhibitors (boosted with ritonavir or cobicistat) gi, metabolic (dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, lipodystrophy); atv: indirect hyperbilirubinemia (jaundice), nephrolithiasis instis dtg, bic, ral, evg/c integrase strand transfer inhibitors well-tolerated; dtg: weight gain, insomnia (rare); ral: rhabdomyolysis (rare) entry inhibitors maraviroc (ccr5 antagonist), enfuvirtide (fusion inhibitor) block viral entry tropism testing required for maraviroc (only works on ccr5-tropic virus) 7. monitoring on art 7. monitoring on art hiv rna (viral load): at baseline, 4 weeks after starting/changing art, then every 3–6 months; goal: undetectable (50 copies/ml) by 6 months cd4 count: at baseline, then every 3–6 months; can stop monitoring once consistently 250 and virally suppressed for 2 years resistance testing (genotype): at diagnosis (before starting art) and at treatment failure metabolic monitoring: lipids, glucose, renal function, hepatic function hla-b5701: must screen before abacavir (abc) — if positive, do not use (risk of fatal hypersensitivity) 8. treatment failure 8. treatment failure virologic failure: viral load 200 copies/ml on two consecutive measurements after 6 months of art causes: non-adherence (most common), drug resistance, drug interactions, malabsorption management: assess adherence first; genotype resistance testing; switch to new regimen with ≥2 active drugs (ideally from different classes) 9. prevention 9. prevention prep (pre-exposure prophylaxis): tdf/ftc daily (or taf/ftc) for high-risk hiv-negative individuals; also long-acting cabotegravir im every 2 months (hptn 083, hptn 084 — superior to oral prep) pep (post-exposure prophylaxis): start within 72 hours of exposure; 3-drug regimen (dtg + tdf/ftc or bic/taf/ftc) for 28 days u=u (undetectable = untransmittable): patients with sustained undetectable viral load do not transmit hiv sexually (partner, partner2, hptn 052 trials) pmtct (prevention of mother-to-child transmission): art for mother throughout pregnancy + delivery; infant prophylaxis (azt or nvp); avoid breastfeeding if safe alternatives available (in resource-rich settings); c-section if viral load 1000 at delivery 10. clinical pearls 10. clinical pearls treat all hiv patients regardless of cd4 (start trial) dtg-based regimen: preferred first-line worldwide screen hla-b5701 before abacavir — mandatory u=u: undetectable viral load = no sexual transmission immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (iris): paradoxical worsening after starting art (especially if cd4 very low); occurs weeks to months after art initiation; common with tb, mac, cryptococcus, pml; treat underlying oi, continue art, steroids if severe when to start art in ois: immediately for most ois; delay 4–6 weeks for cryptococcal meningitis (risk of iris → increased icp → death) and tb meningitis (delay 2–8 weeks) cryptococcal meningitis: check serum crag if cd4 100 before starting art; if positive, treat cryptococcus first top 10 testable facts: 4th generation ag/ab combo: preferred initial screening test treat all patients regardless of cd4 count (start trial) dtg-based regimen: preferred first-line (who/dhhs) hla-b5701: screen before abacavir viral load goal: undetectable (50) by 6 months u=u: undetectable = untransmittable pep: within 72 hours, 28 days of 3-drug art iris: paradoxical worsening after art initiation; delay art for cryptococcal meningitis (4–6 weeks) most common cause of treatment failure: non-adherence cd4 200 or aids-defining illness = aids top 5 pimping questions: when do you start art? (immediately for all hiv patients — except delay for cryptococcal meningitis and tb meningitis) what must you screen before starting abacavir? (hla-b5701) what is the preferred first-line art regimen? (dtg + tdf/taf + 3tc/ftc) what is iris? (paradoxical inflammatory worsening after art initiation due to immune reconstitution) what does u=u mean? (undetectable viral load = untransmittable — no sexual transmission) topic 67: opportunistic infections in hiv topic 67 · opportunistic infections in hiv cd4 thresholds for ois (high-yield — must memorize) cd4 threshold opportunistic infection prophylaxis references any cd4 tb, kaposi sarcoma, lymphoma, bacterial pneumonia — who, dhhs <500 oral candidiasis, herpes zoster (reactivation) — <200 pcp (pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia) tmp-smx ds daily (first-line); alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, aerosolized pentamidine <150 histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis (endemic areas) itraconazole (endemic areas) <100 cerebral toxoplasmosis, cryptococcal meningitis toxo: tmp-smx (same as pcp prophylaxis — covers both); crypto: screen serum crag; fluconazole if crag+ <50 cmv retinitis, mac (mycobacterium avium complex), pml mac: azithromycin 1200 mg weekly (if not on art); cmv: no primary prophylaxis, regular fundoscopy key ois — detailed: 1. pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (pcp) 1. pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (pcp) cd4 200; most common aids-defining illness presentation: subacute onset (days to weeks) of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, fever, hypoxia (especially with exertion); bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities on cxr/ct (interstitial pattern); spontaneous pneumothorax diagnosis: induced sputum or bal → silver stain (cysts), dfa; elevated ldh (nonspecific but sensitive); elevated beta-d-glucan treatment: tmp-smx (15–20 mg/kg/day of tmp component) iv for 21 days; add prednisone if pao2 70 or a-a gradient ≥35 (reduces mortality — steroids given before or with antibiotics) prophylaxis: tmp-smx ds daily when cd4 200; discontinue when cd4 200 for ≥3 months on art 2. cerebral toxoplasmosis 2. cerebral toxoplasmosis cd4 100; reactivation of latent toxoplasma gondii presentation: headache, fever, focal neurological deficits, seizures, altered mental status imaging: multiple ring-enhancing lesions (basal ganglia predilection) on contrast ct/mri diagnosis: clinical + imaging + positive toxoplasma igg (serology); empiric treatment → if no improvement in 2 weeks → biopsy (to rule out cns lymphoma) treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin (folinic acid — prevents bone marrow toxicity) for ≥6 weeks differential: cns lymphoma (single ring-enhancing lesion, ebv+ in csf, thallium spect positive) vs toxoplasmosis (multiple lesions, toxo igg+) 3. cryptococcal meningitis 3. cryptococcal meningitis cd4 100; cryptococcus neoformans presentation: subacute headache (weeks), fever, altered mental status, elevated icp (papilledema, cn vi palsy, vomiting); minimal meningeal signs (indolent) diagnosis: csf — elevated opening pressure (often 25 cmh2o), india ink (budding yeast with capsule — 60–80% sensitive), csf crag (cryptococcal antigen — 95% sensitive), csf culture (gold standard); serum crag (highly sensitive screening test) csf profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis (may be minimal), elevated protein, low glucose treatment (who 2022): induction: amphotericin b deoxycholate (1 mg/kg/day) + flucytosine (100 mg/kg/day) for 1 week (preferred); then fluconazole 1200 mg/day for 1 week consolidation: fluconazole 800 mg/day for 8 weeks maintenance: fluconazole 200 mg/day for ≥1 year (until cd4 200 for ≥6 months on art) icp management: serial therapeutic lumbar punctures (remove 20–30 ml csf to reduce pressure to 20 cmh2o or 50% of opening pressure); vp shunt if refractory delay art for 4–6 weeks (coat trial — early art increases mortality due to iris) 4. cmv retinitis 4. cmv retinitis cd4 50; most common cause of blindness in aids presentation: painless progressive visual loss, floaters, scotomata; fundoscopy: "pizza pie" or "cottage cheese and ketchup" appearance (yellow-white retinal necrosis with hemorrhage) treatment: valganciclovir (oral — preferred) or ganciclovir (iv) for induction (14–21 days) then maintenance; foscarnet if ganciclovir-resistant or intolerant side effects: ganciclovir — neutropenia (avoid with azt); foscarnet — nephrotoxicity, electrolyte abnormalities (hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia) 5. mycobacterium avium complex (mac) 5. mycobacterium avium complex (mac) cd4 50; disseminated infection presentation: fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, elevated alkaline phosphatase diagnosis: blood cultures (afb — mycobacterial blood culture); bone marrow biopsy treatment: clarithromycin (or azithromycin) + ethambutol ± rifabutin prophylaxis: azithromycin 1200 mg weekly when cd4 50 (if not on effective art) 6. kaposi sarcoma 6. kaposi sarcoma associated with hhv-8 (human herpesvirus 8); any cd4 count but more common with low cd4 presentation: violaceous (purple) papules/nodules on skin, oral mucosa, gi tract, lungs treatment: art (immune reconstitution is primary treatment); chemotherapy (liposomal doxorubicin) for extensive/visceral disease 7. progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (pml) 7. progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (pml) cd4 50–100; jc virus reactivation presentation: progressive focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, visual field deficits, cognitive decline) without mass effect or enhancement imaging: multifocal white matter lesions (non-enhancing, no mass effect) on mri diagnosis: csf jc virus pcr; brain biopsy if pcr negative treatment: no specific antiviral; art (immune reconstitution) is the only treatment; prognosis poor 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls pcp: bilateral ggo + elevated ldh + cd4 200; add steroids if pao2 70 toxo vs cns lymphoma: multiple ring-enhancing = toxo; single = lymphoma; empiric toxo treatment → biopsy if no response in 2 weeks crypto meningitis: elevated icp is the killer → serial lps; delay art 4–6 weeks cmv retinitis: cd4 50; "pizza pie" fundus; valganciclovir mac: cd4 50; disseminated; clarithromycin + ethambutol tmp-smx prophylaxis covers both pcp and toxoplasmosis screen serum crag when cd4 100 before starting art top 5 pimping questions: at what cd4 count do you start pcp prophylaxis? (200) what is the treatment for pcp and when do you add steroids? (tmp-smx 21 days; steroids if pao2 70 or a-a gradient ≥35) how do you differentiate toxoplasmosis from cns lymphoma? (toxo: multiple ring-enhancing lesions, igg+; lymphoma: single lesion, ebv+ csf) why delay art in cryptococcal meningitis? (risk of iris → increased icp → death; delay 4–6 weeks) what prophylaxis covers both pcp and toxoplasmosis? (tmp-smx) topic 68: dengue fever topic 68 · dengue fever 1. definition 1. definition acute febrile illness caused by dengue virus (denv serotypes 1–4), transmitted by aedes aegypti mosquito. endemic in southeast asia (including indonesia — highly relevant for fkui-rscm). 2. who 2009 classification 2. who 2009 classification category criteria references dengue without warning signs fever + 2 of: nausea/vomiting, rash, aches/pains, leukopenia, positive tourniquet test who 2009 dengue with warning signs above + any: abdominal pain/tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusion), mucosal bleeding, lethargy/restlessness, hepatomegaly ≥2 cm, rising hct with rapid platelet drop severe dengue any of: severe plasma leakage (shock — dss, respiratory distress from fluid accumulation), severe bleeding (gi), severe organ impairment (liver: ast/alt ≥1000, cns: encephalopathy, heart) 3. clinical phases (critical for management) 3. clinical phases (critical for management) febrile phase (days 1–3): high fever (40°c), headache (retro-orbital pain), myalgia/arthralgia ("breakbone fever"), maculopapular rash, facial flushing, positive tourniquet test, leukopenia critical phase (days 3–7 — around defervescence): plasma leakage occurs → hemoconcentration (rising hct ≥20%), pleural effusion, ascites, hypoproteinemia; thrombocytopenia nadir; risk of shock (dss) and hemorrhage; this is when patients deteriorate — not during fever recovery phase (days 7–10): reabsorption of extravasated fluid → risk of fluid overload/pulmonary edema if over-resuscitated; rash (confluent with white islands of sparing — "islands of white in a sea of red"); platelet recovery; appetite returns 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup test timing notes references ns1 antigen day 1–5 (early) most useful for early diagnosis; highly specific who igm anti-dengue day 5+ (after day 5) indicates recent/current infection igg anti-dengue secondary infection: rises early (day 1–2); primary: rises later high igg early = secondary infection (higher risk of severe dengue) cbc serial leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, rising hct (hemoconcentration = plasma leakage) tourniquet test bedside ≥20 petechiae per 1 inch2 = positive; suggests capillary fragility 5. management — primarily supportive (no antiviral) 5. management — primarily supportive (no antiviral) dengue without warning signs (outpatient): oral rehydration, paracetamol for fever/pain avoid nsaids and aspirin (increase bleeding risk) avoid im injections daily cbc monitoring; return immediately if warning signs develop dengue with warning signs (hospitalize): iv fluid resuscitation: isotonic crystalloid (ns or ringer's lactate) start: 5–7 ml/kg/hr for 1–2 hours → reduce to 3–5 ml/kg/hr for 2–4 hours → reduce to 2–3 ml/kg/hr → maintain based on clinical response monitor: hct every 4–6 hours, urine output (target ≥0.5 ml/kg/hr), vital signs reduce fluids when: hct drops, stable vitals, adequate urine output avoid excessive fluids (risk of fluid overload in recovery phase) severe dengue / dengue shock syndrome (dss): aggressive iv fluid bolus: 10–20 ml/kg over 15–30 minutes (crystalloid); repeat if needed if no improvement: switch to colloid (dextran, gelatin, or albumin) if hct rising despite fluids: ongoing plasma leakage → more fluids if hct dropping + unstable: suspect hemorrhage → blood transfusion (packed rbc) platelet transfusion: not routinely indicated for thrombocytopenia alone; only if active significant bleeding + platelets very low (20,000) icu admission for dss, severe bleeding, organ failure 6. red flags / warning signs (memorize) 6. red flags / warning signs (memorize) abdominal pain or tenderness persistent vomiting clinical fluid accumulation (ascites, pleural effusion) mucosal bleeding lethargy or restlessness hepatomegaly ≥2 cm lab: rising hct with rapid platelet drop 7. clinical pearls 7. clinical pearls critical phase occurs at defervescence (day 3–7) — not during fever; this is when patients crash rising hct = plasma leakage (hemoconcentration) — key monitoring parameter avoid nsaids/aspirin — use paracetamol only secondary dengue infection (different serotype): higher risk of severe dengue/dss (antibody-dependent enhancement — ade) tourniquet test: simple bedside screening tool fluid management is the cornerstone — too little → shock; too much → fluid overload in recovery platelet transfusion: not for numbers alone; only for significant bleeding dengue can mimic: leptospirosis, chikungunya, zika, typhoid, malaria — consider in differential top 5 pimping questions: when does the critical phase occur? (at defervescence — days 3–7, not during fever) what analgesic is safe in dengue? (paracetamol only — avoid nsaids/aspirin) what does rising hct indicate? (plasma leakage / hemoconcentration) when do you transfuse platelets? (only for significant active bleeding, not for low platelet count alone) why is secondary dengue more dangerous? (antibody-dependent enhancement — ade) topic 69: malaria topic 69 · malaria 1. definition 1. definition parasitic infection caused by plasmodium species, transmitted by female anopheles mosquito. five species infect humans: p. falciparum (most dangerous), p. vivax, p. ovale, p. malariae, p. knowlesi. 2. species comparison 2. species comparison feature p. falciparum p. vivax p. ovale p. malariae p. knowlesi severity most severe; cerebral malaria, death moderate mild mild can be severe geography tropical africa, se asia widespread (asia, latin america) west africa tropical se asia (zoonotic — macaques) rbc preference all ages (high parasitemia) reticulocytes (young rbcs) reticulocytes old rbcs all ages fever pattern irregular/daily tertian (48h) tertian (48h) quartan (72h) quotidian (24h) hypnozoites (liver dormancy) no yes yes no no relapse no (but recrudescence) yes (hypnozoites) yes (hypnozoites) no (but recurrence years later — persistent blood stage) no band forms on smear rare (sequestration) common common common common 3. clinical presentation 3. clinical presentation classic triad: fever (paroxysmal — cold stage → hot stage → sweating stage), anemia, splenomegaly nonspecific: headache, myalgia, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea severe/complicated malaria (p. falciparum): cerebral malaria: altered consciousness, seizures, coma (gcs ≤11) severe anemia: hb 5 g/dl or hct 15% hypoglycemia: blood glucose 40 mg/dl (2.2 mmol/l) — caused by parasite glucose consumption + quinine-induced insulin release metabolic acidosis: ph 7.25 or bicarbonate 15 mmol/l acute kidney injury: creatinine >3 mg/dl pulmonary edema / ards jaundice: bilirubin >3 mg/dl with parasitemia >100,000/μl hyperparasitemia: >5% (or >10% in some guidelines) dic / spontaneous bleeding shock (algid malaria) hemoglobinuria ("blackwater fever") 4. diagnostic workup 4. diagnostic workup test details references thick blood smear most sensitive for detecting parasites (screening) who thin blood smear species identification + parasitemia quantification rapid diagnostic test (rdt) detects hrp2 (p. falciparum) or pldh/aldolase (all species); useful in resource-limited settings; hrp2 can remain positive weeks after treatment (false positive) cbc anemia, thrombocytopenia (very common — does not indicate severity alone), leukopenia or normal wbc metabolic panel hypoglycemia, acidosis, renal function, lfts, bilirubin gold standard: microscopy (thick + thin smear) — repeat every 12–24 hours x 3 if initial smear negative and clinical suspicion high parasitemia quantification: essential for p. falciparum to assess severity and monitor treatment response 5. treatment 5. treatment a. uncomplicated malaria: species first-line treatment notes references p. falciparum (uncomplicated) artemisinin-based combination therapy (act): artemether-lumefantrine (coartem) or dha-piperaquine act is who-recommended first-line for all uncomplicated falciparum malaria; 3-day course who 2022 p. vivax / p. ovale act or chloroquine (if chloroquine-sensitive area) + primaquine (14 days) for radical cure (kills hypnozoites) must check g6pd before primaquine — risk of hemolytic anemia in g6pd deficiency; alternative: tafenoquine (single dose — also requires g6pd testing) p. malariae act or chloroquine no hypnozoites — no primaquine needed p. knowlesi act (preferred) or chloroquine can cause high parasitemia — monitor closely; treat as falciparum if severe b. severe/complicated malaria (medical emergency): iv artesunate: drug of choice for severe malaria (superior to iv quinine — seaquamat and aquamat trials showed 35% mortality reduction) dose: 2.4 mg/kg iv at 0, 12, 24 hours, then every 24 hours until patient can take oral medication switch to oral act to complete 3-day course once patient can tolerate oral side effect: delayed hemolytic anemia (post-artesunate delayed hemolysis — padh; occurs 1–3 weeks after treatment; monitor hb) if iv artesunate unavailable: iv quinine dihydrochloride (loading dose 20 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 10 mg/kg q8h); monitor for hypoglycemia (quinine stimulates insulin release), qtc prolongation, cinchonism (tinnitus, hearing loss) pre-referral treatment (if unable to reach hospital): rectal artesunate (single dose) or im artesunate/artemether → then refer immediately c. supportive care for severe malaria: correct hypoglycemia (dextrose bolus — check glucose frequently, especially with quinine) blood transfusion for severe anemia (hb 5) treat seizures (benzodiazepines) manage aki (fluids, dialysis if needed) exchange transfusion: no longer routinely recommended (who); consider in extreme hyperparasitemia (<30%) with organ dysfunction if available avoid: steroids (no benefit, may worsen outcomes), aspirin, heparin 6. primaquine — key points 6. primaquine — key points kills hypnozoites (liver dormant forms) of p. vivax and p. ovale → prevents relapse (radical cure) also has gametocidal activity against p. falciparum → reduces transmission (who recommends single low-dose primaquine 0.25 mg/kg with act for falciparum in low-transmission settings) must check g6pd status before prescribing — primaquine causes oxidative hemolysis in g6pd-deficient patients if g6pd deficient: use weekly primaquine (0.75 mg/kg x 8 weeks) with close monitoring, or tafenoquine is contraindicated in g6pd deficiency 7. chemoprophylaxis (for travelers) 7. chemoprophylaxis (for travelers) drug dosing notes references atovaquone-proguanil (malarone) daily; start 1–2 days before, continue 7 days after well-tolerated; expensive; contraindicated in severe renal impairment cdc doxycycline daily; start 1–2 days before, continue 28 days after cheap; photosensitivity, gi upset; also covers leptospirosis/rickettsial infections mefloquine weekly; start 2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after neuropsychiatric side effects (vivid dreams, anxiety, psychosis); contraindicated in seizure/psychiatric disorders chloroquine weekly; start 1–2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after only for chloroquine-sensitive areas (central america, middle east); not for most of africa/se asia 8. malaria in pregnancy 8. malaria in pregnancy higher risk of severe malaria, maternal anemia, low birth weight, stillbirth treatment: iv artesunate for severe malaria (all trimesters — benefit outweighs risk) uncomplicated: 1st trimester — quinine + clindamycin (7 days); 2nd/3rd trimester — act (artemether-lumefantrine) primaquine and tafenoquine: contraindicated in pregnancy (unknown fetal g6pd status) chemoprophylaxis: mefloquine (safe in all trimesters); doxycycline contraindicated 9. drug resistance patterns 9. drug resistance patterns chloroquine-resistant p. falciparum: widespread (most of africa, se asia, south america) → use act chloroquine-resistant p. vivax: parts of indonesia (papua), png → use act artemisinin partial resistance: greater mekong subregion (cambodia, myanmar, thailand, vietnam, laos) — delayed parasite clearance; partner drug resistance emerging → longer act courses or triple act being studied 10. clinical pearls 10. clinical pearls iv artesunate: drug of choice for severe malaria (not quinine) — seaquamat/aquamat trials check g6pd before primaquine — mandatory thick smear: screening (sensitive); thin smear: species id + parasitemia hypoglycemia in malaria: caused by parasite + quinine-induced insulin release → check glucose frequently thrombocytopenia is very common in malaria but does not define severity p. vivax/ovale: hypnozoites → relapse → need primaquine for radical cure p. falciparum: no hypnozoites but can recrudesce (incomplete treatment) blackwater fever: massive hemolysis → hemoglobinuria; associated with p. falciparum and quinine use post-artesunate delayed hemolysis (padh): monitor hb for 4 weeks after iv artesunate top 10 testable facts: p. falciparum: most dangerous; cerebral malaria; infects all rbc ages → high parasitemia iv artesunate: first-line for severe malaria (seaquamat/aquamat) act: first-line for uncomplicated falciparum malaria primaquine: kills hypnozoites (p. vivax/ovale); check g6pd first thick smear: sensitive (screening); thin smear: specific (species id) hypoglycemia: parasite consumption + quinine-induced insulin release p. vivax/ovale: tertian fever (48h); hypnozoites → relapse p. malariae: quartan fever (72h); no hypnozoites chloroquine resistance: widespread in p. falciparum; also in p. vivax (indonesia/papua) pregnancy: iv artesunate for severe (all trimesters); primaquine contraindicated top 5 pimping questions: what is the drug of choice for severe malaria? (iv artesunate — not quinine) why must you check g6pd before primaquine? (risk of oxidative hemolytic anemia in g6pd deficiency) which plasmodium species have hypnozoites? (p. vivax and p. ovale — require primaquine for radical cure) what causes hypoglycemia in malaria? (parasite glucose consumption + quinine-induced insulin release) what is the difference between thick and thin blood smear? (thick = sensitive/screening; thin = species identification + parasitemia quantification) topic 70: sepsis topic 70 · sepsis 1. definition — sepsis-3 (2016) 1. definition — sepsis-3 (2016) sepsis: life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection; defined as suspected/documented infection + acute increase in sofa score ≥2 points septic shock: subset of sepsis with circulatory, cellular, and metabolic abnormalities; defined as sepsis + vasopressor requirement to maintain map ≥65 mmhg + serum lactate <2 mmol/l despite adequate fluid resuscitation; mortality <40% old sirs criteria (≥2 of: temp <38°c or 36°c, hr <90, rr <20 or paco2 32, wbc <12,000 or 4,000 or <10% bands) — no longer used to define sepsis (too sensitive, not specific) but still clinically useful for screening 2. qsofa (quick sofa) — bedside screening 2. qsofa (quick sofa) — bedside screening ≥2 of 3 criteria (no labs needed): altered mental status (gcs ≤13) respiratory rate ≥22/min systolic bp ≤100 mmhg qsofa ≥2: identifies patients at higher risk of poor outcomes → prompts further assessment for organ dysfunction (sofa score), icu consideration note: qsofa is a prognostic tool, not a diagnostic criterion for sepsis; sepsis-3 uses sofa score for diagnosis 3. sofa score (sequential organ failure assessment) 3. sofa score (sequential organ failure assessment) assesses 6 organ systems: respiratory (pao2/fio2), coagulation (platelets), liver (bilirubin), cardiovascular (map/vasopressors), cns (gcs), renal (creatinine/urine output) each scored 0–4; total 0–24 acute increase ≥2 from baseline = organ dysfunction = sepsis (in setting of infection) 4. etiology 4. etiology most common sources: pulmonary (pneumonia — #1), urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft tissue, bloodstream (line-related) most common organisms: gram-negative (e. coli, klebsiella, pseudomonas), gram-positive (s. aureus, streptococcus, enterococcus); fungi in immunocompromised 5. surviving sepsis campaign (ssc) 2021 — hour-1 bundle 5. surviving sepsis campaign (ssc) 2021 — hour-1 bundle start all within 1 hour of sepsis recognition: measure lactate — if <2 mmol/l, remeasure within 2–4 hours (target: lactate clearance ≥20% or normalization) obtain blood cultures (≥2 sets, aerobic + anaerobic) before antibiotics — but do not delay antibiotics if cultures cannot be obtained quickly administer broad-spectrum antibiotics — within 1 hour of recognition; each hour of delay increases mortality by ~4–8% rapid iv fluid resuscitation: 30 ml/kg crystalloid (ns or balanced crystalloid — ringer's lactate preferred per smart trial) within first 3 hours for hypotension or lactate ≥4 vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation → target map ≥65 mmhg 6. empiric antibiotic selection 6. empiric antibiotic selection source empiric regimen references unknown source broad-spectrum: piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem, or cefepime ± vancomycin (if mrsa risk) ssc 2021 pulmonary (cap) ceftriaxone + azithromycin; or respiratory fluoroquinolone pulmonary (hap/vap) piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem or cefepime + vancomycin (or linezolid) if mrsa risk urinary ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone; piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem if esbl risk intra-abdominal piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem (covers anaerobes); or ceftriaxone + metronidazole skin/soft tissue vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam (covers mrsa + gram-negatives + anaerobes) neutropenic fever cefepime or meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam (anti-pseudomonal monotherapy) de-escalate antibiotics based on culture results and clinical response (within 48–72 hours) duration: typically 7–10 days (shorter courses increasingly supported — idsa) procalcitonin: can guide antibiotic de-escalation (trending down → consider stopping) 7. vasopressor management 7. vasopressor management agent mechanism role references norepinephrine alpha1 > beta1 agonist first-line vasopressor for septic shock ssc 2021 vasopressin v1 receptor agonist second-line: add to norepinephrine (up to 0.03 u/min) if map not at target; catecholamine-sparing vasst trial epinephrine alpha1 + beta1 + beta2 agonist third-line: add if map not at target with ne + vasopressin; increases hr, lactate (confounds monitoring) phenylephrine pure alpha1 agonist avoid in septic shock (reduces cardiac output);
