only if significant tachyarrhythmia dopamine dose-dependent (d, beta, alpha) not recommended as first-line (more arrhythmias than ne); may consider only in highly selected patients with bradycardia 8. corticosteroids in septic shock 8. corticosteroids in septic shock iv hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg q6h or continuous infusion): recommended for septic shock refractory to fluids + vasopressors (norepinephrine ≥0.25 μg/kg/min for ≥4 hours) adrenal trial: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone reduced 90-day mortality in septic shock approcchss trial: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone improved survival do not use random cortisol or acth stimulation test to guide steroid use in septic shock (ssc 2021) taper when vasopressors discontinued 9. additional management 9. additional management source control: identify and control source of infection (drain abscess, remove infected device, debride necrotic tissue) — ideally within 6–12 hours glucose management: target 144–180 mg/dl (avoid tight glucose control — nice-sugar trial: tight control increased mortality) dvt prophylaxis: lmwh preferred over ufh stress ulcer prophylaxis: ppi or h2 blocker for patients at risk (mechanical ventilation, coagulopathy) transfusion: restrictive strategy — transfuse if hb 7 g/dl (tricc trial, triss trial); exception: active hemorrhage, myocardial ischemia mechanical ventilation: if ards develops → low tidal volume ventilation (6 ml/kg ibw — ardsnet protocol) renal replacement therapy: for standard indications (refractory hyperkalemia, acidosis, fluid overload, uremia); no benefit of early initiation (starrt-aki trial) 10. lactate — key biomarker 10. lactate — key biomarker elevated lactate (<2 mmol/l): marker of tissue hypoperfusion and cellular dysfunction lactate <4 mmol/l: associated with high mortality; aggressive resuscitation needed serial lactate monitoring: target lactate clearance ≥20% every 2 hours (lactate-guided resuscitation reduces mortality — jansen et al.) lactate is not just from anaerobic metabolism — also from aerobic glycolysis (stress response), hepatic clearance impairment, epinephrine use 11. clinical pearls 11. clinical pearls sepsis = infection + organ dysfunction (sofa ≥2); septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors + lactate <2 hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors — all within 1 hour norepinephrine: first-line vasopressor (not dopamine) balanced crystalloids (lr) preferred over ns (smart trial — less hyperchloremic acidosis, less aki) antibiotics within 1 hour — each hour delay increases mortality source control is critical — antibiotics alone are insufficient if source not controlled hydrocortisone for refractory septic shock (not routine for all sepsis) avoid tight glucose control (target 144–180) procalcitonin: helps guide antibiotic de-escalation, not initiation top 10 testable facts: sepsis-3: infection + sofa ≥2 (organ dysfunction) septic shock: sepsis + vasopressors + lactate <2 mmol/l hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors norepinephrine: first-line vasopressor 30 ml/kg crystalloid bolus within 3 hours for hypotension/lactate ≥4 antibiotics within 1 hour — do not delay for cultures vasopressin: second-line (add to norepinephrine) hydrocortisone: for refractory septic shock (ne ≥0.25 μg/kg/min) lactate clearance ≥20% every 2 hours: resuscitation target source control within 6–12 hours top 5 pimping questions: what is the definition of sepsis per sepsis-3? (infection + acute sofa increase ≥2) what is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock? (norepinephrine) what is the initial fluid bolus for sepsis? (30 ml/kg crystalloid within 3 hours) when do you add hydrocortisone? (refractory septic shock despite fluids + vasopressors) what is the target map in septic shock? (≥65 mmhg) topic 71: septic shock topic 71 · septic shock (covered comprehensively within topic 70 — sepsis. key additional points below.) 1. definition recap 1. definition recap septic shock = sepsis + persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain map ≥65 mmhg + serum lactate <2 mmol/l despite adequate volume resuscitation. hospital mortality <40%. 2. hemodynamic profile 2. hemodynamic profile distributive shock: low svr (vasodilation), high cardiac output (initially — hyperdynamic), low map warm shock (early): warm extremities, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, tachycardia cold shock (late/decompensated): cool/mottled extremities, weak pulses, narrow pulse pressure, altered mental status — indicates cardiovascular collapse 3. vasopressor escalation ladder 3. vasopressor escalation ladder norepinephrine (first-line): start at 0.1 μg/kg/min, titrate to map ≥65 add vasopressin (0.03 u/min — fixed dose, do not titrate) if ne dose escalating add epinephrine if still refractory consider: dobutamine if evidence of myocardial dysfunction (low cardiac output despite adequate filling pressures — septic cardiomyopathy); angiotensin ii (giapreza) for refractory vasoplegia (athos-3 trial) 4. fluid responsiveness assessment 4. fluid responsiveness assessment static measures (cvp, pcwp): poor predictors of fluid responsiveness — do not use cvp to guide fluid resuscitation dynamic measures (preferred): passive leg raise (plr): raise legs 45° → if cardiac output increases ≥10% → fluid responsive pulse pressure variation (ppv) / stroke volume variation (svv): <12–13% in mechanically ventilated patients → fluid responsive ivc ultrasound: collapsibility <50% (spontaneous breathing) or distensibility <18% (mechanical ventilation) suggests fluid responsiveness fluid challenge: 250–500 ml bolus → reassess hemodynamics after initial 30 ml/kg, further fluids should be guided by dynamic assessment — avoid fluid overload (associated with worse outcomes) 5. septic cardiomyopathy 5. septic cardiomyopathy myocardial depression occurs in ~40% of septic shock patients characterized by: biventricular dilation, reduced ejection fraction, elevated troponin usually reversible within 7–10 days if patient survives management: dobutamine (inotrope) if low cardiac output; avoid excessive vasopressors (increase afterload on failing heart) bedside echo (point-of-care ultrasound — pocus): essential for assessing cardiac function, volume status, and guiding management 6. when to suspect adrenal insufficiency 6. when to suspect adrenal insufficiency refractory hypotension despite fluids + vasopressors history of chronic steroid use (iatrogenic adrenal suppression) waterhouse-friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage — meningococcemia) treatment: stress-dose hydrocortisone 100 mg iv bolus, then 50 mg q6–8h 7. endpoints of resuscitation 7. endpoints of resuscitation parameter target references map ≥65 mmhg ssc 2021 urine output ≥0.5 ml/kg/hr lactate normalizing (clearance ≥20% per 2 hours) mental status improving capillary refill time ≤3 seconds (andromeda-shock — crt-guided resuscitation non-inferior to lactate-guided) scvo2 ≥70% (if available — less emphasized in current guidelines) 8. clinical pearls 8. clinical pearls septic shock is distributive shock (low svr) — differentiate from cardiogenic (high svr, low co), hypovolemic (low cvp, low co), obstructive (elevated jvp, low co) cvp is not a reliable guide for fluid resuscitation — use dynamic measures passive leg raise: best bedside test for fluid responsiveness bedside echo (pocus): invaluable in shock — assess cardiac function, ivc, pericardial effusion capillary refill time: simple, effective resuscitation endpoint (andromeda-shock) septic cardiomyopathy: reversible; use dobutamine if low co fluid overload is harmful — reassess after initial bolus; do not blindly continue fluids top 5 pimping questions: what type of shock is septic shock? (distributive — low svr, high co initially) how do you assess fluid responsiveness at the bedside? (passive leg raise — co increase ≥10%) what is the vasopressor escalation order? (norepinephrine → vasopressin → epinephrine) when do you add dobutamine? (septic cardiomyopathy — low cardiac output despite adequate volume) why is cvp unreliable for guiding fluid resuscitation? (poor predictor of fluid responsiveness — does not reflect preload responsiveness)

resources.title: COMPREHENSIVE RAPID REVIEW — ALL 71 TOPICS

blocks.text: Child-Pugh and MELD scores for cirrhosis prognosis
HBV: HBsAg (active infection), anti-HBs (immunity), HBeAg (high replication), tenofovir/entecavir first-line
HCV: DAAs cure <95%; screen all adults once
Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose + rifaximin; West Haven grading
SBP: ascitic fluid PMN ≥250; ceftriaxone empiric; albumin day 1 and 3
Variceal bleeding: octreotide + ceftriaxone + endoscopic band ligation; propranolol/carvedilol for prophylaxis
SAAG ≥1.1 = portal hypertension
TB: 4-drug RHZE x 2 months → RH x 4 months; rifampin turns fluids orange
CAP: CURB-65 for severity; ceftriaxone + azithromycin
HAP: anti-pseudomonal + MRSA coverage if risk factors
Pleural effusion: Light's criteria (exudate vs transudate)
Pneumothorax: tension = needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL → chest tube
Tokyo Guidelines for cholangitis/cholecystitis
Ranson/BISAP/Atlanta for pancreatitis severity
GERD: PPI first-line; alarm features → endoscopy
IBD: UC = continuous/mucosal/rectum always; Crohn = skip/transmural/anywhere
Upper GI bleed: Glasgow-Blatchford (need for intervention); Rockall (mortality)
Lower GI bleed: colonoscopy within 24 hours after prep
AKI: KDIGO staging (Cr and UO); prerenal FENa 1%
CKD: eGFR staging; ACEI/ARB for proteinuria; SGLT2 inhibitors for all CKD with albuminuria
Nephrotic: <3.5 g/day proteinuria, edema, hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia
Nephritic: hematuria (RBC casts), HTN, oliguria, mild proteinuria
Dialysis: AEIOU (Acidosis, Electrolytes-K, Ingestion, Overload, Uremia)
Hyponatremia: assess volume status → correct slowly (≤8–10 mEq/L/24h — osmotic demyelination)
Hyperkalemia: ECG changes → calcium gluconate → insulin/dextrose → kayexalate/patiromer → dialysis
Iron deficiency: low ferritin (most specific), low iron, high TIBC, low MCV
Anemia of chronic disease: low iron, low TIBC, high/normal ferritin
Hemolytic anemia: ↑ LDH, ↑ indirect bilirubin, ↑ reticulocytes, ↓ haptoglobin, + Coombs (autoimmune)
DIC: ↑ PT/aPTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer, ↓ platelets, schistocytes
Neutropenic fever: ANC 500 + temp ≥38.3°C; cefepime/meropenem/pip-tazo immediately
Transfusion reactions: febrile nonhemolytic (most common), acute hemolytic (ABO mismatch — most dangerous), TRALI, TACO
ADHF: wet/warm most common; IV furosemide first-line; nitroglycerin if SBP <90
HFrEF: "four pillars" — ACEI/ARB/ARNI + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i
Hypertensive emergency: IV nicardipine/labetalol/nitroprusside; reduce MAP 25% in first hour
ACS: STEMI → PCI within 90 min (or fibrinolysis within 30 min); NSTEMI → risk stratify (GRACE/TIMI)
AF: CHA2DS2-VASc for stroke risk → anticoagulate if ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women); rate vs rhythm control
DM2: metformin first-line; SGLT2i/GLP-1RA if ASCVD/HF/CKD
DKA: insulin drip + aggressive fluids + K replacement; close AG gap before stopping insulin drip
HHS: more dehydrated, higher glucose, higher osmolality, no significant ketosis; fluid resuscitation is priority
Hypothyroidism: TSH best screening; levothyroxine; myxedema coma → IV T4 + hydrocortisone
Hyperthyroidism: Graves most common; RAIU differentiates causes; thyroid storm → PTU before iodine
RA: anti-CCP most specific; methotrexate + folic acid first-line; treat early
OA: LOSS on X-ray; weight loss + topical NSAIDs first-line; Heberden (DIP) / Bouchard (PIP) nodes
SLE: ANA most sensitive; anti-Sm most specific; anti-dsDNA correlates with nephritis; HCQ for ALL patients
Lupus nephritis Class IV: most common/severe → MMF or cyclophosphamide + steroids
Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone positive; spares kidneys/CNS; HIP drugs
Vasculitis: c-ANCA/PR3 = GPA; p-ANCA/MPO = MPA/EGPA; GCA = immediate steroids (visual emergency)
Anaphylaxis: epinephrine IM first-line — NO exceptions; glucagon for beta-blocker patients
SJS/TEN: stop drug immediately; SCORTEN for prognosis; HLA-B5801 (allopurinol), HLA-B1502 (carbamazepine)
HIV: treat ALL regardless of CD4 (START trial); DTG-based regimen preferred; HLA-B5701 before abacavir
OIs by CD4: PCP 200 (TMP-SMX), toxo 100 (pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine), crypto 100 (ampho B + flucytosine), CMV 50 (valganciclovir), MAC 50 (clarithromycin + ethambutol)
Delay ART 4–6 weeks for cryptococcal meningitis
Dengue: critical phase at defervescence (NOT during fever); paracetamol only; rising HCT = plasma leakage; platelet transfusion only for active bleeding
Malaria: IV artesunate for severe (SEAQUAMAT/AQUAMAT); ACT for uncomplicated; check G6PD before primaquine; thick smear = sensitive, thin smear = species ID
Sepsis-3: infection + SOFA ≥2; Hour-1 bundle; norepinephrine first-line vasopressor
Septic shock: NE → vasopressin → epinephrine; hydrocortisone for refractory shock; PLR for fluid responsiveness

resources.title: MASTER DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS QUICK REFERENCE

blocks.text: Fever + Altered Mental Status:
Meningitis/encephalitis, cerebral malaria, sepsis, DKA, HHS, hepatic encephalopathy, CNS toxoplasmosis (HIV), PML, thyroid storm, heat stroke, drug intoxication
Acute Kidney Injury — Prerenal vs Intrinsic vs Postrenal:
Microcytic Anemia Differential (MCV <80):
Iron deficiency anemia (most common worldwide)
Thalassemia (Mentzer index: MCV/RBC — <13 suggests thalassemia, <13 suggests IDA)
Anemia of chronic disease (can be normocytic or microcytic)
Sideroblastic anemia (ring sideroblasts on bone marrow)
Lead poisoning (basophilic stippling)
Normocytic Anemia (MCV 80–100):
Anemia of chronic disease (most common)
Hemolytic anemia (reticulocyte count elevated)
Acute blood loss
Aplastic anemia (pancytopenia)
CKD (decreased EPO)
Mixed deficiency (iron + B12/folate)
Macrocytic Anemia (MCV <100):
Megaloblastic: B12 deficiency, folate deficiency (hypersegmented neutrophils on smear)
Non-megaloblastic: liver disease, hypothyroidism, myelodysplastic syndrome, reticulocytosis, alcohol, drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, AZT)
Pancytopenia:
Aplastic anemia, MDS, leukemia (marrow infiltration), megaloblastic anemia (severe B12/folate deficiency), hypersplenism, SLE, HIV, miliary TB, HLH
Transudative vs Exudative Pleural Effusion (Light's Criteria):
Shock Classification:

rows: Tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade, massive PE

resources.title: MASTER EMERGENCY PROTOCOLS — RAPID REFERENCE

blocks.text: Epinephrine 0.3–0.5 mg IM (thigh) → repeat q5–15 min → IV fluids → supine position → adjuncts (antihistamines, steroids, nebulized salbutamol) → observe ≥4–6 hours → prescribe auto-injector

Calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% IV over 2–3 min (cardiac membrane stabilization — does NOT lower K) → Regular insulin 10 units + D50 25g IV (shifts K intracellularly) → Nebulized salbutamol 10–20 mg → Sodium bicarbonate (if acidotic) → Kayexalate/patiromer (K elimination) → Dialysis if refractory

IV NS 1–1.5 L/hr first hour → Regular insulin 0.1 U/kg bolus then 0.1 U/kg/hr drip → Replace K if <5.3 (hold insulin if K <3.3) → When glucose 200–250: switch to D5 + ½NS, reduce insulin to 0.02–0.05 U/kg/hr → Close anion gap before stopping drip → Overlap SC insulin 1–2 hours before stopping drip

Activate cath lab → Aspirin 325 mg chew + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor/clopidogrel) + heparin → PCI within 90 min (door-to-balloon) OR fibrinolysis within 30 min (door-to-needle) if PCI unavailable → Morphine (only if refractory pain), O2 (only if SpO2 <90%), nitroglycerin (if SBP <90, no RV infarct, no PDE5 inhibitor)

Airway protection (intubation if massive) → IV octreotide 50 μg bolus then 50 μg/hr → IV ceftriaxone 1g → Urgent endoscopy within 12 hours → Endoscopic band ligation → If refractory: balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore) as bridge → TIPS
Measure lactate → Blood cultures (≥2 sets) → Broad-spectrum antibiotics → 30 mL/kg crystalloid (if hypotension or lactate ≥4) → Norepinephrine if MAP <65 despite fluids

Clinical diagnosis (do NOT wait for CXR) → Needle decompression: 14–16G needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 4th/5th ICS anterior axillary line) → Followed by chest tube insertion (5th ICS anterior axillary line)

Sit upright → O2 (target SpO2 ≥94%) → IV furosemide 40–80 mg → IV nitroglycerin (if SBP <90) → CPAP/BiPAP (reduces intubation and mortality) → Morphine (use cautiously — respiratory depression) → Intubation if refractory

PTU 500–1000 mg loading then 250 mg q4h (blocks synthesis + peripheral T4→T3 conversion) → Iodine (SSKI/Lugol's) 1 hour AFTER PTU → Propranolol (beta-blocker — controls symptoms + blocks T4→T3) → Hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h (blocks T4→T3 + treats relative adrenal insufficiency) → Cooling measures → Treat precipitant

2 large-bore IVs → Crystalloid resuscitation → Type and crossmatch → Transfuse pRBC if Hb <7 (or <8–9 if active bleeding/hemodynamically unstable) → IV PPI 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr → Urgent endoscopy within 12–24 hours → If variceal: add octreotide + ceftriaxone

resources.title: MASTER LANDMARK TRIALS — QUICK REFERENCE

rows: Immediate ART (any CD4) reduces morbidity/mortality vs deferred
IV artesunate superior to IV quinine (35% mortality reduction)
Balanced crystalloids (LR) reduce MAKE vs NS
Tight glucose control (81–108) increases mortality vs conventional (144–180)
Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW) reduces mortality
Restrictive (Hb 7) non-inferior to liberal in most ICU/sepsis patients
SGLT2 inhibitors reduce HF hospitalization/CV death
Rituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide for induction
U=U — undetectable viral load prevents sexual transmission
Early ART increases mortality → delay 4–6 weeks
Hydrocortisone (± fludrocortisone) improves outcomes in refractory shock
Low-dose cyclophosphamide equally effective with fewer side effects
Plasma exchange no benefit for renal outcomes

resources.title: FINAL EXAM PREPARATION — "THE ULTIMATE 50 FACTS"

blocks.text: Sepsis = infection + SOFA ≥2; norepinephrine first-line; antibiotics within 1 hour
DKA: AG metabolic acidosis + hyperglycemia + ketonemia; insulin drip; replace K first if 3.3
STEMI: PCI within 90 min; DAPT + heparin + statin + beta-blocker + ACEi
HFrEF four pillars: ARNI/ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i
Hypertensive emergency: reduce MAP 25% in first hour; IV nicardipine or labetalol
AF: CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) / ≥3 (women) → anticoagulate with DOAC
Variceal bleed: octreotide + ceftriaxone + band ligation
SBP: PMN ≥250 → ceftriaxone + albumin
Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose (target 2–3 BMs/day) + rifaximin
SAAG ≥1.1 = portal hypertension
Child-Pugh A/B/C; MELD for transplant priority
HBV: tenofovir/entecavir; HCV: DAAs cure 95%
TB: RHZE 2 months → RH 4 months; rifampin = CYP inducer
CAP: CURB-65; ceftriaxone + azithromycin
Light's criteria: exudate if protein ratio 0.5, LDH ratio 0.6, or LDH 2/3 upper normal
Tension pneumothorax: clinical diagnosis → needle decompression → chest tube
Tokyo Guidelines for cholangitis/cholecystitis severity
Pancreatitis: NPO, IV fluids, pain control; no prophylactic antibiotics; early feeding
GERD: PPI 8 weeks; alarm features → EGD
UC: continuous, mucosal, always involves rectum; Crohn: skip, transmural, anywhere
AKI: prerenal FENa 1%; intrinsic FENa 2%
CKD: SGLT2i for all with albuminuria; ACEi/ARB for proteinuria
Nephrotic: 3.5 g/day proteinuria; nephritic: RBC casts + HTN
Dialysis indications: AEIOU
Hyponatremia: correct ≤8–10 mEq/L/24h (osmotic demyelination risk)
Hyperkalemia: calcium gluconate → insulin/dextrose → dialysis
Iron deficiency: low ferritin (most specific); ACD: high/normal ferritin, low TIBC
Hemolytic anemia: ↑ LDH, ↑ retics, ↓ haptoglobin, ↑ indirect bilirubin
DIC: ↑ PT/aPTT, ↓ fibrinogen, ↑ D-dimer, schistocytes
Neutropenic fever: ANC 500 + fever → immediate anti-pseudomonal (cefepime)
Transfusion: TRALI (within 6h, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema) vs TACO (fluid overload)
DM2: metformin first-line; SGLT2i/GLP-1RA if ASCVD/HF/CKD
HHS vs DKA: HHS = higher glucose, higher osmolality, no significant ketosis, more dehydrated
Hypothyroidism: ↑ TSH, ↓ free T4; myxedema coma → IV T4 + hydrocortisone
Hyperthyroidism: ↓ TSH, ↑ free T4; thyroid storm → PTU → iodine → beta-blocker → steroids
RA: anti-CCP most specific; MTX first-line DMARD
SLE: ANA sensitive; anti-Sm specific; anti-dsDNA = nephritis activity; HCQ for ALL
Lupus nephritis Class IV: most common/severe → MMF or cyclophosphamide
GCA: immediate steroids (do NOT wait for biopsy) — visual loss is irreversible
c-ANCA/PR3 = GPA; p-ANCA/MPO = MPA/EGPA
Anaphylaxis: epinephrine IM first-line; glucagon for beta-blocker patients
SJS/TEN: stop drug; HLA-B5801 (allopurinol); HLA-B1502 (carbamazepine)
HIV: treat ALL; DTG-based preferred; HLA-B5701 before abacavir; U=U
PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX when CD4 200; add steroids if PaO2 70
Crypto meningitis: ampho B + flucytosine; delay ART 4–6 weeks; serial LPs for ICP
Dengue: critical phase at defervescence; paracetamol only; rising HCT = plasma leakage
Malaria: IV artesunate for severe; ACT for uncomplicated; G6PD before primaquine
Septic shock: distributive (low SVR); PLR for fluid responsiveness; CVP is unreliable
Balanced crystalloids (LR) preferred over NS in sepsis (SMART trial)
Source control within 6–12 hours is critical in sepsis — antibiotics alone are insufficient

resources.title: NOTES

blocks.text: This concludes the FKUI-RSCM Internal Medicine High-Yield Study Guide covering all 71 topics across 9 subspecialties. This guide is designed for rapid review before ward rounds, case discussions, oral exams, and OSCE stations. All content is based on current international guidelines (WHO, KDIGO, ADA, AHA/ACC, ESC, IDSA/ATS, EASL, APASL, SSC, ACR/EULAR, NCCN, ACG, ECCO, Tokyo Guidelines) and landmark clinical trials.
